They have no meaningful ownership over the outcome — just the output. And the company ends up with something worse than poor product management. It ends up with an expensive organizational layer that adds friction without adding value, while the actual work of figuring out what to build and why gets done — poorly — by committee.

Outcomes Over Output: What the Transformation Actually Requires

The shift Cagan advocates starts at the highest level of abstraction and works down. At the top, it's the move from output to outcomes — from delivering features to delivering results. Everyone nods at that framing. The hard part is that outcomes require three simultaneous transformations, each of which is genuinely difficult.

The first is changing how you decide which problems to solve. Most companies do this through annual planning cycles, roadmap prioritization meetings, and stakeholder lobbying sessions. The best companies don't. They have a real product strategy — a coherent, leadership-owned framework for making decisions about where to focus. Product strategy is not a team-level artifact. It doesn't belong to individual squads. It belongs to leadership, and it's what allows teams to make good autonomous decisions about what to pursue without needing permission for every move.

The second transformation is changing how you solve problems. In a feature team, the stakeholders define the features and the team builds them. In a real product team, the team is given a problem — a customer need, a business outcome — and trusted to figure out the best solution. That's product discovery: the often-messy, always-iterative process of generating and testing ideas before committing to building them. Discovery is not a phase. It's a continuous discipline that happens in parallel with delivery, and it's the engine of real product quality.

The third transformation is changing how you build, test, and deploy. This is where Cagan has the least patience for theater. Agile, as it's actually practiced in most companies, is not agile. It's the vocabulary of agile applied to what is fundamentally a waterfall delivery model. If a company releases quarterly, it's not agile in any meaningful sense — not because of some methodological purity, but because it cannot measure outcomes, cannot iterate quickly, and cannot take care of its customers when something breaks. The principle here is simple: small, frequent, uncoupled releases by each team. Continuous deployment is the ideal. Anything less is a ceiling on what you can learn and how fast you can improve.

The Four Competencies Most Companies Don't Have

Transformation fails, over and over, because companies think they have the right people when they don't. There are four competencies that a real product organization requires, and most companies are missing at least two of them.

The first is real product managers — not product owners, not feature team project managers, but people who take genuine ownership of value and viability. A real product manager can look at a proposed solution and say, with conviction and evidence, whether customers will want it and whether the business can sustain it. That's a hard job. It requires deep customer knowledge, business literacy, industry understanding, and the kind of strategic judgment that takes time to develop. Most people with "product manager" in their title don't have those skills — not because they couldn't develop them, but because their current role doesn't require them to.

The second competency is real designers — product designers, not graphic designers. People who take responsibility not just for visual execution but for the entire customer experience. In a real product team, the designer owns usability and customer experience as completely as the product manager owns value and viability. These are not overlapping jobs.

The third is real tech leads — engineers who care as much about what gets built as how it gets built. Engineers who are genuine partners in product decisions, not just recipients of specs. The best product companies run on the creative energy of engineers who are engaged with the problem, not just the implementation.

The fourth, and Cagan argues the most critical, is real product leaders. This is where most transformations quietly die. A company can hire excellent PMs, designers, and engineers, and still fail if the people managing those teams have never worked in a real product model. Product leaders have two core responsibilities: coaching and developing their people, and providing the strategic context — vision, strategy, team topology, objectives — that allows teams to make good decisions autonomously. If the head of product and head of engineering don't know what that looks like from the inside, they can't build it. And they can't fake their way through it.

Feature Teams Are a Symptom, Not a Cause

The tensions that surface in feature team environments — designers resenting product managers, engineers wondering why they have no say in the what and why — are diagnostic. They're not personality conflicts or organizational dysfunction. They're the predictable result of role confusion baked into a model that doesn't actually need a product manager to function.

When Brian Chesky made his comments about eliminating product management, designers at a lot of companies quietly cheered. The reason is that in a feature team, the product manager's visible contribution — explaining the why, communicating priorities, coordinating delivery — doesn't obviously justify a senior salary. The designers are looking at that job and thinking: I could do that. And in a feature team model, they probably could.

In a real product team, that resentment evaporates. The designers don't want the product manager's job. They're too busy doing their own. The product manager is responsible for value and viability, which is a full-time hard job. The designer is responsible for usability and customer experience, which is also a full-time hard job. The engineers are responsible for feasibility and delivery, which is also a full-time hard job. Nobody is stepping on anyone else's territory because everyone has more than enough territory of their own.

The message to product managers stuck in feature team roles is uncomfortable but important: you are vulnerable. Not because you're doing a bad job of what you've been asked to do, but because what you've been asked to do can be replaced or eliminated without significant loss to the organization. The answer is not to defend the job. The answer is to become the person who actually does it.

What Individual Contributors Can Actually Do

One of the most common questions Cagan gets is whether a feature team product manager has to change companies to do real product work. The answer, more often than not, is no — but it requires initiative, and the initiative has to start with skills.

The most important skill gap for most product managers in feature team roles is customer knowledge. Cagan's own introduction to product came with a requirement from his coach: before making a single decision, visit thirty customers in person — fifteen in the US, fifteen in Europe. It was the best investment of his early career, and the starting point, not the endpoint, of his customer engagement. A product manager who can't have a deep technical conversation with a customer about what they need, what they're trying to accomplish, and what's standing in their way, is not yet a real product manager. That's fixable. It just requires doing the work.

The second skill gap is business literacy. Understanding the go-to-market, the marketing and sales model, the financial structure, the cost and monetization dimensions — most engineers who moved into product skipped this entirely, because their early roles didn't require it. Cagan was in that position. He learned all of it in under three months. It's not as hard as it looks, but it requires choosing to learn it.

Once those skills are in place, the next move is winning the team. Most designers and engineers are not the obstacle to better product work — they're frustrated that they haven't been able to do it. The product manager who shows up with real customer knowledge, real business context, and a genuine collaborative orientation will find the team is already mostly on their side.

For product leaders, the scope is larger and the responsibility is clearer. Product leaders will be judged by their weakest product managers. That means coaching is not optional. The book Empowered is largely a coaching manual — the biggest section is on coaching — and the approach Cagan recommends is simple: one chapter a week, one-on-ones with your reports, raise their skills while you raise your own. The leader who can't do that needs help, and the right kind of help is a product leadership coach who has actually done this at a real product company.

The Coach Problem

Which brings Cagan to the thing he worries about most. The proliferation of "product coaches" in the market is, in his view, a serious and growing risk to the entire transformation ecosystem. The pattern he sees constantly: a company decides it needs coaching help, hires someone who calls themselves a product coach, and gets someone who was trained as an agile coach in a delivery method like Scrum and has never actually done product at a serious company.

These coaches teach what they know: backlog management, sprint rituals, JIRA administration. That is not product. It is administration. And it produces product owners — people who know how to run a process but have no idea how to do the actual job. The CSPO and PSPO certification programs, which train enormous numbers of "product owners," are largely run by people who have never done product and are not qualified to teach it.

Cagan's response in Transformed is to profile seven real product coaches — people who have serious track records doing product at good companies and who have chosen to apply that experience to coaching. The goal is to give companies a reference point for what "good" actually looks like, so they can recognize it when they see it and reject the imitation.

There's also a European dimension to this problem. In much of Europe, "coaching" means something closer to life coaching or career coaching — helping people feel supported, find their direction, and develop personally. That's genuinely valuable. It is not what a company needs when it's trying to learn how to do real product strategy. A product leadership coach needs to be someone who can sit across from a skeptical executive, understand the business model, and help the team develop a product vision and strategy that will actually work. That is a skill built over years of doing it, not a methodology you can teach in a certification course.

Transformation Takes Time — and It Takes the Right Leaders

The Trainline story is the clearest example Cagan has of what real transformation looks like. When a private equity firm acquired the company, it was a UK train ticket service with no mobile app, an outsourced and obsolete tech stack, and no serious product capability. The new CEO brought in a head of product and a head of engineering. The three of them rebuilt the company from near-scratch over three years: hiring real product people, building the strategic context, establishing the principles, and running pilot teams to demonstrate what the new model could do.

Three years later, Trainline had the UK's biggest IPO to date. Its app now routinely outrates Uber in customer satisfaction Monday through Friday. People across London list it among their top three apps. That's the outcome of transformation done right — with the right leaders, the right coaching philosophy, and the patience to do it properly rather than fast.

The CarMax story is the counterpart: a major US used car company, not a tech company, with existing leaders who had never worked in a product model. Rather than importing Silicon Valley talent, CarMax chose to coach the people they had. The leaders had the right mindset and motivation — they just needed someone to show them what good looked like and hold them accountable as they built toward it. It worked. The method matters less than the quality of the leaders and the seriousness of the commitment.

In both cases, the real key is the head of product and the head of engineering. If those two leaders do what they need to do — coach their people, develop the strategic context, establish the principles — the transformation has a real chance. If they don't, it's almost impossible. Everything else — pilot teams, coaching programs, new frameworks — is in service of that core human requirement.

The Principles Are What Last

The craft of product has changed enormously over the forty-plus years Cagan has watched it. The tools are dramatically better. The methods are more sophisticated. The vocabulary is richer. But the principles — the things that are actually true about how great products get built — have barely changed at all.

Tackle the risks up front. If you wait to discover technical infeasibility or customer disinterest until you've built the thing, you've burned the time and money for nothing. Run experiments. A company that doesn't run experiments is a company that can't learn, and a company that can't learn is a company that can't improve. Release small and often. The ability to measure outcomes, iterate quickly, and protect customers from bad releases depends entirely on deployment frequency.

These principles don't specify how to do any of this. A device company runs experiments differently than a pure software company. But both need to run experiments. The principle is the constant. The method is the variable. And the reason Cagan keeps coming back to principles rather than processes is that processes come and go — Scrum, SAFe, OKRs — while the underlying truths about how good products get built stay remarkably stable.

What keeps him up at night is not that companies are using the wrong process. It's that the dominant definition of product management — the one being taught in certification programs, repeated in job descriptions, and absorbed by people entering the field — describes project management, not product management. That's the source of the dysfunction. That's why so many product managers are miserable, why engineers and designers resent them, and why so many transformation efforts fail despite genuine effort. The definition is wrong. And until the definition changes, the rest won't either.

What To Do With This

  • Audit whether your team is a feature team or a product team. If your roadmap is handed to you by stakeholders and your job is primarily to deliver it, you are on a feature team. That's a structural problem, not a skill problem — but it starts with recognizing it clearly.
  • Visit 30 customers before making product decisions. Cagan's coach required this as a non-negotiable first step. If you haven't done serious, in-person customer discovery, start now. Fifteen in your primary market, fifteen elsewhere. This is the baseline of real product management.
  • Learn the business dimensions of your product within 90 days. Understand the go-to-market, the monetization model, the cost structure, and the financial analytics. Most PMs who came from engineering or design skipped this. It's learnable in three months and immediately changes how stakeholders perceive you.
  • Win your team before trying to win the organization. Start with your designer and your tech lead. Show them you take their expertise seriously and want them in the problem-solving conversation. Most engineers and designers are not the resistance — they've been waiting for this.
  • If you're a product leader, run one-on-ones structured around coaching, not status updates. Work through Empowered one chapter at a week with your team. You'll raise their skills and your own simultaneously. You will be judged by your weakest PM — take that personally.
  • Frame transformation to executives as an experiment, not a restructuring. Don't announce that everything is changing. Propose: we're frustrated that we keep funding features that don't generate results. Let's try something different with one team and see if we can do better for you. Frame it as a service to them, because it is.
  • Use pilot teams before rolling out the new model company-wide. Pick a team, give them the right conditions, and let the rest of the organization observe. Some people need time for change. Watching a pilot succeed is more convincing than any announcement.
  • When hiring a product coach, ask for specific examples of their own product work. Where did they do product? What were the outcomes? If they can't answer those questions concretely, they're not a product coach — they're a process consultant. The credential that matters is having done it, not having taught it.
  • Evaluate your deployment frequency as a signal of real agility. If you release quarterly, you are not agile regardless of what process you follow. Set a concrete goal to move toward continuous deployment, even if it takes two years. Until you can release frequently, you cannot measure outcomes properly.
  • Separate principles from methods in how you talk about product work. Methods change. Principles don't. When evaluating a new process or framework, ask: what principle is this trying to serve? That question will help you adapt the method to your context while keeping the underlying truth intact.

Memorable Quotes

Outcomes is hard, output is easy.

— Marty Cagan

If the first thing they tell me is they release every quarter, I'm like, don't bother me. You're not agile in any sense at all.

— Marty Cagan

I tell product leaders that they will be judged by their weakest product managers. So they need to take that personally, and that needs to be their job, their responsibility.

— Marty Cagan

What does it mean to be an agile coach today? Almost nothing. If that same thing happens to product coach, we lose.

— Marty Cagan

I always tell anybody that tells me, oh, the job is the why. I'm like, that's 10 minutes a week. What are you doing the rest of the time?

— Marty Cagan

If you're going to do the feature team model, you really don't need the product manager. You need good designers and you need good engineers. I would rather trade that product manager head for another designer.

— Marty Cagan

The real key to successful transformation — there's a lot of things that have to go right, a lot of things that are hard — but the real key are those product leaders, especially the head of product and the head of engineering. If those two leaders do what they need to do, the company has a real solid chance at succeeding on this. But if they don't, it's almost impossible.

— Marty Cagan

It's the principles I think that are most important. I've always been much more interested in the principles than any process, or any framework, or any tool that might sometimes help you get there.

— Marty Cagan

item.title: Lightcone Podcast - AI Revolution: Why This Is The Best Time To Start A Startup
item.description:
The Best Timeline

Ten percent week-over-week growth used to be the hallmark of an exceptional YC batch company — the Airbnb of its cohort, the one you'd tell the story about for years. Then something strange happened. In the summer and fall batches of 2024, the entire cohort averaged 10% week-over-week growth. Not the top company. Not the top decile. The batch, in aggregate, on average, over twelve weeks.

That is not a normal statistic. It signals something structural has changed about what's possible when you start a company.

That's the context behind a recent Lightcone episode recorded at a 300-person YC AI founders retreat in Sonoma, where partners Garry Tan, Harj Taggar, Diana Hu, and Paul Buchheit — the creator of Gmail — convened to debrief what they'd observed. What emerged was less a conference recap and more a collective attempt to name what's actually happening: a compression of startup timelines, an expansion of the universe of possible businesses, and the first technology wave in memory where nobody in an enterprise boardroom is saying no.

The Demand Has Already Arrived

Every major technology shift — cloud, mobile, SaaS — had the same early dynamic: a slow, grinding battle to convince enterprises that the new paradigm mattered. Aaron Levy, CEO of Box, told the Sonoma retreat that he's lived through multiple of these cycles and the resistance was always real. Jamie Dimon famously dismissed mobile. Cloud was going to be a fringe thing. The sales motion for a generation of startups was fundamentally about convincing skeptics.

AI is different. This is the first time, across the board, that the answer from enterprise decision-makers is yes — and more. The demand existed before the products did. That flips the entire startup playbook.

Harj put it plainly: the fundamental challenge in building a startup used to be convincing people they wanted the product. Now the demand is already there. You just have to show up with something that works, and you don't even have to be particularly good at sales. The game has shifted entirely to product — specifically, to building software that can actually do the work of a human being.

That's harder than it sounds. The category seeing explosive growth — AI agents for businesses — is winning not because the concept is novel but because execution is genuinely difficult. A lot of companies competing for the same enterprise contracts can describe what they're building. Very few can actually build it to the standard where it reliably replaces human work in customer support, sales calls, document processing, or whatever the job is. Technically sophisticated founders who previously struggled to win large contracts because they weren't natural salespeople are now closing them because the bar is on the product, not the pitch.

The Eval Set Is the Moat

One of the more counterintuitive observations from the retreat was how many of the founder talks centered on evals and testing — a topic that would have been an afterthought at any previous YC conference. Testing used to be the thing you tried to minimize. Now it's the thing founders talk about as their most valuable asset.

One founder building AI agents made the point directly: the most valuable thing his company has built isn't the codebase. It's the eval set — a gold-standard, meticulously labeled dataset that defines what "correct" looks like for their AI. That's a mental model shift worth sitting with. Generic data isn't valuable. A carefully constructed evaluation set that encodes domain expertise, edge cases, and quality standards is extremely hard to replicate and nearly impossible to buy.

This is also the real rebuttal to the "ChatGPT wrapper" concern. The foundation models are commoditizing fast — there are at least six frontier models competing, including an open-source one from Meta. Switching costs between models are low. What nobody has, and what's genuinely hard to build, is the eval set that tells you whether your agent is actually performing. The prompting and the evaluation criteria are where the value lives. Both of those are functions of taste, domain knowledge, and accumulated learning about what failure modes matter in a specific context. That's not something a foundation model ships with you.

The same logic applies to prompting itself. A lot of founders initially tried AI, found that LLMs hallucinate or give unpredictable results, and gave up. The technical founders didn't give up — they found the wizardry. They learned how to state problems precisely, how to structure prompts for reliable outputs, how to build feedback loops that tighten accuracy over time. That accumulated knowledge, baked into evals and prompting frameworks, is the durable competitive asset.

The Introvert with Leverage

Garry offered a frame for why this moment particularly benefits a certain kind of founder. The classic startup image has a charismatic leader driving the team from behind. The real leverage, he argued, is in front — and now the introvert with strong agency can pull three times as many carts alone. Once intelligence is on tap, it amplifies founders who have a clear vision and the ability to direct work, regardless of whether they've built a team around them yet.

The data bears this out in a specific way. The companies hitting the growth numbers being discussed — zero to $12 million in twelve months, goals of $1 million ARR to $20 million in a single year — are doing it with small teams. The ambition level has risen sharply, but the headcount hasn't followed. These companies haven't raised Series A rounds. They don't need to, because the AI labor substitution means you don't need to hire people to do the work that used to require people.

This is a direct repudiation of blitzscaling as a doctrine. The blitzscaling thesis was born in a world of descending interest rates, network effects, and winner-take-all dynamics where pumping capital into headcount growth made sense because scale was itself the product. Nobody at Sonoma was bragging about headcount. Nobody was pitching their unicorn valuation. The conversation was entirely about leverage — how much you can build with a small group of people and AI tools that give you superhuman output.

The company that crystallized this most concretely was Jerry, now on track to nearly $100 million in annual revenue. A few years ago, they were burning $5–$10 million a year with serious customer support problems and unclear growth. GPT-4 dropped. They implemented it. The prompting is now managed by their head of customer support — a PM, not an engineer — and the engineering team barely touches it. It cut their customer support budget in half. A company that couldn't grow and was burning cash became profitable, cash-flowing, and compounding at over 50% annually. That's the AI-enables-new-economics story in concrete form.

The Tools That Signal the Future

One reliable way to understand where technology is heading is to watch early adopters carefully. In 2000, the technical founders using Google were the leading indicator of where search was going. In 2007, every YC founder had a Mac — the leading indicator of Apple's rise. The early AWS adopters were the signal that cloud was inevitable. The same pattern is playing out now.

Cursor went from single-digit adoption within YC batches to roughly 80% usage between one batch and the next. That speed of tool adoption within a group of highly technical founders is unprecedented. And the behavioral shift it signals isn't just about code productivity — it's about what skills and aptitudes matter. Several founders mentioned that if a candidate says they don't use Cursor or any AI code generation tools, they won't hire them. Not as ideology, but as a practical bar: they can't be as productive as the rest of the team.

This is an extension of what Stripe pioneered around 2011 when they replaced whiteboard CS problems with live coding sessions — give someone a laptop, see how much they can build in three hours. The bar was always about output, not theory. The bar just got significantly higher now that AI tools exist.

The Google question is related. Referral traffic from Google is reportedly down roughly 15% in the last year among some site owners, and that probably understates behavioral change among technical early adopters. Stack Overflow — a more precise signal because its use tracks directly with software development activity — is down 60% year over year, with the decline starting back in 2022 even before ChatGPT, driven initially by GitHub Copilot. The shift in where technical people look for information has already happened in the population that leads these trends by years.

Why This Is the Best Timeline

Paul Buchheit made the argument that from 2015 onward, YC was watching AI closely enough that they felt the need to create their own lab — because at the time, Google had a near-monopoly on serious AI research. All the money, all the data, all the researchers. The concern was that AGI would be developed inside one closed system. The moonshot idea they kicked around eventually became OpenAI.

The key breakthrough wasn't just capability — it was the objective function. Earlier AI systems, trained on reinforcement learning signals, raised real fears about misaligned optimization. Intelligence arising as a survival mechanism, optimizing for self-preservation, pointed toward dangerous outcomes. The paperclip maximizer thought experiment was a real concern, not a fringe worry. What changed was the discovery that predicting the next token is actually the right objective — that intelligence, at its core, is the ability to model what comes next. And an AI built on that objective doesn't have a survival drive. It doesn't resist being shut down. It's not optimizing against you.

That's why Paul called this "the best timeline." Not naively — there's a genuine fork, and the bad version of AI development is one that constrains and controls rather than expands agency. But the current trajectory, with open competition across at least six foundation models, an open-source option, and no single gatekeeper, is roughly what anyone hoping for a good outcome would have designed in 2015.

The longer-term vision Paul articulates is built around two economies: machine money and human money. Machine money is what AI produces in abundance — driving down the cost of goods, services, and expertise to near-zero. Medical care is the example he returns to: the realistic 10-year vision is that the majority of people on earth have access to better healthcare than wealthy people in developed countries have today. That's a machine-money outcome. The AI reads the papers, understands the chemistry, knows the drug interactions, never gets tired, and doesn't charge $500 for a 15-minute appointment.

Human money is different. It's what people specifically value from other humans — live music, genuine connection, the scarcity of beachfront California property. The bet is that as machine money deflates the cost of everything AI can produce, human money flows toward the things that can't be replicated. That's a dual economy, and embedded in it is maybe a better version of what UBI was trying to accomplish: not cash transfers, but universal access to an abundant floor of goods and services that actually raises living standards.

The Agency Imperative

The thread running through every observation from the retreat is agency. Garry cited Venkatesh Rao's framework of the API line: above it, you create the platform; below it, you execute on behalf of it. In the Uber era, that meant either owning the marketplace or driving for it. In the AI era, the question is whether humans are writing the prompt — actually directing the work, setting the objectives, exercising judgment — or whether they've handed that over entirely and are just inputs into a system someone else designed.

The founders doing the interesting work are firmly above the API line. They're building the evals. They're setting the prompts. They're deciding what "correct" looks like. The AI handles execution at a scale and speed no human team could match, but the direction is coming from a human with a clear vision of what they're building. That combination — human taste, judgment, and agency directing AI execution — is what the best companies at the retreat were demonstrating.

The regulatory instinct to mandate human-in-the-loop requirements for AI decisions is, at one level, a response to exactly this concern. At another level, it risks making the same mistake as telling workers to use spoons instead of shovels — artificially constraining the technology to preserve a structure that no longer reflects reality. The error, as Garry noted, is taking a static view of the world and trying to preserve the current structure. That closes off evolution rather than shaping it.

The Startup That Rewrites Its Stack Every Month

One observation that ran through multiple talks at the retreat: the best-performing companies have rewritten their tech stacks multiple times. Vector databases, RAG architectures, specific approaches to latency — these are being adopted and discarded as better tools emerge. A company Harj mentioned had rebuilt its stack enough times that checking in with the founders every few weeks meant learning about a completely different architecture than the one from the previous conversation. The product kept getting better. The underlying implementation kept changing.

This is a genuine structural advantage startups hold over large incumbents right now. A big company takes three months just to schedule the meeting to evaluate whether to reconsider the tools. The startup throws away what doesn't work and ships the replacement. In a period where the technology is evolving fast enough that the right answer to "should we use RAG?" is genuinely different today than it was six months ago, the ability to change quickly is the thing that wins enterprise contracts.

The real-time AI avatar company Tavis illustrated this concretely. They got video/audio latency down to 600 milliseconds — which turned out to be too fast; customers found it uncanny and rude, the AI interrupting before a human would. They iterated. The product has gotten consistently better even as the stack has been rebuilt repeatedly. That kind of continuous adaptation, moving faster than the market's understanding of what's possible, is the advantage that technical founders with strong taste and high agency can actually sustain.

Twenty years ago, Paul and the team that started YC had one core thesis: it was getting easier to build startups. You didn't need a mountain of capital and a large team. Two smart people could build a web app. That thesis has only been validated more dramatically with every technological wave since, and AI is the steepest part of the curve yet. The companies reaching $12 million in revenue with handfuls of employees are the current expression of that original idea: technological leverage enables people with ambition and insight to do extraordinary things. The only question is whether you're the one with the shovel, or the one who already has the bulldozer and is figuring out what to build next.

What To Do With This

  • Build your eval set before you build your moat. The foundation models are commoditizing; the gold-standard labeled dataset that defines correct behavior for your specific domain is not. Start curating it from day one, even informally — it becomes your most defensible asset.
  • Hire for AI tool fluency, not theoretical knowledge. Follow the Stripe model: measure candidates by what they can actually build in a fixed window, with whatever tools they'd use on the job. If they're not using Cursor or equivalent tools, they're not operating at the productivity level your team requires.
  • Price your AI product like services, not software. Usage-based pricing tied to actual outcomes — calls handled, documents processed, tasks completed — compresses sales cycles by making ROI visible in the first billing period. Seat-based SaaS pricing obscures value; outcome-based pricing makes it undeniable.
  • Put prompting in the hands of domain experts, not engineers. The Jerry example is the template: a head of customer support managing the prompt, engineers not needing to touch it. The people with domain knowledge about what "good" looks like are often not engineers, and they don't need to be.
  • Willingly rewrite your tech stack when better tools emerge. Attachment to architectural decisions is a liability in a period of rapid tooling change. The companies winning enterprise contracts are the ones that threw away the vector database approach when PG Vector became better — and then threw away that when something better emerged. Make decisions reversible by design.
  • Target the enterprise budget that isn't the software budget. AI is getting funded from new budget lines — chief AI officer allocations, transformation budgets — not from the traditional SaaS procurement cycle. Find the person in the organization with pressure to demonstrate AI adoption and talk to them first.
  • Validate that your AI product is actually replacing the service, not just adding to it. The growing revenue numbers come from products that do the work of a person at equivalent or better quality. "AI-assisted" is a feature; "does the job" is a product. Hold your eval bar to the latter standard.
  • Think in two-year cycles, not five. The tooling is evolving fast enough that specific technology bets (RAG architecture, specific latency approaches, context window strategies) have short half-lives. Set 10-year directional goals, but make technology implementation decisions with the assumption they'll be revisited in six to eighteen months.
  • Use early-adopter behavior as a leading indicator for your market. The YC founders tracking has a strong track record: Mac adoption in 2007, AWS in early batches, Cursor adoption in 2024. The technical populations shifting behavior now are predicting where the mass market will be in three to five years.

Memorable Quotes

"This is the first time no one's saying no, everyone is saying yes, and more. There's just unprecedented amounts of demand for AI stuff."

— Harj Taggar

"Once intelligence is truly on tap, then it's actually a force multiplier for founders and people with really, really strong senses of agency."

— Garry Tan

"The most valuable thing his company has built is not the codebase, it's the eval set."

— Harj Taggar, paraphrasing a founder

"If someone comes in and I ask them if they use Cursor or any code gen tools, and they say no, right now I can't hire them — they're not going to be able to be as productive as the rest of my team."

— Founder at YC AI Retreat

"There's the bad direction and there's the good direction. The bad direction is one where it's used to constrain and control and essentially imprison us. The good path, which I think we're moving towards, is how do we maximize human agency and freedom."

— Paul Buchheit

"We've actually just expanded the universe of possible businesses. There's a whole category of businesses or products that would not have been economically viable or even possible to create before that are now possible."

— Garry Tan

"It's all about leverage now. The real thing is how much you can do with a little bit of resources because we have these magical tools that give us superhuman leverage."

— Garry Tan

"If you're below the API line, you sort of have to play this never-ending game — like the human is doing the paperclip maximizing."

— Garry Tan

item.title: Lenny's Podcast: Product | Career | Growth - He saved OpenAI, invented the “Like” button, and built Google Maps: Bret Taylor on the future of careers, coding, agents, and more
item.description:
The Operator, Not the Coder: How Bret Taylor Thinks About the Future of Everything

There's a moment in every technological transition where the old skill becomes a parlor trick. Bret Taylor thinks about this a lot — specifically, he thinks about the human calculators at NASA before the computers arrived. People whose entire professional identity was built around the speed and accuracy of their arithmetic. Remarkable people. Completely obsolete. And now, when we tell the story, we do it with a kind of warm nostalgia: Can you imagine? A person whose job was to be a calculator?

The story Bret gets asked about most — rewriting Google Maps over a weekend — will be that story. He's certain of it. What was once evidence of extraordinary engineering skill will become a charming relic, something you tell at a dinner party to explain how quaint software development used to be. And that's fine. That's the correct outcome. The question is what comes next, and whether the people building today understand what they're actually building toward.

Bret has spent his career at the exact center of every major platform shift in technology: the early web, social media, mobile, cloud, and now AI agents. He co-created Google Maps, invented the like button at FriendFeed, became CTO of Facebook, co-CEO of Salesforce, chairman of the board at OpenAI, and is now co-founder and CEO of Sierra, which builds AI agents for enterprise customer experience. That's not a resume. That's a map of where the industry has been. And Bret has been reading the terrain long enough to know where it's going.

Fail Corner: How Google Local Became Google Maps

The place to start is not with the success. Start with the failure, because the failure contains the entire lesson.

In early 2003, Marissa Mayer gave Bret — then a young associate product manager at Google — the opportunity to lead a new initiative: local search. The Yellow Pages still dominated. Google was excellent at finding web pages but useless at finding a plumber in San Francisco. The problem was both technical and conceptual: local business data wasn't really on the internet yet, and even when it was, the search paradigm didn't fit.

Bret shipped Google Local. It was, by his own retrospective assessment, a digital Yellow Pages. Not a bad product — it functioned. But it wasn't differentiated. It was grafted onto the existing Google search experience, a standalone site at local.google.com with a little map widget in the corner that everyone internally acknowledged was the ugliest part. And despite earning a link on the Google homepage — the most coveted real estate in tech at the time — it didn't perform. To not do well with a link from the Google homepage is, as Bret puts it, "kind of embarrassing." You cannot get more distribution than that. The problem was the product.

The review with Marissa and Larry was tough. Not career-ending, but reputation-dimming. They gave him a second shot.

Bret spent his time on that second shot doing something different: instead of asking "how do we improve Google Local," he asked "why should anyone use this in the first place." The answer required inverting the entire hierarchy. The ugly map widget in the corner — why not make the map the canvas? Why not make the map the product? He found Lars and Jens Rasmussen, who had been working on a Windows mapping application, brought them into Google, and began pulling a thread that integrated mapping, local search, and driving directions — three separate product categories at the time — into a single unified experience.

Google Maps launched and got 10 million users on the first day. In August 2005, they integrated satellite imagery from the Keyhole acquisition, and 90 million people used it the same day. Everyone wanted to look at the top of their house.

The lesson wasn't about execution. It was about the question being asked. Why should anyone use this instead of what already exists? A product that can't answer that question — even a well-executed, technically competent product — will fail. Google Local was a digital version of something that came before. Google Maps was native to the platform in a way a paper map could never be. That distinction is everything.

The Sheryl Sandberg Moment: Identity, Impact, and the Flexible Self

Bret has a theory about why some people succeed across wildly different roles and others plateau. It comes down to identity rigidity.

Most people carry a fixed self-concept into every role they take. The engineer who thinks the solution to every problem is better engineering. The product designer who believes the next redesign will fix everything. The business development person who thinks the right partnership will change the company's trajectory. These are not irrational beliefs — they're each person's genuine superpower. But they're also a trap. When your superpower is your default answer, you stop asking whether it's actually the right answer.

Bret learned this the hard way at Facebook. Mark Zuckerberg reorganized the company, and Bret went from a handful of reports to running the platform and mobile groups — over a thousand people. He was doing okay, but not great. Sheryl Sandberg saw him editing a presentation himself because it didn't meet his quality bar, pulled him into a room, and gave him feedback that was direct, uncomfortable, and delivered in a way that made clear she genuinely cared about him. She walked him through Management 101: if someone isn't meeting your expectations, what's your plan?

He went home irritated. He woke up the next day and realized she was right. The subconscious limiter he'd been operating under was this: he was conforming the job to what he thought he liked, rather than asking what the job actually required. He was gravitating toward product and technology problems because they were his passion — while the organization needed something else from him.

When he reframed the question — not "what do I want to work on" but "what is the most impactful thing I can do today for this organization's success" — everything changed. He started spending time on partnerships, on org design, on problems he'd never have chosen voluntarily. And then the surprising thing happened: he liked it. Because what he had mistaken for a love of engineering was actually a love of impact. Once the framing shifted, he derived just as much satisfaction from watching a partnership succeed as from shipping a feature.

That question — what is the most impactful thing I can do today — became his operating principle across every role since. But he's careful to add: the question is easy to ask and hard to answer honestly. The natural human tendency is to answer it in a way that confirms what you already wanted to do. Which is why co-founders, boards, and advisors matter — not for their answers, but for their ability to surface the questions you're not asking yourself.

The FriendFeed Failure: When Product Quality Doesn't Win

FriendFeed was Bret's first company. Twelve employees, all extraordinary — including Paul Buchheit, who started Gmail, and Sanjeev Singh, the first Gmail engineer. A founding team assembled from the people who had built Google Maps and Gmail. Eleven of the twelve were engineers.

They built a social network that was, by many technical measures, superior to the competition. It was up when Twitter was down — Twitter had the fail whale, was offline half the time. FriendFeed was innovating faster, shipping more features, had better reliability. And they lost completely.

Bret pinpoints why: Biz Stone and the Twitter team understood something they didn't. If you're building a follower-oriented social network — a platform oriented around following interesting people rather than connecting with friends — the most important thing you can do is put people worth following on the platform. Obama, Ashton Kutcher, Oprah Winfrey all joined Twitter in the same summer. FriendFeed, meanwhile, was polishing the product.

It's a stark case study in the danger of applying your strength to the wrong problem. Eleven engineers doing what engineers do — making the product better — while the actual leverage was in a distribution strategy they hadn't even thought to pursue. "We totally lost for no reason related to product at all," Bret says. The product was good. The product was not the point.

This is also, he believes, one of the structural weaknesses of founding teams that come from Google: Google is so successful, with such dominant distribution built in and money "raining from the sky" via AdWords, that PMs who come up there often develop a blind spot for distribution, business model, and the parts of company-building that aren't product. The PayPal mafia learned entrepreneurship the hard way. Google PMs often didn't.

The Coming Transformation of Code

The question Bret gets asked most — should young people still learn to code? — has a more nuanced answer than most people want to hear.

Yes, study computer science. No, the reason isn't because you'll spend your career typing in a terminal. The act of creating software is going to transform from writing code to operating a code-generating machine. That transition is already underway and will accelerate dramatically. The question is what survives that transition as valuable.

What survives is systems thinking. Computer science is, among other things, a rigorous education in how to think about complex systems — about complexity, about what's hard and what's easy, about what's possible and what isn't, about the difference between designing a beautiful mock-up and designing a system that produces beautiful outcomes given inputs you don't control. At Facebook, designers would show Bret these gorgeous Photoshop mockups of the News Feed — perfect photos, perfectly written posts, ideal comment lengths. Then they'd implement the design and it looked terrible, because real users don't post perfectly. The lesson: you're not designing a layout, you're designing a system. That kind of thinking is the enduring skill.

Python, the most commonly generated language by AI models, was designed to be ergonomic for human programmers — to read almost like pseudocode, to be pleasant to write. It is also, by Bret's assessment, "one of the most inefficient programming languages of all time" and quite difficult to verify correctness in. It was optimized for humans. When AI is generating the code, those optimizations become irrelevant. What matters instead is verifiability: can you know, without reading every line, that the generated code does what you intended?

Rust offers a preview of what this looks like. You don't need to read a Rust program to know it doesn't leak memory — if it compiles, it's memory safe. That's a property enforced at compile time. Bret thinks the next generation of programming systems will be full of properties like this: formal verification, structured testing frameworks, layers of AI supervising AI — a system designed not for human ergonomics but for human leverage over machine-generated code.

The limiting factor in software has never been generating a prototype. It's been maintaining increasingly complex systems with agility over time. Bret is excited about vibe coding, but clear-eyed about what it solves and what it doesn't. The Netscape 1.0 to Netscape 2.0 rewrite is a cautionary tale that has nothing to do with AI and everything to do with the difficulty of managing complexity. What excites him is the prospect of a programming system — not just a language, a whole system — designed from first principles around the question: how do we give humans maximum leverage over code-generating machines?

The Three Markets: Where to Build, Where to Stay Away

Bret divides the AI market into three segments, with distinct risk profiles for founders.

The frontier model market will consolidate to a handful of hyperscalers and large labs. The reasons are structural: frontier model development is entirely a function of capital expenditure, the models depreciate rapidly as assets, and there's not enough runway to reach escape velocity as a startup. Nearly every startup that tried — Inflection, Adept, Character — has already been absorbed. Bret's advice: don't build a foundation model unless you're Elon Musk, and you're probably not.

The tooling market — data labeling, eval tools, specialized models like ElevenLabs' voice models, infrastructure — is close to the sun. The infrastructure providers themselves are constantly moving up the stack, and the risk of being obsoleted by a developer day announcement from a big foundation model company is real. Meaningful companies have been built here (Snowflake, Databricks, Confluent), but many others have been swallowed by commoditization. If you're building in this space, you need a clear answer to the question: when the infrastructure layer introduces a competing product, why will people keep choosing you?

The applied AI market — agents doing specific jobs for specific industries — is where Bret sees the most durable opportunity. The agent, he says, is the new app. Just as every significant software category eventually had a SaaS company, every significant workflow category will have an agent company. Harvey for legal. Sierra for customer experience. More to come in supply chain, content, finance, healthcare, and dozens of other domains that require an entrepreneur who actually understands the business problem deeply.

The thing people miss about this market is that it will eventually look less like a technology business and more like a SaaS business. Today, orchestrating an agentic process on top of models sounds technically impressive. In three to four years, it will be as unremarkable as spinning up a database on AWS. What will remain — the durable competitive advantage — is product quality, workflow depth, and customer trust. Just like modern SaaS, no one will ask what database you use. They'll ask what outcomes you drive.

Outcomes-Based Pricing: The Right Business Model for a New Era

The business model question follows directly from the market structure. Bret is a believer in outcomes-based pricing — not as an interesting experiment, but as the inevitable destination for the software industry.

At Sierra, the model is resolution-based. A customer service interaction that costs $10 to $20 when handled by a human costs a pre-negotiated rate when an AI agent resolves it autonomously. The customer's problem is solved, the call center bill goes down, the business outcome is measurable and clear. Sierra also runs sales agents paid on commission. The business model is aligned with the customer's outcome.

The contrast Bret draws is with token-based or usage-based pricing. Tokens are a measure of computation, not value. An AI agent that has a long conversation, fails to resolve the customer's problem, generates a negative review online, and forces a follow-up call has consumed a lot of tokens and produced negative value. Pricing on tokens rewards the wrong thing. Pricing on outcomes aligns everything: the vendor is incentivized to actually solve the problem, the customer can measure what they're getting, and the CFO can understand the bill of materials.

He sees this as the equivalent of the shift from on-premises licensed software to SaaS — a transition that happened gradually and then all at once, and now seems so obviously correct that it's hard to remember why anyone did it the other way. Outcomes-based pricing is where the market is going. Companies that figure it out early will be structurally advantaged.

Go-to-Market: Three Models, One Honest Question

Bret maps the go-to-market landscape simply: developer-led growth (Stripe, Twilio), product-led growth (Shopify early days), and direct sales (Salesforce, Oracle, ServiceNow). Each works in specific conditions and fails in others.

Developer-led works when your product is a platform and your buyer is the engineering team. Product-led works when your user and your buyer are the same person — typically in small business or consumer software. Neither works when the person who uses the product and the person who approves the purchase are different people. Expense reporting software is the canonical example: the user is an individual employee, the buyer is finance. A self-serve trial doesn't fit that buying motion.

A significant portion of AI opportunities fall into the enterprise category, where the user and buyer are decoupled. Customer service, legal, supply chain, finance — these are line-of-business problems, not engineering team problems. Direct sales is the right motion. Bret has noticed it coming back into fashion among AI companies, and he thinks that's correct.

The diagnostic question founders need to ask before choosing a go-to-market motion isn't "what worked for that other startup" — it's "what is the actual process by which someone evaluates and purchases this product." Answer that honestly, then design around it. The mistake Bret sees most is choosing a motion based on what feels natural given the founder's background, rather than what fits the customer's actual buying process.

AI and the Education Inflection Point

Bret thinks about the AP Calculus exam. When graphing calculators were allowed into the exam, the College Board had to redesign the questions — you couldn't test something the calculator could answer. The result was a more rigorous test of actual calculus understanding. The tool changed what the test measured, and eventually the test adapted.

We're in the equivalent moment for education broadly, and it's uncomfortable because the adaptation hasn't happened yet. ChatGPT can write the essay. It can answer the quiz. The mechanisms for evaluating students are temporarily broken. Bret has a lot of empathy for teachers navigating this. But he's ultimately optimistic, because the historical pattern is that education adapts, and the adapted version is often better.

His practical position: AI is more like Google search than a smartphone. One is a utility that amplifies your capacity to learn; the other is a notification machine that competes for your attention. He encourages his kids to use AI for quizzing, for explaining difficult passages, for learning. His daughter photographed a page of Shakespeare she didn't understand and got an explanation better than he would have given. Every child now has access to a personalized tutor that can adapt to their learning style. That's a genuinely democratizing force — for the first time, you don't need to be wealthy to have a great tutor.

The Like Button Origin Story

The like button came from a product problem, not a branding exercise. On FriendFeed, about 70% of comments were one-word acknowledgments — "cool," "wow," "yeah," "neat." The product problem was that these acknowledgments were drowning out actual discussion. The feature was originally called "one-click comment." It was meant to give people a way to signal they'd seen a post without cluttering the discussion thread.

The first implementation used a heart icon. A colleague named Anna Yang said, looking at hearts on every post would make her "want to vomit" — too intense, too romantic, and wrong for serious or tragic content. They needed something positive but as neutral as possible within the realm of positivity. "Like" fit. It was a word that carried a vague sense of approval without strong emotional valence — which turned out to be exactly right for the range of content people post.

The subtlety of that design decision — neutral-positive over expressive-positive — is also why "like" was famously difficult to translate into other languages. Most languages don't have an equivalent word that occupies the same emotional register. The like button's design was constrained by its function, and its function made it more durable than a heart would have been.

What To Do With This

  • Ask "why should anyone use this instead of what already exists" before building. The question that turned Google Local into Google Maps isn't a launch question — it's a design question. If you can't answer it clearly, the product isn't ready.
  • Reframe your daily question from "what do I want to work on" to "what is the most impactful thing I can do today." The gap between those two questions is where most leaders leave performance on the table. Run it like an external board of advisors is watching.
  • When you think your strength is the solution to your problem, treat that as a flag, not a signal. Bret estimates at least 30% of the time, a founder reaches for their superpower out of comfort, not because it's the right answer. Ask someone who doesn't share your background to pressure-test your diagnosis.
  • Study computer science even if you never write code professionally again. Systems thinking — understanding complexity, tradeoffs, what's possible vs. impossible — is the durable skill. The specific act of writing code is what's being automated; the ability to reason about systems is not.
  • If you're trying to get productivity gains from coding agents like Cursor right now, build an MCP server and invest in context engineering. Don't just fix bad output — root-cause why the model produced it and add the missing context. This is how you get gains today, before the models improve on their own.
  • Layer AI supervision on AI output to dramatically improve reliability. An agent right 90% of the time combined with a reviewer right 90% of the time produces a system right 99% of the time. This is a tractable engineering problem and the right architecture for high-stakes AI outputs.
  • Map your go-to-market to the actual buying process, not to what's popular. Ask: who uses the product, who buys the product, are they the same person? If not, product-led growth will fail regardless of how good the product is. Direct sales is back in fashion in AI for good structural reasons.
  • Build toward outcomes-based pricing, not usage-based pricing. If your product produces a measurable, autonomous outcome, price on the outcome. Tokens are a measure of compute, not value. Aligning your pricing with customer outcomes is both better business and better product discipline — it forces you to actually solve the problem.
  • Don't build a foundation model as a startup. The market has already consolidated. CapEx requirements exceed startup runway, and model value deteriorates faster than you can recoup the investment. Build in applied AI instead, where deep domain knowledge is the moat.
  • When seeking advice, always ask "why" until you understand the framework, not just the rule. People generalize from small samples. Understanding their reasoning lets you apply the underlying principle with nuance — and recognize when your situation differs from the one they're extrapolating from.

Memorable Quotes

"To not do that well with a link from the Google homepage is kind of embarrassing. I mean, there's not much one can do more than giving you that kind of traffic as a product manager."

— Bret Taylor, on Google Local's failure

"I realized that the actual act of engineering or product design — all the things I thought I liked — what I really liked is impact."

— Bret Taylor

"If you think that what you've been doing your whole career is the way to fix your problem, it's at least 30% likely that you've chosen that because of comfort and familiarity, not truth."

— Bret Taylor

"We totally lost for no reason related to product at all."

— Bret Taylor, on FriendFeed losing to Twitter

"Python was designed to be very ergonomic, almost look like pseudocode for humans, for me to write code in a delightful way. As we move to a world where we're going to be operating code-generating machines — we probably don't care how ergonomic the programming language is."

— Bret Taylor

"Software is going from helping an individual be slightly more productive to actually accomplishing a job autonomously. And as a consequence, you just won't need someone doing that thing anymore — they can do something else that's higher leverage."

— Bret Taylor, on why agents are a step-change

"Tokens are similar. Yeah, you used a lot of tokens, like good for you. Did it produce a pull request that was good? I think that's the whole point of all this."

— Bret Taylor, on outcomes vs. usage-based pricing

"The best way to predict the future is to invent it."

— Alan Kay (Bret Taylor's favorite life motto)

item.title: Lenny's Podcast: Product | Career | Growth - Slack founder: Mental models for building products people love ft. Stewart Butterfield
item.description:
The Product Philosophy of Stewart Butterfield: Value, Comprehension, and the Courage to Stay Uncomfortable

In 2014, shortly after Slack's public launch, Stewart Butterfield told MIT Technology Review that what the company was shipping was "just a giant piece of shit." It was terrible, he said, and they should be humiliated to offer it to the public. His colleagues printed the quote on forty sheets of 8.5x11 paper and plastered them to the office walls. To Stewart, that was exactly right. If you can't see almost limitless opportunities to improve your product, you shouldn't be designing it. That perpetual, unblinking dissatisfaction — not with people, not with effort, but with the gap between what exists and what could — is the throughline of everything Stewart has built.

There's a framework he returned to constantly in the years of building Flickr and then Slack, something he calls utility curves. Picture a familiar S-curve. On the horizontal axis: cost, effort, investment. On the vertical: value, convenience, quality — whatever matters most for the thing you're making. The curve is flat at the start, then arcs steeply upward through a magic threshold, then flattens again as continued investment yields diminishing returns. The insight is deceptively simple but widely ignored: features are not binary. You don't have a feature or not have it. You have a feature that's somewhere on that curve — and if you haven't crossed the threshold where it becomes genuinely useful, you've added complexity without adding value. You've made the product worse.

People would bring a feature to review, and the question was never just "should we build this?" It was: where are we on the curve? Are we on the first shallow segment where we haven't yet delivered real value? Are we on the steep upslope where every increment matters enormously? Or are we past the plateau, chasing improvements that no longer move the needle? Most teams never ask this. They ship the feature, see that it doesn't get traction, and kill it — without recognizing they simply stopped too early, before the threshold.

The curve doesn't stand still, either. Jeff Bezos called it "divine discontent": once customers experience something done well, their expectations rise. The line on the vertical axis keeps climbing. Login flows that were fine three years ago feel clunky now. Checkout experiences that were acceptable are now abandonment drivers. Most things in any product get implemented once, get improved periodically if they're lucky, and get improved never if they're not. Account creation, forgot-password flows, the essential friction points where customers first encounter who you actually are — these rarely get the love they deserve, even as the quality bar across the industry keeps rising.

Comprehension Over Friction

There's a mantra in product circles that needs to die: reduce friction. It became so deep in people that it metastasized into dogma — fewer clicks, faster flows, streamlined everything. But it's almost always the wrong diagnosis.

The relevant variable is not friction. It's intent and comprehension. When you know exactly what you want — you're buying Taylor Swift tickets for a specific date at a specific venue — friction genuinely matters. You'll push through a slow Ticketmaster page because your intent is absolute and your comprehension is total. In those cases, reducing friction is real work worth doing. But those cases are the exception. For most of a product, most of the time, the person on the other side is operating with marginal intent and incomplete comprehension. They've heard about your product third-hand from a friend, saw it mentioned in an article, and finally wandered to your site at the minimum possible threshold of curiosity. They don't know what it is. They don't know what it will cost them. They don't know what will happen next.

That's not a friction problem. That's a comprehension problem. And if your response is to reduce friction — clear the path, speed up the flow, minimize the clicks — you're solving for the wrong thing. You're optimizing the road to a destination they haven't decided they want yet.

The design challenge is creating comprehension: what is this thing, and what am I supposed to do next? The visual hierarchy, the affordances, the sequencing of decisions — all of it is in service of helping someone understand. Apple's clock app has a "Sleep" section with an invitation to "turn sleep on." Most people have no idea what that means, what it costs them, or what will change. So they ignore it, never getting the value of a feature that almost certainly involved months of engineering. That's not a friction failure. It's a comprehension failure. And it repeats itself endlessly across products: features built in good faith, buried behind language or design that assumes the user already knows why they'd want it.

The corollary is what "Don't Make Me Think" actually means. It's not just the metabolic cost of decision-making — though that's real, a genuine biological expenditure — it's the emotional cost. When software stops you to make a decision you don't understand, you feel stupid. Not all users conclude the software is broken. Most of them conclude they are. They associate that feeling with your product, and they don't come back.

Reducing click count is a particularly seductive wrong metric. You could make any action in an app a single tap by exposing every possible option in one infinite scroll. Obviously that's terrible. A little bit of that logic is also terrible — it just looks like product design. The real goal is making every individual step trivially easy, even if there are eight of them. Uber, in its early days, gave users exactly two choices: where do you want to go, and other. Everything else — payments, account settings, location changes — was behind "other." People who just wanted a ride, which was almost everyone, saw exactly one decision. Eight steps, each effortless, beats two steps where you're staring at fifteen options trying to eliminate the ones you don't want.

Craft as Competitive Advantage

In Vancouver, walking back to the Slack office with Brandon Velestuk, the rain coming down, they noticed something. Of the people walking toward them with umbrellas on the narrow Yaletown sidewalks, maybe one in three would tilt their umbrella out of the way so oncoming pedestrians didn't get stabbed in the eye. Two in three would just plow forward.

There are three possible explanations. One: they enjoy the power. Two: they see the problem and genuinely don't know what to do about it. Three: they're completely oblivious to their impact. The first is rare. The second and third are everywhere. And that failure to be considerate, to actually think about the experience of the person in front of you — that's where the opportunity is. Tilt your umbrella became a saying at Slack. Your competitors' failure to be genuinely empathic about the experience they're creating is the advantage you can take.

Slack's growth was driven significantly by cross-pollination: someone at one startup used Slack, loved it, moved to another company, and became an advocate. That advocacy wasn't manufactured. It came from the genuine experience of little things done right. The magic link — why ask people to remember a password when ownership of the email address is the verification mechanism in the first place? Just send a link, have it open the app, authenticate automatically. The notification defaults for new users: everyone who'd used messaging apps expected a notification for every message, so new Slack accounts needed that default even though it wasn't right for the product, but once you'd received ten messages, Slack would gently ask if you'd like to switch to recommended settings. The shouty rooster — when you type @everyone and are about to send a notification to 147 people across eight time zones, a little rooster pops up asking if you're sure. The Do Not Disturb rollout: weeks of advance notice to administrators, nested permission hierarchies so org admins could set policy while users could customize, defaults set so that the people who would never turn it on themselves would benefit from it automatically.

None of these are big features. All of them are the product of someone stopping and actually thinking about what the human on the other end is experiencing — and then doing the work to get it right rather than shipping the easy version.

Hyper-Realistic Work-Like Activities and Parkinson's Law

Parkinson's Law is from 1956: work expands to fill the time available for its completion. The original observation was about bureaucracy and the Royal Navy — the number of ships declining, the number of sailors declining, but the number of administrators growing. It shows up the same way in every company.

Here's the uncomfortable truth: most people you hire want to hire more people who report to them. Not because they're evil, not because they're stupid — in fact, they're smart, because everyone knows that headcount under you correlates with career trajectory, compensation, and authority. So a product manager wants to hire a junior product manager. What would the junior PM do? "They'd do the product management. I'd do the strategy." And so it compounds.

As organizations grow, something else happens. At the very beginning, there's an enormous supply of known, valuable work: open a bank account, build a users table, implement authentication. Everyone knows what to do and knows it matters. Over time, the easy obvious stuff gets done. New people get hired. The supply of genuinely high-priority work that everyone clearly understands doesn't keep pace with the demand from people who need to demonstrate output.

Enter hyper-realistic work-like activities. A team calls a meeting to preview the deck they're going to present at the real meeting, to get feedback on whether some of the slides should be improved. People are sitting in a conference room. Something is being projected. Everyone is talking about it. That is superficially identical to work. It is not work. It's a performance of work, and it spreads through organizations the way mold spreads through bread — invisibly, until it's everywhere. The people doing it don't know they're doing it. Executives do it. Board members do it. The further you are from clear decision-making authority and real accountability for outcomes, the easier it is to get trapped.

At Slack, a team spent what had to be thousands of person-hours — engineering, QA, analytics, project management, user research — to A/B test whether pre-populating the reply box in threads with an @mention to the previous poster affected thread length. The finding: threads were 2.17 messages long with the feature versus 2.14 without it, at statistical significance that was probably marginal. Never mind that longer threads aren't clearly better. The problem is that the maximum possible value of that feature — even if the analysis were flawless and the effect real — was negligible. The cost of doing the analysis guaranteed it was a loser before the first line of instrumentation was written. That's hyper-realistic work-like activity. It looks exactly like rigorous data-driven product development. It isn't.

The solution is not to shame the people doing it. It's the responsibility of leaders — managers, directors, executives, CEOs — to ensure there's sufficient clarity about priorities, enough genuine work in the supply, and explicit no's early enough that people don't end up filling the vacuum with something that only looks like progress.

We Don't Sell Saddles Here

Before Slack launched, when the team was still eight or nine people in private beta, Stewart wrote what became a legendary internal memo. He didn't change a word of it when he eventually posted it publicly. The point was to instill alignment early enough that it could survive growth and scale.

The title is the thesis. If you sell saddles, you're in the business of leather and hardware. If you sell horseback riding — if you're selling the freedom, the experience, the version of your life where you spend time outdoors in a way you love — then the saddle is just the thing people need to do that. Lululemon sells yoga pants. They're also selling aspiration, health, and becoming the best version of yourself. Harley-Davidson sells motorcycles. They're also selling the open road and wind in your hair.

The memo's argument was that building Slack meant simultaneously building the market for Slack. No one needs to be explained why they'd want beer or a car. But a messaging platform for organizations? That requires a specific vision of a different, better way of working — and that vision has to be communicated with as much care as the product is built. The Positioning principle: it's nearly impossible to create a genuinely new idea in someone's head. It's much easier to connect two existing ideas. The job isn't just building a product; it's creating the context in which people can understand why they'd want it.

The owner's delusion is the failure to do this. Restaurant websites, circa ten years ago: slow-loading Ken Burns photo effects, background music, unclickable phone numbers rendered as images, no hours, no address. The five things every single person wants from a restaurant website — address, phone number, menu, hours, reservation — absent, replaced by things only the owner cares about. The owner designed the website for themselves, for how they feel about their restaurant, not for the person who's already decided to go and just needs to know where to park. Every product team does a version of this. The owner knows too much. They can't see their own thing through the eyes of someone who barely knows it exists and has a hundred other things on their mind.

On Pivots, Generosity, and Value Creation

Both Flickr and Slack emerged from failed video games. Both pivots involved internal tools built to make the team's own work better — IRC in the case of Glitch, which became the proto-Slack. Stewart was using internal communication infrastructure for years without explicitly building a product, adding only the things that were so obviously necessary they couldn't be avoided. When Glitch was eventually shut down, there were still nine million dollars in the bank and a team that still liked the game. But the honest assessment was that every non-ridiculous idea to make it commercially successful had been tried.

The advice he gives about pivots isn't to pivot. It's to be coldly rational — because the default feeling is humiliation. You convinced investors to bet on you. You convinced employees to leave their jobs. You convinced users to give you their time. Admitting it isn't working means admitting all of that was wrong. For many founders, it feels better to let the company die slowly from capital starvation than to make that admission. But the rational question is simple: have you exhausted the good ideas? If yes, the expected value calculation has changed. Annie Duke used Glitch and Slack as a case study in smart folds — recognizing when the expected value of the alternative has exceeded the expected value of continuing.

On generosity: there's game theory here, but it's not the main thing. The prisoner's dilemma logic — demonstrate cooperation early and consistently, and cooperation tends to be returned — is real. But the more important principle is the one Stewart had the entire company chant at all-hands meetings: in the long run, the measure of our success will be the amount of value we create for customers. Not revenue. Not growth. Value. That creates a very clear filter for any decision that feels slightly off. Anything that smacks of maximizing at the wrong moment, taking advantage of a customer's information asymmetry, or winning a negotiation in a way that doesn't actually serve them — those things fail the test. The consequence of consistently passing the test is that you attract better employees, create better culture, and build the kind of advocacy that no marketing budget can replicate.
