The SLA that backfired — 100x money back for any downtime, which made perfect sense when revenue was small and became an $8 million liability after going public — is not a counterargument. It's evidence of the principle working too well at too large a scale. The terms got adjusted. The orientation didn't change.

Stewart spent years building products and companies. He's now mostly chilling, spending time with a two-and-a-half-year-old. The problems he finds genuinely interesting are the ones that don't have obvious software solutions — if anything could help people use their phones less, that would be a win. But the framework he built for thinking about products — utility curves, comprehension over friction, the owner's delusion, hyper-realistic work-like activities, tilt your umbrella — is a coherent system, not a list of tips. It all flows from one thing: the ability to see clearly what's actually being experienced, not what you intended to create. That's taste. It can be developed. Most people don't bother. That's the opportunity.

What To Do With This

  • Map your features to the utility curve before shipping. Before any feature ships, ask explicitly: are we on the first shallow segment (not enough to matter), the steep slope (every increment counts), or past the plateau (diminishing returns)? If you're below the threshold of genuine usefulness, don't ship — you're adding complexity without delivering value.
  • Reframe every "reduce friction" conversation as "increase comprehension." When someone argues for fewer steps or fewer clicks, ask whether the real problem is comprehension — does the user understand what this is, what it will do, and what comes next? If not, removing friction doesn't help. Clarity does.
  • Audit your highest-traffic but lowest-love flows. Account creation, forgot-password, checkout, onboarding — these get implemented once and rarely revisited. Pick one and ask: does it reflect current quality standards, or is it embarrassingly behind the bar your customers now carry from other products?
  • Apply the Uber "where do you want to go / other" test to every screen. Identify the one or two things users actually want to do on any given screen and surface only those. Put everything else behind "other" or a second menu. Eight easy steps beats two steps with fifteen options each.
  • Name and discuss owner's delusion explicitly on your team. Pull up your own website or product from the perspective of someone who barely knows you exist and has ten other things on their mind. What do they actually need in the first thirty seconds? Is it there? If the answer is uncomfortable, fix it — and then keep asking.
  • Identify and kill one hyper-realistic work-like activity this week. Look at recurring meetings or analysis projects and ask: what is the maximum possible value this could create, and does it exceed the cost of doing it? If the cost is guaranteed to be higher than any plausible upside, stop. Say explicitly why, so the team understands the principle.
  • Write your "We Don't Sell Saddles Here" document. Articulate the life or work outcome your product enables — not its features, not its category. What does the person's work or life look like after they've deeply adopted it? That's what you're selling. Make sure your marketing, onboarding, and sales all lead with that, not the saddle.
  • Set your defaults with intention. Most users will never change default settings. Whatever you ship as the default is what most of your users will experience forever. For Do Not Disturb, the right default was on during sleep hours — because without a default, most people wouldn't turn it on. Audit your defaults and ask whether they serve most users or just the power users who customize everything.
  • Ask "have we exhausted the good ideas?" before deciding to pivot — or to persist. The question isn't whether things are hard. It's whether you've genuinely tried every realistic path forward. If you have, the expected value calculation has changed. If you haven't, "it's hard" isn't sufficient to stop.
  • Make your measure of success the value you create for customers, and say it out loud. Anything that feels like maximizing at the wrong moment — an overly aggressive contract term, a renewal tactic that benefits you at the customer's expense — fails this test. Saying the principle explicitly and repeatedly creates a filter that shapes a thousand small decisions you won't be in the room for.

Memorable Quotes

"I feel like what we have right now is just a giant piece of shit. It's just terrible and we should be humiliated that we offer this to the public."
— Stewart Butterfield, 2014 (on Slack shortly after launch)
"If you can't see almost limitless opportunities to improve, then you shouldn't be designing the product."
— Stewart Butterfield
"It became an assumption that you should always be trying to remove friction. When the challenge is really comprehension."
— Stewart Butterfield
"If your software stops me and asks me to make a decision, and I don't really understand it, you make me feel stupid."
— Stewart Butterfield
"Your failure to really be considerate and exercise courtesy and really be empathic about other people's experience is an advantage you can create. A critical advantage."
— Stewart Butterfield
"In the long run, the measure of our success will be the amount of value that we create for customers."
— Stewart Butterfield (Slack company chant)
"The reason I say you have to be coldly rational about it is because it's fucking humiliating."
— Stewart Butterfield, on deciding to pivot
"Everything is simple if you have no idea what you're talking about. If something seems simple, probably you don't understand it."
— Stewart Butterfield

item.title: Lenny's Podcast: Product | Career | Growth - The new AI growth playbook for 2026: How Lovable hit $200M ARR in one year | Elena Verna (Head of Growth)
item.description:
The Playbook Is Dead. Long Live the Playbook.

Fifteen years of growth expertise, and only thirty to forty percent of it still applies. That is what Elena Verna discovered when she joined Lovable as Head of Growth — a company that had already rocketed to $100 million ARR in seven months and would double that in the next four. The conventional growth playbook, the one she had refined across Miro, Dropbox, SurveyMonkey, Amplitude, and Netlify, was largely irrelevant. Not wrong, exactly. Just insufficient for the velocity, the chaos, and the category-defining moment Lovable was living inside.

The numbers alone demand attention. Lovable launched in the third week of November 2024. Less than a year later, over 200 million dollars in annual recurring revenue. Eight million users. Hundreds of thousands of paid subscribers. A Series B at a six-billion-dollar valuation. And a team of one hundred people — thirty of whom existed just six months prior. These are not numbers that yield to standard frameworks. They require a different way of thinking about growth entirely.

What Elena found when she arrived was a company already in motion, growing ferociously before any formal growth function existed. Her first honest observation: she was not the engine. The company was growing because of something more elemental than any playbook could manufacture. Her job was to not stand in its way — to grease the wheels, to spot the walls before the car hit them, and to figure out which new loops could be built for a category still in full eruption.

Innovation Over Optimization: The 95/5 Inversion

In every previous role, Elena spent roughly ninety to ninety-five percent of her time optimizing — tightening funnels, improving activation flows, running A/B tests, squeezing more from what already existed. At best, five to ten percent of her bandwidth went to genuine innovation. At Lovable, that ratio has inverted completely. Ninety-five percent of her time now goes to building new growth loops from scratch. Five percent to optimization.

This is not a stylistic preference. It is a structural reality. When the market is moving this fast, when competition is proliferating daily, optimization is a form of standing still. To be ahead of every company entering the vibe-coding space is not a problem of tuning — it is a problem of reinvention. You cannot optimize your way to a category lead you have not yet earned. You have to invent it.

What does growth innovation look like in practice? The Growth team at Lovable ships integrations. They built a Shopify connector to enable e-commerce storefronts. They launched voice mode so users could converse with the product instead of only typing. These are core product features, not traditional growth surface work. The line between growth and product has dissolved. Growth engineers at Lovable are building agentic workflows and codifying agent instructions to improve activation. They are going deeper into the product, not skating its outer edges.

The area Elena expected to spend most of her time on — activation — she has spent almost none of it on. In past companies, activation was always the highest-leverage intervention: get people to the aha moment, smooth out the setup flow, reduce the distance between signing up and genuinely understanding the product's value. At Lovable, the product's core team is already obsessed with activation because activation is just the agent working well. Every time the model improves, every generation becomes better — not just the first one. The entire lifecycle improves simultaneously. Elena does not need to own activation because the entire company already does.

The Growth Levers That Actually Work

Build something people genuinely love. This is not a platitude at Lovable — it is load-bearing infrastructure for every other growth lever. The word of mouth loop, the social sharing, the ambassador program, the hiring brand: all of it depends on the product delivering experiences that feel like superpowers. Elena frames it precisely: users at Lovable feel pride in what they have created. They feel like they have unlocked a secret. And people who feel that do not stay quiet about it.

The company name is not incidental here. Every interaction is evaluated against a single question: is this lovable? When it is not, the bug gets fixed immediately. That cultural forcing function keeps the emotional quality of the product from eroding under shipping velocity. It is brand work done through product design rather than through campaign spend.

Make noise in the market, constantly. Lovable ships every day, sometimes multiple times a day. Each engineer who ships is expected to announce it — to their social networks, not through a marketing filter. This is not a marketing strategy so much as a philosophy: velocity of shipping is the core value, and everyone on the team has a marketer inside them. The resulting noise keeps the product feeling alive to lapsed users, triggers re-engagement, and creates a steady drumbeat of reasons to pay attention. Big tier-one launches happen too — the amplification moments designed to step-function shift perception — but the baseline hum of daily shipment is what sustains momentum between them.

Founders and employees posting authentically on LinkedIn and X has replaced SEO as the primary organic channel. This is a shift Elena finds underappreciated. Five years ago, organic growth meant search engine optimization. Today it means social presence — what is the CEO posting, what are team members sharing, what are users saying in public. B2B is not exempt from this. That is where the eyeballs are, and corporate scrubbing has to come off for it to work. Personality, vulnerability, authenticity. It feels uncomfortable for companies trained on polished brand voice. It is also unavoidable.

Influencer marketing outperforms paid social by a factor of ten at Lovable. The reason is format-function fit. Vibe coding is something you have to see to believe. Written copy describing it falls flat. But a ten-second video of someone prompting an app into existence and watching it work — that lands. That drives someone to open a tab and try it themselves. Paid social is being optimized but remains expensive and slow to pay back. Influencers are the demonstration layer that makes the product legible to new audiences.

Community amplifies everything else. Lovable's Discord has hundreds of thousands of members and it runs lively, not as a managed marketing channel but as a genuine gathering place for people learning what is possible. An ambassador program extends that energy outward. The community accelerates word of mouth, increases retention, and creates social density around a product that thrives when people discover it together.

Give the product away. This is the growth lever Elena describes as her growth secret sauce, and it is the one she has never used at any previous company. Lovable gives credits freely — to hackathons, to events, to any user who wants to host a session at their company. The strategic logic is clean: LLM costs that would normally depress margins get reclassified as marketing spend. Instead of competing on Google AdWords against every other vibe-coding company, Lovable puts its product directly in front of people who want to spread it. A user who volunteers to run a Lovable hackathon at their workplace is doing distribution work the company would otherwise have to pay for. The answer is always yes, how much do you need.

The cost structure at Lovable makes this sustainable in a way that surprises people. Two hundred million dollars ARR. One hundred employees. Almost no paid search spend. A lean sales function just beginning to develop enterprise capacity. When you are not burning on ads and sales headcount, you have budget to invest in the product experiences that create word of mouth — and to give the product away as a marketing vehicle. It is product-led growth at its most aggressive possible expression.

Product Market Fit Is Now a Treadmill

Elena has crystallized something that most AI companies have not yet named clearly: product market fit is no longer an achievement. It is a treadmill, and the speed is set to a cadence of roughly three months.

Historically, the evolution of product market fit was measured in years. You found fit, scaled aggressively, and only after approaching saturation did you seriously interrogate whether a second or third horizon was needed. The product could hold still while the company caught up. The market could be trusted to move slowly enough that your positioning remained valid.

Neither condition holds now. On the product side, LLMs step-function improve with each major model release — and those releases come every few months. Each new capability ceiling means a new set of things that can be built on top. Companies cannot wait for a model to release and then build; they have to build in anticipation and have the features ready when the model catches up. The company is constantly betting on where technology is going before it arrives.

On the market side, consumer expectations have never evolved this quickly. What users expected from AI eight months ago versus today is unrecognizable. The distance between what is technically possible and what users expect has collapsed. Users who were impressed by a capability in January are impatient with it by April. The tolerance for falling behind is essentially zero.

The result is an organization that looks strange by historical standards. Lovable is a two-hundred-million-dollar ARR company that cannot simply hand the keys to sales and marketing and shift into scaling mode. It has to simultaneously scale and reinvent. It has to find people capable of doing both, which is genuinely a different kind of talent than either role has traditionally required. Every three months, there is a throttle on pure growth execution to re-engage with the product market fit question — and then a short blitz to scale before the next cycle starts again.

Elena carries one active worry about this cycle. The people using vibe-coding tools right now are mostly pioneers — early adopters excited by the capabilities. The latent majority, the adjacent users who would represent the next phase of growth, are not yet captured. And the pioneers are being pulled further and further forward with each model improvement. The risk is that the gap between pioneers and the latent majority grows too wide, that the product never becomes legible to people who are not already wired to explore new technical capabilities. It is not a crisis yet. But it is a structural question that will matter enormously in the next phase of category development.

How Lovable Hires, and Why It Works

The company runs lean on purpose. A hundred people generating two hundred million dollars in ARR requires a particular kind of person — someone for whom this work is a calling, not a paycheck. Elena is explicit about the hiring filter: passion, fire in the belly, a sense that this is the biggest opportunity of their life. Impressive logos from previous employers matter less than whether the person would choose this over every other option available to them right now.

High agency and high autonomy are screened for directly. At Lovable, engineers announce their own launches. Marketers build prototypes in Lovable instead of waiting for design sprints. Growth team members ship product integrations and agentic workflows without waiting for traditional product channels. This only works if people can figure out adjacent problems themselves, own things end to end, and do not need structure handed to them.

The company uses paid trial periods to see candidates in action before committing. Probation windows are genuine — not everyone fits, and the pace makes that clear quickly. Elena describes returning from a ten-day vacation and feeling like she needed to re-onboard. The evolution that reads as gradual from the inside is a revolution when seen from the outside. That pace filters candidates more efficiently than any interview process.

Two hiring archetypes that are newly valuable: AI-native new graduates who do not carry the accumulated assumptions of how things have always worked, and former founders — people with high agency who have owned outcomes under pressure and failed, learning something in the process that corporate experience rarely teaches. Neither archetype was particularly prized in traditional tech. Both are now hot.

Women and the AI Divide

Elena is worried about something she did not expect to worry about. When she looked at Lovable's user base — a product with a pink and purple brand, explicitly designed to be warm and inviting, named Lovable — women accounted for roughly twenty percent of users. Not fifty. Twenty.

The adoption gap between men and women in AI tools is not new data, but seeing it persist even in the most welcoming possible product context crystallized something. The AI wave is generating extraordinary economic opportunity: the acqui-hire premiums, the hiring demand, the startup valuations. And women are largely absent from it. Not because the barriers are technical — coding skill is no longer a prerequisite for building software. Something else is happening, and Elena does not claim to have the complete answer.

What she is doing about it: She Builds, a women-only hackathon running on Lovable, giving participants forty-eight hours of unlimited access and a community to build inside. The things that get built in those hackathons are not the apps a tech audience would predict. Women build tools for elderly care, for their kids' schools, for their church groups, for neighborhood basketball teams. Hyperlocal problems that mattered but were never economically viable to build before. The ceiling of what someone can create has dropped dramatically, and for the people who find out about it, the results are immediate and personal.

The initiative is not just about representation as a value. It is about who builds software shaping what software gets built. If the people making software are not representative of the people using it, the blind spots compound. Elena wants more voices in the room — not to slow the pioneers down, but to make sure the latent majority gets a chance to lead.

What To Do With This

  • Invert your innovation-to-optimization ratio immediately if you are in an early or fast-moving category. If ninety percent of your growth time is optimization, you are tuning a car that is about to be lapped. Identify at least two new growth loops this quarter that do not exist yet.
  • Reclassify your LLM and freemium costs as marketing spend, not cost-of-goods. If your AI product costs money to give away, giving it away to hackathons, events, and user-led activations is almost always cheaper per acquisition than competing on paid search — and it creates distribution you cannot buy.
  • Replace the MVP with a minimum lovable product standard before shipping anything. Ask whether the experience you are about to release creates the feeling that users want to tell someone about it. If it does not, do not ship it yet.
  • Have your CEO, founders, and engineers announce their own launches on LinkedIn and X, without a marketing filter. Social has replaced SEO as the primary organic channel for B2B. Corporate voice kills the signal. Personal, authentic posts from the people building the product are the mechanism.
  • Put your team's brand effort inside the product, not outside it. If you do not have a brand marketing team, that is fine — invest in the small interaction details, the micro-moments, the personality of the interface. That is where brand actually lives at this stage.
  • Set a hard calendar cadence of re-evaluating product market fit every three months. Do not wait until growth slows to ask the question. Schedule the review, surface what has changed in the underlying technology and in user expectations, and build that into your planning cycle.
  • Actively recruit AI-native new graduates and failed startup founders alongside experienced operators. New graduates who have never learned the old way can leapfrog constraints that slow everyone else down. Former founders have owned outcomes and built tolerance for ambiguity that corporate careers rarely develop.
  • Build a community infrastructure early, even if it is just a Discord. Community amplifies word of mouth, increases retention, and creates a space where users teach each other — reducing your activation cost and generating the social density that makes the product feel like a movement rather than a tool.
  • Screen every hire for high agency and high autonomy, not just skill set. At Lovable, growth engineers build product features and product engineers announce their own launches. That only works with people who can own the full chain. Hire for that, not for specialization.
  • Make your engineers ship features and talk about them publicly. If your team does not have enough marketers to cover every release, stop funneling announcements through marketing and start training engineers to own their own launches on social. The velocity requires it.

Memorable Quotes

"To be ahead of them is not optimization of the problem, it's reinvention of the solution."
— Elena Verna
"I usually spend maybe 5 percent innovating on growth in my previous roles. Right now, I'm spending 95 percent innovating on growth and only 5 percent on optimization."
— Elena Verna
"It's not a ha moment anymore. It just needs to be a wow moment."
— Elena Verna
"The best way to fix a bug at Lovable is to say 'this is not lovable.' Like when everybody just jumps on it to fix it right there and then."
— Elena Verna
"Viability is left back in the 2010s. Now it's minimum lovable product. That's the only thing that matters."
— Elena Verna
"Every three months, I feel like we have to recapture our product market fit — and not just recapture on the same technology and with same customers. It's both of those pieces of the equation that change every three months."
— Elena Verna
"If somebody, one of our users stands up and says, hey, I'm going to have a hackathon at my work on Lovable, can you give us some free credits to play with? Why would we prevent a person who wants to do all of the marketing and activating job for us from using us? We're like, take it. How much do you need?"
— Elena Verna
"I often call AI average intelligence that helps me get the platform up. And then I add my human thinking and my human creativity on top of it to get it to the next level."
— Elena Verna

item.title: Lenny's Podcast: Product | Career | Growth - A guide to difficult conversations, building high-trust teams, and designing a life you love | Rachel Lockett
item.description:
The Human Side of Building: What Most Tech Leaders Get Wrong

There's a reason the most effective leaders aren't the ones with all the answers. Rachel Lockett has spent more than a decade coaching CEOs, co-founders, and technical executives at some of the fastest-moving companies in tech—Pinterest, Stripe, Coinbase, and beyond—and the pattern she sees again and again is the same: leaders who climbed the ladder by being the smartest person in the room can't figure out why that same approach is breaking down now that they're actually in charge.

The problem isn't competence. It's that they've trained their teams to bring them every hard problem. Every decision, every blocker, every escalation flows upward. The leader becomes the bottleneck—and resents it—while the team waits for answers instead of developing the capacity to find them.

The antidote isn't a management philosophy. It's two learnable skills that most leaders never bother to develop: active listening and powerful questions. Together, they form the foundation of what Rachel calls leader-as-coach—and she's emphatic that this doesn't mean setting up hour-long coaching sessions with every report. It means carving out 15% more space in the conversations you're already having.

Three Levels of Listening—and Why Most Leaders Are Stuck at Level One

Most people aren't listening. They're waiting for their turn to talk. This is especially true in tech, where speed is rewarded and pausing to truly hear someone can feel like inefficiency.

But there are three distinct levels of listening, and the gap between them determines whether you're actually useful to the people around you. Level one is internal listening—you're hearing someone's words, but your attention is on yourself: the implications for your own work, your own agenda, your own reaction. Most people operate here most of the time.

Level two is focused listening: you can repeat back what was said, you're tracking the content, you're present to the words. This is what a solid one-on-one looks like. Better than level one, but still incomplete.

Level three is global listening—hearing beneath the words. You're noticing tone, body language, what's being communicated versus what's being said. You can reflect back insight that the person didn't even fully articulate. This is what great leaders do when they're influencing, when they're selling a vision, and especially when they're coaching.

The demonstration Rachel runs live makes this concrete. She asks Lenny what it's like to be a dad. He gives an answer about joy and challenge. She reflects back not just the content but the emotional undertow—the way he squirmed before answering, the coexistence of deep love and the discomfort of sleep training and disappointed toddlers. "Nailed it," he says. And then she makes the key point: that took less than a minute. Active listening isn't a program. It's a different quality of attention you bring to conversations you're already having.

The GROW Model: A Framework That Fits in Your Pocket

The second skill is powerful questions. Not leading questions—those are just advice in disguise. Genuine, open-ended questions that help someone unlock a solution they already have access to but can't quite see.

The GROW model organizes these into four categories. Goal: what does success look like? What's the outcome you want? Reality: where are you stuck, what have you tried, what are the current constraints? Options: what paths could you take, what haven't you considered? Way forward: what are you going to do next?

The order doesn't matter. You might skip directly to reality if the goal is already clear. The point is that any of these questions, asked with genuine curiosity and paired with level-three listening, will help someone move from stuck to unstuck. Rachel demonstrates this with a live coaching session on Lenny's own challenge—the endless treadmill of content creation, the Indiana Jones boulder always in the rearview mirror. In fifteen minutes, she walks him through the GROW model in real time. He comes out with two concrete commitments: skip at least one newsletter that's already been promised flexibility, and revisit his policies on what he says yes to.

The reason coaching works better than advising—most of the time—is simple: people are more bought in to what they came up with themselves. You could hand someone a perfectly correct list of things to do and they'll half-do it. But when they generate the answer from their own understanding of the problem, they own it.

The heuristic for when to advise versus coach is also simple. Advise when there's urgency and you have the specific answer. Advise when the person genuinely lacks the skill or context to solve the problem. Advise when you know exactly what you want them to do and coaching would just be a game of "guess what I'm thinking." Every other situation is a candidate for coaching—and most leaders over-rotate toward advising by default.

Burnout Isn't Inevitable—It's a Design Problem

The people who stay energized over a decade-plus career aren't just tougher. They've solved a different problem: they've designed their work around their gifts.

This sounds aspirational to the point of uselessness until you sit with the evidence. Rachel watched Stripe's top 50 technical executives—some of the most capable, committed people in tech—and saw how exhausted they were. People who started with enormous inspiration and capacity had been grinding so long they'd lost access to what made the work meaningful in the first place. Meanwhile, she's watched other people maintain energy and curiosity across genuinely hard years. The difference isn't luck or constitution. It's whether they're spending most of their time on things they're naturally good at and genuinely drawn to.

The practical target is 80%. You're always going to have 20% of your work be things you don't love—logistics, obligations, things that come with the territory. But if you can get to 80% in your zone of genius, the energy is fundamentally different. The work itself refuels you.

The diagnostic is concrete: for two weeks, every evening, write down five things that gave you energy today and five things that depleted you. Look at the patterns. That's your data. Alternatively, send a short email to five to ten people who know you well and ask: when I walk in a room, what shows up? What are my gifts? Sometimes the people around us see our zone of genius more clearly than we do—and sometimes what we discover is that we've drifted far from it without noticing.

Rachel's own path made this real. She was running the UX research team at Remind when the CEO offered her a product manager role. She took it. She was effective at it—within a month the team was reviewing each other's code, disagreeing productively, connecting more deeply to users. But at home she was losing sleep over design decisions, over-relying on data scientists, swirling on choices that didn't warrant that kind of anxiety. A colleague watched this and told her plainly: her gift wasn't product strategy. It was people. He could see what she'd done to that team in a month and knew it wasn't replicable by someone who didn't lead the way she led.

She was offended. And then she knew he was right. She left product, trained as a coach, moved into HR leadership, and built toward the work that now lets her spend the day fully in her gifts. "Life is more fun when you're in your gifts," she says. "And you have more inspiration and capacity to offer the world."

For people who can't just redesign their roles immediately: start small. Stop attending the optional meetings that drain you. Build buffers after the depleting things you can't avoid—a walk, a reset, something that refuels the tank. Tell your manager what energizes you and what doesn't. Most managers want to retain great performers and will work with this information if you give it to them honestly. No one else is going to do this for you. It's not your manager's job to make your work interesting. It's yours.

Co-Founder Relationships: The Conversation Nobody's Having

Sixty-five percent of startups fail because of co-founder conflict. The relationship is one of the highest-stakes in business—comparable to a marriage in terms of interdependence and emotional charge—and it gets almost no infrastructure. People don't take it to their board. They don't talk about it openly. It keeps them up at night while they're supposed to be building the future.

The first thing that helps is understanding the dance you're already in. CTOs tend to be skeptics—they want facts, depth of understanding, self-sufficiency. CEOs tend to be optimists—selling ahead of reality, influencing, big-picture vision. These archetypes create predictable tensions. Knowing the dynamic you're in means you're not just stepping on each other's toes blindly. You're dancing with awareness.

The second thing is intentional commitment. Just like couples need date nights, co-founders need time specifically for the relationship—not the business, the relationship. Quarterly in-person check-ins. Regular lunches or dinners. Space to ask: are we still aligned on vision? What have I been doing that's frustrated you? What's gone unsaid? These conversations don't happen by accident in the middle of a scaling startup. You have to design for them.

Rachel co-wrote a piece with First Round that offers weekly, monthly, and annual questions co-founders can work through together. The vows aren't a one-time ceremony—they're a framework for revisiting what you've committed to and whether you're still living up to it.

When co-founders arrive already fractured—which is more common than people admit—the work is harder but not impossible. What tends to help is the same thing that helps marriages: naming current state honestly, acknowledging what each person is missing from the other, and creating a rhythm of connection that the scaling chaos had eroded. Sometimes what comes out of that process is renewed commitment. Sometimes it's clarity that one person needs to leave. Both outcomes are success. The alternative—muddling through in unconscious frustration, unable to name what's wrong—serves no one.

Difficult Conversations: Reframing the Marketing

The marketing on conflict is terrible. "Difficult conversation" is positioned as something to avoid, something unpleasant, something that drains energy. Rachel argues the opposite: if a conversation feels hard, it's because something important is at stake. You care about this person or this work or this relationship. The difficulty is information.

The first reframe: ambivalence and interpersonal friction are growth opportunities. You're not failing. You're encountering something important that you have something to learn from.

The second reframe: you are always contributing to the conflict. Even when the other person seems clearly in the wrong. Something you're doing—or not doing, or not saying—is part of the dynamic. Entering with that humility makes the conversation possible in a way that righteous certainty never will.

The framework Rachel uses is Marshall Rosenberg's Nonviolent Communication, condensed to four steps. Observations: what is factually happening, stated in a way no one could argue with. ("In the last three sprint planning meetings, you didn't include me.") Feelings: what emotion does this create for you, expressed without blame. ("I felt anxious. I felt confused.") Feelings are emotions—words like "anxious," "confused," "sad," "frustrated." Not "I feel like you're being a jerk." That's a judgment, not a feeling. Needs: what universal human need is unmet here. Clarity. Collaboration. Connection. This doesn't ask anything of the other person yet. It just helps them understand the stakes. Request: something specific, achievable, small. An olive branch, not a demand.

The goal of this entire structure is mutual understanding—not convincing someone they were wrong, not proving your case, not winning. When you stay on your side of the net, describing your own experience rather than characterizing theirs, the defensive response you'd normally trigger doesn't come. And without defensiveness, the other person can actually hear you.

The question Rachel uses at Stripe to force clarity on talent: would you enthusiastically rehire this person for the same role? The answer is physical. You know it before you think it. And if the answer is no, that's not a verdict—it's a prompt to act. Maybe it's a conversation about performance. Maybe it's a role change. Maybe it's a search. But the clarity that comes from asking that question honestly is what makes action possible.

The One-Page Plan: Simplicity as Strategy

Most companies have their vision in one place, their values in another, their OKRs in a third, their quarterly goals in a fourth. Ask anyone in the company how their work ladders up to the big picture and you'll get a shrug. The strategy exists—it's just distributed in ways that make it inaccessible.

The one-page plan collapses this. Four columns: vision and values, strategic intentions and KPIs, annual goals, quarterly goals. Everything visible at once, so the connection from daily work to long-term direction is never more than one glance away. Rachel adapted this from Alpine Investors' People First Operating Rhythm, which their portfolio data shows correlates with higher returns.

The plan matters less than the rhythm around it. Who gathers to create it, how they reflect on it quarterly, what questions they ask to surface what's not working, how they celebrate wins. The question she likes in quarterly reviews: what's an inconvenient truth? The thing everyone knows but no one's saying because there's always something more urgent.

Leadership teams need time on the balcony—away from the dance floor, looking down at what's actually happening. Not just executing but periodically working on the business rather than in it. The one-page plan gives the balcony a view worth having.

Connection in an Era of Loneliness

The world is getting lonelier. The research confirms it, and Rachel sees it in her coaching sessions—people who are building alongside others feel more alienated than ever. The AI boom gives leaders more capability at their fingertips. But the default state of modern work—blind, grind, loneliness—hasn't changed.

Building businesses is an inherently human endeavor. The technical founders who discover three years in that they're now managing a board and a team, doing a completely different job than the one they loved, are living proof that the human side catches up with you whether you plan for it or not. Better to attend to it deliberately. Create environments where connection is inevitable. Equip the people around you with the coaching questions, the NVC framework, the emotional vocabulary to actually reach each other.

The quality of your relationships determines the quality of your life. That's not a soft claim. It's the thing that sits under everything else Rachel does—why she believes that technical leaders who lean into the human side of their work don't just feel better, they build better companies.

What To Do With This

  • Practice level-three listening in your next one-on-one. After someone shares a problem, reflect back not just what they said but what you sensed they were feeling underneath it. Ask if you got it right. This takes less than two minutes and dramatically changes the quality of the conversation.
  • Ask "what does success look like?" before jumping to solutions. When someone brings you a problem, run them through the GROW model—goal, reality, options, way forward—before offering your own answer. Only advise if they've demonstrated they lack the knowledge or context to get there themselves.
  • Try the two-week energy audit. Every evening for 14 days, write down five things that gave you energy and five things that depleted you. Look for patterns. This data tells you where your zone of genius actually is, not where you assume it should be.
  • Email five to ten people who know you well and ask: "When I walk in the room, what shows up? What are my gifts?" You'll get useful signal you can't generate from self-reflection alone.
  • Use the NVC framework before your next difficult conversation. Structure what you want to say as observations (facts only), feelings (emotions, not judgments), needs (universal human needs), and a specific, achievable request. Practice staying on your own side of the net.
  • Ask "would I enthusiastically rehire this person for the same role?" about anyone on your team you've been uncertain about. If the answer is no, you have a decision to make—but at minimum you have clarity, which is more than most leaders let themselves have.
  • Schedule a co-founder check-in—not a business meeting, a relationship check-in. Use questions from Rachel's First Round piece. Ask what's gone unsaid. Ask what you've been doing that's been frustrating. Do this quarterly at minimum, in person when possible.
  • Build a one-page plan with four columns: vision/values, strategic intentions/KPIs, annual goals, quarterly goals. Make it accessible to the whole company. Use it as the backbone of quarterly reviews, and add one question to every review: "What's the inconvenient truth we're not saying?"
  • Whenever you feel an urge to avoid a conversation, ask "what do I have to learn here?" Reframe the discomfort as a signal that something important is at stake, not a reason to wait. The cave you fear contains the treasure you seek.
  • Tell your manager explicitly what energizes you and what drains you. Don't wait for them to figure it out. Managers want to retain good performers and can work with this information. It's your job to navigate your career—not theirs.

Memorable Quotes

"When you try to advise and have the answer all the time, you're not actually equipping your team to go solve the hard problems. You're training your team to come to you with all of the hard problems."

— Rachel Lockett

"Most people aren't listening, they're just waiting for their turn to talk. This is rampant in tech."

— Rachel Lockett

"It's no one else's job to help you live in your gifts. Your manager's job is to help you perform in the job you were hired to do. It's your job to navigate your career."

— Rachel Lockett

"There's a misguided view that the goal is to convince the other person that what they're doing is wrong. Actually, the goal of any conflict is to create mutual understanding."

— Rachel Lockett

"If you can see your path laid out in front of you, step by step, it's not your path. Your own path you make with every step you take. That's why it's your path."

— Joseph Campbell (Rachel Lockett's desk quote)

"I want to encourage listeners to think of themselves as leaders who bring humans together to self-actualize—and that they have to actively overcome the default state, which is blind, grind, and loneliness."

— Rachel Lockett

"The quality of our relationships determines the quality of our life. And if you cannot have conflict, you can't have healthy relationships."

— Rachel Lockett

"When people are in their gifts and their strengths firmly, most of the time, they have more energy. We all have more energy when we're operating from the things we naturally are good at and the things we innately love doing."

— Rachel Lockett

item.title: The Tim Ferriss Show - #851: Dr. Tommy Wood — How to Future-Proof Your Brain from Dementia
item.description:
The Brain Is Not Destined to Fail

Human babies are born fat. Grotesquely, disproportionately fat compared to every other mammal on the planet, including our closest primate relatives. The reason, Tommy Wood has come to believe, is one of the most elegant clues biology offers us about the brain. All that fat is a repository — for DHA, for ketone precursors, for the raw material a rapidly developing brain needs when it can't yet get it anywhere else. The newborn brain is so metabolically hungry, so demanding, that the body pre-loads the infant with fuel reserves just to get through the first weeks of life. That's how much the brain matters. And that's how much it costs to maintain.

Wood is an associate professor of pediatrics and neuroscience at the University of Washington, where he studies brain health across the entire human lifespan — from hypoxic injuries in newborns to late-onset dementia in the elderly. He holds degrees from Cambridge, Oxford, and Oslo. He has competed in the world's first fully off-road Ironman triathlon and placed second at Washington's Strongest Man. He is also head scientist for motorsport at INEOS Performance, overseeing health programs for Formula One drivers. His forthcoming book, The Stimulated Mind, is the distillation of that research. His central argument is simple, and it should be more widely known: somewhere between 45% and 72% of dementia cases are preventable. The decline is not inevitable. The machinery degrades on a schedule that lifestyle can meaningfully interrupt.

The 45% Number and What It Actually Means

The 45% figure comes from the Lancet Commission on Dementia Prevention — a rigorous, periodically updated analysis that tallies up population attributable risk across known modifiable factors: smoking, low education, hypertension, hearing loss, obesity, physical inactivity, alcohol, air pollution, depression, and several others. Each contributes a few percentage points. Together, they add up to 45%. And that's probably an undercount, since things like poor sleep and late-life physical inactivity weren't even included in the most recent edition.

A separate analysis from the UK Biobank, led by Professor Yun Tai Yu, pushed the estimate to 72%. Much of that remaining gap is driven by socioeconomic factors — low educational opportunity, disadvantaged environments — which are harder for individuals to control. But a meaningful slice, somewhere around 15–20%, is within direct personal reach: whether you exercise, whether you smoke, how well you sleep, how aggressively you manage blood pressure and blood sugar.

None of this is a guarantee. Someone in your family can do everything right and still get dementia. These are population-level probabilities, not individual fate. But the deck is being shuffled every day by choices that most people aren't thinking about in terms of their brains.

The Supply-Demand Problem Nobody Talks About

The dominant framing of Alzheimer's disease as a kind of metabolic failure — "Type 3 diabetes," insulin resistance in the brain — has always contained a blind spot. PET scans showing reduced glucose uptake in Alzheimer's brains are real. The interpretation of what that means is not settled.

A PET scan cannot distinguish between glucose that can't get in and glucose that isn't being asked for. Several elegant studies have now shown that in early-stage Alzheimer's, if you cognitively stimulate the brain, it can take up glucose just fine — returning to normal healthy-brain levels of uptake. The brain isn't broken. It's idle. It's like a car up on blocks: the engine works, but no one's been running it.

This reframe matters enormously for prevention. Most of the conversation around brain energy focuses on the supply side — ketones, MCT oil, metabolic flexibility — and those tools are genuinely valuable, especially as therapeutics in early Alzheimer's. But the demand side has been largely ignored. If you want the brain to maintain its metabolic machinery, you have to keep giving it real cognitive work. Not crossword puzzles. Not brain-training apps that get easier once you've learned the trick. Real challenges: things you can fail at, things that require adapting, things that push beyond current capacity.

Neuroplasticity — the formation of new connections, new synaptic branches, the maintenance of the supporting cellular machinery — only gets triggered when there's a gap between what you can do and what you're trying to do. Exactly like muscle training to failure. If you never reach the edge of your current capability, nothing needs to change. The brain doesn't adapt because it doesn't have to.

The Right Kind of Exercise

Not all physical activity does the same thing to the brain. Walking is genuinely valuable — 4,000 steps a day reduces dementia risk by roughly 25%, and 10,000 steps daily is associated with hippocampal growth at a rate that reverses the typical age-related shrinkage. If you're currently sedentary, walking is where to start. But it has a ceiling.

Two distinct mechanisms need to be activated for maximal brain benefit from exercise, and most people only get one of them.

The first is intensity. High-intensity exercise generates lactate, and lactate crosses the blood-brain barrier. Inside the brain, it acts as a histone deacetylase inhibitor — it directly drives the production of BDNF, the brain's most important growth factor. (The BDNF released by muscles during exercise doesn't readily get into the brain; most of it is produced locally, and lactate is what triggers that local production.) A landmark Australian study had people do the Norwegian 4x4 protocol — four minutes at 85–95% of maximum heart rate, four times, with four minutes of rest — three times per week. The result was significant hippocampal improvements that were still measurable five years after the intervention ended. A few months of deliberate suffering, compounding for half a decade.

You don't need to do the Norwegian 4x4 specifically. Thirty seconds flat out on a rowing machine or bike, with several minutes of rest, repeated six to ten times, will get you there. Blood flow restriction training — partially occluding venous return in the arms and legs with cuffs, then doing bodyweight movements — generates comparable lactate at a fraction of the load. The threshold is approximately 6–7 millimoles of blood lactate. It feels terrible. That's the signal you've hit it.

The second mechanism is open-skill exercise — activities with a high level of unpredictable variety and complex motor skill demands. Ball sports, martial arts, racquet sports, rock climbing, dance. In studies comparing activities with equivalent physical load but different cognitive demands, the open-skill activities consistently show greater improvements in brain structure and cognitive function. They activate more of the brain because you can't just run the same motor program over and over. You have to respond, adapt, read the environment in real time.

Dance deserves special mention. It combines open-skill motor learning, social engagement, musical attention, and navigational complexity in one package. Across multiple study designs — longitudinal, interventional, depression studies — dance shows the largest effect size of any single physical activity on both cognitive function and mental health. The improvised Argentine tango, where partners navigate a floor in response to unannounced music, may be close to an optimal brain exercise if the evidence were ever to get specific.

Creative Complexity and the Brain Clock

A recent study called "Creative Experiences and Brain Clocks" looked at what happens to the brain's networks — the frontal-parietal network, the salience network, the attention network — when people develop genuine expertise in complex creative domains. They compared tango dancers against non-dancers, bilingual speakers against monolinguals, musicians against non-musicians, visual artists, and video gamers (Starcraft, specifically; for the record, Super Mario 3D World has the best evidence in the literature). The finding was consistent across all of them: as expertise increases, the discreteness and structural integrity of these critical cognitive networks improves. The networks that are most vulnerable to aging get more tightly organized, not less.

This is not about doing these activities occasionally. It's about building genuine expertise — which means regularly encountering the edge of your current capability, making errors, being pushed to adapt. Duolingo actually works, but only if you're actively progressing through it rather than repeating completed exercises. Music learning in older adults produces the same kind of executive function improvements as language learning. Attentive music listening — really tracking what's happening in a piece, not just ambient background noise — seems to carry some of the same benefits as playing.

Bilingualism produces a specific and reproducible cognitive advantage: dramatically better response inhibition. Because bilingual speakers spend a lifetime suppressing one language while activating another, they develop an unusually efficient neural mechanism for suppressing competing responses. This shows up not just in language tasks but in general executive function. The research from Wood's University of Washington colleagues Andrea Stocco and Chantal Pratt suggests this advantage is real and specific — bilinguals don't score higher on everything, but on tasks requiring controlled suppression of a competing impulse, they are consistently better.

The Nutritional Stack

The most important nutritional interventions for long-term brain health are not exotic. They're nutrients most people are deficient in without realizing it.

DHA is foundational. It concentrates at the synapses and in mitochondria; the DHA content of mitochondria is directly correlated with their capacity for energy production. It is also the precursor for neuroprotectin D1, resolvins, and protectins — signaling molecules critical for regulating and resolving the brain's immune response. A study in football players found that 1–2 grams of DHA daily significantly reduced the accumulation of neurofilament light across a season, a blood marker of brain injury. That effect came from subconcussive impacts — routine practice-level contact. Two to three servings of fatty fish per week, or a reasonable supplement, is probably enough to consistently maintain adequate levels.

But DHA alone doesn't work. Neither do B vitamins alone. The VITACOG trial and the B-PROOF trial found that B vitamin supplementation (specifically B12, folate/B9, riboflavin/B2, and B6) only significantly reduced brain atrophy and improved cognitive function in people who already had adequate omega-3 status. Conversely, the Omega-AD trial found omega-3s only helped in people with adequately low homocysteine. The mechanism is methylation: DHA needs to be incorporated into phospholipid membranes in the brain, and that process requires adequate methylation, which requires adequate B vitamins, particularly in people who are poor methylators. The orchestra needs all the instruments.

Get homocysteine tested. Levels above 13 indicate inadequate methylation status and predict poor response to omega-3 supplementation alone. If you're a poor methylator, supplementing methylfolate and methylcobalamin — the active forms of folate and B12 — is not optional.

Creatine has accumulated enough evidence across enough domains — acute cognitive performance, sleep deprivation, depression, traumatic brain injury recovery — that Wood takes 10 grams daily, all at once in the morning. There isn't a single RCT proving creatine prevents dementia. But the positive asymmetry is strong: high plausibility of benefit, low risk, remarkable safety profile. He finds it cognitively stimulating enough that taking it in the afternoon disrupts his sleep.

Choline, particularly CDP-choline (also called citicoline) at 500–1,000 mg daily, has randomized controlled trial evidence for improving cognitive function in older adults with early decline, and meta-analytic support for neuropsychological recovery after traumatic brain injury. Eggs and liver are the richest dietary sources; most people in the modern diet are becoming increasingly deficient.

Polyphenols from berries, coffee, tea, and the skins of roasted nuts and seeds show consistent associations with cognitive function in both acute and longitudinal studies. They're not magic bullets but they're genuinely useful and essentially risk-free.

The Overlooked Risks: Oral Health, Air Quality, and Sensory Loss

Three categories of dementia risk are consistently underweighted in popular discussions.

Periodontal disease — gum disease, especially when it advances to periodontitis — is significantly associated with dementia risk. The bacteria involved (Porphyromonas gingivalis and others) enter the bloodstream, have been found in atherosclerotic plaques, and have been found in amyloid plaques in the brain. The mechanism is probably dual: systemic inflammation and direct bacterial translocation to the brain. Go to the dentist. Use xylitol gum or mouthwash — multiple studies show it meaningfully shifts the oral microbiome away from cavity- and periodontitis-causing bacteria. For ApoE4 carriers specifically, anything that reduces systemic inflammatory burden appears to have an outsized protective effect.

Air pollution is a significant and underappreciated risk factor for dementia. Living near heavily trafficked roads is associated with increased cardiovascular risk and higher blood pressure in the short term. High blood pressure and high blood sugar are the two metabolic risk factors most consistently linked to dementia — controlling for everything else, those two dominate. HEPA air filters in the home, particularly in bedrooms, are a low-effort intervention with real evidence behind them. Recommended models for various room sizes and budgets: Healthmate, JSPR, Blueair, and Coway Airmega.

Sensory loss — hearing loss and vision loss through cataracts — meaningfully increases dementia risk, and that risk is reversible. Cataract surgery reverses the elevated risk. Hearing aids reverse it. The mechanism isn't only that the deprived brain regions atrophy; it's that sensory loss causes people to withdraw from the world. They socialize less, leave the house less, engage in stimulating activity less. The loss of input becomes the loss of the demand signal. Get hearing aids as soon as they're indicated. Don't treat them as a sign of defeat.

Sleep: The Non-Negotiable

If Wood had to pick one thing that he considers truly non-negotiable, it's sleep. Not because a few bad nights matter that much — they don't. During short periods of sleep deprivation, processing speed slows but accuracy doesn't change much, and mood is far more affected than actual performance. Most of the dread about poor sleep is self-induced, and that cognitive overhead makes things worse than the sleep loss itself.

The long-term picture is different. Inadequate sleep is a consistent, strong predictor of dementia risk. This is partly because sleep is when the brain clears metabolic waste products — including amyloid, but also many other things. Matt Walker's lab has shown that improving sleep quality in older adults is associated with lower overall amyloid burden, even later in life.

On dual orexin receptor antagonists (DORAs like suvorexant/Quviviq or lemborexant): they are now the best pharmaceutical option for people with genuine insomnia who have exhausted behavioral interventions (CBT-I first). They improve sleep architecture without the negative effects on sleep stages seen with older sedatives. But for people who are sleeping adequately, there's no evidence that adding DORAs reduces Alzheimer's risk — the sleep that isn't impaired is already doing its job.

Practical sleep optimization: stop work before dinner, use blue-light blocking glasses as a behavioral cue, read fiction before bed, cool the room and use a temperature-regulating sleep system if available, use an eye mask (the evidence on blocking early morning light is stronger than most people realize), and keep the room ventilated. High CO2 in sealed rooms significantly disrupts sleep quality.

Acute Brain Injury: What to Do Right Now

If you suffer a significant concussion, the priorities in roughly descending order of evidence are: prevent fever (acetaminophen/paracetamol; the metabolic gap between brain demand and supply worsens with elevated temperature), manage blood sugar (avoid refined carbohydrates; glucose spikes worsen acute injury), take omega-3s and creatine, consider exogenous ketones (not yet in the published evidence base but with strong mechanistic plausibility), add riboflavin and branched-chain amino acids for sleep support, use melatonin if sleep is disrupted, avoid caffeine in the first few days (it increases brain metabolic rate when the supply is compromised), and return to physical activity as early as possible — low-intensity aerobic exercise that doesn't worsen symptoms. In pediatric sports concussion, this last intervention has the most robust RCT evidence of anything on the list.

What To Do With This

  • Add at least one open-skill activity to your weekly routine. Dance, martial arts, racquet sports, rock climbing, or any ball sport where you're constantly responding to unpredictable stimuli. These produce cognitive benefits that unimodal aerobic exercise cannot, because they force the brain to adapt, not just sustain.
  • Do high-intensity exercise that produces real lactate at least once or twice per week. You don't need to memorize the Norwegian 4x4. Any protocol that has you working truly flat out for 20–45 seconds, repeated 6–10 times with several minutes of rest, will get you there. If you can use blood flow restriction cuffs, you can generate comparable lactate with bodyweight exercises. The intensity is the signal — uncomfortable enough that the words "quite hard" are an understatement.
  • Test homocysteine and omega-3 levels — and address both simultaneously. B vitamins and omega-3s only produce cognitive benefit when both are adequate. Getting one right without the other doesn't work. Homocysteine above 13 indicates inadequate methylation; address it with methylfolate and methylB12 in addition to DHA/EPA supplementation.
  • Take 5–10 grams of high-quality creatine monohydrate (CreaPure) daily, in the morning. The evidence for cognitive benefit — acute performance, sleep deprivation resilience, post-injury recovery — is consistent enough that the risk-benefit ratio is strongly favorable even without a specific dementia prevention RCT.
  • Treat oral health as a brain health intervention. See a dentist regularly, address any gum disease aggressively, and use xylitol gum (Epic or Kaigum) or xylitol mouthwash daily. Periodontal bacteria have been found in amyloid plaques; this is not a soft association.
  • Get sensory deficits corrected immediately, without hesitation. Hearing aids and cataract surgery both reverse the elevated dementia risk associated with sensory loss. The reason to avoid them is vanity; the cost of that vanity is real and measurable in cognitive reserve.
  • Start or resume learning a genuinely difficult skill that requires real expertise — a language, a musical instrument, improvisational dance. The cognitive benefit comes from sustained engagement at the edge of your current capability, which means you need to keep progressing, keep failing, keep adapting. Duolingo works, but only if you're actually advancing.
  • Put HEPA air filters in your bedroom and main living space. Affordable models (Coway Airmega for budget; Blueair or Healthmate for higher performance) reduce particulate matter that drives blood pressure elevation — one of the two most important metabolic risk factors for dementia.
  • After a concussion, avoid large carbohydrate loads in the first 24–48 hours and prevent fever with acetaminophen. Both glucose spikes and elevated temperature worsen the metabolic gap between brain demand and supply in the acute injury window. Begin gentle aerobic activity (walking, easy cycling) as soon as you can do so without symptoms worsening.
  • Stop catastrophizing about bad nights of sleep. Short-term sleep deprivation affects mood far more than it affects performance accuracy. The anxiety about poor sleep compounds the damage. Address chronic sleep deficits structurally (CBT-I, behavioral changes, temperature regulation); don't let acute bad nights become a spiral of rumination.

Memorable Quotes

"We think a lot about the supply side, the energetic supply side. But I don't think we think enough about the demand side, how to create energetic demand in the brain such that we are maintaining glucose uptake, maintaining energetic state."

— Dr. Tommy Wood
"To drive neuroplasticity, you essentially have to have a difference between capacity and expectation. The best way to uncover that is with mistakes or errors. If you don't ever get to the point where you're no longer capable of doing the thing that you want to do, nothing needs to change."

— Dr. Tommy Wood
"In order to see benefit from both omega-3s and B vitamins, both are required. If you only have one or the other, you won't see benefit."

— Dr. Tommy Wood
"If I can only focus on one thing, then sleep is really critical for me. I don't mind if my diet gets a bit crappy for a few days, or I can't get to the gym for a few days. This stuff integrates over months and years. But sleep — that's the non-negotiable."

— Dr. Tommy Wood
"In short periods of time, over a few days, processing speed slows down, but accuracy doesn't. Mood is affected much more than performance. So actually, we don't perform less well — we just feel more grumpy about how we performed."

— Dr. Tommy Wood
"If you have a complete societal overhaul — managing all of these risk factors — the idea is that maybe even up to three quarters of dementia cases could be entirely prevented. That's very different from saying I could guarantee that you will not get dementia."

— Dr. Tommy Wood
"When you look at different physical activities and how they affect the brain, different types of exercise affect the brain differently. You certainly need a smorgasbord of all of them to get global support for the brain. But something that seems particularly beneficial is open-skill exercise — sports where you're constantly having to respond to the environment and adapt."

— Dr. Tommy Wood
"A lot of the various things that support the brain through exercise — we often talk about BDNF. But the BDNF you can measure in the blood that's produced by the muscles doesn't really get into the brain very readily. Most of the BDNF in the brain is produced locally. And it's actually driven by things like lactate."

— Dr. Tommy Wood

item.title: Lex Fridman Podcast - #458 – Marc Andreessen: Trump, Power, Tech, AI, Immigration & Future of America
item.description:
The Architecture of a Boom

The United States is structurally primed for the kind of explosive growth it hasn't seen since the 1980s — and Marc Andreessen, co-creator of Mosaic, co-founder of Netscape and Andreessen Horowitz, believes the fuse is lit. Not because of any single policy or administration, but because the fundamentals were always there: physical security, natural resources, energy independence available on demand, and the most aggressively self-selecting immigrant talent pool in human history. The problem has never been the raw material. The problem has been what Andreessen calls a decade-long demoralization campaign — a comprehensive suppressive blanket of regulation, bureaucratic veto culture, and an ideological message that essentially told everyone who tried to build something: you're terrible, and if you try anything, you're still terrible.

The comparison to the 1970s is not casual. That decade had everything: energy crisis, hostage crisis, stagflation, foreign wars, demoralization. Jimmy Carter gave the malaise speech. Nobody got inspired. Then Reagan came in and declared it was morning in America — and it was. The national spirit came roaring back and ran hard for a full decade. The machinery of that comeback wasn't magic; it was permission. Someone in authority stood up and said that striving, building, and winning were good. And then the 60 percent of the population who'd been waiting for a signal they could follow — they followed.

That's the mechanism Andreessen keeps returning to: not policy, not legislation, not even economics, but the signal that unlocks dormant energy. And he thinks that signal just fired.

The Sociology of Conformity

To understand what's actually changing, you have to understand what the last decade was. Andreessen's framework for this draws on two books that have become essential to how he thinks about social dynamics. The first is Timur Kuran's Private Truths, Public Lies, which formalized the concept of preference falsification. The second is Vaclav Havel's The Power of the Powerless, which gave us the parable of the greengrocer.

Preference falsification has two faces. The first is believing something you can't say. The second — and this one is more corrosive — is saying something you don't believe, because you must. Havel's greengrocer in 1985 Prague puts the "Workers of the World Unite" sign in his shop window every morning, not because he believes it, but because the secret police will come if he doesn't. Everyone walking past knows the sign is fake. The greengrocer knows they know. The humiliation is the point. It's not ideological enforcement anymore — it's enforcement of compliance itself.

The United States spent the last decade cycling through its own version of those required signs. The specific content changed — the mechanisms didn't. And what Andreessen observed in Silicon Valley, in Hollywood, in corporate boardrooms, was the same pattern: no genuine disagreement at dinner parties, no open debate, just total lockstep on whatever the New York Times, Harvard, and the Ford Foundation agreed on that week. The lockstep itself mattered more than the content — you could track the positions changing over years while the unanimity never broke.

His rough taxonomy of any social preference-falsification regime is 20-60-20. Twenty percent are true believers. Twenty percent know it's wrong and won't sign on. The sixty percent in the middle are not stupid — they're just human. They have jobs, kids, and limited appetite for culture-war combat. By default, they conform to whatever the organized minority signals as correct. And organized minorities can be small and still dominate: only a minority can organize. That's Michels's Iron Law of Oligarchy — democracy is structurally fake, there's always a ruling elite, the question is only which elite and how they obtained their position.

The mechanism of social change, then, is the counter-elite: someone who stands up and says the emperor has no clothes, doesn't get immediately destroyed for it, and triggers a cascade. The first defector is the riskiest. The second defector watches to see what happens to the first. If the first survives, the numbers compound: two becomes four, four becomes eight, eight becomes sixteen. And if it turns out that sixty percent of the population had been quietly waiting for permission, things can move with remarkable and surprising speed — as they did in Eastern Europe in 1989, and as they appear to be doing now.

The mechanism of heretical idea transmission in a conformist environment, Andreessen notes with evident delight, is humor. Laughter is involuntary. You can't fake it convincingly. When someone says something genuinely true that has been officially forbidden, the laugh is the reveal: yes, I've been thinking that too. The joke provides deniability while the laughter provides confirmation. Group chats — encrypted, disappearing-message group chats — became the samizdat of the 2010s: a distributed infrastructure for the heretical whisper network. And every group chat, he observes, eventually becomes a comedy festival, with participants constantly testing how close to the line they can get.

The Ring of Power

The censorship regime that calcified over roughly 2013 to 2023 did not begin with malice. It began with something defensible. You can't run an internet service with zero content moderation — child abuse material and terrorist recruitment are not covered by the First Amendment for good reason. The problem is that once the machine exists, it becomes what Andreessen calls the ring of power. And as Tolkien understood, the ring is inherently corrupting. The temptation to use it for more than its original purpose is overwhelming. It corrupts the people who wield it, and it does so gradually enough that they often don't notice.

The original "hate speech" category started reasonably — prohibiting slurs — and by Jordan Peterson's logic, expanded to mean "things that make people uncomfortable." The misinformation category was seeded by the Russiagate era and then weaponized. The culminating moment — the one that broke something inside these companies, the thread in the sweater that started unraveling everything — was the suppression of the lab-leak theory. Inside the boardrooms and content moderation councils, the moment Jon Stewart went on Colbert and said it was simply not plausible that a lethal coronavirus appeared three hundred yards from the Wuhan Institute of Lethal Coronaviruses was the moment people looked at each other and asked: if we were this catastrophically wrong about censoring that, what does that say about everything else we've been doing?

But the censorship wasn't just platform-driven. What Marc Zuckerberg described on Joe Rogan, what the Twitter Files documented, what the congressional Weaponization Committee put in the public record across two thousand pages — all of it points to the same thing: the federal government decided, sometime around 2020, that the First Amendment didn't apply to it. Not through legislation, which is the cartoon version of how government works. Through regulation, through administrative power, through direct calls from the White House telling companies what to do. Through letters from senators to major banks that didn't technically say "don't work with this company" but communicated, in the language of The Godfather, that it would be a shame if something happened to their regulatory standing.

The specific federal statutes Andreessen cites are 18 U.S.C. §241 and §242 — conspiracy against rights and deprivation of rights under color of law. These are felonies. They apply to government employees and to private actors conspiring with government employees. And the de-banking campaigns that wiped out crypto businesses, Trump officials and their families, and various politically disfavored individuals fall under the same legal framework: the right to access the financial system is as protectable as the right to speak.

Power and Its Mechanics

Andreessen's political philosophy is grounded in what he calls the Machiavellians — specifically James Burnham's rehabilitation of the Italian realist school, including Robert Michels and his Iron Law of Oligarchy. The lesson is not cynical but clarifying: democracy is structurally fake, there is always a ruling elite, and the way to preserve any shred of freedom is to understand the mechanics honestly rather than pretend the machine works the way the civics cartoon said it did.

What the founders understood — and what makes the American constitutional system a singular achievement — is that given the inevitability of oligarchy, the design problem becomes: how do you create sufficient balance of power among elites to prevent any one faction from running away with everything? Three branches of government, two houses of Congress operating on different time scales and apportionment logics, the deliberate creation of friction and counter-pressure. They were engineers of political machinery, not philosophers writing down ideals.

The practical shorthand Andreessen uses for power analysis: who can get whom fired? The newspaper columnist making $200,000 a year has more power than the CEO making $200 million, because the columnist can get the CEO fired and not vice versa. The tenured professor has more power than the university president, because the president can't fire the professor, and the professor can outlast any president. The federal bureaucrat has more power than any administration appointee, because they can't be fired and the administration will be gone in four years. What makes DOGE potentially interesting — and what the Washington cynics underestimate — is the combination of presidential power at the top with social media transparency at the bottom, bypassing the oligarchy in the middle. It's a structural sandwich: rule of the one combined with rule of the many, squeezing the permanent administrative class that has operated as an unaccountable elite for decades.

The University and the Cartel

The university system is funded through four federal channels: student loans (many trillions, compounding fast), research grants (with universities skimming up to 70% overhead), operating tax exemptions premised on non-profit status, and endowment tax exemptions. Remove those sources and every major university goes bankrupt immediately. The thing preventing any new competitor from accessing those funding streams is accreditation — which is controlled not by the Department of Education but by accreditation bureaus that are themselves composed of the existing universities. The incumbents control the gate to their own replacement. It is a government-funded and government-protected cartel that has, in Andreessen's view, gone sideways in essentially every possible direction.

His counter to the optimistic "fix it from within" argument is Charlie Munger's dismissal of the same hope applied to General Electric: "I couldn't even fix the culture at a restaurant." The tenured professors know they can outlast any university president. The feral students and the radicalized faculty are in a mutual amplification loop. The incentive structure for brave behavior doesn't exist. Andreessen would love to be wrong about this. He has friends working heroically on reform efforts from within. But he has yet to see a single institution actually demonstrate compliance with the Supreme Court's affirmative action rulings — a ruling that is now several years old and appears to be flagrantly ignored by the entire sector.

Immigration, DEI, and the Dog That Didn't Bark

Andreessen has made the high-skilled immigration argument many times. He has been to Washington lobbying for more H-1Bs under multiple administrations. He believes the core logic — that importing exceptional people increases American productivity and creates more jobs than it displaces — is largely sound. He still believes it. But he spent Christmas reading every message in the public debate and waiting for something that never appeared: a single prominent advocate for high-skilled immigration who was also pounding the table to develop native-born American talent.

The argument that the domestic talent pool is insufficient — that America simply doesn't produce enough engineers and scientists — collides awkwardly with the observation that the same education system is considered good enough for foreign nationals to preferentially seek out, but not good enough to educate the people already here.
