The farm kid from rural Wisconsin who doesn't show up in Silicon Valley is not obviously less capable than his great-grandparents, who built the railroads, industrialized the country, and put men on the moon. Something happened to the pipeline. And the Harvard and UNC Supreme Court cases put into the public record, for the first time with legal precision, exactly what happened: a systematic, statistically documented exclusion of Asians, Jews, white applicants from rural and working-class backgrounds, and — most surprisingly — American-born Black students in favor of foreign-born Black students, primarily Nigerian and West Indian.

The 2004 New York Times story Andreessen cites, quoting Henry Louis Gates and Lani Guinier, makes the point with striking directness: Harvard was struggling to admit and retain American-born Black students, so it used high-skilled immigration as an escape hatch, importing Blacks from other countries to hit diversity numbers while the intended beneficiaries of affirmative action — descendants of American slavery — received no benefit at all. The irony is structural: affirmative action was designed to compensate for the specific historical injustice of American chattel slavery; using it to benefit Nigerian immigrants who experienced no such history is not just ineffective but morally incoherent.

The more uncomfortable global dimension: the brain drain that benefits the US is a direct loss to the countries being drained. Every highly ambitious, high-IQ individual who leaves India or Nigeria or Ukraine to build their life in America is an individual their home country doesn't have. We understand resource extraction as colonial evil when it's oil or diamonds. We celebrate the identical dynamic when it's human capital. Andreessen doesn't have a resolution — he just thinks it's a moral reckoning that will eventually arrive, and that pretending otherwise is a form of delusion.

The AI Question

On the AI landscape, Andreessen offers a framework rather than predictions: the trillion-dollar questions. These are the open empirical and political questions where the answer will determine who makes and who loses a trillion dollars, and where there is currently genuine, expert-level disagreement about the answer. Big models versus small models. Open versus closed. Synthetic data — will it work at scale, and if so, in which domains? Chain-of-thought reasoning and reinforcement learning — how far can they push AI performance on tasks where outputs can be verified? The hallucination problem: in domains with checkable, provable answers (math, coding), you can in principle train it away; in domains without clear ground truth, maybe you can't.

The most important trillion-dollar question that is most often overlooked: the political and regulatory one. How many countries want to run their education, healthcare, news, and political systems on an AI trained according to the most extreme left-wing California politics? The EU is already trying to lock in a draconian censorship regime for AI severe enough that American companies are declining to launch new products there. Whatever the technical trajectory, the political and regulatory trajectory will shape what gets built, who deploys it, and at what cost — and that fight is now openly joined in a way it wasn't two years ago.

On AI coding specifically: Andreessen's nine-year-old is in coding classes and will stay in them through at least eighteen. The ability to evaluate AI-generated code, to manage AI systems productively, to understand what's happening under the hood — these remain differential advantages for people with technical backgrounds. But the ceiling on what an individual builder can accomplish with AI assistance has moved dramatically. One-person companies can now get quite far. And code has what Andreessen calls maximum elasticity: the easier it is to produce, the more of it gets made, because the demand is essentially infinite. In the late 1980s, people wrote books predicting the end of the programmer. The number of programming jobs then exploded by a factor of one hundred. He expects the same dynamic to hold.

On Loneliness, Power, and What Success Actually Means

Running a company like Meta at scale is a profoundly lonely job, and almost no one has sympathy for this because CEOs are paid a lot and that seems to settle the moral accounting. But the loneliness is structural: you can't talk honestly to executives who have agendas, boards who can fire you, shareholders who will dump your stock, or the press who will use anything you say against you. The only relief valve is other CEOs, and even that is fraught with insider trading risk and confidentiality concerns. Add to that the daily experience of being legally and illegally pressured by government agencies, internally pressured by activist employees, and publicly attacked by everyone from academics to NGOs to nonprofit censorship bureaus — and you have a situation where, as Andreessen puts it, he would be a little puddle on the floor after a decade of it.

His observation on the distinction between external and internal pressure is practical and important: companies can withstand almost any form of external pressure as long as internal coherence holds. Investor panic, customer defection, negative press — all of that can be survived and can even become bonding. The moment that cannot be survived is the internal crack. When the LA mayor and fire chief turned on each other during the fires, that government was finished. You can see the moment, and once you see it, you know the whole thing is coming apart.

On success and what it means: Andreessen doesn't believe happiness in the hedonic sense is a coherent goal. The first ice cream cone is transcendent. The thousandth is just eating. Jefferson's "pursuit of happiness" was closer to the Greek eudaimonia — fulfillment, satisfaction at having done something that mattered. The people he knows who seem genuinely at peace are the ones who can sit alone at four in the morning and honestly answer: have I lived up to my own expectations? That's the accounting that matters. The sensation seekers — the extreme sports, the drugs, the escalating escalation of stimulation — tend to go off the rails. The people who are building something they believe in, who feel they've been useful, tend not to.

What To Do With This

  • Read Timur Kuran's Private Truths, Public Lies. It is the definitive analytical framework for understanding preference falsification, how authoritarian conformity spreads, and — critically — how it collapses. If you want to understand the political and cultural dynamics of the last decade, this is the map.
  • Use humor as a diagnostic tool in your own organization. Andreessen's observation about humor as the mechanism for detecting genuine agreement versus performed conformity is directly applicable to teams. If no one is laughing at anything off-script, your organization may have a preference falsification problem. What are people saying in the parking lot that they're not saying in the meeting?
  • Apply the "who can get whom fired" power analysis to any institutional problem you're trying to solve. Before attempting to change a system, map the actual power structure — not the org chart, but who can force whom out. If the tenured faculty can outlast the president, the president does not run the university. Act accordingly.
  • Evaluate AI tools for actual internal coherence, not surface integration. Andreessen's distinction between "AI as sixth bullet point" (a feature bolted on) and "AI as first citizen" (rebuilt from scratch around AI capabilities) is a useful diagnostic. When evaluating tools or startups, ask: was this designed with the assumption that AI exists, or was it designed before AI and retrofitted?
  • Learn to code, or significantly deepen your existing coding skills, specifically for AI orchestration. The ceiling on solo builders has moved dramatically. Even non-coders can get surprisingly far with AI assistance, but people with coding backgrounds will have differential ability to evaluate, direct, and debug AI-generated work. Khan Academy and Replit (Andreessen mentions both) offer accessible entry points.
  • Pay attention to the trillion-dollar questions in AI rather than the headline horse race. The OpenAI vs. Google vs. Meta framing obscures the more important questions: synthetic data effectiveness, big vs. small models, open vs. closed, hallucination mitigation in non-verifiable domains. These are the questions that will determine who wins, and most coverage ignores them entirely.
  • If you're hiring, think about the National Merit Scholar system as a model. Universities recruit athletes nationally with full-time scouts and systematic outreach. Almost none of them use the National Merit database — which is a race-blind, gender-blind, standardized talent identification system identifying the top 0.5% of high school students — as a recruiting pipeline. The same structural gap exists in corporate hiring. The talent is there; no one is going to get it.
  • When building new ventures, distinguish between problems that require incumbents to fail versus problems that can be solved in parallel. Andreessen's view on universities — that they cannot be reformed, only replaced, and replacement requires defunding — applies more broadly to any institution protected by government-funded cartel dynamics. If the accreditation system protects incumbents and bars competitors, building a better product is not sufficient. You need to change the funding structure or build something outside it.
  • Read the Twitter Files and the congressional Weaponization Committee reports. Andreessen says explicitly that the Twitter Files are fully representative of what he saw at every major platform. The two thousand pages of Facebook documents produced to the Weaponization Committee are the Facebook version of the same record. If you operate in any regulated industry, or if you run any platform business, these are operational reality, not political controversy.
  • When assessing internal team pressure, watch for the crack, not the squeeze. External pressure — investors, press, regulators — is survivable as long as your internal team stays coherent. The failure mode is almost always internal fracture first. Monitoring team cohesion under pressure is more predictive than monitoring any external metric.

Memorable Quotes

"We've just been through this period where whether it started that way or not, it ended up basically being this comprehensive message that says, you're terrible and if you try to do anything, you're terrible and fuck you."
— Marc Andreessen
"The big thing I have now come to believe is that the idea that people have beliefs is mostly wrong. I think that most people just go along, and I think even most high status people just go along, and I think maybe the most high status people are the most prone to just go along, because they're the most focused on status."
— Marc Andreessen
"The thing that the tenured professors at all these places know is it doesn't matter who the president is because they can outlast them. Because they cannot get fired. By the way, it's the same thing that bureaucrats in the government know."
— Marc Andreessen
"The original social state is living in the state of the absolute imperative for survival with absolutely no technology. Maximum fascism combined with maximum communism. And then it was all intricately tied into their religion — veneration of ancestors, and veneration of nature. We have actually worked our way all the way back to their cult religions without realizing it."
— Marc Andreessen
"I did not see a single example of somebody pounding the table for more high-skilled immigration who was also pounding the table to go get more smart kids who are already here into these educational institutions and into these jobs."
— Marc Andreessen
"Hallucinations — if you are trying to use these tools creatively, you're thrilled because they can draw new images and they can make new music and they can do all this incredible stuff. The flip side of that is if you need them to be correct, they can't be creative. And that's the term hallucination."
— Marc Andreessen
"If a man does not have a real religion, he makes up a fake one. And the fake ones go very, very badly."
— Marc Andreessen (quoting Ben Horowitz's father)
"Who has more power — the newspaper columnist who makes $200,000 a year, or the CEO who makes $200 million a year? I know for sure that the columnist can get the CEO fired. I have yet to see a CEO get a columnist fired."
— Marc Andreessen

item.title: BG2Pod with Brad Gerstner and Bill Gurley - Inside OpenAI Enterprise: Forward Deployed Engineering, GPT-5, and More | BG2 Guest Interview
item.description:
The Infrastructure Nobody Talks About: How OpenAI Actually Deploys AI in the Enterprise

Physical autonomy is ahead of digital autonomy in 2025. Think about that for a moment. You can hail a Waymo in San Francisco and ride from one end of the city to the other with zero human involvement. Meanwhile, if you try to get an AI agent to book a flight online, it will probably fail. The bar for physical safety — where lives are at stake — is higher than the bar for digital, and yet the self-driving car has lapped the AI agent. Why?

That question sits at the center of how Sherwin Wu and Olivier Godement think about enterprise AI. Sherwin leads engineering for the OpenAI platform. Olivier leads product. Between them, they have overseen hundreds of enterprise deployments — T-Mobile, Amgen, Los Alamos National Laboratory — and they have developed a precise mental model of why some of those deployments work brilliantly and why most do not. The answer is almost never the model. The answer is the scaffolding.

The Platform Most People Forget Exists

Most people know OpenAI as the company that built ChatGPT. What they tend to forget is that the original OpenAI product was not ChatGPT at all — it was a B2B API aimed at developers. Sherwin joined in September 2022, months before ChatGPT launched, to work on that API. The platform is how OpenAI believes it will actually fulfill its mission of distributing the benefits of AGI to everyone. ChatGPT reaches billions of consumers, but the API reaches everyone else — every corner of the world, every industry, every type of end user that only gets touched through a business. Healthcare patients who receive better drugs. Telecom customers who get their support call handled gracefully. Government scientists working on research that cannot be discussed in public. None of those people know they are interacting with OpenAI. That is by design.

The platform today has three main product lines: the developer API, a government and public sector offering, and an emerging enterprise product set that goes beyond raw API access. Olivier spent nearly a decade at Stripe before joining OpenAI, and he describes the platform's evolution in those terms — the early customers were indie hackers and startups taking massive risks on something new, and then one day the large enterprises woke up, saw that the models were actually working, and came flooding in.

What It Actually Takes to Deploy at Scale

T-Mobile has tens of millions of customers, and a massive share of its support volume is voice calls. The goal was to automate not just text support but voice — real-time, human-sounding, low-latency voice — so that when a customer calls because they were charged wrong or their service is down, they are handled by an OpenAI model without knowing it. Getting from "tokens in, tokens out" to that finished experience is enormously complex, and almost none of the complexity is in the model itself.

OpenAI has built what it calls a forward deployed engineering team — a term borrowed from Palantir — people who embed deeply with customers and build things specific to those customers' systems. For T-Mobile, that meant orchestrating models against a CRM that had never exposed clean APIs to a third party before. It meant standing up API gateways. It meant defining what good looks like — an eval problem that is especially brutal for voice, where a five-minute call transfer is extraordinarily hard to grade. It meant wrestling with latency and interruption handling until the experience felt natural enough to ship. The real-time API that OpenAI GA'd recently — a native speech-to-speech model that drops the old stitched-together pipeline of speech-to-text, reasoning, and text-to-speech — came in significant part from lessons learned working with T-Mobile. The improvements in the GA model snapshot came from understanding what good sounded like in actual T-Mobile calls.

Amgen offers a different window into the same dynamic. The pharma giant is using OpenAI to speed up drug development and commercialization. There are two buckets of need at a company like Amgen. The first is pure R&D — brilliant scientists generating and testing hypotheses against enormous datasets. The second is something less glamorous but equally enormous: the administrative and regulatory documentation burden that sits between a locked drug recipe and actual market approval. You have to submit to regulatory bodies. You have to author and review reams of documents. Amgen has become one of the heaviest users of GPT-5 for exactly this reason. The model is extremely good at the document-heavy work, which means the scientists can spend more time doing science. If this doubles the rate of breakthrough drugs over the next decade — and Olivier thinks that is plausible — the impact on human lives is almost incalculable.

Los Alamos National Laboratory is the most extreme case. O3, OpenAI's reasoning model, is running inside an air-gapped supercomputer called Venado, deployed on-premises in a facility where you cannot bring a cell phone. Sherwin's team literally carried the model weights physically into the lab. They cannot see telemetry in the normal sense — the lab controls its own environment. But the researchers there are using O3 as a thought partner on novel scientific problems, which is something Sherwin emphasizes the older models simply could not do. O3 is smart enough to be a genuine intellectual collaborator, not just a lookup engine. The deployment is shared across Los Alamos, Lawrence Livermore, and Sandia National Laboratories through the supercomputer infrastructure.

Why Most AI Deployments Fail

The MIT report that landed a few weeks ago claimed 95 percent of AI deployments do not work. Olivier pattern-matches against hundreds of deployments and arrives at three things that consistently separate success from failure.

The first is the combination of top-down commitment and a tiger team. At T-Mobile, leadership declared it a priority and then let a focused team organize and start small. The tiger team needs to be mixed — technical people alongside people who carry institutional knowledge in their heads. This last point is deceptively important: in most enterprises, the standard operating procedures live in people's heads, not in documentation. You cannot build an AI deployment without the humans who hold that tacit knowledge.

The second is evals first. Define what good looks like before you start building. This sounds simple and is actually very hard. Evals for voice are especially difficult. But whenever a customer fails to nail down their evals, the project becomes a moving target. Nobody can tell whether the hill is being climbed or not. OpenAI has been building tooling here specifically because it keeps being the bottleneck.

The third is patient hill-climbing. You start at 46 percent on your eval and you need to get to 99. There is art involved, not just science — knowing the quirks of the model, knowing when fine-tuning is needed, knowing which prompts to adjust. The teams that succeed are the ones that are willing to grind that process and not ship until they are at a level of quality that the experience actually feels natural.

Sherwin's diagnosis of the deeper structural problem is the scaffolding gap. Self-driving cars benefit from a world that has, in a sense, been scaffolded for them — roads are standardized, traffic laws are learnable, stoplights follow known patterns. AI agents get dropped in the middle of nowhere. Most enterprises have not built the connectors, data organization, and integration layer that would allow an agent to operate effectively. The FDE team's most important work is often not model-related at all. It is building that scaffolding so the model has something coherent to interact with. Once enterprises have that infrastructure in place, deployment speeds up dramatically. Right now, most don't have it.

GPT-5: What It Got Right and What It Is Still Working Through

GPT-5 is the first time OpenAI has shipped what it describes as a full system rather than a model or a collection of models. The intelligence is genuinely remarkable — on SWE-bench and similar evaluations it is performing at a level that earlier models could not approach. But for Olivier, the more important achievement is behavioral. For months before release, the team worked in unusually close feedback loops with enterprise customers to identify the concrete blockers — not raw capability gaps but behavior gaps. Does the model follow instructions well? Does it know when to say no? Does it behave professionally in an enterprise context? GPT-5 is the first large model release where that customer-driven behavioral iteration was really baked in at scale.

The biggest trade-off in the model is reasoning tokens versus latency. Extended thinking is extraordinarily powerful — GPT-5 Pro has been one-shotting problems that no other model could crack — but you can wait ten minutes for the answer. For API use cases with real users, that is often not acceptable. The team has been working on making the thinking more dynamic, so simpler problems get quick answers and only genuinely hard problems trigger long chains of reasoning. The goal is to squeeze as much intelligence as possible into as few reasoning tokens as possible.

The other piece of constructive feedback has been a kind of monkey's paw problem with instruction-following. Enterprise customers asked for a model that really follows instructions. GPT-5 really follows instructions — sometimes so literally that prompts written for older models, which required multiple lines of "be concise, also be concise, really be concise" to overcome verbosity, now produce one-sentence responses. The fix is to rewrite the prompts. GPT-5 does not need to be begged to be concise. That adjustment is forcing a quality upgrade in how enterprises write prompts, which is ultimately good, but it has created a transitional friction.

Reinforcement Fine-Tuning: The New Customization Paradigm

Model customization has been part of the OpenAI API from the beginning. Supervised fine-tuning — providing prompt-completion pairs to move the model toward a specific behavior — has been available since the pre-ChatGPT era. But the development that is genuinely exciting right now is reinforcement fine-tuning, which OpenAI announced in the 12 Days of Christmas last year and has since GA'd.

RFT introduces reinforcement learning into the fine-tuning process. It is more complex and more finicky than supervised fine-tuning, but it is an order of magnitude more powerful. Instead of telling the model exactly how to behave through labeled examples, you give it a set of tasks that are highly gradable, and you use an objective grader to run RL over those tasks. The result is not just a model customized for your use case — it is potentially the best model in the world for your specific domain.

Rogo, a financial services AI startup that has recruited deeply from DeepMind, is using RFT to build best-in-class performance on parsing and reasoning over financial documents. Accordance is doing the same in tax, targeting the TaxBench evaluation of CPA-style tasks. Because both domains have well-defined, gradable task structures, they can turn the RL crank and push model performance to levels that general fine-tuning cannot reach.

Olivier's view is that base models are now so good at instruction-following that behavioral steering — getting a model to act in a certain style or follow certain rules — no longer requires fine-tuning at all. You just describe what you want. Fine-tuning's remaining value is in capability extension: if you are actually trying to push the frontier of what the model can do in your specific domain, RFT is where that work happens. The data for RFT, critically, tends to come from the same bottom-up sources as the institutional knowledge required for good evals — it requires domain experts who deeply understand the task and can grade it correctly.

The Architecture of Voice

The old approach to voice AI was a pipeline: speech-to-text, then a reasoning model, then text-to-speech. This stitching worked as a starting point but introduced latency at every seam and lost signal — emotion, accent, tone, pauses — at each handoff. The real-time API is a native speech-to-speech model that takes audio in and generates audio out, with no intermediate text representation.

The current priority on voice is closing the intelligence gap between text and voice. For a long time, users noticed that the voice model felt somewhat less smart than the text model — which is jarring once you notice it. That gap is narrowing but not yet closed. The second priority is teaching the model the intuitions that experienced phone operators have. A simple example: if someone is spelling out their Social Security number and one digit sounds fuzzy, the model should ask them to repeat it, not guess. Those micro-behaviors are only surfaced by running the model against actual customer support calls. It is genuinely hard to discover those gaps in advance.

From a pure engineering standpoint, Sherwin finds it almost philosophical that any of this works at all. The fact that an LLM trained on text autoregressively predicts the next token and sounds intelligent is already mind-bending. The fact that a speech-to-speech model trained on audio tokens does the same thing — and can understand accents, track conversation context, and handle the interruption patterns of a real phone call — is something else entirely.

The Long View: Where This Goes

AI agents are roughly where self-driving cars were ten to fifteen years ago. The technology is real, it is working, it is being deployed. But the progress curve is much steeper. ChatGPT launched in late 2022. The reasoning paradigm — which is what actually enables robust agents — only started with O1 preview less than two years ago. Self-driving had a decade of hard grinding before the current deployment wave. AI agents have had a year and a half of the reasoning paradigm. The crossover, by some metrics, may have already happened.

On the question of whether there will be more or fewer software engineers in ten years, both Sherwin and Olivier land firmly on more — but with a major asterisk. The amount of software engineering happening in the world will explode. The Reddit post Sherwin references captures it: a non-engineer built a custom application for his nonverbal brother using ChatGPT — tools tailored to exactly one person's needs, built by someone whose job title has nothing to do with engineering. That kind of creation will become commonplace. Olivier's prediction is that product managers will code, that the product requirements document will be replaced by a working prototype built in a couple of hours, and that the long-standing software shortage — an unspoken constraint on what organizations can do — will start to dissolve.

The advice both give to young people entering the workforce is a version of the same insight. Olivier: prioritize critical thinking above everything else. Fields that require genuine reasoning — math, physics, philosophy — will be durable regardless of what AI does. Fields built on memorization and pattern-matching are more fragile. Sherwin: AI-native young people have an enormous advantage in the current workplace that most of them are not fully aware of. The interns he has seen come through OpenAI are Cursor power users of a caliber that would have been unimaginable five years ago. That advantage compounds. Use it.

The "feel the AGI" moment is different for each of them. For Olivier, it was the voice models — the first time he talked to something that understood his accent, understood tone, and crossed from cold deterministic logic into something that felt genuinely human. For Sherwin, it was two things: the first time he spoke to GPT-4 internally, before ChatGPT existed, and threw things at it that he was certain it could not answer and watched it knock them out of the park — and then, later, deep research, the first model that would pursue a hard question with real persistence and produce write-ups that felt like serious intellectual work. Both of them describe the same inflection: throwing something at the model you are certain it will fail, and watching it succeed.

What To Do With This

  • Build your evals before you build anything else. Every successful enterprise AI deployment starts with a precise definition of what good looks like. Vague success criteria guarantee a moving target. For voice use cases, invest extra time here — audio evals are disproportionately hard and disproportionately important.
  • Audit your institutional knowledge before you pick tools. Most enterprise AI failures happen because the knowledge required to run the deployment lives in people's heads, not in documentation. Identify the human subject matter experts who carry that knowledge and make them part of your tiger team before you write a line of integration code.
  • Create scaffolding for your agents before you deploy them. The reason agents fail in enterprise is not model intelligence — it is that they get dropped into environments with no clean interfaces, no organized data, no standardized connectors. Build the infrastructure layer first. Think of it as building roads before deploying the cars.
  • Rewrite your prompts when you upgrade to GPT-5. Prompts written for older models often contain redundant instructions meant to overcome previous model limitations. GPT-5 follows instructions literally, which means over-specified prompts produce over-compliant responses. Strip out the historical scaffolding and write prompts that say what you actually want.
  • Evaluate reinforcement fine-tuning if you have a gradable domain task. If your use case has a well-defined task structure that can be objectively graded — legal review, financial document parsing, medical coding, tax compliance — RFT can produce models that outperform anything achievable through supervised fine-tuning or prompt engineering alone. The key requirement is an objective grader, not just labeled examples.
  • Move voice infrastructure toward the real-time API, not the stitched pipeline. The speech-to-text → reasoning → text-to-speech pipeline loses emotional signal and introduces latency at every seam. Native speech-to-speech models are demonstrably more natural and lower latency. For new voice deployments, default to the real-time API; for existing stitched deployments, plan the migration.
  • Implement the tiger team model for enterprise AI rollouts. The team needs both technical people and deep domain/institutional knowledge holders. Neither alone is sufficient. Without technical staff you cannot build the integration layer. Without domain experts you cannot define what good looks like or surface the tacit SOPs that no documentation captures.
  • Use AI to replace the PRD with a working prototype. OpenAI's product team stopped writing product requirements documents and started having PMs build coded prototypes — in a few hours, using Cursor and Codex. A working prototype communicates more information about the product decision than a five-page document and gets to feedback faster. Adopt this workflow.
  • Treat AI-native employees as a strategic asset, not a novelty. New entrants to the workforce who have grown up using these tools are genuinely more productive at AI-augmented work. Build onboarding and workflow structures that let them teach the rest of the organization, rather than having them absorb the existing culture's skepticism.
  • For high-stakes voice applications, train explicitly on domain-specific verbal intuitions. Models are excellent at casual conversation but need explicit examples to handle economically important scenarios correctly — e.g., knowing to ask for repetition when a digit is unclear rather than guessing. Gather real call recordings, build a golden set of examples for these edge cases, and use them to fine-tune or guide the model.

Memorable Quotes

Physical autonomy is ahead of digital autonomy in 2025. In San Francisco, you could take a car from one part of SF to the other fully autonomously. As opposed to the digital world, I can't book a ticket online right now.

— Apoorv Agrawal

AI agents are just kind of dropped in the middle of nowhere. Self-driving cars have roads — they're pretty standardized, you have stoplights. AI agents get dropped in the middle of nowhere and they kind of have to feel around.

— Sherwin Wu

The vast majority of the knowledge is in people's heads. You would think in customer support that everything is perfectly documented in JIRA. The reality is the standard operating procedures are largely in people's heads.

— Olivier Godement

Once you have a model that is extremely literal, it essentially forces you to express extremely clearly what you want. It's almost like the monkey's paw — developers asked for better instruction following, so we gave them really good instruction following. But it follows it almost to a T.

— Sherwin Wu

With RFT, the discussion is no longer about creating a custom model for your use case. You can actually use your own data and turn the crank on RL to create a best-in-class model — maybe the best in the world — for your particular domain.

— Sherwin Wu

The first one was the realization in 2023 that I would never need to code manually, like ever, ever again. Realizing that what I thought was a given — that humans would have to write machine language forever — is actually not a given.

— Olivier Godement

I feel the AGI moments are: I throw something at the model that I was certain it would not be able to get, and it just knocks it out of the park. That is the feel AGI moment.

— Sherwin Wu

There is a massive software shortage in the world — we've been accepting it for twenty years. But the goal of software was never to be a super rigid, super hard-to-build artifact. It was to be customized, malleable. I expect we'll see way more people coding.

— Olivier Godement

item.title: BG2Pod with Brad Gerstner and Bill Gurley - AI Enterprise - Databricks & Glean | BG2 Guest Interview
item.description:
We Already Have AGI. Now Stop Pretending Otherwise.

The debate over whether artificial general intelligence has arrived is, at this point, a distraction — and possibly a convenient one for people whose business model depends on the answer being "not yet." Ali Ghodsi, CEO of Databricks, has a simpler view: we already have AGI. We had a definition for it for thirty years, we hit it, and then we moved the goalposts. Anyone who tells you we need to "reach AGI" first before AI becomes transformative is working from a false premise. The more interesting question — the one that actually matters to the 95% of enterprise AI deployments that are still struggling — is how you make the AGI we already have genuinely useful inside organizations.

That reframe drives everything Ali and Arvind Kumar, CEO of Glean, think about enterprise AI. Both of them have watched every technology super cycle up close: the internet, mobile, cloud, the data era. And both of them are operating in what they consider the third camp of AI — not the frontier labs racing toward superintelligence through ever-larger GPU clusters, not the original researchers like Yann LeCun and Rich Sutton who say the whole approach is wrong and the real solution is 20 years out, but the practitioners building things that work now. That camp doesn't need recursive self-improvement or godlike reasoning. It needs AI that can process an earnings report in 15 minutes instead of two hours. It needs agents that understand a company's gene regulatory network. It needs marketing stacks that can segment audiences and generate personalized content at a scale no human team could match. That's already happening. The question is why it isn't happening everywhere.

The 95% Problem Is a Feature, Not a Bug

The MIT study showing 95% of enterprise AI deployments fail reads differently depending on your priors. To Ali, it's exactly what you'd expect and exactly what you'd want. When new technology is genuinely transformative, most experiments should fail. If everything works, you're not pushing hard enough. The number will probably look similar next year, and that's fine — the point is to keep experimenting, keep learning what the 5% that works actually requires.

What separates the 5% from the 95% comes down to something most companies don't want to hear: LLMs are a commodity. Not in the dismissive sense, but in the precise economic sense — they're interchangeable. This week one model is better, next week another is, and the price difference matters more than the capability difference. You can switch LLMs in a day. You can't switch your customer data, your proprietary business processes, or your institutional knowledge in a day. That's where the competitive moat lives, and that's what most companies are ignoring while they build commodity AI that any competitor can replicate with the same off-the-shelf tools.

The companies winning right now are the ones who asked a different first question: What data do we have that our competitors don't? What process is unique to how we deliver our product or service? Then they built AI on top of that. Royal Bank of Canada built agents with Databricks that pull earnings reports, competitor filings, and market data, and produce equity research reports in 15 minutes — what used to take two hours of analyst time. Merck built TEDI, a transformer model that predicts gene regulatory behavior for drug discovery. 7-Eleven automated audience segmentation and content creation across its entire marketing stack. None of these is a demo. All of them are in production, and all of them are built on data and process knowledge that belongs to the company, not to a foundation model provider.

Why This Isn't RPA With Better Branding

The fair skeptic's question is: haven't we been here before? Robotic process automation had a moment, attracted serious investment, and largely fizzled. The enterprise graveyard is full of automation technologies that worked in demos and broke in production. What's actually different now?

Ali's answer is structural. RPA was brittle because it was rule-based and couldn't learn. You defined the rules, it followed them, and when something unexpected happened — which in any real business environment happens constantly — you had to go back and manually expand the rules. There was no generalization, no pattern recognition, no adaptability. Current AI systems learn. They generalize. They can handle the unexpected because they're not operating from an if-then-else decision tree but from something closer to genuine understanding of context.

There's still an unsolved problem at the boundary: AI systems today are trained on a snapshot of data, then frozen. They can be given context at inference time, but the model itself doesn't continue learning from every new interaction. For applications that require continuous adaptation — like truly automated computer use that's updating its own behavior in real time as it navigates a desktop — this is a real limitation. The paradigm of bake-then-freeze needs to evolve. But the gap between RPA and current AI is so fundamental — rule-based versus generative, brittle versus adaptive — that the comparison is almost misleading. This isn't a bigger budget version of the same movie.

Where Value Accrues in a World Where Models Are Commodities

If LLMs become like TSMC fabs — essential infrastructure that's available to everyone at roughly comparable quality and cost — then the value in the AI stack has to accumulate somewhere else. The obvious layers are data, intelligence, and application. Arvind's view is that the intelligence layer will be thicker than people expect — perhaps capturing around half of enterprise value — because even if individual models are interchangeable, the orchestration, fine-tuning, and governance infrastructure around them represents durable engineering work. But most of the eventual value, Ali believes, will flow to applications. Not necessarily the ones anyone can name today.

The 2000 internet analogy is apt and uncomfortable. In 2000, the smart money was on portals — those web pages with a hundred links that would organize the internet for you. Nobody predicted that the winning applications would be Facebook, Airbnb, Uber, Twitter. Amazon and Google already existed in 1998, but most people missed what they would become. Databricks and Glean are already in the market, doing real revenue, serving real enterprises. Some of the companies that will define the AI application layer in a decade are probably already founded. Others haven't been started yet. The portfolio approach — experiment with more vendors, do short-term contracts, pick products that prove value quickly — isn't just financially prudent. It's epistemically honest about what anyone actually knows right now.

The one governance layer that will definitely matter: data security and access control. As AI agents proliferate inside organizations, they're accessing HR records, salary data, strategic plans, and customer information. The risk of an agent blurting out a colleague's compensation in a customer meeting isn't hypothetical — it's a design problem that has to be solved before enterprises will trust agents with their most sensitive data. The companies that figure out agent-native governance, not bolted-on access controls inherited from pre-AI software architectures, will have a significant advantage.

The Software Question: Databases or Living Systems?

There's a popular frame circulating in enterprise tech circles — including from Satya Nadella — that traditional SaaS applications are becoming just databases, and AI will dynamically render whatever UI or workflow a user needs on top of that data. Arvind thinks this is an oversimplification that misses what software companies actually do well. Salesforce isn't valuable because it stores CRM data. It's valuable because years of product thinking went into how that data gets captured, organized, surfaced, and acted on in ways that map to how sales teams actually work. You can't replace that by asking an LLM to generate a UI. Most users won't know what they want until they see it, and getting that experience right requires sustained product design work, not a prompt.

What will change is data entry — and this is where the real disruption is coming. The dominant interface for enterprise software today is still a human typing structured information into forms. That's inefficient, incomplete, and increasingly unnecessary. A meeting happens, and the most important outcomes from that meeting should flow automatically into whatever system of record tracks customers, projects, or decisions. Arvind sees Zoom — and products like it — as perfectly positioned to be the data entry layer for the entire enterprise software stack. The conversation happens, the AI understands what was discussed and decided, and it propagates that information into the downstream systems without anyone filling out a form. Glean is already running agents that take meeting recordings, extract customer commitments and action items, and push them into Salesforce automatically. This is early, but it's real, and it's pointing at something significant: the interface layer for enterprise software is about to become entirely conversational.

Change Management Is the Actual Hard Problem

Arvind's most honest admission from the conversation is about Databricks' own internal AI adoption. He wanted to build an agent that would track each employee's weekly priorities, roll that up to give him a real-time view of organizational alignment, and flag when people's work was drifting from what mattered most. Simple in concept. Hasn't happened yet. Not because the AI can't do it, but because building complex enterprise systems is still hard, and AI doesn't magically change that. It's one more powerful tool in the toolkit, not a shortcut around organizational reality.

The engineering failures are equally instructive. Databricks invested in fine-tuning work — building specialized models for specific internal use cases — and most of it didn't pan out. The off-the-shelf foundation models, whether large hosted ones or smaller open-source models, outperformed the bespoke work. The lesson isn't that fine-tuning never works, but that the pace of model improvement is so fast that by the time you've trained a specialized model, the baseline has often leapfrogged what you were trying to build. This dynamic — where a promising internal project becomes obsolete in two weeks because of an external development — is genuinely new in engineering, and it requires a different posture toward R&D investment.

At Glean, Arvind Kumar's own workflow has been reshaped around a "Daily Prep" agent that briefs him every morning — what's on his calendar, what context he needs for each meeting, what he should read. The behavioral shift underneath this is as important as the tool: he's changed the instinct to ask a direct report for information and redirected it toward AI first. As CEO, every casual question he asks triggers a disproportionate organizational response — prep meetings before prep meetings, thirty people spending a week to answer something that might have a simple answer. The discipline of asking AI first, accepting that the answer might be 90% of what you need, and only escalating when genuinely necessary is a leadership behavior change that ripples through the whole organization.

What Glean Is Actually Building

At $200 million in annual recurring revenue and closing $10 million enterprise deals, Glean is past the question of whether enterprise AI search works. The question now is what the product becomes as AI capability compounds. Arvind's vision: a personal companion that every employee in every company in the world carries in their work life. Not a search engine. Not an assistant you query when you remember to. Something that knows your calendar, your goals, your relationships, your career ambitions, what you're good at, and what you're not — and that uses all of that context to surface work to you rather than waiting for you to come find it.

The distinction between "you come to Glean" and "Glean comes to you" is the product roadmap in a single sentence. Today, Glean is a pull product — valuable when used, but dependent on the user's habit of using it. The future is a push product that understands enough about your work to act proactively: preparing you for the meeting you haven't thought about yet, flagging the document that became relevant to your project this morning, surfacing the customer issue that's about to become your problem. The infrastructure for this is mostly built. The gap is getting enough users to the point where the product has the context it needs to be genuinely proactive rather than just reactive.

The variance between Glean's power users and casual users is the company's current product challenge — and Ali's answer to closing that gap is "AI literacy." Not training users to use features, but helping them develop the belief that AI is a genuine collaborator. That's a harder sell than it sounds, because the payoff isn't immediate. For the first few months of deep AI use, you may not save time. You will, eventually, produce better output. But organizations reward speed, not quality, in the short run, and changing that calculus requires leadership commitment more than product changes. The companies succeeding with AI internally are the ones where senior leadership — starting with the CEO — has visibly changed their own workflow and made that change legible to the organization.

What To Do With This

  • Audit your data for genuine differentiation before building any AI product. Ask: what data does your company have that competitors cannot replicate? If the answer is "not much," the AI you build will be commodity AI, and any competitor can match it. Start with the proprietary asset, then design the AI application around it.
  • Set short-term contracts with AI vendors and plan to switch. The field is moving fast enough that locking into multi-year deals with any single vendor — including foundation model providers — is probably a mistake. The best vendors today know this and should make it easy to test and exit.
  • Stop measuring AI project failure rate as a KPI. A 95% failure rate on AI experiments is appropriate for an organization that's actually pushing the boundaries of what's possible. If your failure rate is low, you're only running safe experiments. Track what you're learning from failures, not the failure count.
  • Identify your highest-information-density meetings and instrument them first. Customer calls, deal reviews, executive team standups — these conversations contain decision-relevant information that currently evaporates or gets incompletely captured in CRM. Deploy an AI note-taker that pushes structured data directly into your system of record (Salesforce, HubSpot, Notion, etc.) and treat this as an infrastructure investment, not a convenience feature.
  • As a leader, change your own information-seeking behavior visibly and publicly. Ask AI before asking your team. When AI gives you a useful answer, say so in the meeting. When it misses, say that too. The behavioral modeling from senior leadership is the most effective change management lever available — more effective than mandates or training programs.
  • Build evaluations before you build agents. Agents are not "unleash and it works." They require systematic evaluation frameworks to know when they're performing and when they're not. Before deploying any agent in production, define what correct behavior looks like and build the test suite to measure it continuously.
  • Prototype against existing foundation models before investing in fine-tuning. Databricks learned this the hard way: by the time a fine-tuned specialized model is ready, the baseline has often improved past it. Default to off-the-shelf, measure the gap, and only invest in fine-tuning when you've proven the gap is both real and durable.
  • Pay close attention to the governance and access control layer as agents proliferate. Agents that can access all company data can also accidentally expose sensitive data — compensation, HR records, strategic plans. Before expanding agent permissions, audit what they can see and implement role-based access controls designed for agentic patterns, not just human user patterns.
  • Evaluate AI products on time-to-first-value, not feature breadth. The products most likely to actually get adopted are the ones that prove value in days or weeks, not after months of implementation. When evaluating new AI tools, demand a working pilot in under 30 days. If a vendor can't deliver that, the product probably isn't ready for production use.
  • Watch speech interfaces as the next keyboard replacement. Ali's conviction: keyboards are going to disappear. Voice as an interaction paradigm for enterprise software is underestimated and closer than it looks. If your roadmap doesn't include a voice-native interface, start thinking about what that would unlock for your users — particularly in high-context, hands-busy environments.

Memorable Quotes

"I think the LLM is a commodity. People are not saying that, but it is a commodity. You can get gas from this gas station or you can get gas from that gas station. It doesn't matter, just compare price."

— Ali Ghodsi, CEO of Databricks

"We already have AGI. I came to UC Berkeley in 2009 and was in an AI lab. Back then, the definition of AGI we had, we've already satisfied that. But we've changed the definition and moved the goalpost. We've already hit the original definition — now we're haggling over exactly how smart it is."

— Ali Ghodsi, CEO of Databricks

"You hear these 95% of projects fail, but that's actually what you want. When you are actually experimenting with new technology, if all of your projects are succeeding, that means you're just not trying enough."

— Ali Ghodsi, CEO of Databricks

"It's an engineering art. If you're going to have a company that's going to be really differentiated and you want to beat the competition, you can't just quickly put something together and think that your competition is not going to do the same thing."

— Ali Ghodsi, CEO of Databricks

"It was interesting with engineering today — you build systems and never before have you been in this mode where you start with a great idea and it doesn't seem like a good idea anymore within two weeks, because we see a new development that happened."

— Arvind Kumar, CEO of Glean

"We thought that AI would simply magically do all of this work because it has all the context inside the company to make it happen, but I still don't have it. AI is just one more tool that you have in the toolkit. It does not suddenly make building complex enterprise systems something you can do in one day."

— Arvind Kumar, CEO of Glean

"When you're the CEO, you're the boss and everybody listens to you, and whenever you have a small question, you ask somebody — and they're going to put 30 people on the task. So today my instinct is, whenever I have curiosity, whenever I have questions, I use AI first."

— Arvind Kumar, CEO of Glean

"Today, you have to come to Glean to get most of that work done. In the future, we want Glean to actually come to you and do that work."

— Arvind Kumar, CEO of Glean

item.title: 80,000 Hours Podcast - #145 Classic episode – Christopher Brown on why slavery abolition wasn't inevitable
item.description:
The Abolition That Almost Didn't Happen

Slavery is as old as recorded human civilization. It existed in classical Greece and Rome, across the Sahara, throughout medieval Europe, in the Islamic world, across East Asia and South Asia. Vikings raided for slaves. Empires were built on them. For the entirety of human history up to the 18th century, there is not a single documented case of a slave-trading society or slave-holding community deciding, on its own, that it had had enough. Not one.

That is the foundation from which Christopher Brown, professor of history at Columbia University and the author of Moral Capital: Foundations of British Abolitionism, begins his argument. The abolition of the Atlantic slave trade and, eventually, slavery itself in the British Empire was not the inevitable destination of human moral progress. It was not the logical conclusion of Enlightenment philosophy, industrial capitalism, or increasing wealth. It was a genuinely contingent event — the product of a specific and unlikely confluence of political circumstance, religious community, individual commitment, and historical accident. In nearby possible worlds, it never happens.

This is not a comfortable claim. It violates common sense for most modern readers, who instinctively generate economic and cultural arguments to explain why legal slavery simply couldn't have survived into the contemporary era. But those arguments, Brown argues, don't survive contact with the historical record. And the stakes of getting this wrong extend well beyond the history books. If moral progress doesn't have an automatic engine — if it requires specific, unlikely conditions to get off the ground — then we cannot coast on the assumption that things will keep improving. The world does not trend toward compassion on its own.

The Deepest Filter: Getting Anyone to Care

The most fundamental challenge for any abolition movement was simply the gap between moral awareness and moral action. That gap is not a bug in human psychology; it is a feature. Brown is blunt about this. Walking through Manhattan in winter, he passes homeless people lying on warm grates most mornings. He notices. He feels something. He keeps walking. Most people, most of the time, live with vast distances between their stated values and their actual behavior. This is not hypocrisy so much as the ordinary condition of being human in a world full of urgent competing demands.

The same was true throughout history on slavery. It does not take sophisticated moral philosophy to see that slavery raises serious ethical problems. The defenses of it — Aristotle's claim that some people are "natural slaves," the Roman Stoic argument that the body is enslaved but not the soul, the medieval legal tradition that classified slavery as a creature of conventional law rather than natural law — are themselves evidence that people felt something required defending. The moral insight was not new in the 18th century. What was new was putting it to political use.

As Brown points out, the logical extension of natural rights theory to enslaved Africans was available to European thinkers for centuries before anyone seriously pursued it. It had not been thought applicable to them — not because people lacked the concept, but because extending it would have required overthrowing an economic and social order on which entire worlds were premised. The people best positioned to act were also the people with the most to lose.

This is why Brown draws a sharp distinction between slave societies and societies with slaves. In slave societies — classical Rome, the Caribbean, Brazil, the American South — slavery is load-bearing. The entire political economy, the social hierarchy, the cultural world are organized around it. To challenge slavery there is to challenge everything. In societies with slaves, the institution exists but isn't structural; you can question it without risking total social collapse. That distinction matters enormously for where abolition could possibly begin.

The Quakers: A Necessary but Insufficient Seed

The first sustained community to question the moral foundations of slaveholding was the Society of Friends, the Quakers, in the late 17th century. Understanding why requires understanding what made them unusual — and what made their particular environment propitious.

Quakers had no ordained ministry. Anyone could offer religious witness, which meant internal dissenters couldn't simply be silenced as unorthodox. They believed in near-equality across sexes at a time when almost no Christian denomination did. They were deeply committed to pacifism. And crucially, when they established strong communities in Pennsylvania, New Jersey, and parts of New York and New England, they were in precisely the right position: living in a world where slavery was present and visible, but not foundational to the social order. Slaves never exceeded 10-20% of the population in those regions, and enslaved people were doing work that free white workers also did. Quakers in Barbados — a true slave society, the first major English sugar colony — produced no meaningful abolition movement. The institution was too central, dissent too costly.

Over the course of the 18th century, Quakers went through a period of religious self-examination, worried that they had become comfortable and conventional, falling away from the fervor of their founders. Antislavery testimony became part of a broader internal project of identity formation: to be Quaker meant not owning slaves, not participating in the slave trade. By the 1760s and 1770s, the Philadelphia Yearly Meeting had made it a condition of membership. This was initially inward-looking — about who they were, not about changing British imperial policy. But it created the nucleus that would eventually turn outward.

The figure who made that turn was Anthony Benezet, a Quaker publicist and pamphleteer who Brown describes as essentially inventing the model of the antislavery activist: studying the subject obsessively, producing template publications that were reproduced and adapted across two decades, sending them throughout the North American colonies and to England. Benezet took the Quaker internal witness and argued that the moral case applied to everyone, not just members of the Society of Friends.

The American Revolution: An Accidental Accelerant

What transformed a minor religious community's internal testimony into a mass political movement was a political dispute that had nothing to do with slavery: the conflict between the American colonies and the British Parliament over taxation and representation.

When American colonists began challenging parliamentary authority, they found that invoking "the rights of Englishmen" didn't help them — Parliament was sovereign over Englishmen, which was precisely the problem. So they pivoted to natural rights: rights that preceded parliamentary rule, that were inalienable, that government could not legitimately override. This was politically useful rhetoric in their fight against British authority.

Quaker polemicists — Benezet foremost among them — saw their opening. If natural rights were the currency of political argument, they would ask: what about the natural rights of enslaved Africans? They began pressing leading colonial political figures to reckon with the incoherence between their liberty rhetoric and their slaveholding practice. At the same time, British polemicists on the other side of the Atlantic were making the same point, but as a cudgel: you don't actually believe in natural rights, they told American colonists. You're just trying to avoid paying taxes. You're hypocrites.

Neither side was making an antislavery argument out of genuine concern for enslaved people. They were throwing mud. But in doing so, they were inadvertently establishing something new in political discourse: the idea that commitment to slavery was a character flaw, a mark of depravity, something your enemies could use against you. And its logical inverse: that opposition to slavery was a sign of virtue. Once that framing took hold, once being antislavery said something good about you — and, critically, said something good about you at very low personal cost since the institution's impact was entirely overseas — it became available as a component of individual and national identity in a way it had never been before.

Thomas Clarkson, the person Brown identifies as most decisive in the founding of the actual movement, was the first person to envision a national public campaign against the British slave trade and to dedicate his life entirely to achieving it. It took until 1807. Without the political controversy of the American Revolution, without Anthony Benezet's decades of groundwork, without Clarkson's particular vision and obsession, the movement may never have cohered at all.

Why the Economic Arguments Fail

The most persistent alternative account of abolition — associated with Eric Williams' 1944 book Capitalism and Slavery — holds that slavery was abolished for economic reasons: it had become unprofitable, or the industrial capitalists who superseded the plantation economy found it in their interest to destroy it. Brown finds this unconvincing, and the economic historians who have "run the numbers" largely agree.

Slavery in the British West Indies at the time of emancipation in 1833 was not dying. It was profitable. Economic historians have argued that had the slave trade not been abolished in 1807, British slavery would likely have thrived through the 19th century. There is not a single documented case anywhere in the Americas of slaveholders deciding to liquidate slavery because it was no longer economically useful. The people actually invested in the institution were quite sure it was profitable; it was people elsewhere who theorized its demise.

More fundamentally, the "economic inefficiency" argument misunderstands what slavery is. It is not only an economic institution. It is a strategy of domination, a way of acquiring loyal servants and soldiers, a form of luxury consumption, a system for sexual exploitation. The predominant slave in human history has not been the field hand but the young girl or the child. Slavery is extraordinarily malleable, and even in a knowledge economy, uses for coerced labor — domestic service, bureaucratic functions, military capacity — would not have disappeared. The Roman Empire staffed much of its government with slaves. Imagining that economic modernization would have naturally displaced it requires wishful thinking.

The Long Slog: Persistence Against Overwhelming Resistance

Even after a movement existed — after slavery had become politically controversial, after antislavery identity was a feature of British public life — the distance from there to actual legislative change was enormous and deeply uncertain. The 50 years between the founding of the abolitionist movement in the 1780s and emancipation in 1833 were not a steady march forward. The pro-slavery interest was powerful, skillful, and well-connected. They drafted the regulations meant to govern their own industry. They cultivated the right friends in the Tory governments that dominated the 1820s.

What made success possible, both for slave trade abolition in 1807 and for emancipation in 1833, was political change that had nothing directly to do with slavery. The Grenville-Fox ministry of 1806 came to power for reasons unrelated to abolition, but contained the politicians who finally pushed the slave trade bill through. The Reform Act of 1832, designed to widen the franchise and reduce parliamentary corruption, brought in a Parliament far more open to abolitionist lobbying than any that had preceded it. The movement succeeded not because it finally won the argument — it had been winning the argument for decades — but because external political shifts happened to align it with parliamentary power.

When emancipation finally came, it came with massive financial compensation to slaveholders, both those resident in the West Indies and those in Britain, because the sanctity of private property ran straight through elite political culture. Emancipation to slaveholders was confiscation. The state recognized the capital sunk in enslaved human beings and paid it out. The principle of property was preserved even as the property itself was withdrawn.

The Dangerous Comfort of Inevitable Progress

The most important implication of Brown's historical argument is about the present. The widespread belief that moral progress is automatic — that economic development, education, or increasing wealth naturally generates more compassionate values — is not only historically unsupported, it is actively dangerous as a guide to action.

Brown dismisses the World Values Survey-style argument that richer, more educated societies reliably shift toward harm-aversion and equality as universal moral values. Wealthy societies have produced stunning artistic achievement alongside appalling exploitation throughout history. The wealthiest nations of 18th century Europe were deeply committed to slavery. The high-imperialism of Belle Epoque France and Edwardian England was underwritten by the colonization of sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia. Nazi Germany was one of the most technologically and culturally advanced societies in the world. And perhaps most pointedly: the abolition of legal slavery in the 19th century became the ideological cover for colonialism in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. "We're here to stamp out slavery in Africa" was the alibi for economic extraction and political domination. Moral progress in one dimension licensed new forms of harm in others.

The gap between moral insight and moral action is not a temporary condition that education and wealth gradually close. It is structural. We know, right now, that factory farming involves suffering on a staggering scale. We know it while we eat meat. Future generations may look back on us as we look back on slaveholders — not as people who lacked moral awareness, but as people who had it and acted otherwise anyway. That is how humans are, Brown says, now and in the past.

What follows from this, though, is not despair. It is something more demanding. If moral progress isn't automatic, it requires human agency. Movements begin somewhere. Specific people making specific choices in specific historical moments are what generated the anti-slavery campaign, and without those choices, it may never have started. The world gets better or worse on the choices we make individually and collectively, not because things trend in the right direction. That sober assessment is, Brown argues, a call to action rather than a counsel of resignation. We got here because people chose to fight. We stay here — and go further — only if people keep choosing to.

What To Do With This

  • Stop waiting for moral progress to happen on its own. The history of abolition is a case study in the gap between moral insight and moral action — and the insight existed for centuries before the action. If you see something that matters, the structure of history suggests it won't fix itself. Someone has to start.
  • Find the equivalent of "societies with slaves" — contexts where challenging a harmful norm is possible without overthrowing an entire social order. The Quakers in Pennsylvania succeeded where Quakers in Barbados failed because the stakes of questioning the institution were lower. When choosing where to push for change, look for environments where the opposition isn't existential for those in power.
  • Pay close attention to which groups are in the structural position to make advocacy "costless." British antislavery achieved mass petition support partly because the institution's costs fell entirely on people overseas. Movements gain easier traction when supporting them requires no real sacrifice from supporters. Design campaigns that put that dynamic to work rather than fighting it.
  • Take the "gap between attitudes and actions" seriously when designing advocacy. Brown's observation — that awareness almost never naturally translates into movement — should be treated as a baseline assumption, not an exception. Effective advocacy requires more than generating moral awareness; it requires creating conditions where action follows. Study what made the rare successful movements close that gap.
  • Scrutinize economic arguments for why bad things will "naturally" end. The argument that slavery would have ended on its own because it was unprofitable was wrong — and slaveholders knew it was wrong at the time. When someone argues that a harmful practice is economically unsustainable and will therefore fix itself, demand the evidence. The people invested in harmful practices are often right that they are economically viable.
  • Watch for how moral progress in one dimension can license harm in another. The identity that Britain acquired as "the nation that abolished slavery" became the ideological cover for colonialism. When a movement succeeds, examine what new rationalizations its success enables. Victories can create moral blind spots.
  • Identify the Thomas Clarksons in important causes — the people who have the vision of a national movement and the obsessive commitment to see it through. Brown concludes that founding movements require specific individuals with unusual combinations of vision, persistence, and willingness to make a cause the sole work of their lives. Treat finding and supporting those people as a strategic priority, not an afterthought.
  • Take seriously the role of political contingency in reform, and work to build durable coalitions rather than waiting for the ideal political moment. Both slave trade abolition and emancipation depended on political shifts — changes in ministry, electoral reform — that had nothing to do with the abolition cause. A movement that persists for decades is one that is positioned to capitalize on political openings when they arrive. Build the infrastructure now; the openings come unpredictably.
  • Challenge the assumption that richer, more educated societies are morally safer societies. The historical record does not support that claim. Evaluate the specific institutional, cultural, and political factors that protect against moral catastrophe — don't assume that economic development handles it automatically.

Memorable Quotes

"There is no record, at least that I'm aware of, of slave traders or slave holding societies saying that they had had enough and they weren't going to do this anymore. Slaving is as old as human history. And I think we tend to forget it was a norm rather than an exception."

— Christopher Brown

"It's not difficult at all to see the moral and ethical problems with eating meat. Someone like me, who eats a lot of meat, I am wholly aware of all of the ethical, really indefensible grounds for consuming meat as much as I do. And yet I do it anyways... It's not hard to imagine 20, 50, 200 years from now, when people will look back on our time and say, what was wrong with these people? No, we know exactly what we're doing. And that's how humans are, to some extent, now and in the past."

— Christopher Brown

"When the other thing happens, when there is a movement of some kind, when there's a commitment, when there's a collective effort — that's the thing that we should regard as strange and try to make sense of, rather than the routine forms of man's inhumanity to man, which unfortunately is all too typical."

— Christopher Brown

"The identity that Western Europe acquires as the part of the world that abolished slavery then becomes the alibi — maybe even the apology — for colonizing the less developed world. We're here to stamp out slavery in Africa. We're here to bring economic development. Oh, it just so happens to serve our economic interests as well."

— Christopher Brown

"There is no known instance, at least one that I can think of, in the Americas, where slaveholders decided that they were going to essentially liquidate slavery, abandon it, because it was no longer economically profitable. That just doesn't happen. This never happens."

— Christopher Brown

"If you take away the notion of inevitable cultural progress, then what you put in is the necessity of human action, individual and collectively. The world gets — to put it crudely — better or worse on the choices we make individually and collectively, not because things are just sort of trending in the right direction."

— Christopher Brown

"As of 1750, this was not the direction that things were likely to go by 1850. The grounds that folks have offered for thinking that slavery in one way or another was going to end in the 19th century because of 18th century trends are just not convincing, if you know the 18th century and you know the 19th century."

— Christopher Brown

"Our wrestling with the legacies of slavery in the modern day don't become easier by refusing to look at the history, by avoiding it or having complacent notions about how the world changed. I think we need to watch ourselves individually and watch the worlds that we live in and think about what harms we do or what harms we authorize or permit because they just seem basic to the world in which we live."

— Christopher Brown

item.title: 80,000 Hours Podcast - #142 Classic episode – John McWhorter on why the optimal number of languages might be one, and other provocative claims about language
item.description:
The World Looks the Same in Any Language — And That's Not a Tragedy

There is an idea that has spread through educated circles, through anthropology classrooms and Sunday supplements and TED talks, that becomes more seductive the more you know about language: that the tongue you were born into shapes the lens through which you see the world. That to speak Mandarin is to think differently than to speak English. That to speak Kuuk Thaayorre, the Australian language where speakers use cardinal directions instead of left and right, is to inhabit a fundamentally different perceptual reality. That the six thousand languages we are watching die are six thousand irreplaceable ways of being human, vanishing forever.

John McWhorter — Columbia professor, host of the Lexicon Valley podcast, and one of the most entertaining linguists alive — thinks most of this is wrong. Not all of it. Not in the crude way of dismissing linguistic diversity as uninteresting. But the core Sapir-Whorf hypothesis, the idea that grammar sculpts thought, that language is the lens through which a people encounters reality: that, he argues, fails on the evidence, misleads on the ethics, and flatters the speaker more than it illuminates the world.

This is the animating argument of his book The Language Hoax, and it threads through a wide-ranging conversation on the 80,000 Hours Podcast with host Rob Wiblin. Along the way, the conversation covers language death, creole languages, Shakespeare, bilingualism, and why Creoles might actually be the best languages ever made.

The Sapir-Whorf Problem

Benjamin Lee Whorf, writing in the 1930s, proposed that the grammar of one's native language is not merely a tool for expressing thought but a constraint on it — that we "cannot talk at all except by subscribing" to the conceptual organization embedded in our language. The idea is, as McWhorter freely admits, deeply seductive. It makes the world's linguistic diversity feel like a treasure chest of distinct forms of consciousness.

The psychological experiments are real. English and Indonesian speakers, whose languages speak of "a long time," are measurably better at predicting when a line on a screen will reach its endpoint. Spanish and Greek speakers, whose languages speak of "a lot of time," are better with a filling container. Japanese speakers, who must use different counting words depending on what material an object is made of, group objects differently than American children do. McWhorter has catalogued around twenty-five such findings. They are genuine. They are also, he insists, not worldviews.

The problem becomes clear when you zoom out. Evidential markers — grammatical features that require speakers to specify whether they know something by sight, hearsay, or inference — are the Wharfian poster child. Linguists have argued that Amazonian peoples have these markers because they are unusually attuned to their environment, unusually sensitive to sources of knowledge. But evidential markers are vanishingly rare in Africa and Polynesia. They are most common on the West Coast of North America and almost absent from the East Coast. Are sunny Californian tribes really more epistemically careful than the people surviving New England winters? And in Europe, the only languages with evidential markers are Bulgarian and Macedonian. Are Bulgarians the most skeptical, environmentally aware people on the continent?

The distribution is essentially random. Languages develop features the way bubbles emerge in a pot of soup — unpredictably, not because of any cultural imperative, but because of the chaotic cascade of historical accidents that shape any living system.
