Some languages distinguish he from she. Equally many do not. There is no more cultural meaning to that distinction than to which dust particle happens to go up your nose versus down your shirt.

The ethical problem is sharper still. If you believe that grammatical features encode worldviews, you have to apply that logic consistently. Mandarin Chinese allows a single sentence to express what English would require multiple tenses to convey. In the 1980s, a Wharfian psychologist concluded from this that Mandarin speakers are slightly less sensitive to counterfactuality — to hypothetical reasoning — than English speakers. The conclusion is logically coherent given the premises. It is also, essentially, an argument that Chinese people are a little dim. African American Vernacular English allows the copula to be dropped: "she my sister." By Wharfian logic, that would mean speakers are less proficient at reasoning about equations and equivalence. This is precisely where the framework led in the late 1960s. McWhorter's point is that you cannot keep the flattering implications of Warfianism — Amazonian people are wonderfully attuned to their environment — and discard the unflattering ones — East Asian speakers are slightly less hypothetical in their thinking — just because one tastes better. The framework either works or it does not.

Language, he concludes, demonstrates human similarity. Culture demonstrates human diversity. Both are worth celebrating. But confusing one for the other does real harm.

The Languages We Are Losing — And Whether We Should Grieve

Of the roughly seven thousand languages spoken today, somewhere between five hundred and six hundred will likely remain in a century. This is not primarily a story of government suppression — that era, when Native American and Australian Aboriginal children were punished for speaking their native languages, is largely past. It is a story of rational individual choices, compounding across generations.

The mechanism is simple: a man from one village marries a woman from another. They move to a city. They share no common language except the regional lingua franca — Swahili, perhaps, or Mandarin, or Spanish. That is what they speak to each other and to their children. The children grow up in that language. They marry others who grew up in that language. The village languages, spoken by a dwindling population of elders, fade. Nobody forced this. Everybody chose it.

There is an uncomfortable reversal hidden in the usual framing of language death as tragedy and injustice. When Western academics mourn the loss of small languages, they are often asking very poor, very busy people to spend enormous amounts of time learning extraordinarily complex linguistic systems — many small languages are among the most grammatically elaborate in the world, precisely because they have never served as contact languages and have therefore accumulated complexity without pressure to simplify — so that educated outsiders can feel good knowing those languages still exist. The Dahalo-speaking tribesman that linguist Peter Ladefoged interviewed in Tanzania was happy his son was learning Swahili and English. He wanted his son to make money. Who was Ladefoged to tell him he was wrong?

McWhorter does not conclude from this that nothing is lost. Something is lost. The small languages that are vanishing tend to be the most grammatically complex and ornate in the world, the ones with tone systems and evidential markers and noun incorporation. They are extraordinary intellectual objects. Documenting them before they disappear is urgent and worthwhile. Revival movements deserve support. But intellectual honesty requires acknowledging that after the age of thirteen or fourteen, acquiring a language like Saramaccan or Navajo to any real depth is close to impossible for most people — not because they are lazy, but because the human brain's capacity for language acquisition calcifies. And revival movements have rarely, in practice, produced the one metric that actually matters: toddlers growing up speaking the language and on course to pass it to their own toddlers.

A more practical path, he suggests, is cultural preservation through linguistic absorption. Guaraní and Spanish merged into the unique creole spoken by most Paraguayans. Jewish Americans who speak no Hebrew or Yiddish are nonetheless quite Jewish. Culture can survive the loss of its dedicated linguistic code, imperfectly but genuinely. That might be the realistic path forward in many cases — not a solution anyone would have chosen from a blank slate, but a livable one.

And from a blank slate? Asked what the optimal number of languages would be if we were designing humanity from scratch, McWhorter is blunt: one. Not because linguistic diversity is unbeautiful, but because nobody, if they had to choose, would actually choose a world where most people cannot understand each other. The seven thousand languages we have are the product of geographical accident and the chaotic drift of isolated groups. They were not chosen. We can appreciate them without pretending we would have designed them.

Creoles: The Best Languages in the World

If most languages are gunked up with millennia of accumulated grammatical complexity — tones, irregular verbs, meaningless gender, elaborate case systems that once served a purpose and now just make learning miserable — creole languages are what happens when you strip all of that away and start over.

The conditions for a creole are specific and historically grim: a group of adults forced to live together who share no common language, speaking different pidgins of some dominant tongue, needing to actually communicate. Under slavery. In colonial labor camps. In military conscription. The pidgin — a minimal shared vocabulary with almost no grammar — gets expanded first by the adults trying to live in it, then crystallized into a full language by the first generation of children born into it. Sometimes within a single generation. Cape Verdean Portuguese is a creole. Haitian Creole is a creole. The Surinamese language Saramaccan — spoken by the descendants of escaped slaves who fled into the rainforest — is a creole. Dozens of sign languages are creoles.

What gets stripped away in creolization is illuminating. Grammatical gender disappears almost immediately — adults trying to communicate have no patience for arbitrary noun categories that carry no information. Irregular verb forms go. Tone systems go (you cannot build a language from scratch using tones when half the adults present don't speak a tonal language). Opaque idiomatic compounds — "understand," "turn out" — have to start over, because words need to be transparent when your vocabulary is small and your audience is broad.

What stays is everything that actually matters for communication: tense distinctions (expressed with a single invariable word rather than a battery of inflected endings), a full vocabulary that grows rapidly, and all the constructions — the idioms, the pragmatic expressions, the "what are you doing here?" that means "why are you here?" — that make a language feel alive. Haitian Creole has tens of thousands of words and genuine grammatical rules. It is simply French with everything that makes French hard to learn stripped away, because the people who made it frankly had better things to do.

The result: creoles are easier to learn than almost any established language, communicate with equal expressiveness and nuance, and demonstrate that most of what makes European languages difficult — the ablemos and ablais, the gendered articles, the irregular strong verbs — is pure overhead. It's like the cans dragging behind a wedding car. Festive, historically meaningful, entirely unnecessary.

If a universal language were to be designed, a creole would be the obvious model. This is also why sign languages, which tend to form as creoles in deaf communities, are in a similar sense "better" — young, ungunked, efficient. The languages that are best for communication may be the ones born from the most desperate circumstances.

Language, Thought, and What We Actually Know

The deeper question running under all of this is what thought actually is and how much language shapes it. McWhorter's answer is conservative and probably correct: thought precedes language, and language is a tool for expressing and sharing it, not a cage that determines its shape.

The evidence is everywhere. Babies reason before they can speak. Bonobos have thoughts, even if they cannot articulate them. When we reach for a word and cannot find it, we know the concept we want — we have not confused it with the word. Homophones are funny precisely because we are not actually confused by them; we know which meaning we intend. Language does, once it exists, enable new kinds of thought — concepts like "respect" (as opposed to mere fear-deference) require language to instill and transmit. Culture and complex social knowledge can only be preserved and passed down through linguistic encoding. But the idea that grammar determines thought at the level of worldview has not survived empirical scrutiny.

On Shakespeare: the same basic principle applies. The difficulty of Shakespeare is not cultural snobbery failing to appreciate its depth. It is that English has changed enough in four centuries that genuine comprehension requires prior study. "Generous" meant "noble" in Shakespeare's time, not "magnanimous." These silent semantic shifts stack up until you're watching the play in what is functionally a foreign language — except you don't know it, because the words are familiar. McWhorter has argued for adjustments (not translations, adjustments) to Shakespeare's text to make it comprehensible. The objection that this violates Shakespeare's intent is answered simply: Shakespeare would want people to actually understand and enjoy his plays. He was a working dramatist, not a monument.

What To Do With This

  • Treat the Sapir-Whorf hypothesis with heavy skepticism when you encounter it in popular writing. The finding that language affects thought at the margins is real; the claim that grammar encodes fundamentally different worldviews is not supported by global evidence. Ask whether the language feature being praised in culture A is absent in culture B — and if so, what that implies about culture B's speakers.
  • If you want to preserve an endangered culture, focus on cultural transmission rather than linguistic revival alone. Language and culture are intertwined but separable. Encoding vocabulary, stories, and conceptual frameworks into the dominant language may be more durable than attempting full language revival, which rarely produces the toddler-to-toddler transmission that actually sustains a language.
  • If you want a second language and care about practical return on investment, study a creole or a younger, less inflected language. Haitian Creole, Tok Pisin, or Papiamento will get you functional communication with far less overhead than languages with complex case systems, tonal distinctions, or irregular morphology. The "difficulty" of prestige languages is largely historical accident, not communicative necessity.
  • Start language learning before age 13 if you want real acquisition. The window for native-like language learning closes around early adolescence. Programs that wait until high school are largely wasting their students' time and producing the 1-in-40 success rate the data show. Push for early immersion or accept that school language programs are mostly symbolic.
  • When editing speech or writing, distinguish genuine hesitation markers from pragmatic particles. "Um" and "ah" are usually safe to cut. "Like," "sort of," and "you know" sometimes carry real communicative load — signaling approximation, managing the listener's expectations, or conveying social warmth. Cut the pure hesitations; preserve the ones doing work.
  • When reading about linguistic features as windows into culture, demand global comparison. If a grammatical feature is presented as reflecting a cultural value, check whether cultures without that feature lack that value. Evidential markers are presented as markers of environmental awareness — but they're absent from most of Africa and Polynesia. That pattern should make you suspicious of the causal story.
  • Read or revisit Shakespeare with a prepared text first, not as a live performance cold. McWhorter's point is not that Shakespeare is overrated but that you cannot meaningfully appreciate a play you cannot follow in real time. Pre-reading is not cheating — it's the minimum viable preparation for a text four hundred years old.
  • When thinking about language extinction, audit whose interests are actually at stake. The argument for preservation often places significant burdens on marginalized, time-poor communities to satisfy the cultural preferences of educated outsiders. Be honest about that trade-off before making claims about what "should" be preserved.

Memorable Quotes

"They're cheaper and just as good."

— John McWhorter, on creole languages as candidates for universal languages

"Languages are like soup. They're all much more complex and specific than they need to be. It's not about culture. All languages overdo it in some ways, in ways that are no more predictable or connected to anything psychologically specific or cultural than where in a pot a soup bubble emerges at any given time."

— John McWhorter

"If you like Warfianism, then you really have to believe that almost everybody speaking most languages of East and Southeast Asia is a bit of a dumb-dumb. Because those languages are much more telegraphic than European languages... you can't keep the good worldviews and throw out the bad ones just because they don't taste good."

— John McWhorter

"Dahalo is something we speak here, but we're poor. I want my son to make money, and so I want him to speak Swahili and English."

— Dahalo-speaking tribesman quoted by linguist Peter Ladefoged

"Language demonstrates not our differences but our similarities. Both of those things are worth celebration."

— John McWhorter

"It's not that it's difficult, it's impossible. And I think that's just not fair."

— John McWhorter, on watching Shakespeare cold without prior preparation

"When language breaks, we fix it. You couldn't make language degrade if you wanted to."

— Rob Wiblin and John McWhorter, in agreement

"To be bilingual apparently holds off dementia by a few years, which somehow isn't as handy a nugget as the idea that it makes you smarter."

— John McWhorter

item.title: 80,000 Hours Podcast - #144 Classic episode – Athena Aktipis on why cancer is a fundamental universal phenomena
item.description:
You Are 30 Trillion Acts of Cooperation. Cancer Is What Happens When They Stop.

Start with the thing that almost nobody says out loud: cancer isn't primarily bad luck. It isn't primarily a failure of medicine. It is a fundamental consequence of being alive — specifically, of being multicellular. Anytime you build a body out of cooperating cells, you create the precise conditions for some of those cells to stop cooperating. Cancer isn't an aberration of life. It is life's oldest, most persistent adversary, woven into the physics of what it means to have a body at all.

Athena Aktipis has spent her career sitting at the intersection of evolutionary biology, cooperation theory, and cancer research — a vantage point almost no one else occupies. She directs the Cooperation and Conflict Lab at Arizona State University, co-hosts the Zombified Podcast, and wrote The Cheating Cell, a book that reframes cancer from a disease of genetic misfortune into a disease of cellular defection. The reframe isn't semantic. It changes what you'd want to do about it, and how you'd expect it to behave. Her core claim is this: the opposite of cancer is not health. The opposite of cancer is multicellular cooperation. The opposite of cancer is us.

The Five Things Every Cell Agreed To

Multicellularity required a deal. When cells stopped living as independent agents and joined collectives, they gave up evolutionary autonomy in exchange for the benefits of collective life — protection, specialization, reliable resources. That deal rests on five foundations, and cancer breaks every one of them.

The first is inhibition of proliferation. Cells in a healthy body divide when needed and stop when told. Cancer cells divide when they shouldn't, ignoring the molecular stop signals that coordinate tissue growth. The second is control of cell death. Cells are supposed to die on cue — apoptosis is not a failure but a feature, the cellular equivalent of planned obsolescence. Cancer cells evade this, persisting past their expiration date. The third is resource sharing. As bodies grew large, diffusion became insufficient to deliver nutrients, so organisms evolved circulatory systems and coordinated resource delivery. Cancer cells monopolize resources — they are the rubbish housemate who eats everything in the fridge and contributes nothing to rent. The fourth is division of labor. Different cells differentiate into different types — neurons, hepatocytes, immune cells — each performing specialized functions. Cancer cells lose this specialization, reverting toward a generalist, self-serving phenotype. The fifth is maintenance of the shared environment. Cells contribute to the extracellular matrix — the protein scaffolding and chemical environment that makes tissue function possible. Cancer cells trash this environment, producing acids and degrading the shared infrastructure, like tenants who rip out the plumbing to sell the copper.

These five breakdowns map cleanly onto what oncologists call the Hallmarks of Cancer, the canonical framework in cancer biology. But differentiation — the fourth foundation — was originally missing from the Hallmarks. Aktipis's theoretical work helped push it in: "A breakdown in differentiation should be considered a hallmark of cancer. And now things are kind of coming around to that." The cooperation framework, it turns out, is a better map of cancer than the purely molecular one.

Evolution Runs Faster Than You Think — Inside Your Body

Here is the part that should recalibrate your intuitions about cancer risk and treatment. The cells inside a human body are subject to natural selection — real Darwinian selection — right now. Their generation times can be under twenty-four hours. Their populations number in the trillions. When a mutator phenotype emerges — a cell that has lost the machinery for accurate DNA replication — the rate of variation explodes.

What this means is staggering: "The opportunity for evolution by natural selection to operate over the course of cancer progression is much vaster than all of the evolutionary time we have had as Homo sapiens." Your body is not just fighting a bad cell. It is fighting an evolutionary arms race conducted at speeds biological evolution has never run before. The cancer cells that survive your immune system, your chemotherapy, your targeted therapy — they survive because they were selected to. They are, in the most literal possible sense, the fittest.

This is why high-dose chemotherapy so often fails in the long run. Blast a tumor with maximum chemotherapy and you eliminate all the sensitive cells. What's left is a population that, by definition, is resistant. You have run an accelerated selection experiment with a predetermined result. The treatment creates the monster it was trying to kill.

The Three Layers of Surveillance Your Body Runs Constantly

The body isn't passive about this. It runs three nested levels of anti-cancer monitoring simultaneously, and understanding them changes how you think about both cancer and its treatment.

The first layer is cell-intrinsic. Genes like TP53 — often called the "guardian of the genome" — monitor the cell's own internal state: Is there DNA damage? Are expression levels normal? Is the cell behaving appropriately given its context? TP53 can halt the cell cycle, trigger repair, or initiate apoptosis. It is, as Aktipis puts it, "kind of like the cellular conscience." When TP53 is mutated — as it is in over half of all cancers — the conscience goes silent.

The second layer is neighborhood-level signaling. Cells in healthy tissue constantly exchange signals: survival signals, anti-apoptosis signals, growth signals. A cell that stops receiving "you're doing fine" messages from its neighbors faces pressure to die. This creates a social monitoring system at the tissue level — cells that act out of character face immediate social consequences. Tumors disrupt this signaling, creating false environments where cancer cells receive the survival signals they need without earning them.

The third layer is systemic immune surveillance: the broader immune system scanning for abnormalities across the entire organism. This is the layer most people know about, but it's the last line of defense, not the first. Cancer's success often begins not by evading the immune system but by defeating the two inner layers first.

TP53's architecture reveals something deeper about biological information processing. Rob Wiblin raised the puzzle during their conversation: why put so much critical function in a single gene? Why not distribute the decision across multiple independent systems?

The answer is integration. A cell needs to make a coherent decision about whether cellular proliferation is appropriate given the full context: Is this wound healing? Is this normal growth? Is something wrong? You can only answer that by integrating signals from many domains simultaneously. Distribute the decision across independent evaluators with partial information and you degrade the quality of the output — the same reason you wouldn't run a company with two CEOs each looking at half the data. The single-point-of-failure architecture isn't a design flaw. It is the cost of integrated decision-making.

Cancer Doesn't Just Defect. It Cooperates.

Here is where the standard mental model of cancer breaks down most completely. Cancer cells are cheaters relative to the organism — but they can be cooperators relative to each other. And this distinction turns out to matter enormously for why metastatic cancer is so hard to treat.

Wherever the conditions for cooperation exist, cooperation evolves. Cancer cells share growth factors among clones — a kin selection dynamic. They develop division of labor: some cells specialize in producing growth factors, others in evading the immune system, others in reproduction (these are sometimes called cancer stem cells). "There's a sort of proto-multicellularity almost going on," Aktipis says. Tumors don't just grow. They organize.

And they can get cancer themselves. When cooperating cancer cells produce public goods — like growth factors — a mutant that stops contributing gains an advantage. These "hyper-tumors" can destabilize a tumor's cooperative structure from within. Whether this happens depends on population dynamics, but in principle, the logic of cheating that gave rise to the cancer applies recursively within the tumor. This is not merely theoretical. Tumors that grow rapidly often exhaust their own blood supply through overexploitation — the same tragedy-of-the-commons dynamic that collapses irrigation systems when every farmer opens their tap simultaneously.

Metastasis is where this cooperative capacity becomes deadly. By the time a metastasis is clinically visible, it represents the endpoint of many generations of selection on cancer cell collectives — groups selected for their ability to cooperate in extracting resources, surviving hostile environments, and moving effectively to new territory. "It's quite likely that by the time you have a metastasis that is visible in any sort of screening, that very often that might have really come from a long lineage of clusters of cancer cells that were selected on the basis of being already able to cooperate effectively." Metastatic cancer is not just more cancer. It is more evolved cancer.

The Evolutionary Trap — and the Escape

The most important practical consequence of the evolutionary framing is adaptive therapy. Developed by Bob Gatenby at Moffitt Cancer Center, adaptive therapy inverts the treatment logic that has dominated oncology for decades.

Standard care: administer maximum tolerated doses to kill as many cancer cells as possible. The evolutionary consequence: you select intensely for drug resistance. Whatever cells survive are precisely those with the highest intrinsic resistance. Once sensitive competitors are eliminated, resistant cells expand unopposed.

Adaptive therapy: administer a dose sufficient to shrink the tumor, then stop until growth resumes. In the absence of drug pressure, drug-resistant cells — which carry metabolic costs to maintain their resistance — lose their competitive advantage. Sensitive cells, which reproduce more efficiently, expand. When the tumor starts growing again, treat again. "You can kind of manage the tumor by treating it when it's growing too much and then you back off." Clinical trials at Moffitt have shown patients living substantially longer than expected — not because the cancer was eliminated, but because it was managed as an ecological system rather than attacked as an enemy to be destroyed.

There's a secondary mechanism at work too. Treating tumors when they are actively growing may disproportionately kill the most rapidly dividing cells, which are metabolically most vulnerable in those moments. Less drug, better timing, less toxicity to the patient — and arguably better outcomes. The commercial obstacle to this approach is not scientific but economic: you cannot patent the idea of giving someone less medicine.

Other evolutionary approaches complement adaptive therapy. Providing a tumor with a constant, stable food supply reduces selection pressure for dispersal — if resources are reliable and local, staying put is adaptive, and motile phenotypes that lead to metastasis don't gain a foothold. Disrupting the cooperative signaling within tumors — interfering with the division of labor cancer cells develop — can destabilize their collective behavior. And NSAIDs, including aspirin, appear to reduce somatic mutation rates by reducing inflammation, potentially slowing the evolutionary engine that generates new cancer variants in the first place.

Life History, Elephants, and the Trade-offs You Can't Design Away

Peto's Paradox: elephants have roughly a hundred times more cells than humans and live much longer, yet they get deadly cancer less often. By naive probability, they should get cancer constantly. They don't. Why?

Life history theory. Organisms face a fundamental trade-off between investing resources in reproduction versus somatic maintenance — keeping the body running well. Mice are fast-life-history strategists: they reproduce quickly, invest minimally in long-term body maintenance, and would likely be predated before cancer matters anyway. Elephants are slow-life-history strategists: large, long-lived, heavily invested in somatic maintenance. They couldn't survive to reproductive age without robust cancer suppression — so they evolved it.

This trade-off has a corollary that cuts against a naive view of what "better biology" would look like. Organisms that heal wounds quickly need cells primed for rapid proliferation. That same priming makes them cancer-prone. "The physiology inside the tumor kind of looks like a wound that doesn't heal." Fast wound healing and cancer resistance are in fundamental tension. You cannot have both. Evolution has never found a way around this trade-off because there isn't one — it reflects a deep logical constraint in how cell behavior is organized.

Conflict Goes All the Way Down — and All the Way Up

The cooperation-defection framework doesn't stop at the cell. It reaches all the way down to genes within cells — and all the way up to civilizations.

Transposable elements, sometimes called jumping genes, are sequences that replicate themselves within genomes, acting as gene-level cheaters within the very DNA that encodes all other functions. Our genomes contain massive suppression machinery evolved specifically to contain them. "The very design of how our DNA works has all of these cheater suppression mechanisms already built in. That's the only reason that it works." What we used to call junk DNA is, in part, the residue of ancient gene-level conflicts — sequences that won the self-replication race but lost the war for genomic integration.

The conflict extends to parent-offspring dynamics in ways that are viscerally concrete. In sexually reproducing species, paternal genes in a fetus have different evolutionary interests than maternal genes. The mother benefits from distributing resources across present and future offspring; maternal genes favor moderate resource transfer during any single pregnancy. Paternal genes in the fetus have no stake in the mother's future reproductive success with other mates, so they favor extracting as much as possible now. This conflict is genomically instantiated through epigenetic imprinting — genes tagged with paternal marks tend to upregulate resource extraction from the mother; maternal marks counteract this. When this arms race runs out of control, the result is preeclampsia — a condition where fetal genes drive maternal blood pressure to dangerous levels while maternal systems shut down blood flow in response. "Everybody can end up dead, which fucking sucks." Not a metaphor. A literal consequence of a genetic tug-of-war.

Scale up. Human societies face the same structural dynamic. Division of labor creates surplus. Surplus creates the opportunity for defection — individuals or subgroups that take from the cooperative system without contributing. Every institution humans have ever built is partly an apparatus for solving this problem: property rights, legal systems, social norms, taxation, trade agreements. Cancer is not an analogy for social defection. It is the same underlying logic operating at a different scale.

Rob Wiblin summarized this during the conversation in a line Aktipis immediately endorsed: "Cooperation between different elements to achieve common goals creates a surplus. That surplus can then be grabbed by sub-components that decide to deviate from the rules that created it. This is recapitulated at different scales." Genes, cells, organisms, institutions, civilizations — the same game, different players.

The Spacefaring Civilization Problem

Follow this logic to its extreme. If humanity becomes spacefaring — spreading to other planets, extracting their resources, establishing colonies — this looks structurally like a transmissible cancer. Cells (organisms) escape their original body (planet), colonize new environments, extract resources from those environments, and potentially undermine them. "Maybe there's a certain way in which spacefaring intelligent life is more like a transmissible cancer."

The Great Filter problem — the question of why we see no evidence of other civilizations despite the vast number of candidate planets — may be fundamentally about this dynamic. The level of cooperation required to not destroy your home world, and then to cooperate effectively enough to become spacefaring without destroying everything else, is the filter. Technology is not the bottleneck. Cooperation is.

This reframes AI risk in a particular way. The primary concern isn't autonomous AI pursuing misaligned goals — it's the human-AI hybrid: humans with destructive intentions using AI's computational power to undermine cooperative systems at scales previously impossible. "When you bring together human minds and artificial minds, that allows for a level of exploitation and undermining of systems that you just can't get with one or the other separately." AI doesn't need to be malevolent to be dangerous. It just needs to amplify existing human defection incentives.

When the World Falls Apart, Humans Cooperate

Aktipis's broader research program — the Human Generosity Project, the Zombified Media universe, the book Everything is Fine! How to Thrive in the Apocalypse — circles back to a finding that should be reassuring but often isn't: in acute disasters, humans spontaneously cooperate. Crime fell during COVID lockdowns. People carry strangers out of burning buildings at personal risk. Disaster relief from communities themselves typically precedes formal emergency response. The intuition that disasters produce immediate social breakdown is wrong, full stop.

Across nearly a dozen small-scale societies, fieldwork in the Human Generosity Project finds that need-based transfer systems are universal. When someone is genuinely in need, people give — not as formal loans with repayment expectations, but as gifts that keep social networks alive and robust. Arizona and New Mexico ranchers call it "neighboring." Mongolian herders, facing such extreme environmental variance that reactive strategies fail, are the one exception — they manage proactively all year, not just in crisis. The norm is: when things go badly wrong, humans help each other.

The apocalypse prep Aktipis recommends isn't bunkers and ammo. It's 72 hours of shelf-stable food (she recommends couscous — stores well, doesn't need hot water), embedded casually into existing household routines. It's a "Z team" — the small social network of people who would genuinely show up for each other in an emergency. These relationships probably already exist; the work is making them explicit. And it's community infrastructure: the neighborhood networks, sister-city relationships, and local organizations that make resilience possible from the bottom up rather than the top down.

The playful framing — zombies, apocalypse dinner parties, Zombie Apocalypse Medicine Meetings — isn't frivolity. It's strategy. Risk planning conversations collapse under the weight of jargon, status, and existential dread. Make it a game and people engage. "Anytime you have tension, you have conflict. We like humans, we like to learn about that and engage about that. It's inherently rewarding to think and talk about it in terms that are humorous because it allows us to wrap our heads around it without taking it so seriously that it has to make us depressed." The humor isn't avoidance. It's the cognitive technology that makes hard thinking possible.

This is the final move in Aktipis's argument, and it connects back to the beginning. Cancer persists because multicellularity requires cooperation, and cooperation always creates the conditions for defection. Human civilization faces the same structural problem at every scale. The answer — in cells, in societies, in the face of existential risk — is not to eliminate defection (you can't) but to build robust systems of monitoring, suppression, and management that keep defection from cascading. Cancer doesn't have to kill you. It just has to be managed. And so, perhaps, does everything else.

What To Do With This

  • Reframe cancer as an evolutionary management problem, not a war to win. The "maximum dose to kill everything" model creates drug resistance by eliminating sensitive competitors. Ask your oncologist about adaptive therapy trials or the logic of treatment holidays — it's not giving up, it's playing evolutionary dynamics in your favor.
  • Take aspirin or NSAIDs seriously as a cancer prevention tool. Evidence suggests they reduce somatic mutation rates by lowering inflammation. This matters most if you have elevated baseline inflammation. Discuss with a physician whether the risk-benefit math works for your situation, but understand why it works, not just that it does.
  • Apply the "adaptive therapy" logic to chronic social and institutional problems. Total-elimination approaches to crime, addiction, and institutional defection often select for resistant, harder-to-manage variants. Managing for stability rather than eradication — setting boundaries, enforcing them intermittently based on behavior — is structurally what works biologically. Consider where this applies in organizations you run or influence.
  • Build a Z team explicitly. Identify the three to five people who would genuinely show up for you in a serious emergency, and make that relationship mutual and explicit. The relationship almost certainly exists informally — naming it and discussing it converts latent social capital into reliable infrastructure.
  • Stock 72 hours of shelf-stable food and water as a baseline household norm. Couscous, canned goods, water storage. Embed it into your existing household rotation so it doesn't feel like prepping — it's just having a buffer. This handles the most likely disruptions (storms, supply chain hiccups, illness) without requiring any lifestyle change.
  • Use the cooperation-defection framework as a diagnostic tool when institutions fail. When an organization, market, or regulatory system stops working, ask: what cooperative surplus is being created, and which sub-components have found a way to extract it without contributing? The cancer model gives you a search image for the defector and often points toward what kind of suppression mechanism is missing.
  • Treat your body's inflammation levels as a cancer risk factor, not just a comfort issue. Chronic inflammation elevates somatic mutation rates. Exercise, sleep, diet, and stress management are not just quality-of-life improvements — they're cancer prevention mechanisms that operate at the evolutionary-information level.
  • When planning disaster preparedness or resilience for organizations, design for cooperation under pressure, not just for material stockpiles. The evidence is that spontaneous cooperation emerges in acute disasters. The failure modes are prolonged scarcity and large anonymous environments. Design systems that preserve community legibility — people knowing and trusting each other — because that's what prevents breakdown when resources genuinely run low.
  • When evaluating AI risk, focus attention on human-AI hybrid threats rather than autonomous misalignment scenarios alone. The near-term risk is humans with destructive goals using AI to undermine cooperative systems at unprecedented scale. This is a different threat model and requires different mitigation: transparency, monitoring, and norm-setting around AI-assisted coordination rather than just alignment research.

Memorable Quotes

"The opposite of cancer... is us. It's having a functional multicellular body that is cooperating effectively."

— Athena Aktipis

"The opportunity for evolution by natural selection to operate over the course of cancer progression is much vaster than all of the evolutionary time we have had as Homo sapiens."

— Athena Aktipis

"To me, the scariest thing about cancer is the cooperation side... it's quite likely that by the time you have a metastasis that is visible in any sort of screening, that very often that might have really come from a long lineage of clusters of cancer cells that were selected on the basis of being already able to cooperate effectively."

— Athena Aktipis

"You can't patent the idea of giving someone less medicine."

— Athena Aktipis

"My forefathers were free, but I was born a slave. I keep the memory of freedom in my DNA."

— from Revenge of the Somatic, a rap co-created by Athena Aktipis and Baba Brinkman, written from the perspective of a cancer cell

"Everybody can end up dead, which fucking sucks."

— Athena Aktipis, on the preeclampsia arms race between maternal and paternal genes

"Cooperation between different elements to achieve common goals creates a surplus. That surplus can then be grabbed by sub-components that decide to deviate from the rules that created it. This is recapitulated at different scales."

— Rob Wiblin (summary endorsed by Athena Aktipis)

"People just do this spontaneously when the shit hits the fan."

— Athena Aktipis, on spontaneous cooperation in disasters

item.title: Lenny's Podcast: Product | Career | Growth - Why most AI products fail: Lessons from 50+ AI deployments at OpenAI, Google, and Amazon
item.description:
The Two Things Most AI Teams Get Wrong — And the Framework That Fixes Both

There is a version of AI product development that looks like this: a team picks a powerful model, stacks up a bunch of tools, wires together a multi-agent workflow that can route tickets, issue refunds, file feature requests, and draft responses — all on day one. Then they ship it. Then they spend six months firefighting, hot-patching, and eventually shutting the whole thing down because the problems compound faster than they can fix them.

Aishwarya Reganti and Kiriti Badam have watched this play out dozens of times. Ash was an early AI researcher at Alexa and Microsoft and has published over 35 research papers. Kiriti works on Codex at OpenAI and spent the previous decade building AI and ML infrastructure at Google and Kumo. Together they've led or supported over 50 AI product deployments across Amazon, Databricks, OpenAI, Google, and a range of startups and enterprises. They also teach the top-rated AI course on Maven, aimed squarely at product leaders who are trying to figure out why their AI products aren't working — or want to avoid that fate before it hits them.

Their diagnosis of the field is blunt: the execution is still all over the place. 2025 marked a meaningful shift in that leaders stopped treating AI with crypto-wave skepticism and actually started rethinking their user experiences and workflows from the ground up. That's progress. But most teams are still building wrong — either ignoring the fundamental properties that make AI different from traditional software, or chasing the wrong version of autonomy before they've earned the right to it.

Why AI Products Are Fundamentally Different — and Why That Changes Everything

The first thing to get right is the mental model. Most teams build AI products like they build software products, and this is where the failure starts. There are two structural differences that change almost everything about how you should build.

The first is non-determinism — on both the input and output side simultaneously. In traditional software, you design a decision engine around mapped inputs and outcomes. Think of Booking.com: a user has an intent, the interface converts it into specific actions through clicks and forms, and the system executes. It's predictable. With AI, the interface is natural language, which means users will express the same intent in an enormous variety of ways you cannot anticipate. That's the input side. On the output side, you're working with a probabilistic API — the LLM — that is sensitive to prompt phrasing, behaves like a black box, and will produce different outputs even for identical inputs. You don't control the input surface, and you don't control the output surface. You're trying to achieve a deterministic outcome using non-deterministic technology at every stage of the stack.

The second is what Ash calls the agency-control trade-off — and it's the concept she says is shockingly underappreciated given how much people talk about building agents. Every time you hand decision-making authority to an AI system, you give up some corresponding amount of control. There's nothing inherently wrong with that — it's the whole point. But the question is whether the system has earned the right to that autonomy. An agent that hasn't been validated can make decisions that damage customer trust, expose legal liability (as Air Canada found out when their agent hallucinated a refund policy they had to honor), or simply compound errors in ways that become impossible to untangle. The mistake is giving the agent autonomy before building confidence in what it actually does.

These two properties — non-determinism and the agency-control trade-off — are not bugs to be engineered around. They're the design space. Recognizing them changes how you scope your roadmap, how you test, how you log, how you iterate, and what success looks like at each stage.

The CCCD Framework: Building Confidence Before Building Autonomy

Out of pattern-matching across dozens of deployments — including watching full agents get shut down after too many hot fixes — Ash and Kiriti developed a framework they call Continuous Calibration, Continuous Development (CCCD). The name is a deliberate nod to CI/CD in traditional software engineering, and the logic is analogous: continuous integration and deployment replaced the big-bang release cycle; CCCD replaces the big-bang agent deployment with an iterative loop of behavior calibration.

The right side of the loop is continuous development: scope a capability, curate a dataset of expected inputs and expected outputs (not comprehensive — just enough to get started and force alignment within the team), build the application, design evaluation metrics, deploy, and run those metrics. This data-curation step is deceptively valuable because it surfaces disagreements within the team about how the product should behave — disagreements that otherwise go invisible until they're production failures.

The left side is continuous calibration: after deployment, analyze actual behavior, spot emerging error patterns, apply fixes, and — critically — design new evaluation metrics to capture patterns you hadn't anticipated. Some errors you fix and move on. Others reveal systematic gaps that require instrumenting a new metric. You don't always need an LLM judge. Sometimes a tool-calling error caused by a poorly defined tool spec is just a bug — fix it and continue.

Layered on top of this development loop is the progression of autonomy. Each iteration should be constrained. Start with high human control, low AI agency. Build only what you need to validate the core behavior. Then, once user behavior has stabilized and you're no longer learning new things from calibration, move to the next level. The signal that you're ready to advance isn't a percentage score on an eval — it's that your calibration sessions have stopped producing surprises.

The Customer Support Walkthrough: What This Looks Like in Practice

The clearest worked example is customer support — the domain Kiriti was dealing with directly at OpenAI during product launches like GPT-5, when support volume would spike dramatically with unfamiliar problem types.

Version 1 is routing. Can the system correctly classify an incoming ticket and route it to the right department? This sounds trivially easy, but at enterprise scale, routing is genuinely hard. Taxonomies are almost always messy — categories duplicated at wrong hierarchy levels, dead nodes from years-old reorganizations that nobody cleaned up, rules that exist only in the heads of senior agents who learned them through experience. Building routing first exposes all of this. Humans stay in full control; even if the AI routes incorrectly, a human can immediately correct it. And you start logging what humans do — which becomes free error analysis for the next iteration.

Version 2 is co-pilot: the agent drafts a suggested resolution based on standard operating procedures, and the human customer support agent reviews and edits it before sending. You're logging everything the human does with the draft — what they keep, what they cut, what they rewrite. That edit data is a flywheel input, showing you exactly where the model's understanding of the domain is incomplete.

Version 3 is end-to-end resolution: the agent handles tickets autonomously, drafts and sends resolutions, and closes tickets without human review. By the time you reach this stage, you've validated routing at scale, you understand the common failure modes in draft generation, and you've built enough confidence in the system's judgment to extend it trust. If you'd tried to build Version 3 on day one, you'd be fighting hundreds of failure modes simultaneously with no way to prioritize or systematically address them.

The same progression applies to coding assistants (inline suggestions → block generation for review → autonomous PRs), marketing tools (draft copy → multi-step campaigns → autonomous A/B testing), and virtually any other AI-enabled workflow. The shape is always the same: constrain first, earn trust, then expand.

What Separates Teams That Succeed

The companies that consistently build AI products that work share three characteristics — what Ash calls a success triangle — and only one of them is technical.

The first is leadership that rebuilds its intuitions. Leaders who have spent fifteen years developing sharp instincts about their business now operate in a domain where many of those instincts don't transfer. Gajen, the CEO of Rackspace, blocks out 4 to 6 AM every morning to stay current on AI — not chatting with chatbots, but systematically following two or three high-quality sources and then pressure-testing what he's learning against AI experts. He treats it as a discipline. The payoff is that he can actually guide decisions across the company from a foundation of real understanding rather than overfit mental models or vendor hype. Bottom-up AI adoption fails. When engineers can't get buy-in from leaders who either don't trust the technology or have wildly miscalibrated expectations about what it can do, nothing ships. The direction has to come from the top.

The second is a culture of empowerment rather than fear. Subject matter experts are irreplaceable in AI product development — they're the only people who can tell you whether your system is behaving correctly, where the edge cases are, and what the ideal behavior should look like. But in enterprises where the message is "AI will replace you," subject matter experts go silent. They don't participate in reviews. They don't flag problems. They protect their positions instead of sharing their knowledge. The companies that succeed create the opposite environment: AI augments what you do, you become 10x more capable, and your expertise is what makes the system work.

The third is technical obsession with workflow before technology. The best builders understand their workflows in granular detail — which steps are high-risk versus low-risk, which decisions benefit from AI versus deterministic code versus ML models, where human review adds necessary value. They're not obsessed with the technology itself. They're obsessed with the problem, and they choose tools that fit it. When a 74-75% enterprise reliability failure rate (from the UC Berkeley study led by Matei Zaharia and Ion Stoica at Databricks) keeps companies from deploying customer-facing AI, the problem is usually that they tried to deploy before they understood either their workflow or their failure modes.

The Eval Confusion — and What Actually Matters

Few topics in AI product development have been more thoroughly muddled than evals. The word is now doing so much semantic work for so many different communities — data labeling companies ("our experts write evals"), product managers ("evals are the new PRD"), benchmark sites (LM Arena, Artificial Analysis), LLM judge frameworks — that it's become nearly meaningless in conversation. This is what Martin Fowler called "semantic diffusion": a term starts precise and useful, gets adopted widely, gets stretched by different communities with different definitions, and eventually loses its ability to communicate anything reliably.

The actual decision isn't "evals vs. production monitoring." It's a much more practical question: what is it you're trying to know, and what's the most direct way to learn it? Pre-deployment, you need some set of expected behaviors that shouldn't regress — call these your test suite, your evaluation dataset, whatever — and you run it before shipping changes. Post-deployment, you need signals from actual user behavior because users will surprise you in ways your pre-deployment dataset cannot anticipate. Those implicit signals (regeneration requests as a proxy for dissatisfaction, ticket close rates, feature toggle-offs) are often more informative than explicit feedback, and they reveal failure patterns you'd never have designed an eval for.

Codex's approach is illustrative. They maintain evaluations for core behaviors that shouldn't regress across model changes. But for something like Code Review — a product gaining significant traction, catching real bugs for both internal and external users — the most important signal when shipping a new model or RL mechanism is whether users are finding the reviews helpful or annoying enough to switch the feature off. You can't design an eval dataset that covers every code review scenario across every engineering culture and workflow. You watch the behavior and respond to what you see.

What's Misunderstood and What's Underrated

Multi-agent systems are misunderstood rather than overhyped. There are genuinely successful multi-agent implementations, and the supervisor-subagent pattern — a coordinator agent delegating to specialized sub-agents — works well. What doesn't work is the fantasy of peer-to-peer agent gossip protocols, where responsibilities are divided by function and agents coordinate laterally. The current state of model capabilities and infrastructure doesn't support this at production reliability standards. When your agents are communicating via some informal protocol and one of them is talking to a customer, you've lost control of which guardrails apply and who's accountable for the output.

Coding agents are genuinely underrated. The chatter on Twitter and Reddit makes them seem ubiquitous, but the penetration inside actual companies — especially outside major tech hubs — is still low. The efficiency gains available to engineering teams that adopt them seriously are substantial, and the friction of adoption is falling. 2026 will accelerate this.

The next frontier is proactive agents — systems that are embedded deeply enough in actual workflows that they understand your context, anticipate needs, and prompt you rather than waiting to be prompted. ChatGPT's daily digest feature is an early version of this. A coding agent that reviews your Linear tickets overnight and has patches ready to review when you wake up is a more developed version. The limiting factor is context: agents create value in proportion to how much of your actual work they can observe. As that integration deepens, the value compounds.

Pain Is the New Moat

The most durable competitive advantage in AI right now isn't a fancy model, a novel architecture, or first-mover status. It's the accumulated knowledge that comes from going through the hard iterations — trying approaches that don't work, understanding why, developing judgment about what does work, building that judgment into your processes and your systems.

There are no textbooks for this. There are no playbooks. There is only the organization that has built 50 versions of its pipeline, learned from each failure, and developed proprietary intuition about what works in their domain. That knowledge is extraordinarily hard to acquire without doing the work — and it's extraordinarily hard to replicate from the outside. "Pain is the new moat" isn't about suffering for its own sake. It's about recognizing that the teams and companies who are willing to go through the learning curve systematically — who treat each failure as a data point rather than a crisis — are building something that compounds over time in ways that shortcut-seekers can't match.

Implementation is becoming cheap. Building is becoming cheap. What remains expensive is judgment, taste, and the earned understanding of what your users actually need and how your system actually behaves. That's where the work is now.

What To Do With This

  • Map your current AI initiative to the agency-control spectrum before writing a line of code. Draw out your target V3 state, then identify what V1 looks like with maximum human control and minimal AI decision-making. Start there, not at V3 — the failure modes you'll encounter in V1 are prerequisites for knowing how to build V2 safely.
  • Build a behavior dataset before you build the application. Gather expected inputs and expected outputs with your PM, subject matter experts, and engineers in the same room. The alignment exercise itself is valuable — disagreements that surface here would otherwise surface in production. This doesn't need to be comprehensive; it needs to be honest about what the product should actually do.
  • Instrument implicit user signals from day one. Explicit feedback (thumbs up/down) captures a fraction of what users are telling you. Regeneration requests, feature disablement, session abandonment, time-to-action — these implicit signals often reveal failure modes that no pre-deployment eval would catch. Wire these up before launch, not after problems appear.
  • Use the "minimizing surprise" heuristic to decide when to advance autonomy levels. If your calibration sessions over the past one to two days have stopped producing new behavior patterns — if users are interacting predictably and your error analysis isn't surfacing new categories — you're ready to move to the next level of agency. This is more useful than any arbitrary accuracy threshold.
  • Treat subject matter experts as product co-builders, not consultants. The people who know your domain deeply are your primary source of ground truth for whether AI behavior is correct. If they're not actively engaged in reviewing outputs and flagging problems, you're flying blind. Create the conditions — psychological safety, clear framing that AI augments rather than replaces — that make them want to participate.
  • Require your leadership to rebuild their AI intuitions actively. "Staying informed" is not enough. Leaders need to develop genuine hands-on familiarity with what current models can and can't do — not to implement, but to calibrate expectations and guide organizational decisions. A structured morning block, a curated set of expert sources, weekend experimentation — the specific format matters less than the consistency.
  • When evaluating multi-agent architectures, default to supervisor-subagent over peer-to-peer. A single orchestrating agent that delegates to specialized sub-agents is significantly more controllable than agents coordinating laterally. You know where the guardrails need to live, you know who's responsible for the output, and you can debug failures systematically. Peer-to-peer agent gossip at production scale is currently more aspirational than practical.
  • Distinguish between eval types before prescribing a solution. If someone says "we need evals," get specific: Are you talking about a regression test suite to prevent capability degradation across model updates? LLM judges for automated quality scoring? Human expert annotation? Production monitoring dashboards? Implicit signal aggregation? Each serves a different purpose, and conflating them leads to over-investing in the wrong tool for the actual problem.
  • Log all human-in-the-loop decisions as structured training data from day one. When a human reviews an AI draft and modifies it, that edit is free error analysis. When a human overrides a routing decision, that's a labeled data point. Design your early product stages to capture this data systematically — it becomes the flywheel for improving subsequent iterations without running expensive annotation campaigns.
  • Plan for the four-to-six month runway before significant ROI on critical workflow automation. Even with clean data infrastructure, even with the best models, replacing a critical workflow or achieving meaningful ROI takes time. One-click agent claims are marketing. Budget and plan accordingly, and treat the early months as data and flywheel investment rather than expecting immediate operational returns.

Memorable Quotes

"You don't know how the user might behave with your product, and you also don't know how the LLM might respond to that. So you're now working with an input, output, and a process you don't understand all three of very well. You're trying to anticipate behavior and build for it."
— Aishwarya Reganti
"Every time you hand over decision-making capabilities or autonomy to agentic systems, you're relinquishing some amount of control. You want to make sure that your agent has gained your trust or is reliable enough that you can allow it to make decisions."
— Aishwarya Reganti
"As of today, it's not about being the first company to have an agent among your competitors. It's about have you built the right flywheels in place so that you can improve over time."
— Aishwarya Reganti
"If someone's selling you one-click agents, it's pure marketing. You don't want to buy into that. I would rather go with a company that says, we're going to build this pipeline for you and that will learn over time."
— Aishwarya Reganti
"Eighty percent of so-called AI engineers, AI PMs spend their time actually understanding their workflows very well. They're not building the fanciest and the most cool models or workflows around it. They're actually in the weeds understanding their customer's behavior and data."
— Aishwarya Reganti
"Pain is the new moat. Successful companies right now building in any new area — they went through the pain of understanding what are the set of non-negotiable things and traded them off exactly with what are the features or modern capabilities they can use to solve that problem. That knowledge, built across the organization through lived experience, is what translates into the moat."
— Kiriti Badam
"Leaders have to get back to being hands-on. Not because they have to be implementing these things, but more of rebuilding their intuitions, because you must be comfortable with the fact that your intuitions might not be right. And you probably are the dumbest person in the room and you want to learn from everyone."
— Aishwarya Reganti
"Building is really cheap today. Design is more expensive — really thinking about your product, what you're going to build, is it going to really solve a pain point. That is way more valuable today. And it will only become more true in the near future."
— Aishwarya Reganti

item.title: The Logan Bartlett Show - EP 150: Bret Taylor (CEO, Sierra): A New Class of Software Winners
item.description:
The $20 Phone Call That Changes Everything

Bret Taylor has a thought experiment he uses to explain why AI agents aren't just a cost-cutting story. Imagine you run a telecom company. Your entire business hinges on subscriber lifetime value — who upgrades to a higher tier, who churns when a competitor runs a promotion. Today, a phone call with a customer costs you $20. With AI agents, that same conversation costs twenty cents. The obvious move is to bank the operating expense savings. But that's the wrong frame entirely.

"When the price of a phone call starts to approach the price of a page view," Taylor says, "you're going to do a lot more of them." At twenty cents a conversation, you don't just automate the same volume of calls — you rethink the entire relationship. You proactively reach out to subscribers before they see a competitor's ad. You upsell at moments of high intent. You turn what was a cost center into a revenue engine. The unit economics of customer engagement just got inverted, and most companies haven't started working through what that means.

This is the core of how Taylor thinks about Sierra, the company he co-founded with Clay Bavor to build AI agents for customer experience. The firm recently closed a major financing round, and Taylor came on the Logan Bartlett Show to think through the landscape — where the AI wave parallels the original internet era, where the analogy breaks down, and what it actually takes to win in a market where the obvious opportunities are obvious to everyone.

AltaVista Was First Too

The parallel Taylor reaches for most often is the dot-com era, and not in a cautionary way. He sees genuine structural similarities. In both cases, the biggest markets were legible from the start. "You don't need to be that savvy about the internet to think, wow, it might be useful if people could buy things digitally," he observes. Search, e-commerce, payments — the concept was obvious. The question was always execution: who gets the product right, who picks the right go-to-market model, who builds the right business model for the moment.

He uses Inktomi as a sharper example than AltaVista. Inktomi had strong engineering; Taylor argues PageRank was better, but it wasn't a blowout. What killed Inktomi was a B2B model — they licensed their search engine to portals, which meant they never had the chance to build AdWords, "which turned out to be the greatest business model of all time." Google was first in the right direction; Inktomi was first in the wrong direction.

The AI equivalent is playing out in real time. Software engineering, customer service, content, legal, visual effects — these are the obvious categories, analogous to search and e-commerce in 1997. As a result, one of Sierra's official company values is "competitive intensity." The first line of that value, Taylor notes, is: "We know we're not entitled to our success." It's an unusual thing to codify, but in a market where the opportunity is visible to everyone, humility about that entitlement is actually the realistic stance.

The New TAM: When Agents Do the Work

One place where the AI wave genuinely diverges from prior platform shifts is in how it redraws the map of addressable markets. Taylor points to Harvey — an AI company focused on legal work — as his favorite illustration. Legal tech has never produced a top-ten enterprise software company. The TAM for selling productivity tools to lawyers simply wasn't that large. Law firms are small businesses that don't buy much software per lawyer.

But Harvey isn't selling productivity enhancement. Harvey is doing the antitrust review. "If you're actually doing the work," Taylor says, "all of a sudden the total addressable market looks huge because the addressable market of legal advice and legal labor is actually quite large." The shift from "tools that help humans work faster" to "agents that do the work" unlocks sectors that traditional enterprise software never penetrated — not because the software wasn't good enough, but because the labor market it was addressing was never the software's to capture.

This reframes how you value an AI agent entirely. A Sierra agent that completes a sale doesn't get valued on software productivity metrics. It gets valued on the margin of that sale, or on the commission you would have paid a human to make it. "The way you would value it is not really even related to AI or software," Taylor says. That's not a subtle distinction — it's a categorical shift in how software companies justify their existence and price their products.

From Impressions to Clicks: The Outcome-Based Pricing Argument

When Logan Bartlett asks whether competition will eventually compress outcome-based pricing the way it compressed per-seat pricing for Zoom or Slack, Taylor offers an analogy that reframes the question. Moving from seat-based to outcome-based pricing isn't leaving money on the table — it's moving from impression-based to cost-per-click advertising. "That would be like making a modern cost-per-click ads auction and saying you're leaving impressions on the table," he says. No one in digital advertising thinks that way anymore because history has settled the question.

The reason horizontal productivity tools like Zoom end up commoditized, Taylor argues, is that the value they deliver is genuinely hard to measure. You pay the same per-seat price for your most senior engineer and your newest hire, regardless of how much they rely on the product. There's no clean line from "this video call happened" to "this business outcome occurred." In contrast, vertical enterprise software — ServiceNow for ITSM, Salesforce for CRM, SAP for ERP — has always commanded dramatically higher per-seat pricing precisely because it sits closer to measurable business value.

Outcome-based pricing takes that logic to its endpoint. In Sierra's case, companies already know their cost per contact in their call centers. They already know what they pay a salesperson to close a deal. When an AI agent does that work, the value is legible, and the pricing can reflect it. "For companies, it means they can model this not proportional to the cost of a technology, but proportional to the value that they're getting as a business."

Will competition eventually put downward pressure on those prices? Probably. Taylor acknowledges that in ten years, agents will be compared to other agents rather than to human labor costs, and that will create its own compression dynamic. But the floor will be inference costs, not labor costs — a structurally different and likely lower floor — while the ceiling, tied to business outcomes, will remain high. The value capture accrues toward whoever is closest to the measurable outcome.

The Innovator's Dilemma, in Slow Motion

The part of this conversation where Taylor's thinking becomes most specific — and most valuable — is his analysis of why incumbents fail to navigate platform shifts. The conventional answer invokes "innovator's dilemma" and stops there. Taylor, having briefly run a public company as co-CEO of Salesforce, goes deeper into the mechanics of why these transitions are harder than they look from the outside.

His illustrative scenario: a customer paying you a million dollars a year. You have a new technology that, over ten years, would grow that relationship to ten million dollars a year. But in year one, rolling it out would cause revenue to drop to $200,000. "How do you actually do that?"

For a public company, that looks like your business is decelerating even though you're building the future. For an unprofitable startup, your burn rate spikes at the worst moment. Even for a theoretically ideal private company that has somehow resolved both of those constraints, your employees start leaving midway through because they're not sure the bet pays off. "It's so easy for armchair strategists to be like, of course, that's the correct thing to do. Then the question is, well, if you don't care about the messy middle, yeah, it's totally obvious, but everyone in the world cares about the messy middle."

His examples are instructive. Siebel Systems understood that Salesforce's model was the future — they could see the slow-motion car wreck — and still couldn't turn the wheel fast enough. Microsoft, by contrast, navigated multiple platform transitions: the internet, cloud, now AI. Adobe made the transition from perpetual license to subscription, which Taylor calls an act of genuine craft. "It changes your balance sheet, changes your accounting. All of a sudden you look more or less profitable, even though the cashflow is the same, but all these numbers shift around." Then you have to bring employees, investors, and customers along with you while the headline numbers look temporarily worse.

Taylor's conclusion: some incumbents will come out of the AI transition stronger, like Microsoft did from cloud. Some will be Siebel. The default, for most, is disruption — not because the technology is beyond them, but because the business model transition required is genuinely hard to execute from inside a large organization with quarterly report cards and entrenched incentive structures.

Where Foundation Models Stop and Products Begin

The sharpest strategic question in the AI landscape right now is where foundation model providers will expand their own products versus where there's durable space for applied AI companies. Taylor's framework for thinking about it tracks the maturity of a technology cycle.

In the early days of any major platform — the web in 1995, mobile in 2007 — most of the value in the ecosystem is in making the platform work at all. Scaling a website to consumer traffic was hard. Making a mobile app that didn't crash was hard. Startups and incumbents sold the vision of the new platform, and "it works" was a legitimate value proposition. But that doesn't hold as the platform matures. By 2025, deploying a scalable web application is an afternoon project on Vercel. No one pays a premium for "it scales."

"I assume that will happen with reasoning agents and action-taking agents and voice agents and all the things that are really, really, really hard today." The hard part of building agents will become infrastructure. The baseline will commoditize. And at that point, the applied AI companies that built their identity around making agents work will need a different answer to the fundamental product question: once the technology working is no longer the differentiator, what is?

This is Taylor's version of the "database in the cloud" problem he raised about SaaS. Reduced reductively, Salesforce is a database in the cloud. But they built an enormous amount of value on top of that database — workflows, ecosystem, integrations, institutional knowledge. No one asks whether Salesforce's database scales anymore. The question is what's on top of it. Applied AI companies need to be building that layer now, while they still have the technology differentiation to earn customer relationships that will persist after the technology advantage evens out.

The Long Tail and the Internet's Real Legacy

One of the most interesting tangents in the conversation is Bartlett's question about whether AI will look more like the internet (where net-new companies captured most of the value) or mobile (where existing companies like Google, Apple, and Facebook were the primary beneficiaries). Taylor pushes back on the premise — gently, but with a genuinely interesting counter-observation.

Sure, the most valuable public companies are the Googles and Amazons and Metas of the world. But the next forty companies in the S&P 500 by market cap include dozens of SaaS companies most people have never heard of. Shopify is a beneficiary of the internet, but Shopify's entire business is empowering entrepreneurs who wouldn't have existed otherwise. The HVAC company that grew from a million dollars in revenue to twenty million by reaching customers more effectively — who gets credit for that? Is JPMorgan with 60,000 software engineers a software company or a bank?

"I'd be interested for someone smarter than me to do that analysis," Taylor says, genuinely. His intuition is that when you aggregate the full long tail of economic value created on top of internet infrastructure, it dwarfs what even the hyperscalers captured. The question is almost unanswerable cleanly because the internet has become so foundational that attributing value to it is like attributing value to electricity.

For AI, he expects a similar distribution — some very large winners at the foundation model layer, a robust tier of vertical enterprise AI companies below that, and an enormous long tail of businesses transformed by agents in ways that won't show up neatly in any single company's market cap. Sierra is building to be in the middle tier: a durable, enduring company that owns a market rather than a feature, in the same way Salesforce owns CRM or ServiceNow owns ITSM.

That's the bet. AltaVista was first in search. Inktomi had better engineering. Google wrote the history book. Bret Taylor is trying very hard to be Google.

What To Do With This

  • Reframe your AI cost-center initiatives as revenue opportunities. Before modeling AI ROI purely as headcount reduction, map out what you'd do with the same interactions at 100x lower cost. The second-order effects on customer engagement, upsell, and retention are almost certainly larger than the first-order cost savings.
  • Audit your company's value proximity to business outcomes. Plot each software product or initiative on a spectrum from "horizontal productivity" to "direct business outcome." Products closer to measurable outcomes (a closed deal, a resolved ticket, an approved contract) will command better pricing and be harder to displace. Shift resources toward the outcome-adjacent end.
  • If you're building applied AI, answer this question now: "Once the fact that our technology works is no longer the differentiator, what does our product become?" Taylor explicitly identifies this as the key due diligence question for applied AI companies. Don't wait until the technology commoditizes to have an answer.
  • Study the Inktomi lesson before picking a business model. When evaluating AI product strategy, separate the question of "does the product work?" from "does the business model capture the right value?" Inktomi's engineering was competitive; their B2B licensing model foreclosed the advertising opportunity that defined the industry. The wrong model can lose even a technically strong race.
  • Model the full transition cost before committing to a business model shift. If you're an incumbent considering a pricing or delivery model change (e.g., seat-based to outcome-based), explicitly map the "messy middle" — the period where existing revenue appears to shrink before the new model matures. Model how employees, investors, and customers will react to those headline numbers. Most transitions fail here, not at the strategy layer.
  • Encode competitive humility as a company value, not just a talking point. Taylor's team formally codified "competitive intensity" — with the explicit acknowledgment that they are not entitled to their success — as a core company value. In markets where the opportunity is obvious to all entrants, teams that feel entitled get outworked. Consider how you operationalize urgency beyond rhetoric.
  • Use the TAM-unlocking test when evaluating AI opportunities. Ask: is this AI application making existing labor faster (productivity enhancement) or actually doing the work (labor replacement)? The latter unlocks TAM that traditional software never could address. Harvey accessing the legal labor market is the model; a legal productivity tool for law firms is not.
  • For large incumbents: identify your Siebel risk. Which of your current product lines or pricing models would require a revenue dip in year one to capture a 10x opportunity in year ten? Naming that trade-off explicitly — and deciding consciously rather than by default — is what separates the companies that navigate platform shifts from the ones that become case studies.

Lines Worth Keeping

"When the price of a phone call starts to approach the price of a page view, you're going to do a lot more of them."
— Bret Taylor
"It's so easy for armchair strategists to be like, of course, that's the correct thing to do. Then the question is, well, if you don't care about the messy middle, yeah, it's totally obvious, but everyone in the world cares about the messy middle."
— Bret Taylor
"AltaVista was first, and Google is the one who writes the history book. So we need multiple years of sustained, impeccable execution, basically, from here."
— Bret Taylor
"Agents aren't simply productivity enhancements for people but actually doing a job. As a consequence, how you evaluate the value of a piece of software starts to move away from traditional software productivity metrics."
— Bret Taylor
"You could see the companies like Microsoft had fits and starts with the internet and cloud and came out quite strong. And so if you're big enough, you get multiple at-bats with these transitions."
— Bret Taylor
