"The analogy I would give for outcomes-based is we're going from impression ads to CPC ads. That would be like making a modern cost-per-click ads auction and saying you're leaving impressions on the table."
— Bret Taylor
"That is the default for companies in our industry — their technology and business model was once great, and they didn't transition to the next one."
— Bret Taylor

item.title: The Logan Bartlett Show - EP 149: Marc Benioff (CEO, Salesforce) Predicts Half of Conversations Will be With AI Agents Next Year
item.description:
The Agentic Enterprise Is Already Here — Marc Benioff Has the Numbers to Prove It

Most AI conversations in 2025 are still in the future tense. Marc Benioff is speaking in past tense. While the industry debates whether agentic AI will transform enterprise software, the CEO of Salesforce has already rebalanced his support headcount from 9,000 to 5,000, run 1.5 million AI-handled customer conversations, and is processing 10,000 sales leads per week through autonomous agents. Salesforce, he argues, isn't just selling the dream — it's customer zero for it.

That posture — proving it internally before asking anyone else to believe it — is the through-line of Benioff's thinking. In a conversation with investor Logan Bartlett, he laid out what's actually working, what's overblown, and what he thinks every enterprise leader is getting wrong about the transition to AI-augmented operations.

A Billion Conversations That Never Happened

The most striking data point Benioff shared wasn't about AI — it was about a failure that predated it. Over Salesforce's 26-year history, more than 100 million leads contacted the company and never received a callback. Not because of negligence, but because there simply weren't enough humans to follow up. "We just couldn't call them back," he said flatly.

That gap is now being closed. An agentic sales system is calling back every inbound lead — 10,000 per week — having full conversations and converting them into pipeline. "Our pipelines have never been more full," Benioff said. The point isn't just efficiency; it's capability expansion. AI isn't replacing the capacity that existed. It's unlocking capacity that was never there.

The same logic applies to support. A year ago, Salesforce had 9,000 support agents globally. Today it has about 5,000, alongside an agentic layer that has handled 1.5 million customer conversations in the same period that humans handled another 1.5 million. What made this operationally tractable wasn't just the agents — it was the omni-channel supervisor layer sitting above them, orchestrating when agents handle cases autonomously and when they escalate to humans. "It's not any different than being in your Tesla and all of a sudden it goes, I don't know what's happening — you take over," Benioff said. The handoff mechanism is the product.

CSAT scores across the human and agentic channels were comparable — "which was stunning," Benioff admitted. And the headcount he freed from support didn't disappear; it moved into sales, expanding distribution capacity while reducing cost per interaction.

The Three-Layer Architecture That Actually Works

Benioff keeps returning to a framework he introduced nine months prior in an earlier conversation with Bartlett: enterprise AI capability rests on three stacked layers, and you can't skip any of them.

The first layer is the data foundation — Salesforce's Data Cloud, MuleSoft for integration, Tableau for analytics, and the pending Informatica acquisition. This layer manages 230 petabytes of customer data across Salesforce's installed base. Without it, Benioff argues, you can't get accuracy out of AI. "If you don't have that data together, you can't have accuracy with the AI." The data foundation product line — previously a niche category — is now a billion dollars in ARR and tracking toward two billion. He describes it as the fastest-growing product line Salesforce has ever had.

The second layer is the application layer: the specialized interfaces and workflows built for specific functions like sales, service, field service, and marketing. These aren't going away. They're getting more powerful because they're integrating with the third layer — the agentic layer — rather than being replaced by it. Benioff pushed back on colleagues who predict the "end of apps." The enterprise professional running a support operation needs a rich interface. A Slack window isn't enough.

The third layer is the agentic layer itself: the LLMs and agent orchestration that run autonomous tasks, respond to customers, and surface context in real time. What makes Benioff's architecture distinctive is the insistence that all three must be integrated. A standalone LLM without your company's data is a parlor trick. A standalone app without an agentic layer is a productivity bottleneck. The value compounds when they're wired together.

He gave a vivid example: when a field service technician from Eaton shows up to service the power connection at Benioff's home, they arrive with Salesforce's field service app on their phone, showing the customer history, the device specs, and the previous service notes. But they're also accompanied by an agent — available in the same interface — that can interpret anomalies, suggest diagnostic steps, and document findings back into the system of record. The next technician inherits everything. "He's not by himself. He's with the agent."

50-50: The Number That Should Get Every Executive's Attention

Benioff offered what may be the most practically useful prediction in the conversation: within the next year, roughly half of all customer interactions across sales, service, marketing, and field service will be handled by agents. The other half by humans. He isn't projecting this — he says it's the trajectory already visible in Salesforce's own operations and in the deployments he's watching across thousands of enterprise customers.

"In sales, in service, in marketing, in field service, in the fundamental collaboration of our employees — all of these pieces are going to have that kind of balance," he said.

The implication for enterprise leaders is direct: the management challenge is no longer just managing people. It's managing a mixed workforce of humans and agents, with clear guardrails defining agent behavior, defined escalation protocols, and KPIs that can be attributed across both. Benioff acknowledged he still doesn't have a clean term for "managing agent workers" — he finds the phrase slightly dystopian — but the reality is already here. Agents need personalities, tonal constraints, escalation triggers, and guardrails. That's a management problem, not just a technology problem.

The Pricing Transition Nobody Fully Understands Yet

For software investors and buyers alike, the shift from per-seat to consumption or conversational pricing is one of the most structurally disruptive questions in enterprise software. Benioff offered a taxonomy that's worth understanding in detail.

Salesforce is operating across four pricing models simultaneously: per-seat (Sales Cloud, Service Cloud, Slack), consumption-based (Data Cloud, Marketing Cloud email), conversational (conversation credits for agent interactions), and — the one he found most surprising — the all-in enterprise license. "Way more aggressively than I realized, customers want a complete agentic enterprise license agreement," he said. Large enterprises want to buy the whole transformation as one package, not license feature by feature.

The cannibalization concern — that agentic productivity will reduce the number of seats needed — is real, but Benioff frames it as an opportunity. If a customer needs 2,000 fewer support seats because agents are handling half the volume, that's headcount they can redeploy. And Salesforce's business grows because the customer is buying the agentic layer on top of existing subscriptions, not instead of them. The unit of value is shifting from person-enabled access to work-accomplished.

Where the Market Actually Is

When Bartlett asked whether we're in an "absorption and digestion" phase — harvesting existing AI capability rather than racing toward new breakthroughs — Benioff gave a segmented answer that's more useful than the usual bull/bear binary.

The market is five segments: small businesses (up to a few hundred employees), medium businesses, large enterprises, extra-large Fortune 500 companies, and government. Each segment is at a different stage and deploying AI at a different pace, with different architecture requirements and different appetites for risk. Benioff's assessment: "We're at the beginning of the beginning." Even among the largest enterprises — the ones most likely to be early movers — most are still in pilot mode. A company like Pfizer, which has deployed AgentForce to 20,000 sales professionals, is a pioneer, not a median case.

One implication Benioff sees as underappreciated: AI will dramatically increase the number of small businesses. Entrepreneurs will be dramatically more capable, and previously unviable ideas will become viable when a team of five can operate with the leverage of fifty. He described the YC founders he met — two 18-year-olds building on Anthropic's API with $30,000 in free credits — as evidence. "Innovation is alive and well in Silicon Valley."

The Contrarian Case Against AGI Hype

The most intellectually provocative part of the conversation was Benioff's pushback on AGI rhetoric. He's not a skeptic of AI capability — he's living proof of its enterprise value. But he thinks the framing of current LLMs as approaching general intelligence misunderstands what these systems are.

"These large language models are relatively finite algorithm sets with relatively finite datasets — and those two things provide you some functionality but not all functionality," he said. As evidence, he cited a recent study showing that human brains begin generating immune cells when in proximity to sick people — a fully autonomous biological response we don't consciously control. "I kind of missed that feature on GPT-5," he deadpanned.

His structural argument is that because all LLMs train on roughly the same corpus of human-generated text, and because that corpus is finite, there's an inherent ceiling on the step-change improvements possible within the current paradigm. GPT-3 to GPT-4 felt revolutionary. GPT-4 to GPT-5 felt evolutionary. That's not a failure of OpenAI — it's what happens when a technology reaches the limits of its dataset and architecture. A new class of models, trained on different data or using different architectures, would be needed to produce another leap.

The business implication: don't bet your enterprise transformation on perpetually improving foundation models. The value is in deeply integrating current-generation models into your data and application layers — where Benioff is putting Salesforce's chips — not in waiting for the models to become so capable that integration becomes trivial.

On Talent, Speed, and What Incumbents Can Learn from Startups

Benioff was candid about what large companies can learn from AI-native startups, and he didn't frame it as an existential threat to incumbents — he framed it as a model to emulate. He described two portfolio companies as exemplars: u.com (Richard Socher's AI-native search company, built on API-first architecture) and Artera (Andre Esteva's AI diagnostics company, recently FDA-cleared for prostate cancer detection). Both are former Salesforce AI researchers. Both have clear missions, specific value propositions, and aggressive revenue growth.

The speed differential between AI-native startups and incumbents is real, but Benioff doesn't think it's insurmountable. What he sees as the constants across every successful company he's built or backed: a clear vision of what you're trying to accomplish, values that match the domain (in healthcare, that means trust above all), quality of execution, and — critically — a willingness to go faster than feels comfortable. "You better go, and you better go faster, and you better go now." That hasn't changed since the era of Arthur Rock.

On the question of hiring young talent, Benioff was emphatic. Companies pulling back from new-grad hiring are making a strategic mistake. The 18- and 22-year-olds entering the workforce today aren't digital natives — they're agentic natives. They understand intuitively what's possible with these tools and can look at a business process and immediately identify where the gaps are. "That's very powerful," he said. "And we need these people."

The forward-deployed engineering model popularized by Palantir — beginning to build the application before a deal is signed — was something Benioff admitted he'd been inspired by and is experimenting with. It represents a shift in how enterprise software is sold: less feature-demonstration, more proof of value in the customer's actual environment before commitment. Combined with the company showcase model he's planning for Dreamforce — where customers like FedEx and OpenAI present their own agentic transformations to prospective customers — it suggests the enterprise sales motion itself is becoming consultative in ways that SaaS traditionally wasn't.

What To Do With This

  • Audit your unconverted leads pipeline, not just your active pipeline. Benioff's 100-million-uncalled-leads revelation is almost certainly replicated in every large company. Agentic outreach to dormant leads is a direct revenue recovery opportunity with low downside risk — start a pilot with a defined cohort before scaling.
  • Implement a human-agent escalation protocol before deploying any customer-facing agent. The omni-channel supervisor model — technology that monitors agent conversations and routes to humans when confidence is low — is what makes the 50/50 split operationally safe. Don't deploy agents without explicit escalation triggers and fallback paths.
  • Treat your data foundation as the prerequisite, not the afterthought. Before evaluating LLMs or agent platforms, inventory whether your company's data is harmonized, accessible, and clean. Agents hallucinate and fail when grounded in bad data. Fix the data layer first.
  • Rethink your software pricing expectations. If you're buying enterprise AI, push vendors toward all-in agentic enterprise agreements rather than piecemeal licenses. Benioff's data suggests customers who negotiate this way get more strategic alignment from vendors and better total cost of ownership as consumption grows.
  • Establish KPIs that span human and agent contributors. Build reporting that shows what percentage of work units — conversations handled, leads contacted, cases resolved — are being done by agents vs. humans, and track this weekly. Without this, you can't manage the transition or defend headcount decisions internally.
  • Design agents with explicit personas, guardrails, and tonal constraints. An agent representing your brand in a customer conversation needs the same thought put into it as hiring a customer-facing employee. Define what it can say, what it can't, how it handles complaints, and what escalation looks like. Treat agent configuration as a management task, not a technical one.
  • Don't wait for better models before deploying current ones. Benioff's structural argument — that LLMs are hitting an evolutionary plateau — suggests the companies that win won't be those who waited for GPT-6. They'll be those who integrated GPT-4/5-class models deeply into their operations now. The competitive moat is in integration depth, not model recency.
  • Hire new graduates, especially those who grew up using AI tools natively. The AI-native generation's intuitive grasp of what's possible makes them disproportionately valuable for identifying automation opportunities in existing business processes. Don't cut early-career hiring budgets as an AI cost-reduction measure — that's exactly backwards.

Lines Worth Keeping

"There were more than 100 million leads that we have not called back at Salesforce in the last 26 years, because we have not had enough people. So we just couldn't call them back."

— Marc Benioff

"It's not any different than being in your Tesla and all of a sudden it goes, I don't know what's happening — you take over. And that's kind of the same thing."

— Marc Benioff, on human-agent escalation

"These large language models are relatively finite algorithm sets with relatively finite datasets — and those two things provide you some functionality but not all functionality."

— Marc Benioff

"Our brains start to create immune cells when we're near sick people. I kind of missed that feature on GPT-5. I don't think it does that quite yet."

— Marc Benioff

"I was going to be the last CEO who was only managing your teams."

— Marc Benioff, recalling what he said nine months prior

"These kids, they're AI natives. They're agentic natives. They're not digital natives. They know intuitively what's possible and where this can go."

— Marc Benioff, on new-grad talent

"Way more aggressively than I realized, customers want a complete agentic enterprise license agreement. Every company is really on a path to become an agentic enterprise."

— Marc Benioff

item.title: The L3 Leadership Podcast with Doug Smith - How to Conduct a Year-End Review
item.description:
The Examined Life: Why Every Leader Needs a Year-End Review

Socrates famously declared that the unexamined life is not worth living. Doug Smith, host of The L3 Leadership Podcast, has built that philosophical imperative into an annual ritual — one he has practiced every year since 2004, and one he believes is among the highest-leverage habits a leader can develop. The year-end review is not productivity theater. It is a structured confrontation with reality: what happened, what it meant, and what you need to become because of it.

The premise is deceptively simple. You don't grow by getting another year older. You grow by reflecting, learning, and making changes as necessary. John Maxwell captured the logic precisely: Experience isn't the best teacher. Evaluated experience is. The year-end review is nothing more — and nothing less — than a disciplined process for evaluating the year that just passed.

Smith has been refining his process for over two decades, learning from mentors, studying how other leaders reflect, and iterating year after year. What he's built is comprehensive enough to feel daunting at first glance, but he's clear that the goal is not to copy his system. The best year-end review process is the one you'll actually follow. The point is to build your own.

Why Bother: Three Arguments for the Practice

Before explaining how to do a year-end review, Smith makes the case for why — and the reasons are more layered than simple self-improvement.

The first argument is the growth argument. Most people mistake longevity for development. They assume that living through another year automatically deposits wisdom. It doesn't. Unexamined experience produces repetition, not growth. The year-end review breaks that cycle by forcing a deliberate audit: what actually happened, what patterns emerged, what worked and what didn't, and what changes are now required.

The second argument is the documentation argument. Imagine being able to return to any year of your life and read a precise summary — the goals you held, the losses you absorbed, the lessons you extracted. Smith takes this further: he does decade reviews, pulling together all his annual reviews when a new decade begins. When he turned 30, he assembled his twenties into a single narrative arc. The result, he says, was extraordinary. You could see the shape of a life — the patterns that only become visible at scale, the growth that's invisible year-to-year but unmistakable across ten years.

There's a third argument he raises almost as an afterthought, but it's worth sitting with: a completed set of year-end reviews is one of the most unusual gifts you could leave your children. Not a financial inheritance, but an intellectual and emotional one — a record of how you thought, what you valued, what you struggled with, and what you learned. Whether or not your kids care when you hand it to them at eighty, the archive exists. The life is documented.

Before You Begin: Setting Up for Success

A year-end review that stays theoretical is worthless. Smith's first practical instruction is blunt: schedule it. Block time during the last week of the year and treat it with the same seriousness you'd treat a board meeting or a critical client conversation. The format is flexible — a few focused hours in the morning, a full day, or even multiple days — but the commitment is not optional.

Location matters more than most people think. The goal is a space where you won't be distracted and where you can actually think. For some, that's a home office with the phone off. For others, it requires more friction — a cabin, a hotel room, somewhere that signals to your brain that this time is different. Whatever it takes to get quiet enough to reflect honestly.

Smith assembles a specific set of materials before he begins, and the list reveals something about how he thinks about the process. He brings:

  • His journal — the primary raw material for the review. If you don't journal yet, Smith recommends starting immediately, and suggests the Five Minute Journal app as a low-friction entry point that takes only two to three minutes daily.
  • His calendar — a chronological record of how time was actually spent, not how it was intended to be spent.
  • Photos from the year — a suggestion he credits to Matt Keller. Photos surface memories that written records miss, and they capture emotional texture that calendars and journals can't.
  • His life plan — a document built from Michael Hyatt's Living Forward framework, which defines what he's building across every dimension of his life. Without a life plan, there's no north star against which to evaluate whether the year moved in the right direction.
  • His goals — written out and tracked throughout the year. Smith writes his goals weekly, so by year-end he has a clear picture of both intention and execution.
  • Financial records — he uses Mint to generate a clear picture of how money was earned, spent, and saved.
  • His bucket list — not just to check items off, but to add new ones. The bucket list is a running inventory of ambition; the year-end review is a natural moment to expand it.

The materials aren't bureaucratic overhead. They're the evidence base. Without them, the review degenerates into vague impressions. With them, you're working from data.

The Report: What to Actually Capture

At the center of Smith's process is a written report — a comprehensive document that becomes the permanent record of the year. He walks through each section methodically, and the scope is ambitious. For a first-year reviewer, some sections will be thin. That's fine. Do it anyway, and build the habit of capturing throughout the year so future reviews have richer material to work with.

Highlights and Lowlights. Start with the obvious poles: the peaks and the valleys. What were the best moments of the year? What were the losses, the disappointments, the things that didn't go as planned? Both matter. The lowlights often contain more learning than the highlights.

Themes. Step back from events and ask what overarching themes defined the year. For Smith, this is often a spiritual category — what was God emphasizing in his life this year? For a secular practitioner, the question still applies: what kept coming up? What did the year seem to be about?

Lessons Learned. Smith calls this the biggest section in his report, and it makes sense. Everything else in the review is raw material; lessons learned is the synthesis. What do you now know that you didn't know at the start of the year? What changed your thinking? What experience taught you something that a book couldn't have?

Memories. Distinct from highlights, this is a comprehensive catalog of the year's memorable moments — funny stories, unexpected encounters, small victories. The goal is to make the year retrievable as experience, not just data.

How I Spent My Time. This section requires honesty. Smith looks at his calendar and asks whether his time allocation matches his stated priorities. Were there too many breakfast meetings? Not enough time at home? The calendar doesn't lie about where your attention actually went.

Tough Questions I Was Asked. Smith actively seeks feedback from leaders throughout the year, and those conversations often surface hard questions he needs to sit with. Keeping a record of them ensures they don't get lost. It's a way of holding yourself accountable to the discomfort of being challenged.

Areas to Grow. Once the lessons start accumulating, patterns emerge about where growth is most needed. This section turns lessons into a development agenda for the coming year.

Action Steps I Didn't Follow Through On. Honest self-assessment requires tracking not just what you did, but what you said you'd do and didn't. This section prevents the convenient amnesia that lets unfinished commitments quietly disappear.

Encouragement. What did someone say to you this year that genuinely fired you up? What affirmations, recognitions, or words of belief do you need documented so you can return to them on difficult days? Smith keeps this section precisely because leadership can be isolating, and having a record of real encouragement matters when the hard stretches come.

Prayers and Answered Prayers. For Smith, this is a faith practice — tracking what he brought to God in prayer and where he saw those prayers answered. The principle transfers to any reflective framework: what did you ask for or work toward? What actually came through?

Opportunities. What doors opened this year that you didn't expect? What possibilities emerged that weren't visible at the start of the year? Documenting opportunities builds a pattern of recognizing them.

Accomplishments. Separate from goals — this is about what you're actually proud of. Achievements worth claiming explicitly, not just logging.

Most Influential People. Smith identifies the top three people who most shaped his year. This section connects directly to one of his most powerful post-review practices.

Goal Assessment. How did each goal perform? Which ones were hit, which were missed, and why? The "why" matters as much as the outcome — missed goals often reveal faulty assumptions, unrealistic timelines, or misaligned priorities.

Stats. Smith tracks hard numbers: speaking engagements, podcast downloads per episode, social media metrics, website analytics. The specific stats will vary by person and profession, but the practice of tracking quantitative data creates accountability and surfaces trends that qualitative reflection can miss.

Books Read. A log of the year's reading, which can also become a record of intellectual development over time.

He also includes lighter sections — best movies, funniest YouTube videos, places traveled, bucket list items completed — because a year is a full human experience, not just a professional performance review. The lighter sections keep the document honest about what life actually contains.

After the Review: What to Do With What You've Built

The report is not an end in itself. Smith has a specific set of practices for what happens after the review is complete, and they're worth treating as seriously as the review itself.

First: save it in multiple places. Hard drive, cloud storage, printed copy in a physical folder. You're building an archive. Treat it like one.

Second: plan the year ahead. The year-end review and the year-beginning planning session are natural complements. The review tells you where you are and what you've learned. The planning session sets the direction for what comes next. Smith does both in sequence.

Third — and this is the practice Smith highlights with the most enthusiasm: write handwritten letters to the top three most influential people in your life that year. Let them know specifically what they meant to you. Deliver the letters in person if possible; mail them if not. The impact of receiving a handwritten letter telling you that you were one of the three most influential people in someone's year is difficult to overstate. It's a practice that costs almost nothing and returns enormous relational capital.

More broadly, he reaches out to everyone who made an impact — texting or emailing people to thank them specifically for what they contributed. The year-end review surfaces these moments; the follow-through honors them.

For those with spouses, Smith advocates involving them directly. His wife Laura completes her own year-end review, and they go out for a New Year's dinner to share what they each learned, what their highlights were, and what they're carrying into the next year. A year-end dinner with a partner is one of the highest-leverage relationship rituals Smith describes — a structured conversation that deepens understanding of each other's inner life.

Finally, Smith publishes his lessons. Each year he writes a post or records an episode sharing the top things he learned, because teaching is the most powerful form of consolidation. If you want something to stick, find a way to transmit it.

The Compound Interest of Reflection

The year-end review is a practice that improves with repetition. The first one will be thin — if you haven't been journaling or capturing throughout the year, the raw material will be sparse. Do it anyway. The act of building the habit matters more than the quality of the first output.

What Smith is describing is, at its core, the application of compound interest to self-knowledge. Each review builds on the previous ones. Each year's lessons inform the next year's choices. Each decade review reveals patterns that no single year could surface. The person who has done this for twenty years doesn't just know more than the person who hasn't. They have a fundamentally different relationship with their own experience — one characterized by intentionality, continuity, and the hard-won capacity to actually learn from what they've lived through.

The unexamined life is not worth living. The year-end review is the practice of examination made concrete.

What To Do With This

  • Schedule your year-end review before you finish reading this. Block time during the last week of the year, identify a distraction-free location, and treat it as a non-negotiable appointment. The review that doesn't get scheduled doesn't happen.
  • Download the Five Minute Journal app and start using it daily. Two to three minutes per day builds the raw material that makes future year-end reviews richer. Even a partial year of journaling gives you more to work with than none.
  • Assemble your materials before you sit down. Pull together your journal, calendar, photos, life plan, goals, financial records, and bucket list ahead of time. Arriving prepared removes friction and keeps the review session focused on reflection rather than retrieval.
  • Build the "Lessons Learned" section first and most completely. This is the highest-value section of the review — it synthesizes everything else. If you only have time for one section, make it this one.
  • Create an "Action Steps I Didn't Follow Through On" section. This is the hardest section to complete honestly and the most valuable. Tracking your own follow-through failures prevents the pattern from repeating silently.
  • Track at least three quantitative stats that matter to your work. Decide what numbers reflect progress in your professional life — output, reach, revenue, whatever is relevant — and document them annually. Numbers create accountability that impressions can't.
  • Write handwritten letters to the top three people who shaped your year. Don't email. Don't text. Write by hand, and either deliver in person or mail. This is the single highest-return post-review action Smith describes.
  • If you're married or partnered, do a joint year-end dinner. Have your partner complete their own review independently, then share highlights and lessons over a dedicated dinner. The conversation that results is qualitatively different from ordinary end-of-year talk.
  • Save your report in at least three places. You're building an archive. Cloud storage, local drive, and a printed copy. A decade from now, you'll be glad the document still exists.
  • Read Living Forward by Michael Hyatt and build a life plan. A year-end review without a life plan lacks a north star. The review tells you what happened; the life plan tells you whether what happened moved you in the right direction.

Lines Worth Keeping

You don't grow just by getting another year older. You grow through reflecting, learning, and making changes as necessary.

— Doug Smith

Experience isn't the best teacher. Evaluated experience is.

— John Maxwell, quoted by Doug Smith

The best system for a year-end review is the one that you'll actually follow.

— Doug Smith

If this is your first year-end review, it's not going to be as in-depth as you would like it to be. But do it anyway.

— Doug Smith

Imagine being able to go back to any year of your life and read a summary of where you were that year, what goals you had, what you accomplished, the losses, the lessons learned.

— Doug Smith

The best way to learn something is to teach it to others.

— Doug Smith

The unexamined life is not worth living.

— Socrates, quoted by Doug Smith

item.title: Manifold - Industrial Maximalism and Its Discontents: Dan Wang on US-China Competition – # 104
item.description:
Burying the Last Comfortable Illusion About China

The first myth fell years ago: that China cannot innovate. Dan Wang, author of Breakneck: China's Quest to Engineer the Future, considers that one buried and is glad for it. The second myth is now on life support: that China can scale but not originate — that the zero-to-one creative leap belongs exclusively to the West, and the Chinese are merely excellent at going from one to a hundred. Wang wants to kill this one too, and he brings receipts.

"Numbers are continuous," he says, with the blunt patience of someone who has made this point seventy-odd times on a book tour. The distinction between innovation and scaling is a Western security blanket, not an empirical observation. Bell Labs invented solar panels in New Jersey in 1957. Bell Labs no longer exists. China owns the entire solar supply chain — polysilicon processing, wafer production, module assembly, and the equipment to make all of it. The zero-to-one inventors lost to the one-to-a-hundred scalers so completely that the original invention is irrelevant. "What does it matter," Wang asks, "if you go from zero to one, if you cannot go from one to a hundred?"

DeepSeek, EVs, factory robotics, semiconductor manufacturing equipment — the pattern keeps repeating. Wang estimates roughly half of the serious people he spoke with on his book tour are still clinging to the zero-to-one distinction. He finds this increasingly hard to defend. "It starts sounding a little bit absurd. They can make all the toys and t-shirts, and they can make the large-capacity batteries for the EVs, but they can't..." The sentence doesn't need finishing.

Engineers vs. Lawyers: A Framework That Went Viral

The conceit that made Wang's book a Financial Times Best Book of 2025 — and that Adam Tooze was already using in London Review of Books keynotes without attribution — is the distinction between the engineering state and the lawyerly society. China is governed by engineers trained to execute. The United States is governed by lawyers trained to argue.

Wang is careful to note the idea wasn't original to him. Bill Clinton quipped about it in 1996 standing in China. But what Wang did was weaponize it — turn it into a framework sharp enough to actually explain how China's government operates and why, and how America's dysfunction looks from the outside. His preparation was almost absurdly perfect: years in Beijing, Hong Kong, and Shanghai analyzing Chinese semiconductor and manufacturing companies for Gavekal Research under Arthur Kroeber; then living through zero-COVID, a policy whose ambition was right there in the name; then moving immediately to a fellowship at Yale Law School, embedded among the people who would staff the Biden administration. Jake Sullivan. Gina Raimondo. Brian Dees. All Yale Law.

The contrast was visceral. Zero-COVID had the mathematical clarity of a missile engineer's optimization function — one of China's foundational zero-COVID architects was indeed a cybernetics expert. The lawyers at Yale operated differently. They were trained to make the best argument for whatever position they needed to occupy.

When host Steve Hsu pushes back — shouldn't scientists and engineers be frustrated with lawyers rather than deferential? — Wang flips the challenge. "Rather than decrying the lawyers for having seized all that power for themselves, I would say, why don't we point the finger at the economists, who had power tenuously, but then gave it up... Why don't we say to the engineers and the scientists, can you not get better at organizing society?" Power, as Nancy Pelosi once noted, is not given. It must be seized.

Wang is not naive about the engineering state's failures. Zero-COVID was a perfect expression of technocratic logic followed to a catastrophic conclusion — 25 million people locked in Shanghai because the math said zero. The One Child Policy was similarly the product of engineering-state thinking. Wang's prescription is pluralism: "What we want is pluralism. What we need are some scientists in the ruling elites, we need economists, lawyers, humanists." The problem with America is not the presence of lawyers. It is the near-total absence of everyone else.

The Industrial Party: An Online Meme That Became State Policy

Sometime in the early 2010s, before the Great Firewall had fully clamped down, a loose movement emerged on the Chinese internet. It had no party affiliation — there are only eight tolerated political parties in China, all subservient to the Communist Party, and this was not one of them. It was more of a meme, a current of thought advocating technocratic governance aimed at restoring Chinese scientific and industrial supremacy.

The Industrial Party, as it came to be known, produced canonical texts. One was called "Study or Wash Dishes." Another, "The Morning Star of Linggao," was online fan fiction imagining present-day people sent back to the Ming Dynasty to industrialize the island of Hainan. But Wang argues the deepest canonical text was Liu Cixin's The Three-Body Problem — humanity banding together under technocratic rule, building enormous navy-controlled spaceships to confront an existential extraterrestrial threat. That is the Industrial Party's political fantasy made literary.

Wang sees this worldview as deeply rooted in China's reading of its own history. For centuries, China understood itself as the preeminent civilization. Then, in a period it calls the Century of Humiliation, technologically superior barbarians — British, Dutch, eventually Japanese — arrived and inflicted extraordinary damage. The lesson absorbed: science and technology are what the barbarians used to beat us, and science and technology are what we must master to reclaim our place. This story, Wang notes, was told before the Communist Party and has been amplified by it into the bones of every Chinese person.

Wang offers a sharp counter-note: "What about the quarter century of self-humiliation that the Communist Party inflicted right afterwards?" The Great Leap Forward. The Cultural Revolution. The One Child Policy. The Strike Hard campaigns. These were not inflicted by barbarians. They emerged organically from within. The Industrial Party narrative conveniently omits this chapter.

The Ferocity No One Talks About

One of Wang's most striking claims, delivered almost as an aside: China embodies greater capitalist competition and greater capitalist excess than America does today. This from a country that still officially espouses Marxism.

American tech, Wang observes, is something like a gentleman's club. Google has search. Amazon has e-commerce. Facebook has social. They don't tread aggressively on each other's territory. Peter Thiel's famous dictum — competition is for losers, monopoly is the goal — is essentially the operating philosophy of Silicon Valley's biggest incumbents, and their political influence is sufficient to ensure the government mostly leaves them alone.

In China, everyone is fighting everyone over everything. Kyle Chan's chart of interlocking competitive relationships among Chinese tech companies looks like a circuit diagram from a nightmare. The result: profit margins driven toward zero, consumers with extraordinary buying power, and a Shanghai Composite index that has traded sideways for twenty years. Bad for Chinese equity investors. Spectacular for Chinese consumers. A wealthy American venture investor living in Beijing told Hsu he simply cannot replicate his quality of life in the United States, even as a billionaire. The food delivery, the services, the sheer purchasing power — none of it maps to what money buys in California.

The irony Wang highlights: China arrests Marxist labor organizers. There are no real unions. The workers have no protection from the relentless competitive pressure the party deliberately fosters. "What sort of socialism is this?"

Preparing Without Eagerness, Waging Without Preparing

Wang has a quote — one of his most cited — that he attributes to Viennese satirist Karl Kraus: "In Berlin, the situation is serious but not hopeless. Whereas in Vienna, the situation is hopeless but it is not serious." He applies this to the US-China cold war. Beijing has been preparing for Cold War without eagerness for waging it. Washington wants to wage a Cold War without preparing for it. America is Vienna.

China grew extraordinarily wealthy under the American-built liberal international order — becoming a major maritime trading power without building a large navy, which Wang notes may be historically unprecedented. Britain, Portugal, the Netherlands, the United States — all built navies to become trading powers. China got the trading power for free, underwritten by American security guarantees. That system is now turning against China. The Chinese are reacting, not initiating.

Meanwhile China has been quietly preparing for the siege: the Mayor's Vegetable Basket Program ensures farmland rings every major city for food self-sufficiency. Energy self-sufficiency is a stated national priority. Technological self-sufficiency — the semiconductor drive — was accelerated precisely by American export controls. Wang has been openly critical of those controls for years, writing in the New York Times and Foreign Affairs that they would ultimately stimulate Chinese self-sufficiency rather than prevent it. He appears to have been right.

The American failure, as Wang diagnoses it, is an unwillingness to pay the high costs required to actually compete. Reshoring manufacturing, rebuilding industrial capacity, funding science — these require sustained generational commitments. "America is not a serious country in a lot of different ways," he says, not cruelly but clinically.

Authoritarianism and Science: A More Complicated Relationship

One of the cherished beliefs of the lawyerly society is that political freedom is a prerequisite for scientific innovation. Free speech enables the productive collision of ideas. Suppression stifles breakthroughs. Wang interrogates this and finds it considerably more complicated.

He cites Harvard historian of science Lauren Graham, who studied the Soviet system and found something counterintuitive: the two most catastrophic events for Soviet scientists were the Stalinist purges of the 1930s and the dissolution of the Soviet Union in the 1990s. Which was worse? The dissolution. When the Soviet Union fell, the money stopped. Labs closed. Scientists emigrated or grew potatoes to survive. During the Stalin purges, by contrast, scientists staggered out of gulags and produced Nobel Prize-winning work, because Stalin funded science lavishly. "When you throw a lot of money at science, you're probably going to get a lot of science."

Hsu corroborates this from direct experience: Soviet mathematicians and physicists who emigrated to the US after 1991 were uniformly nostalgic for the closed system. Secret cities adjacent to weapons programs, where scientists wanted for nothing — food, salaries, orchestras, chess — in exchange for avoiding topics like the Ukrainian wheat harvest. The constraint was narrow. Within it, complete intellectual freedom. Hsu found their seminars ran for hours, fighting to the bottom of every question.

Wang will not say authoritarianism is good for science. What he will say: money is good for science. The Trump administration is cutting scientific funding. University budgets are contracting. China is funding science aggressively. "I think they're going to get the science."

The deeper point is structural. A physicist obsessed with improving a language model's attention mechanism is not thinking about politics. He is thinking about the problem. He can do that in Beijing as readily as in Mountain View — and in Beijing, unlike at Stanford, he can likely get direct access to frontier model training because Chinese universities and companies are more tightly integrated than their American counterparts.

Automation, AI, and the Danger of Magical Thinking

There is a fantasy circulating in American policy circles: that AI and automation will close the gap with China's industrial machine without requiring the hard work of actually rebuilding industrial capacity. Wang is not buying it.

Chinese factories are, on average, ahead on automation. Chinese Tesla workers are more productive than California Tesla workers. China installs as many industrial robots per year as the rest of the world combined. And China has massive advantages in AI training data from its manufacturing operations. The idea that American AI investments — currently approaching 2% of GDP in data center and compute CapEx — will magically solve the robotics gap, the port automation gap, the assembly line gap, strikes Wang as a category error. "We have to be careful not to let automation, like superintelligence, become an excuse for magical thinking rather than doing the hard work of capacity."

On the longer-horizon AI question — recursive self-improvement, existential risk — Wang is neither dismissive nor apocalyptic. He believes the recursive inflection point is real but not proximate, probably five to ten years out. He has participated in war games at the Tate Modern, led the China team against Stanley McChrystal's US team, and watched as both sides, facing the recursive moment, negotiated slowdowns and mutual lab inspections. That outcome gave him some optimism — not because it solves the problem, but because it demonstrates the possibility of the narrative.

He notes that Dario Amodei and Demis Hassabis, at Davos, both said they are in communication and watching for the recursive inflection point together, and both expressed a preference for slower timelines. They would slow down if they could. The problem is collective action: it only takes one lab — one nation, one billionaire with a data center in the UAE — to defect from any slowdown agreement. "It's a little bit like global warming."

Wang ends with a point that loops back to the lawyers-versus-engineers frame. Scientists and engineers need to get better at narrative. The war game at the Tate Modern produced an optimistic ending precisely because narrative matters — because the ape brain responds to enacted story in a way it does not respond to technical arguments. "We are all ruled by ape brains. And so this is why the lawyers are in charge. And the scientists and engineers need to get better at narrative."

What To Do With This

  • Retire the zero-to-one / one-to-a-hundred framework when assessing Chinese competition. It is now empirically indefensible and serves mainly to produce complacency in Western strategy and investment.
  • Track where elite Chinese AI researchers are choosing to work, and ask why. The 90% retention figure in the US is real but may not hold; the Beijing academic-industry integration is a genuine structural advantage that could shift talent flows.
  • Study the Industrial Party's canonical texts — especially The Three-Body Problem as political philosophy, not just science fiction — to understand the motivating worldview of Chinese technocrats and the engineers who build for them.
  • Assess any American AI or automation investment against the specific industrial gaps China leads in (port automation, factory robotics, EV supply chain) rather than assuming general AI progress closes those gaps automatically.
  • Evaluate US semiconductor export controls against their actual effect: Wang's long-standing argument — that they stimulate Chinese self-sufficiency rather than preventing it — is tracking as correct, and strategy should update accordingly.
  • When evaluating scientific productivity claims about authoritarian systems, ask whether the constraint is narrow or totalizing. Soviet scientists worked well inside a narrow constraint; the analogy to China is not perfect but is closer than Western commentators typically acknowledge.
  • Take Chinese consumer market purchasing power seriously as a talent retention tool. Quality of life for high earners in Chinese cities, driven by ferocious service competition, is a real factor in decisions about where to live and work.
  • Do not treat AI as a substitute for industrial policy. Capacity building in manufacturing, logistics, and physical automation requires sustained multi-decade investment regardless of what language models can do. Wang's warning against "magical thinking" is the most actionable policy insight in the conversation.
  • Build or support channels for AI lab coordination across geopolitical lines now, before the recursive inflection point. The collective action problem becomes much harder to solve at speed; the infrastructure for cooperation should be established in advance.

Lines Worth Keeping

"I want to suggest, no, let's bury this idea too, that the Chinese are able to both innovate as well as scale, that numbers are continuous. This idea of going from zero to one as just exclusively the remit of the West — I think is not empirical at this point."

— Dan Wang
"What does it matter if you go from zero to one, if you cannot go from one to a hundred?"

— Dan Wang
"Probably the most underrated part of the Chinese system is the ferocity of market competition. It's excusable not to see that, given that the party espouses so much Marxism. I would argue that China embodies both greater capitalist competition and greater capitalist excess than America does today."

— Dan Wang
"Beijing has been preparing for Cold War without eagerness for waging it. The US wants to wage a Cold War without preparing for it."

— Dan Wang
"Rather than decrying the lawyers for having seized all that power for themselves, why don't we say to the engineers and the scientists: can you not get better at organizing society, can you not make a case better to the population, and try to get that power away from them?"

— Dan Wang
"Which event was worse for Soviet scientists — the Stalin purges or the dissolution of the Soviet Union? Actually, it was the dissolution. Because that was when the money stopped."

— Dan Wang, citing Harvard historian Lauren Graham
"We are all ruled by ape brains. And so this is why the lawyers are in charge. And the scientists and engineers need to get better at narrative."

— Dan Wang
"We have to be careful not to let automation, like superintelligence, become an excuse for magical thinking rather than doing the hard work of capacity."

— Dan Wang

item.title: The Neuron: AI Explained - Can AI Improve Customer Service Without Killing Jobs? Crescendo Thinks So
item.description:
The Word That Reveals Everything Wrong With AI Customer Service

Matt Price has a problem with the word "containment." After thirteen years at Zendesk watching customer service software evolve—and now as CEO of Crescendo AI, a company with over 500 deployments—he uses that single word to expose the rotted foundation beneath most AI customer service implementations.

"There is a term in CX called deflection and containment," Price says. "Most bot companies are talking about containment, as if consumers are some kind of virus that you can't let near your organization." The language isn't accidental. It reflects a philosophy—that customers are a cost to be minimized, a problem to be routed away from humans, a liability to be "contained." And Price believes that philosophy, now being encoded into the first generation of AI bots, will prove fatal to the companies that embrace it.

His bet is the opposite: that the companies that survive and thrive over the next five years will be the ones that treat customer service not as overhead but as a competitive frontier—and that AI, properly architected, is the enabler of that transformation rather than a replacement for it.

Why Bolting AI onto Broken Processes Fails

Price joined Crescendo in 2024 after leaving Zendesk Labs, arriving at a moment when LLMs had just made something genuinely new possible. His framing of that moment is stark: the introduction of large language models represents a shift in customer care "bigger than anything since maybe the introduction of the telephone." Not a marginal improvement—a category change.

But the first wave of adoption has largely wasted that opportunity. The dominant approach has been what Price calls "bolt-on": companies take their existing customer service processes, preserve their structure, and attach an AI layer on top. CSAT scores drop. Labor savings don't materialize because the AI and the human workforce aren't integrated—the BPO vendor wants to sell more people-hours, and the bot vendor wants to maximize AI resolutions, and neither is aligned with the actual customer outcome.

"BPO people, all they want to do is provide more people. Technology people, bot vendors, all they want to do is maximize the amount of work the AI is doing," Price explains. The result is that companies invest in AI bots without seeing representative savings in their labor costs, and customers experience the friction of handoffs between systems that weren't designed to work together.

Crescendo's response was structurally radical: they are simultaneously a technology company and a contact center operation. They acquired a large BPO business—thousands of people—because they wanted to understand, from the inside, exactly what it would take to optimize the full process. "We wanted to know from the inside out exactly how to optimize the product and process and conversion of these businesses to AI." This is not a software company selling a platform. It's a company that has eaten the whole problem.

The Architecture of an AI-Native Contact Center

Crescendo's technical approach reflects the same end-to-end thinking. Price distinguishes between "first-generation" LLM bots—heavily scripted, workflow-driven, where chunks of instructions are sequenced and sent to a language model and responses collected—and what he considers the next generation: autonomous, multimodal agents that can reason about which tools to use without being explicitly told.

The clearest illustration is a customer they call RASHO, a smart lawn irrigation company. A customer sets up the device through an app. The AI agent can check device connectivity via an MCP connection, diagnose what's wrong, switch from text to voice if the customer needs their hands free, ask for a photo of the device to scan, and talk them through a physical reset—all within a single interaction, all from the same interface, sequencing modalities as the situation demands.

"What we've done is we've elevated things up a level further and given more autonomy to the AI. We don't have to give it any more instructions. It's smart enough now to know, well, if I'm asking them to check the device, maybe I should check it myself first. Or smart enough to know that if I'm asking somebody to do something with their hands, maybe I should flip it to a voice interaction."

This matters technically because the bolt-on approach forces companies to rebuild everything for each channel. Build a chat workflow, then rebuild it for voice, then rebuild it for email—because the underlying architecture sequences instructions per modality. Crescendo's agentic architecture sits above that layer, which means a single configuration can deploy across channels and adapt in real time.

Reliability is baked in at the infrastructure level. If OpenAI goes down, customer service can't go down with it. Crescendo operates a multi-LLM orchestration layer that can fail over between models—OpenAI, Claude, Gemini—dynamically. This also gives them a competitive advantage as base models improve: they can route to better-performing models as capabilities evolve, without rebuilding anything. Clients, Price notes, generally don't care which model is running—they care about outcomes, and they trust Crescendo to make the model selection.

Accuracy across the system runs at about 99.5 percent. Standalone AI typically operates in the low-to-mid 90s; human agents are also typically in the 90s. The gap is achieved through a combination of curated knowledge (Crescendo helps clients clean and organize their knowledge base before deployment—bad training data is the primary source of inaccuracies, not hallucinations per se), continuous calibration, and human-in-the-loop scoring. Every interaction is analyzed by AI across multiple vectors: customer sentiment, whether the problem was resolved, compliance with regulations, sales intent. Interactions that fall below quality thresholds aren't billed.

The Business Model as a Forcing Function

The pricing model isn't ancillary to the product—it's load-bearing to the entire philosophy. Crescendo charges per successful outcome. Not per conversation, not per interaction, but per solved problem. And critically: they don't charge at all if the customer experience was negative.

"Whether it's handled by the AI or the human, we only get paid when we solve that outcome, and we never charge a customer if it was a negative customer experience, because we can do that now."

This is only possible because the AI tooling now exists to actually assess outcome quality at scale. For years, the theoretical ROI of customer service software never materialized in practice—companies bought seats, invested implementation time, and didn't see the results. Outcome-based pricing shifts the risk to Crescendo and aligns every incentive in the system toward actual resolution. If the AI doesn't outperform the client's existing system, Crescendo doesn't charge until it does.

Price is clear that this is a significant driver of their growth rate. It's also a structural moat: the ability to make this guarantee depends on having both the AI capability and the human backstop—neither alone is sufficient to be confident enough in outcomes to stake revenue on them.

Where Humans Actually Belong

The human-in-the-loop model at Crescendo is not the one most people picture when they hear "AI plus humans." It's not a fallback queue where overflow tickets go when the bot fails. It's a set of specialized roles that have been redesigned around what AI genuinely cannot do.

When the AI reaches its "acceptable boundaries"—when a problem requires judgment, expertise, or authorization it doesn't have—the escalation goes not to the back of an email queue, but to a trained specialist who is ready and equipped. For a medical company client, that means HIPAA-certified representatives. For a financial services firm, it means compliance-trained agents. The specialization is procured and maintained by Crescendo as part of the service.

The more interesting transformation happens in the non-escalation roles. Price describes a QA manager whose traditional job involved sampling calls, scoring them against a rubric, and filing reports. That work is now almost entirely automated. What that person does instead: they review AI-generated insights across all interactions proactively, and surface business intelligence that no human workforce could have produced at scale. "As a shoe manufacturer, you've got a sizing issue on this line and you're generating these refunds. We recommend that you put a sizing guide on your website." The role has shifted from functional compliance work to strategic advising.

Crescendo describes these people as "superhumans"—workers whose capabilities have been amplified by AI such that they can operate at a level of analysis and insight that wasn't achievable in a labor-intensive environment. Six distinct roles have been redesigned in this way. Turnover has dropped significantly. Price's explanation is simple: repetitive work isn't fulfilling, and these roles are no longer repetitive.

Customer Service as Product Innovation

The most ambitious claim Price makes—the one that contextualizes everything else—is that customer service innovation is becoming as strategically important as product innovation. He grounds this in an example from a beauty company client. Their biggest driver of returns was color mismatch: customers bought cosmetics that didn't match their skin tone. Crescendo implemented AI-powered color matching through their customer service interface. The downstream effect isn't just fewer returns—it's a reduced need for physical retail presence, because the service that used to require a trip to Ulta can now happen through a customer's phone.

RASHO, the irrigation company, is moving from reactive to proactive support: when a customer buys the product, they're immediately connected to an AI that walks them through setup. The customer service interaction becomes part of the product onboarding, eliminating the moment where customers consult a paper manual or search YouTube.

LovePop, a pop-up greeting card company with a generous returns policy, had a staffing problem during peak seasons: by the time a human got back to a customer with a return or replacement offer, the emotional moment had passed. The customer needed the card for their mom's birthday now. AI-first response changed the economics of that exchange—the offer arrives in seconds, not eight hours. TrustPilot rating: from 3.6 to 4.6 in a few months.

Price quotes Ken Chenault, former Chairman of American Express, on the emotional architecture of brand: "Brands are a connection of two things. There's the logical, which is the service the product offers. And then there's the emotional connection. And there's no better place where the emotional gets activated to a brand is within customer care, because that's where the human interaction actually takes place." The corollary he draws is counterintuitive: as AI handles more volume, the moments when a human does engage become more meaningful signals of investment. "If I am allowed to speak to a human, then I know that that's a costly resource for you, and you're really going out of your way to make that connection with me."

The Consolidation Play

Crescendo is actively pursuing acquisition of founder-led BPO businesses. Price's logic: most independent contact center operators have tried to enable AI, hit the limits of their technical depth, and wound up as third-party customers of bot vendors—still in the bolt-on trap. The major BPO players are already showing signs of contraction; TTEC attempted a go-private transaction that failed amid declining share prices, and growth is slowing across the category.

For founders who want to give their clients and employees a future in an AI-native world, Crescendo offers a path: bring your talent into the forward-deployed model, upgrade your clients to the AI-native platform, and benefit from the depth of AI capability that an independent services company couldn't realistically attract or retain.

Price is explicit about not wanting quick exits. He's building a category—"AI-native customer concierge companies"—and expects Crescendo to be its defining example. The five-year vision: "People will look back and say, why did we ever try and do that ourselves? It's a bit like in anybody that's a mid-market manufacturer now, why would we ever set up a production line and tool it ourselves and staff it ourselves when we can design a product and work with a contract manufacturer to deliver it for us?"

What This Means for People in the Industry

Price is asked directly what skills someone currently working in customer service should develop to stay relevant. His answer is practical and doesn't lean on reassurance. First: AI literacy is now foundational—learn to prompt, learn to improve your prompts with AI, stay current on capabilities. It's table stakes, like learning to type was once table stakes.

Second, and more important: the skills that made great customer service people great before AI—empathy, careful listening, diligence in follow-up—are exactly the skills that AI cannot replicate and that become more valuable as AI handles more volume. The people who were copying and pasting macros into chat windows were always in a precarious position; AI simply makes that visible. The people who were genuinely good at human connection have been underutilized by an industry that structured their jobs around repetitive throughput.

AI also acts, in Price's framing, as a democratizer. A brilliant electronics technician who doesn't speak fluent English has been limited in who they can serve. AI removes that barrier—translating, smoothing communication—and allows expertise to reach anyone who needs it. That's not a threat to the expert; it's an expansion of their reach.

The industry's 40% annual turnover rate wasn't natural law. It was a consequence of designing jobs that were repetitive, seasonal, and fundamentally not fulfilling. Crescendo's attrition is substantially lower—a direct result of the job redesign that happens when AI takes over the repeatable parts and humans are redeployed to the work that requires judgment.

The CX leader who spent decades budget-starved and relegated to firefighting—called into the boardroom only when a complaint reached the CEO—is the figure Price most wants to rehabilitate. "This is a new era where CX leaders get back in the boardroom and they get to be innovators." The head of customer support at RASHO has gone from being a problem-solver and people-manager to being a customer experience creator. That's not a consolation prize. That's the actual job.

What To Do With This

  • Audit your AI deployment for "containment" thinking. If your current bot is designed to deflect and prevent customers from reaching humans, the architecture is misaligned with customer outcomes. Map what happens when the bot fails—if it routes to a generic email queue, that's the problem to fix first.
  • Redesign human escalation before deploying AI, not after. The most common mistake is dropping AI in and pushing humans back to the old experience. Define specifically who handles escalations, what expertise they have, and how they receive context from the AI before you go live.
  • Switch to outcome-based evaluation of your AI vendor. If you're paying per conversation or per seat, your vendor's incentives aren't aligned with resolution quality. Push for per-successful-outcome pricing, or at minimum build outcome-quality tracking into your contract terms.
  • Map your AI training data for accuracy, not just coverage. Most "hallucinations" are actually inaccuracies from bad source material—outdated articles, inconsistent agent responses, wrong information on your website. Audit your knowledge base before assuming the model is the problem.
  • Build multi-LLM failover into any production deployment. A single-provider AI system has availability risk. Architect for the ability to route between models (OpenAI, Claude, Gemini) so that provider outages don't take down customer-facing systems.
  • Identify which QA roles in your organization can be shifted from sampling to insights. If a QA manager is manually reviewing a 5% sample of calls, AI can score 100% of interactions. Redeploy that person to proactively surface product and process issues from the full data, not a sample.
  • Move at least one customer service touchpoint from reactive to proactive. The RASHO model—connecting customers to AI-assisted setup immediately after purchase—eliminates a support ticket before it's filed. Identify the highest-volume reactive support category and design a proactive intervention for it.
  • Retrain your customer service team on AI literacy before deploying AI to them. Price's experience is that when agents understand what AI can and can't do, their confidence and engagement go up. Prompting skills and AI workflow familiarity aren't optional extras—they're the foundation for the new role.
  • Evaluate multimodal capability before choosing a platform. Single-channel bots (text only, or voice only) force you to rebuild flows for every new channel. A multimodal architecture that can switch between text, voice, image, and device integration within a single session reduces long-term rebuild costs and handles more complex support scenarios.
  • Consider the emotional signal value of human availability as a feature, not just a cost. As AI handles volume, the choice to make a human available becomes a differentiator. Design your escalation paths to make the human moment feel like an upgrade, not a failure state—because customers will notice.

Lines Worth Keeping

"Most bot companies are talking about containment, as if consumers are some kind of virus that you can't let near your organization."

— Matt Price

"Whether it's handled by the AI or the human, we only get paid when we solve that outcome, and we never charge a customer if it was a negative customer experience, because we can do that now."

— Matt Price

"If I am allowed to speak to a human, then I know that that's a costly resource for you, and you're really going out of your way to make that connection with me."

— Matt Price

"Innovation in customer care is going to become as important as innovation in product."

— Matt Price

"To make AI work in automation, we're being incredibly flexible about where you inject people, and then continually evolving that is a valuable service."

— Matt Price

"For 10, 20, 30 years, they've been downtrodden, budget starved. The only time the board and leadership once talked to them about is when they received a direct complaint as to what's going to happen. AI changes that. This is a new era where CX leaders get back in the boardroom and they get to be innovators."

— Matt Price

"LLMs really create a world of abundance in CX. The first time things are not constrained and businesses can open up the door to the interaction."

— Matt Price

item.title: My First Million - Dumb iPhone Apps Are Making People Rich Again (Here’s how)
item.description:
The iPhone App Gold Rush Is Back — and This Time Anyone Can Play

When Pat Walls, founder of Starter Story, sits down to talk with Sam Parr and Shaan Puri, he opens with something that sounds absurd on its face: iPhone apps are one of the hottest business opportunities right now. Not in 2010, when the App Store was new and everyone was scrambling to publish anything with a touchscreen. Right now. Of the twelve founders Pat interviews every week, roughly half of them are making serious money from iOS apps. This isn't something he read on Twitter or generated with ChatGPT. It's what he sees every single week, in the trenches, in the numbers.

The example he reaches for first is deliberately ridiculous: an app called PushScroll that blocks your social media until you do pushups. You hold up your phone. AI watches you do the reps. Then you get to scroll. It's doing $30,000 a month — and that's the disclosed number. A companion app makes you pray before you can open TikTok. "Just like God intended," Shaan notes. Another tracked your nicotine puffs and gamified quitting — it sold for a significant sum before anyone could make the Philip Morris joke.

The question is obvious: isn't this the 2010 opportunity? Isn't this exactly what people tried when the App Store launched? And the answer is yes — but something structural has changed that reopened the window.

The Lollapalooza Effect: Two Tailwinds Arriving at Once

Shaan reaches for Charlie Munger's concept of the Lollapalooza effect: when multiple independent forces line up and push in the same direction simultaneously, you get an outcome that's dramatically larger than any single force would produce. That's what's happening with iOS apps right now, and there are at least two major factors converging.

First, building got cheap. To build a competent iPhone app in 2015, you needed a team of four: designer, product manager, backend engineer, frontend engineer. That team would run you $400,000 to $800,000 a year in salaries alone. A niche app helping people quit vaping would never pencil out. Today, with AI coding tools, a single founder — even a non-technical one — can build a functional, App Store-ready product. It's not perfect, Pat says, but it's 95% of the way there. Apps that simply couldn't have existed before because the economics didn't work are now trivially buildable.

Second, discovery got a new channel. The App Store search algorithm has always been brutal. But TikTok changed how apps get found. A single viral video demonstrating a weird, visually compelling app concept can drive hundreds of thousands of downloads overnight. The PushScroll founders understood this intuitively — in fact, they understood it so well that they reversed the entire product development process.

Fake the App, Then Build It

The PushScroll story is a masterclass in modern product validation. The founders didn't build an app and then try to market it. They created a TikTok video pretending the app existed — acting out what it would look like to be blocked from Instagram until you dropped and did pushups. That video was one of several they made about different app concepts. It was the one that went viral, pulling hundreds of thousands of views. Only then did they scramble to actually build it.

This is, as Shaan puts it, the opposite of Field of Dreams. Instead of "if you build it, they will come," it's: prove they'll come, then build it. The asymmetry is striking. Building ten apps and having none work might take you a year. Making ten videos and seeing which one goes viral takes ten days. The validation cost is almost zero. The signal is real.

For non-technical founders, this model is particularly powerful. You don't need a shipped product to test whether anyone wants your concept. You need a TikTok and a phone. The founder who did the marketing for PushScroll wasn't technical. His co-founder handled the engineering. His job was to be good at pushups — and at creating content people wanted to share.

Apps Are the New Info Products

There's a frame Pat offers that Sam says didn't fully land until he heard it out loud: apps are the new info products. In the early internet era, the dream for aspiring entrepreneurs — the lifestyle business vision — was the info product. Course on how to start a dropshipping store. E-book about copywriting. Membership community around whatever niche you occupied. Low overhead, no inventory, sell it on the beach. The problem was the people selling info products got a reputation for being sleazy.

Apps occupy the same structural role now, but with more legitimacy. The best categories: health, wealth, relationships, productivity, self-improvement. Fitness and gym tracking. Crypto and trading. Habit building. Behavior change. These are enormous, evergreen human problems where people will pay a recurring monthly subscription for something that works — or even just something that's interesting enough to download.

Cal AI is the canonical example. Zach, the founder, came on My First Million during his lunch break from high school. The app used AI to track calories from photos. At the time of the interview, it was doing $30 million a year. By the time this episode was recorded, it appears to be on pace for $70 million. Built by a kid who skipped fifth period to do a podcast interview.

The B2B Video Gap

Pat arrives with several business opportunities. After iOS apps, the one that generates the most conversation is B2B video — specifically, the absence of any functioning system inside most companies for producing video that actually works.

The framing is clean: every major business function has playbooks. Design has playbooks. Engineering has playbooks, especially now with AI. Sales has playbooks so thick they could stop bullets. But video? There's no playbook. Companies know they need it. They see their competitors building YouTube channels and going viral on TikTok. They feel the pressure. And they have absolutely no idea how to execute.

Shaan's framing here is worth quoting directly: "Video is now the native tongue of the internet. It's the language that the internet speaks. And so if you can't make good video, it's like moving to America and not being able to speak English. You might be able to get by, but it's not going to be easy for you."

Companies aren't just bad at video. Most don't even have a job function for it. The task gets handed to the youngest employee, or whoever happens to own a camera, or the social media manager who was already doing four other things. The gap between what companies need and what they have is enormous — and the willingness to pay to close that gap is extraordinary.

Pat knows someone running a YouTube strategy agency targeting Fortune 500 companies: Microsoft, Figma, the big names. The service is essentially: here are the titles, here are the thumbnails, here is the strategy for your YouTube channel. The rate: $50,000 to $100,000 per month. And there aren't many people offering it.

Shaan adds the adjacent plays: a guy named Josh who niched down entirely into man-on-the-street interview content for brands, doing street interviews in New York on commission. An agency that flies to companies, podcasts their CEO for an hour, then chops the interview into clips as if it came from a real show. "Nobody even needs to know there's no long-form podcast underneath this," Shaan explains. You cut out the whole "get booked on podcasts" problem entirely. You just deliver the clips. Ten clients at tens of thousands per month and you're over a million in revenue.

Pat's Video Production System: The Treatment

Because the conversation drifts toward the mechanics of actually making video content, Pat pulls up his production system — a Google Doc framework that Starter Story uses for every YouTube video they make. The centerpiece is something borrowed from Hollywood: the treatment.

In Hollywood, when you're trying to sell a script, you don't hand the director 120 pages. You write a treatment — a sales pitch for the script. A document that answers: why should this video exist? What feeling will the viewer have when it ends? Why will they send it to a friend?

Pat's production workflow starts before filming. Title and thumbnail first — that's the packaging, and it determines everything else.
