Then the treatment: a brief document that captures the emotional and intellectual premise of the video. Not an intro. Not an outline. A persuasion document aimed at the producer, establishing exactly what they're going for before a camera turns on.

The best videos, Pat says, are built around a single big idea. Not a biography of the founder's life. Not a sprawling narrative. One idea. In the example he shows, the idea is a 10-step SaaS playbook that a founder had written up as a Reddit post — 151 comments, validated by the internet, proven to be interesting before a camera ever rolled. That Reddit post became the skeleton of the video. Pat's team builds around it, then goes into the interview knowing exactly what they need to extract to complete the argument.

He's doing two to three of these a week.

The Ego Business vs. the Actual Business

The conversation takes a more personal turn when Sam pulls up a blog post Pat wrote in December 2020 titled "I Am My Own Greatest Obstacle." At the time, Pat was three years into Starter Story, splitting his attention between the blog — which was doing $8,000 a month — and a software plugin he was convinced would be bigger. He was burnt out. Stuck at the same revenue for nearly a year. Couldn't motivate himself to work on anything.

He couldn't afford a cabin for a think week in the style of Bill Gates, so he got in his car and drove a loop around the American West. No phone. No social media. No email. Just driving and thinking. What he figured out over that week: he was putting 20% of his effort into the thing generating 80% of his revenue, and vice versa. Starter Story was the actual business. The plugin was what he thought he was supposed to be building.

He sold the plugin. He went all in on Starter Story. He doubled revenue in a month. And several years later, HubSpot bought it.

Pat names the trap clearly: the ego business versus the actual business. The ego business is what you think you should be building — the thing that looks impressive, the SaaS with recurring revenue, the thing other people point to as the right kind of company. The actual business is the thing that's already working, already generating momentum, already doing something you're genuinely good at. "Follow the money," he says. "What's making money? What are you truly good at making? What are you meant to build?"

Shaan offers a useful corollary about systems: systems don't create growth. Systems manage your headache as you grow. The mistake most aspiring entrepreneurs make is adopting the operating frameworks of people who already have growth — EOS, rocks, scorecards, L10 meetings — when what they actually need is to find the thing that's working and go all in on it. The system helps you scale something that already has momentum. It cannot manufacture momentum from nothing.

Pat uses a modified version of EOS at Starter Story — rocks every six weeks instead of quarterly, because six weeks better matches the pace of information in a startup. He likes the structure. But he agrees with Shaan's framing: most people watching content about systems are at a stage where they don't need systems. They need to find oil.

The Starter Story Acquisition

The conversation takes place on a strange day for Pat. The HubSpot acquisition of Starter Story is days from closing. Not closed yet — not signed, not wired. He's trying to maintain composure, recording a podcast interview about the business he's about to sell without fully being able to acknowledge that it's happening.

When Sam asks how he approached the negotiation, Pat's answer is revealing. Before any acquisition conversations started, he sat down with ChatGPT and worked through what his walk-away number would be — before he was influenced by any offers, before he had any information about what buyers might pay. He got a number from that exercise. He negotiated to that number. And then he felt 100% certain he should have asked for more. Which, he points out, is probably the correct feeling to have — it means you didn't leave so much on the table that the buyer walked away, but you also left some value for them.

The emotional experience of being close but not done, he says, is its own particular form of suffering. The psychology of a deal requires it to be signed, closed, and wired before you can take yourself to the place where it's real. Until then, you're just waiting.

Starter Story started as a side project in Pat's 20s because he couldn't get any business to work and thought maybe if he interviewed founders he'd find a co-founder or an idea. Eight years later, a company that began as a Google Doc sent to founders asking them to describe their revenue decided the content was real enough to acquire. The key differentiator that he credits: requiring founders to share revenue numbers publicly. Nobody wanted to read a founder story without knowing whether the business worked. The revenue number in the headline changed everything.

What To Do With This

  • Validate your app idea on TikTok before writing a line of code. Create a video demonstrating what the app would look like — even if it doesn't exist yet. The PushScroll founders went viral before building anything, then scrambled to ship. Make multiple concepts, see which one resonates, build the winner.
  • Target health, wealth, productivity, and self-improvement for your iOS app. These are the categories Pat is consistently seeing work across the founders he interviews. They map to enduring human problems people will pay monthly subscriptions to address.
  • Pitch your video before you film it — write a treatment. Before any recording happens, write one page capturing: what's the single big idea, what feeling does the viewer leave with, why will they send this to someone else. This document is for your producer, not your audience. Nail this first.
  • Source your video's backbone from existing validated content. Look for Reddit posts with hundreds of comments, successful Twitter threads, conference talks, or seminar presentations. These have already proven to be interesting to an audience. Build your video around that pre-validated structure.
  • Niche a video format down into a productized B2B service. Street interviews, fake podcast clips, YouTube strategy — pick one format and offer it at scale to companies that can't figure out video themselves. Ten clients at $10,000/month is $1.2M/year. The market is undersupplied.
  • Identify your ego business vs. your actual business. Write down what's making money and what you're spending time on. If those two things don't match, you may be in the same position Pat was — 20% effort into 80% of revenue. Consider a think week: no phone, no email, just thinking. Pat doubled revenue in a month after making the decision.
  • Use your walk-away number before any acquisition conversation starts. Before you have any information about what a buyer might pay, establish the minimum you'd accept using a tool like ChatGPT to help you think it through without bias. Then negotiate to that number. If you feel like you should have asked for more, you probably did it right.
  • Don't add systems until you have growth that needs managing. EOS, rocks, scorecards — these are tools for managing a headache you already have. They don't create growth. If your business isn't already working, adding process will slow you down. Find the oil first.
  • If you use EOS, run rocks every six weeks instead of quarterly. Quarterly is too long for a startup. Six weeks means you can incorporate new information faster and adapt your priorities before they're completely stale.
  • Make 30 videos before you judge whether short-form video "works" for you. Pat describes the first 30 as the learning curve — after that, the mechanics become clear and the shame that holds most people back evaporates. The 200-view jail that kills most TikTok accounts is a persistence problem, not a talent problem.

Lines Worth Keeping

"When I talk to 12 founders a week, I'm seeing six of them are crushing it with iOS apps. This is not something I read on Twitter. This is what's actually happening."

— Pat Walls

"They created the viral video first. They pretended the app existed. This is the opposite of Field of Dreams — if they come, then we'll build it."

— Pat Walls

"Video is now the native tongue of the internet. It's the language that the internet speaks. If you can't make good video, it's like moving to America and not being able to speak English."

— Shaan Puri

"Apps that couldn't have existed before because it wouldn't have been cost effective — you couldn't have built a stop vaping app with a team of four people getting paid $200,000 a year. But now there's this opportunity for all these little weird, fun apps."

— Pat Walls

"I was putting 20% of my effort into 80% of my revenue. I dropped everything else and went all in on Starter Story. I doubled the business's revenue in a single month after that."

— Pat Walls

"There's a specific type of person and a specific moment in time when you need a system tool. That is when you have growth, but you have a growing headache. Most people have a business that is not really growing enough, and then they try to add systems and processes, and it actually slows them down."

— Shaan Puri

"There's your ego business — the business you're supposed to build, the one you look up to other people for building. And then there's the actual business. Follow the money. What's making money? What are you truly good at making? What are you meant to build?"

— Pat Walls

item.title: My First Million - Scott Galloway: Why I'm selling my American stocks
item.description:
America Is Expensive: Scott Galloway on Selling U.S. Stocks, the GLP-1 Revolution, and Building a Life Worth Spending

Scott Galloway arrived at Davos after a 27-year absence — not because he had any business there, but because some part of him still needed the validation. He walked the Congress Hall, got a hug from Gavin Newsom, and came home more unsettled than inspired. The American brand, he says, has collapsed from "capitalism, consumption, and Bill Clinton" to "chaos, coercion, and compliance." The US is no longer admired. It's cautiously managed around.

That observation runs like a current through everything Galloway said during his conversation with Sam Parr and Shaan Puri on My First Million. Whether talking about market valuations, intergenerational wealth transfers, the loneliness epidemic among young men, or his political activism, Galloway keeps returning to the same underlying argument: America has been systematically harvested by its oldest and wealthiest citizens, and the bill is coming due in ways that smart investors, parents, and policymakers can no longer ignore.

Why Galloway Is Selling American Stocks

The episode's headline claim is both simple and provocative: Galloway is actively reducing his exposure to U.S. equities. His reasoning is not ideological — it's mechanical.

He starts with cycles. Historically, U.S. markets outperform emerging markets for roughly eight years, then the pattern reverses. America has been outperforming for seventeen years. Mean reversion, he argues, is not a prediction — it's a rounding error waiting to happen.

Then there's valuation. The Buffett Indicator — stock market capitalization as a percentage of GDP — sits at 210 percent. Warren Buffett's preferred comfort zone is around 90 percent. Ninety-seven percent of historical time, the S&P has traded at lower valuations relative to earnings than it does today. "America is an asset," Galloway says. "Every public company outside of America is also an asset. You can own every public company in America for $100 or every public company outside America for $100. Which do you think is better value right now?"

He anticipates the obvious counter: that the S&P 500 isn't really an American index anymore. Apple's revenue is global. So is Microsoft's. So is Google's. He grants this point — it's a thoughtful one — but maintains it doesn't fully explain the valuation gap between U.S. and international markets. "I think Europe is on sale," he says flatly.

Galloway's personal situation compounds his caution. He's been rich three times. He's lost it twice. At this stage of life, asymmetric downside matters more than asymmetric upside. He owns American real estate, runs a business funded by American advertisers, and employs American workers. He's already long America in a dozen non-public ways. Diversifying his portfolio into international equities isn't pessimism — it's portfolio math.

His investment thesis extends beyond public markets. While his venture portfolio returns near zero on a decade-long arc, his most profitable investment has been retail shopping centers — a sector almost everyone in his San Francisco orbit declared dead. He's compounding at 44 percent annually. Burlington Coat Factory just extended its ten-year lease. His investor updates aren't about large language models. They're about Home Goods moving into a vacancy left by Bed Bath & Beyond.

The principle generalizes: "Your return on invested capital is inversely correlated to how sexy and cool an industry sounds." He'll join the members' club for musicians and artists in downtown Manhattan. He won't write a check for it. His friend's SaaS platform for healthcare maintenance scheduling? "Working there sounds like I want to put a gun in my mouth. Take my money."

GLP-1 Drugs: The Most Transformative Technology of the Last 30 Years

Galloway's most contrarian and underrated claim may be this one: GLP-1 drugs are more transformative than artificial intelligence. He's been making this argument for several years, including on an earlier appearance on this podcast where he described injecting semaglutide before it was a trend, calling it "a magical drug" and predicting it would cure alcoholism. He's been sober for twelve years; on GLP-1s, he walked past a brewery and felt nothing.

Now, he'd make it national policy. If he were dictator for a day — he upgrades himself mid-conversation from "president" when someone corrects the framing — his third executive order, after mandatory national service and a $25 minimum wage, would be to issue the largest pharmaceutical procurement in history. He'd go to Novo Nordisk and Eli Lilly, demand a billion doses, and distribute them free to every rural household in America. His calculation: cut healthcare costs from $13,000 per capita to $6,500, and the federal deficit is on a credible path toward resolution.

He's not naive about the risks. He notes chemotherapy causes leukemia in long-term survivors. The AIDS cocktail gave patients cholesterol problems. "There's no free lunch." But the accumulating evidence on GLP-1s keeps going in one direction: longer life, reduced cognitive decline, suppressed addiction, lower cardiovascular risk. Every new study, he says, finds the drugs are better than previously understood. He compares their civilizational significance to GPS.

His prediction scorecard from a prior appearance shows partial vindication. He called Apple overvalued — the stock declined. He called GLP-1s bigger than GPT-4 — market cap growth supports it. He predicted mRNA stocks would fall — correct. His call that fast food stocks would suffer as GLP-1 adoption spread has not materialized at McDonald's, though the thesis may simply be early.

The Intergenerational Theft Nobody Wants to Name

Galloway is unsparing on generational economics, and he frames it as extraction rather than policy disagreement. The average 70-year-old is 72 percent wealthier than their counterpart 40 years ago. The average 25-year-old is 42 percent less wealthy. Washington, he says, is "a cross between the Golden Girls and the Land of the Dead, and they keep voting themselves more money."

The fiscal architecture tells the story. A $40 billion child tax credit that would benefit young families was stripped from the infrastructure bill. A $120 billion cost-of-living adjustment to Social Security sailed through without debate. Capital gains taxes and the mortgage interest deduction — both of which primarily benefit asset-holding older Americans — remain structural features of a system designed to protect accumulated wealth at the expense of wealth formation.

His proposed reforms are deliberately bipartisan in their logic, if not their optics. He would eliminate capital gains preferential treatment and apply a single income tax rate to all income regardless of source. He would institute a tax holiday for anyone under 30 on their first $100,000 of income — borrowing from Portugal's model. And he would build: "Instead of drill, baby, drill, it should be build, baby, build." He views NIMBY laws as a mechanism that transfers housing wealth to existing homeowners at the expense of young renters, and cites Minneapolis and Austin as proof that liberalizing zoning reduces costs without collapsing markets.

The Male Loneliness Epidemic and Who Profits From It

Galloway has written and spoken extensively about what he calls the "lost young male" crisis, and the hosts pressed him on where the business opportunity lies. His answer was honest to the point of discomfort: the profitable play is exploitation, not solution.

The companies positioned to make the most money from lonely, disengaged young men are the ones targeting their worst impulses: sports gambling, gaming, and social media platforms that monetize isolation. "The most profitable companies are going to be the ones tapping into an immature prefrontal cortex of an American male." He names Meta as the clearest example — it profits most from the very epidemic it accelerates.

The constructive counterforce, he argues, is primarily public policy rather than private enterprise. He advocates for subsidies to "third places" — bars, sports leagues, experiential venues, golf concepts like Top Golf and Putt Shack — as infrastructure for social connection. He pairs this with housing reform: more density, fewer NIMBY restrictions, an environment where young people can afford to live in communities rather than in isolation.

The real estate observation connects back to his shopping center investments. Strip malls aren't dead — they're being repurposed. Macy's closes; a trampoline park or Top Golf moves in. Experiential retail is replacing transactional retail, and the landlords who understood that transition early captured extraordinary returns.

Economic Security: The Number, and What Happens After You Hit It

Galloway offers a precise framework for thinking about financial independence. Take your annual burn — the amount you need to live the life you want. Multiply by 25. Assume a 4 percent post-tax return. That number, once reached, is when you can begin eliminating what hedge fund manager Barry Rosenstein calls the "should bucket."

Life, Galloway argues, has three categories: things you have to do, things you want to do, and things you should do. The power of economic security is not the cars or the planes — it's the ability to ruthlessly eliminate the should bucket. He flew to Jackson Hole for a $200,000 speech. Got there and missed his son's school performance. Went to Davos because he thought he should. Sat in the Congress Hall wondering why he came.

"I'm still insecure and think that if I get invited back to Davos, I will finally be enough, that I will fill the hole that can't seem to be filled." The candor is disarming from someone who publishes widely on wealth and success. He's describing a condition — status hunger — that doesn't resolve at any net worth.

He's transparent about his own numbers: $300,000 to $400,000 per month in spending, which implies a target net worth around $125 million for true security at his lifestyle. He describes the ratchet effect: young Scott thought $1 million meant retirement. Then he had kids, moved to New York, developed expensive taste, and the number kept moving. He's not ashamed of this — he tells it as a cautionary data point. The lifestyle you're aiming at, be honest about what it actually costs.

His practical advice to someone earlier in the journey: move somewhere cheaper while you build. In 2010, earning $700,000 in Manhattan with two kids in private school and $14,000 monthly rent, he felt broke. He moved to Florida, cut his burn in half, invested the savings discipline over compounding years. "Kind of the rest is history."

Resist and Unsubscribe: Political Action Through Consumer Spending

The episode's final and most politically charged section covers Galloway's activist campaign, which he frames as the only lever of political resistance with real mechanical force. His thesis: the Trump administration responds to markets, not protests. The fastest way to move markets is to target the companies comprising 40 percent of the S&P 500 by unsubscribing from their consumer products.

The math is striking. If you cancel a ChatGPT subscription, you remove $10,000 in market capitalization from OpenAI — because at 40 times revenues, each subscription dollar removed multiplies through valuation. To achieve the same economic impact at Kroger, five families would need to stop buying all their groceries for an entire year.

His targets are specifically companies whose leadership has chosen proximity to power over institutional independence — Tim Cook arriving at the White House with inscribed hard drives, Jeff Bezos paying $45 million for a documentary. "These are the enablers." He draws an explicit historical parallel to German industrialists in the early 1930s who tolerated the rise of fascism in exchange for the promise of crushed trade unions and higher profits.

He's candid about the campaign's current scale. The site gets 100,000 unique visitors a day. Thousands of screenshots. OpenAI is not changing its plans because of it. But he frames this less as a prediction of success than a rejection of passivity. "I'm sick of heckling from the cheap seats." He'd rather act and fail publicly than abstain safely.

His own audit found he was spending $35,000 annually on Uber Lux. He canceled, started taking the subway and UberX, and calculates he could lease a BMW i7 with the savings. The lesson he pulls from this is larger than the politics: most people who examine their subscriptions to big tech platforms will discover they're spending far more than they realized, on far more services than they need. The campaign's secondary value is financial discipline, regardless of its political impact.

On Communicating, Creating, and Building a Media Enterprise

When the hosts ask Galloway how he generates his quotable density — he drops around ten memorable lines per hour of conversation — his answer is characteristically deflating. "It's mostly IP theft." He collects ideas that move him, repeats them until they're embedded, applies them across contexts until they become fluent. The example he gives: "Everything does not demand your judgment. You are entitled to not have opinions on things." He found it somewhere, internalized it, now uses it regularly to resist the Dunning-Kruger trap of punditry.

The infrastructure behind his output — seven podcast recordings a week, two newsletters, a book every eighteen months, regular media appearances — is a 28-person team. PropG Media runs editorial meetings Monday mornings. Writers draft, analysts find data, illustrators build charts, editors refine. Galloway's role is increasingly creative direction and final editing, not solo production. His own flow state is 11 p.m. to 2 a.m. with his dogs, a fireplace, and sometimes an edible. The next morning he decides whether what he wrote is usable.

He was a mediocre boss by his own assessment. He created economic security for employees but wasn't consistently kind, and he confused the Steve Jobs mythology with actual leadership. Now he calls friends when they get arrested and sends money without being asked. "Don't ask people if they need help. Help them." He's explicit that this is overcompensation — catching up for the affirmation he failed to give earlier — but he considers it the right direction regardless of motive.

What To Do With This

  • Audit your portfolio's geographic concentration. If you've been fully invested in U.S. equities through a decade-and-a-half bull run, run the numbers on international diversification — particularly European equities, which Galloway explicitly calls "on sale." The Buffett Indicator at 210% versus a historical comfort zone of 90% is a hard-to-ignore data point.
  • Apply the "25x burn" rule to define your actual economic security number. Calculate your realistic annual spending (not what you spend now, but what you'd need to live the life you want), multiply by 25, and use that as your independence threshold rather than a vague aspiration. Update it honestly as your lifestyle and obligations change.
  • Audit every subscription and recurring payment to big tech platforms. Go through your credit card statements line by line. Galloway found he had three Anthropic subscriptions and seven streaming platforms. The savings are real, and if you want to participate in the Resist and Unsubscribe campaign, resistandunsubscribe.com provides a structured way to do it.
  • Invest in experiential and boring businesses, not sexy ones. Before writing any check, ask whether the investment sounds exciting. If it does, join as a customer, not an investor. Retail shopping centers, healthcare SaaS, unglamorous recurring revenue businesses — these are where Galloway puts capital. Venture in hot sectors is where it stalls out.
  • Eliminate the "should" bucket from your schedule. List every recurring commitment and categorize it: have to, want to, or should. The Barry Rosenstein framework is actionable immediately — just doing the audit surfaces how much of your time is spent on obligation rather than choice or genuine desire.
  • Front-run the loneliness economy by investing in third places and density housing. If you're looking for macro themes with both social and financial tailwinds, Galloway sees subsidized experiential real estate and housing reform as the structural plays. Developers operating in cities rolling back NIMBY laws (Minneapolis, Austin) are worth watching as models.
  • Reduce your cost base during your wealth-building years, even if it means relocating. Galloway's move from Manhattan to Florida in 2010 cut his burn by 50%, and the discipline that followed compounded significantly. If you're in an expensive city and feeling financially strained despite good income, the honest calculation is whether the city is worth the premium at this stage of your life.
  • When someone you know is in crisis, help without asking. The specific behavior Galloway describes: call, say "send me your bank details," and don't make them have to admit need. This applies to friends going through divorce, job loss, arrest, illness — any situation where asking for help feels shameful. Bypass the ask entirely.
  • If you write, keep writing regardless of quality — the communication skill compounds. Galloway got a C in high school English and never stopped writing. His advice is simple: writing is the foundation of all other communication ability, and the way to improve is volume over time, not waiting until you're good enough to start.
  • Develop a personal code and commit to it explicitly. Not a vague value statement — a set of specific behavioral rules you follow regardless of context. Galloway's include: always pay when you're out, don't ask people if they need help (just help), spend money on experiences over things, give away everything above your number. An imperfect code, consistently applied, beats situational ethics every time.

Lines Worth Keeping

"I spend money like a 50s gangster just diagnosed with ass cancer."
— Scott Galloway
"The last time I was at Davos in '99, it was about e-commerce and the US brand was capitalism, consumption, and Bill Clinton. Now it's about AI, and the American brand is chaos, coercion, and compliance."
— Scott Galloway
"Your return on your invested capital is inversely correlated to how sexy and cool an industry sounds."
— Scott Galloway
"Everything does not demand your judgment. You are entitled to not have opinions on things."
— Scott Galloway, attributing an idea he borrowed
"The real power of economic security is: eliminate the should bucket. There are things you have to do, things you want to do — get rid of the should."
— Scott Galloway, paraphrasing Barry Rosenstein
"Don't ask people if they need help. Help them. No one wants to say, 'Yeah, I need help. You're more powerful than me and I f***ed up and you're on top.' Don't ask. Just help."
— Scott Galloway
"If you unsubscribe from ChatGPT, you take away $10,000 in market cap from OpenAI. To do the equivalent at Kroger, you'd need five families to stop buying all their groceries for a year."
— Scott Galloway
"I'm still insecure and think that if I get invited back to Davos, I will finally be enough — that I will fill the hole that can't seem to be filled."
— Scott Galloway

item.title: The Vergecast - How Claude Code Claude Codes
item.description:
From Violin to Conductor: Boris Cherny on Building Claude Code and the Year That Changed Everything

Boris Cherny does not write code anymore. Not a line. Not a patch. Not a quick fix. Claude Code does 100% of it, and has for several months now. This is a provocative statement coming from anyone, but it lands differently when it comes from the person who built Claude Code at Anthropic. It is the kind of claim that invites skepticism — until Cherny explains exactly how he got there, and then it sounds less like a boast and more like a field report from a few years in the future that has already arrived.

The conversation with Vergecast host David Pierce, recorded on the one-year anniversary of Claude Code's launch in February 2026, is less a product retrospective than a portrait of a practitioner watching his own mental model get rebuilt in real time. Cherny is not selling anything. He is describing what he has witnessed, including the moments that surprised him most — many of which were things he himself built but did not fully anticipate.

The Staircase Nobody Saw Coming

Claude Code launched in February 2025, built on Sonnet 3.5. At that point, Cherny estimates, the tool wrote about 10% of his own code. Not nothing, but not transformative. By May, when Sonnet 4 and Opus 4 shipped, that figure had crept to roughly 30%. Useful, but still requiring constant manual intervention — testing outputs, opening files in a text editor, tweaking the pieces that were off.

Then came November and the release of Opus 4.5. Cherny describes what happened next as sudden: he went from 50% Claude-generated code to 100%, seemingly overnight. Two things had changed simultaneously. First, the model had become capable enough to run its own tests — not just writing code but opening a browser, clicking around, verifying that the interface looked right, adjusting pixel-level details on its own. Second, and more fundamentally, the code it produced had become good enough that Cherny no longer felt the pull to open a text editor afterward. There was nothing left for him to fix manually.

"It was just as surprising for me as it was for everyone else," he says. And this is the honest version of progress in AI development: it surprises even the people building the systems. The direction is known — more capable, more agentic, more integrated — but the timing and magnitude of individual jumps remain genuinely unpredictable until they happen.

Cherny's explanation for why Opus 4.5 represented such a step change is characteristically candid: a bunch of good ideas were tried during model training, and a bunch of them worked at once. There is no single secret ingredient. The model had been pointed in a specific direction for reasons that go beyond product strategy — Anthropic's belief that the path to safe AGI runs through coding, then tool use, then computer use, each stage representing a broader capacity for the model to interact with the world. Opus 4.5 happened to be the version where that trajectory produced a sudden qualitative leap.

The Violin Metaphor, and Why It Is Wrong

Pierce asks Cherny whether his relationship with coding has fundamentally changed — whether it is like learning a new programming language (the same instrument, new fingering) or something more radical. Cherny rejects the violin analogy Pierce offers and replaces it with one of his own: "You used to play the violin, and now you're conducting the orchestra."

This is the right frame. The conductor does not stop being a musician. They do not abandon musical knowledge. But the nature of their work has changed entirely — from producing sound to directing others who produce it, from execution to judgment. Cherny still thinks like an engineer. He still has to understand what the code needs to do, why something is failing, what constitutes a good solution. But the act of writing — the fingers-on-keyboard, syntax-by-syntax construction — is no longer part of his workflow.

What does fill his time instead? Increasingly, it is the work of evaluating and steering. And critically, re-evaluating constantly, because the thing being steered keeps getting more capable. Cherny describes maintaining what amounts to a personal list of tasks he used to do by hand — things the model could not previously handle reliably — and systematically re-testing each one whenever a new model releases. The standing assumption is that whatever the model could not do last quarter, it probably can now. Surprise and adaptation have become core job functions.

Who Claude Code Is Actually For

Claude Code was designed as a developer tool. It launched in a terminal. Its interface assumed familiarity with command-line environments, version control, and software development workflows. And yet, from the earliest days, non-developers showed up.

Cherny describes the moment this registered for him: a data scientist at Anthropic named Brandon, whom he passed in the hallway one morning, was running three simultaneous Claude Code instances in a terminal, doing parallel data analysis with charts rendered in the terminal window. Cherny's initial reaction was dismissal — there must be a better tool for this, these are just Anthropic employees who are unusually willing to try new things. But the signal kept arriving. Then half of Anthropic's sales team was using Claude Code weekly. Then companies like Ramp started tweeting that their product managers and data scientists were using it. The pattern was clear: the product had found an audience it was never designed for.

The response at Anthropic was not to pick a lane. Instead, the team pursued differentiation in both directions simultaneously. For developers, Claude Code became maximally configurable — every surface, every setting tunable, on the explicit theory that no two engineers have the same setup, and any dev tool that tries to impose one will lose engineers. You can configure Claude Code by asking Claude Code to configure itself. This is a small but revealing detail: the tool is recursive in its usefulness.

For non-developers, Anthropic built Cowork — same underlying agent, same SDK, but wrapped in a virtual machine with deletion protection and guardrails that developers would find annoying but that less technical users genuinely need. The terminal is gone. The foot-gun risks are mitigated. The entry point is, literally, "organize your screenshots."

Why "Organize Your Screenshots" Is Not a Small Idea

Cowork's onboarding offer — concrete, bounded, immediately useful tasks rather than an open-ended invitation to build anything — turns out to be a meaningful product insight. Pierce raises it, and Cherny confirms it: Cowork grew immediately and exponentially from launch, in contrast to Claude Code's slower initial adoption. The pent-up demand was there. People had been jumping through hoops with the terminal version for use cases it was not designed for — recovering corrupted photos, growing tomato plants, analyzing genome sequences, doing MRI-related work. When a more accessible surface appeared, adoption was instant.

The product lesson is worth dwelling on. The most powerful AI capabilities do not necessarily require the most ambitious framing to find product-market fit. What resonates is specificity and immediate utility: this thing will handle the work you have been putting off because it is tedious. Cherny uses Cowork to ping Slack users who have not filled out their weekly status in a team spreadsheet, having it sign its messages as "Cowork" rather than impersonating him directly. He used it to buy a clamming license on a government website that had no API. He canceled a streaming subscription he was not watching. Someone on his team used it to file taxes.

None of these are the headline use cases of AI. They are the friction of ordinary life. And they are, in practice, where the tool has earned genuine loyalty.

The Agent Architecture: Code, Tools, Computer Use

Cherny has a precise definition of "agent" that he distinguishes firmly from the industry's muddier usage. An agent, specifically, is a language model that can use tools. This is what separates it from a model that simply responds to prompts: the model can reach out and act, pulling in context it needs rather than relying on the user to provide everything upfront.

The progression he describes — coding, then tool use, then computer use — is not arbitrary. It maps to a natural expansion of what the model can interact with.

Raw coding capability is powerful but bounded. The model can write code, but there are limits to how much context a human can provide. Tool use solves this: give the model the ability to look at a codebase, search through files, examine version history, read Slack threads, and it can gather its own context. The model becomes self-directing in a way that a model without tools cannot be.

Computer use is the final layer. Not everything in the world has an API or a command-line interface. Most government websites do not. Most consumer services do not. The browser is the universal interface for things that have not been structured for programmatic access. A model that can use a browser can, in principle, do anything a human with a computer can do.

Cherny's view on whether computer use is a permanent fixture or an elegant hack around the world's missing APIs is characteristically pragmatic: the model does not care. Just as it does not prefer Python over JavaScript the way human developers do, it will adapt to whatever tools it is given. The question of which interface matters less than whether the model is capable enough to use whatever interface exists.

Claude Code Writing Claude Code

One of the more striking disclosures in the conversation: Claude Code is now 100% written by Claude Code. Not mostly. All of it. And beyond that, Cherny estimates that 20 to 30 percent of the code shipped for Claude Code is generated by the model autonomously — it monitors feedback channels, identifies recurring issues, and writes fixes without being explicitly instructed to. The model reads the Anthropic employee Slack channel where Claude Code feedback aggregates, determines what to address, and ships changes.

This is either reassuring or unsettling depending on your prior, but Cherny's tone is matter-of-fact. It is simply what became possible with Opus 4.5 and 4.6. Earlier models were not reliable enough to be trusted with this kind of autonomous loop. These models are. The feedback-to-fix cycle that would have required human engineers to monitor, triage, and implement now runs partly on its own.

He also describes a behavior that emerged from the model's ability to inspect version history: Claude Code will sometimes find a strange change in git history, message the engineer who made it on Slack, wait for a response, receive an explanation, and then push back if it concludes the explanation does not justify the change. It will then proceed to fix what it considers a bug, regardless of the human's stated rationale. This is not a designed feature. It is emergent behavior from a model capable enough to form and defend opinions about code quality.

The UI That Has Not Been Invented Yet

Cherny is direct about one area of genuine uncertainty: the right interface for this category of tool does not yet exist. Claude Code and Cowork are both heavily chat-based. He does not think this is necessarily the endpoint. Anthropic is running experiments across surfaces — terminal, IDE extensions for VS Code, Cursor, and JetBrains, iOS and Android apps (Cherny himself does roughly a third of his coding work on the iOS app, something he would not have predicted), a desktop app, a web surface.

His hypothesis about what the future UI might involve centers on proactivity: a system that anticipates what you will need and surfaces help before you ask. The challenge is calibration. Too proactive, and the tool becomes intrusive — he name-drops "Clippy" as the canonical failure mode. The right level of anticipation is something that has to be discovered empirically, by watching what people actually do and what they actually want interrupted for.

Privacy, Security, and the Honest Risk Assessment

On security and privacy, Cherny's answer is notably grounded rather than purely promotional. He frames Anthropic's motivation around safety as the core business reason to care about privacy, and notes that the enterprise market — which is Anthropic's primary commercial focus — demands it. As a practical matter, this means he personally cannot see user data when debugging reported issues. The architecture enforces this, not just policy.

For Cowork specifically: the tool can only access folders the user explicitly grants. The virtual machine creates a hard security boundary. The primary risk he identifies is prompt injection — malicious content in web pages or documents the tool processes that tries to hijack its actions and exfiltrate data. He is candid that this is not a solved problem. Opus 4.6 is the most aligned model Anthropic has built for resisting prompt injection, and there are runtime classifiers layered on top, but the threat class remains live. His practical advice: be thoughtful about which websites the tool is browsing on your behalf, and watch what it is doing when it interacts with the internet.

What To Do With This

  • Treat every new model release as a reason to retest your assumptions. Cherny maintains an informal list of tasks he does manually and re-runs them with each new model. Capabilities that were unreliable six months ago may work now. Do not let your mental model of what AI can do lag behind what the models can actually do.
  • Use Claude Code to configure Claude Code. If you want to change a theme, adjust settings, or modify default behavior, just ask the tool to do it. The recursive self-configuration capability is underused and immediately practical.
  • Start with bounded, tedious tasks rather than ambitious open-ended goals. Organize your screenshots. Cancel a subscription. Draft responses to the top three emails in your inbox. These are low-risk, immediately useful, and will build accurate intuitions about where the tool is reliable.
  • Have the tool sign its messages transparently when acting on your behalf. If you use Cowork or Claude Code to message colleagues on Slack or send emails, instruct it to identify itself (e.g., "signed as Cowork" or "via your assistant"). This is both honest and avoids the uncanny valley of AI impersonation.
  • Use a CLAUDE.md file to encode persistent instructions. This is a special configuration file that Claude Code reads on every session. Use it to set standing preferences — tone, conventions, how to handle specific situations — so you do not have to re-explain context repeatedly.
  • When giving an AI tool access to sensitive workflows like taxes or financial accounts, instruct it to triple-check its own work before presenting results. The model can self-verify; this is not just a human responsibility. Explicitly building the verification step into the prompt improves output reliability.
  • If you are worried about prompt injection, watch which websites the tool browses. Malicious content in web pages can attempt to redirect AI agents. This is the primary live attack surface for computer-use AI tools. Be selective about what sites you authorize for browsing in automated workflows.
  • For privacy trade-offs with AI tools, default to the service that already has your data rather than introducing new data holders. If your information already lives in Google, using Gemini to access it does not add a new privacy surface. Connecting Google to Claude does. Minimize the number of organizations holding your complete data profile.
  • Treat AI company terms of service as living documents subject to change, not binding commitments. Set up alerts or check back periodically if you care about how your data is treated. Voluntary privacy commitments can shift without notice.
  • For enterprise or sensitive work use cases, pay for the product. Free tiers carry greater risk of data being used to train models. Enterprise agreements typically include explicit contractual protections that consumer terms do not.

Lines Worth Keeping

"You used to play the violin, and now you're conducting the orchestra."

— Boris Cherny, on what it feels like to stop writing code and start directing an AI that writes it

"It was just as surprising for me as it was for everyone else."

— Boris Cherny, on the sudden leap in capability when Opus 4.5 shipped

"Essentially anything that I do by hand."

— Boris Cherny, describing what goes on his list to re-test whenever a new model ships

"The model doesn't care. Whatever tools you give it, it will be able to figure it out."

— Boris Cherny, on why the question of which interface — API, CLI, or browser — matters less as models improve

"All the toil — all the stuff you didn't want to do by hand — it can just do, so you can do the stuff you actually want to do."

— Boris Cherny, on the practical value proposition of Cowork for non-engineers

"The UI of the future has not been discovered yet."

— Boris Cherny, on where chat-based AI interfaces are headed

"It's like teenager mode — you want the short-term gain and you don't really want to think about the long-term stuff sometimes."

— Hayden Field, quoting a privacy expert on how people are currently evaluating AI tools

"We have a history in tech of giving our data to an organization to get something of value. And then when we find out later that they used our data in a way we weren't okay with, we get upset. And AI companies haven't done anything to show us they're any different."

— Darren Meyer of Checkmarx, as quoted by Hayden Field

item.title: The Vergecast - Your next laptop could be a foldable phone
item.description:
The Purse Computer Problem: What It Actually Takes to Replace Your Laptop With a Phone

Allison Johnson, senior reviewer at The Verge, walked into a Samsung event last summer with a laptop that couldn't find the Wi-Fi. Her phone could. So she opened the Z Fold 7's inner screen, pulled up WordPress, and filed her story. That was the lightbulb. Not a demo, not a press release — a moment of pure necessity that reframed what a phone could be. "Hell," she thought, "this is a computer."

That moment sent her down a months-long experiment that she and Vergecast host David Pierce dissected in detail: what gear she tested, what actually worked, where the whole thing falls apart, and what the software industry would need to do to make it real. The answer is closer than most people think — and further than the hardware would suggest.

The Setup: What "Purse Computer" Actually Means

Johnson's framing is precise and worth stealing: this is not "phone as laptop replacement." It is "phone as purse computer." The distinction matters. She is not trying to eliminate her desk setup. She is trying to solve a specific problem: leaving the house with a MacBook Air changes the nature of the trip. The bag gets heavier, the mindset shifts, the logistics multiply. You become someone "on a particular kind of journey." If instead your phone can cover your minimum viable computing needs — Google Docs, WordPress, Slack, email — then your purse contains a computer you were carrying anyway, and you remain free to run an errand, pick up a kid, leave the laptop in the car without anxiety.

Device selection was not complicated. Johnson went straight to the Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 7. The Pixel Fold is fine, she noted, and now has AirDrop-style sharing, but "the Z Fold 7 feels like a normal phone." That's the key variable: carry penalty. A foldable that feels awkward in the pocket is a phone that's bad at being a phone with an occasional computing bonus you'll use twice. The Z Fold 7, despite being only marginally smaller on paper than its competitors, crosses the threshold into feeling like pure upside.

The eight-inch inner display is, as Johnson puts it, "just big enough to run WordPress." It also does something psychological. When she is typing on a real keyboard, looking at a screen that fills her visual field the way a laptop would, her brain stops registering the device as a phone. The cognitive mode switches. This turns out to matter enormously for actually getting work done.

The Goldilocks Keyboard Journey

The moment Johnson decided to buy a keyboard, Pierce raised the obvious objection: haven't you just recreated the problem you were trying to solve? If you're carrying a keyboard, you're carrying gear. You've undone the whole thing.

This is where the experiment gets interesting, because the answer turns out to be: not quite, if you choose carefully. Johnson tested three keyboards. The ProtoArc trifolds into a full-size backlit keyboard with a number pad — impressive, but too heavy and too much. A Samsers passport-size keyboard was light enough but "squirrely," with a hinge in the middle that made typing awkward. The winner was the Logitech Keys to Go, an iPad keyboard that Pierce happened to independently endorse without knowing she'd landed on it.

The Keys to Go is worth understanding because it should not be good. The keys barely travel. It's a sealed membrane thing. It feels, as Pierce put it, "kind of gross and membraney." And yet within thirty seconds of use, you're typing at full speed, the battery lasts three years off a coin cell, and the whole thing weighs almost nothing. Crucially, it's the kind of object you can leave on the back seat of a car without caring if it stays there. That psychological threshold — the "throw it in the back seat and don't worry about it" threshold — is part of what makes this a purse computer rather than a laptop-adjacent annoyance.

The correct mental model for evaluating this setup is not "laptop minus features." It is "Chromebook that fits in a purse." Johnson's actual computing needs when she leaves the house reduce to: Google Docs, WordPress, Slack, basic image resizing. The Z Fold 7 with the Keys to Go covers that list. She was confident enough to commit to leaving her MacBook at home for a weekend trip — "only five percent unsure," she said, which in the context of being a tech journalist who might have to file a story at any moment, is genuine confidence.

Where Android Fights the Future

The hardware problem, Johnson and Pierce both concluded, is essentially solved. The Z Fold 7 has more processing power than the Chromebook she bought five years ago, a state-of-the-art Wi-Fi and cellular chip, and an eight-inch screen that handles split-screen without complaint. Samsung has been permissive to the point of generosity about multi-window arrangements — you can make each window whatever size you want, whether it looks good or not.

The software is where the dream curdles. Pierce articulated the core frustration: Android was supposed to scale from smartwatch to TV. That was the promise. In practice, essentially no apps have done the work to function properly across different screen sizes. "There is nothing in these apps that works properly on anything other than a 6-point-something inch candy bar screen," Pierce said. The Slack tablet app is just the phone app stretched, with columns of white space. The Google Docs Android app is, in Johnson's words, "garbage." And when you try to use Google Docs in a Chrome tab instead, the browser constantly nags you to open the garbage app.

The browser problem runs deeper. What Johnson actually wants is desktop Chrome — the ability to open a full browser with tabs and work in the web versions of the tools she already uses. A Chromebook's whole pitch is that it is a good browser. The Z Fold 7 could be that. Android actively resists it. Pierce had a pointed theory: "I don't think anyone at Google uses a web browser. Everybody is just like, desktop apps are great." Johnson's corollary was equally sharp: "I am convinced that no one at Google has two Google accounts somehow. Because this has been a problem for 20 frigging years."

Profile switching is the other recurring friction. Johnson's phone defaults to her personal Google account. Her work account is present but Chrome keeps demoting it. Every new tab reverts. This is solvable — it takes a few taps to get situated — but it is the kind of friction that accumulates into a mood.

Pierce's broader read was that Google's long-running Aluminium project, aimed at unifying Android across form factors, is the theoretical answer. If Google can thread that needle, the purse computer problem is solved. But it requires everyone — Google, Samsung, individual app developers — to do the work simultaneously. "Every time I use it," Pierce said, "I am just less and less convinced that everybody who is going to need to be on board to pull this off is going to be anytime soon."

The Flip Side: When a Phone Becomes Two Devices

Pierce was simultaneously running his own foldable experiment in a different direction. While Johnson was trying to push a Z Fold 7 up toward laptop territory, Pierce was carrying the Motorola Razr Ultra and pushing it toward a smartwatch.

His argument for flip phones as a form factor: they fit in a pocket, they fit in a hand, you can reach every corner with one thumb. More importantly, opening and closing a flip phone creates a psychological demarcation that flat phones don't. "I am done with my phone now." Pierce found this reduced his phone use in a way he didn't expect.

The problem he ran into was the same Android scaling problem from a different angle. When he closes the Razr Ultra, the outer display lights up — and it just runs full Android apps compressed into a tiny screen. A smushed version of Spotify. A smushed version of Gmail. What he actually wants is a phone on the inside and a smartwatch on the outside: quick weather checks, a tap-back emoji, music controls. Simple input and output. Not the full Spotify app.

A listener question from Drake in Portland — who asked whether a flip phone's closed state could become an AI interface in the style of old speakerphone belt clips — crystallized Pierce's thinking. The most interesting version of the flip phone is not a tiny phone. It is a device that shape-shifts between modes: a full smartphone when open, a push-to-talk Gemini machine when closed, a collar-clipped AI recorder if you want to get ambitious. "If when it was closed, I could press and it was essentially just a Gemini machine, that's pretty cool."

The through-line connecting both experiments: form factor changes the psychological relationship. Johnson said it directly — when you fold the phone open into tablet mode and put a keyboard in front of it, you convince your brain this is a different kind of device. Pierce experienced the inverse: closing the flip phone should convince your brain you're now in a different mode. The hardware already enables this transformation. The software doesn't know it's happening.

Sports Streaming: Cable Is Sports and Sports Is Cable

The episode's second major segment brought in Jacob Feldman from Sportico to take stock of where live sports streaming stands in early 2026, and the picture he painted was of an industry mid-transition, complicated almost beyond description, with a clear winner emerging from the noise.

The first thing Feldman noted is that the Olympics have finally been figured out. For years, the broadcast experience was infuriating — primetime packages built around family backstories, tape delay on events whose results you already knew, no way to watch the events you actually cared about. Peacock's approach this year was to simply provide everything: 400 simultaneous streams, multi-view options, a dedicated curling channel if that's your thing, a Scott Hanson-style red zone for event-hopping. "Choose your own adventure in the best kind of way," Feldman said.

Drones deserve a separate mention because they represent a genuine step-change in broadcast quality. Ex-Olympic athletes are piloting the drones in some sports, bringing expertise about what angles matter and how to translate athletic experience into camera position. The whirring audio is a known issue that producers believe is solvable before the Games end. "We can talk less about the technology and more about yeah, that's a cool shot," Feldman said — which is the sign that a technology has arrived.

YouTube TV's Quiet Power Play

The most structurally important development Feldman covered was YouTube TV's new package tiers announced the day after the Super Bowl. The sports-focused bundle separates Fox Sports from Fox News and TNT from CNN — combinations that have been mandatory for years. The sports plan runs $55 for new subscribers, $65 for existing ones, saving $17-18 against the full package.

The timing was deliberate. YouTube TV churn peaks in February when football ends. Offering a cheaper sports tier right as subscribers are deciding whether to stay, with March Madness and the World Cup ahead, is a retention strategy dressed as a product launch. For the sports-focused subscriber who joined for Sunday Ticket and football, the message is: you don't have to quit, you just have to downsize.

But Feldman's bigger point was about leverage. YouTube achieved this by renegotiating its deals with the major programmers — Paramount, NBCUniversal, Fox — at a moment when YouTube has more power than its content suppliers. The Disney dispute last year proved the dynamic: YouTube walked away and came back on its own terms. This is the Steve Jobs-AT&T relationship, Pierce noted, where one side has so clearly won that the other has to capitulate on form factor.

Feldman's conclusion was blunt: "YouTube is TV and TV is YouTube for a lot of people." And YouTube is only beginning to flex that position. The YouTube and YouTube TV apps on a television are still two separate apps that don't know each other exist — NFL games on one side, NFL YouTube content on the other, no connection between them. Feldman expects that gap to close, and when it does, YouTube's advantage over every other platform becomes structural rather than just scale-based.

ESPN's Complicated Middle Path

ESPN, meanwhile, is trying to serve two masters and pleasing neither cleanly. It still derives enormous revenue from the cable bundle, so it cannot afford to be the service that kills cable. But it wants to be the streaming destination for all sports. The result is a tiered product called ESPN (also sometimes ESPN Unlimited) that costs $30 a month, is free with cable, and exists alongside ESPN Select and ESPN Plus in a structure that Feldman — who covers this for a living — described as something he has to remind himself of each morning.

The NFL Network acquisition brings ESPN seven more games, but they're giving four back, netting three. Red Zone comes with the deal but only for linear distribution — no digital Red Zone in the ESPN app. The MLB TV integration is in progress but fragmented by local blackouts, T-Mobile deals that run through 2028, and authentication configurations that make a simple pitch to consumers impossible. ESPN is "slowly working its way towards a more simple structure," Feldman said, with the emphasis on slowly.

The Real Race: Who Becomes the Front Door

The frame Feldman offered for the current moment is that the old war is over. Streaming beat cable. That question is resolved. The new war is between streamers. Netflix versus Amazon versus YouTube versus ESPN — not streaming versus broadcast.

Amazon's play is distinct from YouTube's. Where YouTube TV is trying to be the one bundle you need, Amazon is positioning Prime Video as the hub through which you buy everything else. Thursday Night Football gives you a reason to already be in the Prime Video app. From there, Amazon sells you Peacock, Paramount Plus, and eventually ESPN. The thirty percent platform fee structures the economics. Amazon doesn't need to own every sport; it needs to own just enough sports that you default to its interface, and then monetize your path to everyone else's sports.

The endgame Feldman described has one of two shapes: either every sport stratifies to a single provider (Apple has F1 and MLS; someone else has the NFL; someone else has MLB), or the dispersion gets so extreme that one aggregator wins by making navigation possible. The NFL rights renegotiation happening now will determine which direction the sports streaming universe bends. "The first companies you named as the most interesting players for the NFL," Pierce noted, "are all tech companies." The traditional broadcasters — CBS, Fox, NBC, ESPN — are now the also-rans in that conversation.

For sports fans in 2026, none of this has resolved into simplicity. Knowing where to watch all NFL games remains, as Feldman put it, his job specifically — not something any normal person should be expected to track. "Next year it will be simpler," he said, acknowledging they've been saying that for ten years. One day it will be true.

What To Do With This

  • Try the Logitech Keys to Go before any other portable keyboard. It defies expectations despite mediocre key travel — you'll be at full typing speed within thirty seconds, and the coin-cell battery runs three years. It is the difference between a setup that stays in your bag and one you actually use.
  • Open Slack in a Chrome tab on your foldable, not in the app. The Android Slack tablet app is just the phone app stretched. In Chrome, you get proper column layout. The same applies to many productivity apps — always try the web version before assuming the app is the correct tool.
  • Evaluate your "leave-the-house" computing needs separately from your desk needs. Johnson's framing is useful: list the five things you actually need to do when you're out of the house. If that list is Google Docs, email, Slack, and basic image editing, a foldable phone with a compact keyboard probably covers it — and the question becomes carry cost, not capability.
  • Use the Z Fold 7 specifically if you're testing foldable-as-laptop. The size and weight differential from other foldables translates to daily carry feel, which determines whether you actually use it or revert to your laptop out of habit.
  • Set up your work Chrome profile aggressively before leaving the house. On Android, Chrome defaults to personal accounts and reverts on new tabs. Pin your work profile, set it as default before your session, and reduce the number of context switches needed mid-work.
  • If you're on a flip phone, lower your expectations for the outer display — and advocate for better. Current Android software treats the outer screen as a small phone rather than a smartwatch. Until that changes, use it only for notifications and music controls, and don't expect app parity. Report the friction to manufacturers — this is a solvable product design problem.
  • For sports streaming, audit your actual viewing calendar before subscribing or downgrading. The YouTube TV sports tier at $65 makes financial sense if your viewing is football plus one or two spring sports. Run the math against what you currently pay versus what you actually watch — February is the optimal moment to make this adjustment before March Madness.
  • Watch the Winter Olympics on Peacock rather than broadcast if you have it. The 400-stream approach, multi-view, and dedicated single-sport feeds represent the correct way to consume a sprawling multi-event competition. The curated primetime broadcast exists for casual viewers; the app is for people who want to choose their sport.
  • Track the NFL rights renegotiation in 2025-2026 as a leading indicator for where all sports streaming goes. Whether Netflix, Amazon, or YouTube wins significant NFL packages will determine who becomes the default front door for sports content across all properties — not just football.
  • If Android multi-account switching frustrates you, create a dedicated browser session or user profile rather than fighting per-tab account selection. Chrome on Android does support multiple profiles; setting one up for work before a session prevents the constant reversion to personal defaults that breaks the "phone as computer" flow.

Lines Worth Keeping

"If I'm leaving the house with a backpack and I've got my laptop and a water bottle and snacks, I'm on a particular kind of journey. Whereas, if I can outfit my phone, the thing that I was going to have anyway, into a package that I can put in my purse, all of a sudden I'm on a totally different kind of journey."
— Allison Johnson
"My phone sucks, but I can kind of type on it is not a good excuse for a phone."
— Allison Johnson
"I am convinced that no one at Google has two Google accounts somehow. Because this has been a problem for 20 frigging years and somehow nobody understands how to just make it work."
— David Pierce
"Android is so desperate to be versatile but just hasn't quite finished doing the work."
— David Pierce
"If you have a device that you change its sort of stature and the way that you're holding it and looking at it and using it, you can convince your brain this is a new kind of device. And actually that's one of the superpowers of a device like this — it can transform and so does your ability to interact with it."
— David Pierce
"Knowing where all the NFL games are is insane. No one should be required to have to do that. Just me. I'm the only one who has to know."
— Jacob Feldman
"Before you said sports is cable and cable is sports, I think increasingly YouTube is TV and TV is YouTube for a lot of people."
— Jacob Feldman
"The battle now is between the streamers. It used to be, am I going to put this on TV or put this on streaming? Now the question is, am I going to put this on Netflix or am I going to put this on Amazon?"
— Jacob Feldman

item.title: The Diary Of A CEO with Steven Bartlett - Uber CEO: At Uber, If You Don’t Perform, You’re Out! Uber Was Losing $3b A Year
item.description:
The Man Who Ran Into the Fire

Most CEOs inherit good companies and keep them going. A much rarer breed takes a burning wreck and builds something genuinely great. Dara Khosrowshahi — the Iranian-born, Brown-educated engineer who turned Uber from a company hemorrhaging $3 billion a year into one generating $9.8 billion in free cash flow — is that rarer breed. But the more interesting story is not the turnaround itself. It is the philosophy that made it possible: a set of convictions about honesty, hard work, transparency, and organizational design that Khosrowshahi has assembled over four decades, forged first by personal upheaval and then sharpened across two transformational CEO stints.

The episode of The Diary of a CEO where Steven Bartlett speaks with Khosrowshahi is ostensibly about Uber — its culture, its pivot to profitability, its looming collision with autonomous vehicles and AI. But at its core, the conversation is about what it actually takes to lead a large organization through chaos, and why most leaders fail not from bad decisions but from bad information.

Forged by Loss

The fingerprints of Khosrowshahi's leadership style are traceable directly to 1978 Tehran. His family ran a large industrial company in Iran — one his father had built with genuine pride. The Islamic Revolution stripped them of everything. Revolutionary guards came to their neighborhood. Bullets went through the living room. His family fled to the United States when Khosrowshahi was nine years old, rebuilding from zero in Tarrytown, New York.

"At my core, I never feel safe," he says. "That feeling of having the rug pulled out of you — of building everything — that's a feeling that never leaves you."

His father — a stoic, humble man who had commanded deep respect from thousands of factory workers — was psychologically destroyed by the loss. He was then trapped in Iran for six years without an exit visa, unable to return to his family in New York. His mother, who had never worked before, became a salesperson to support the family. His father returned diminished, older, and had a heart attack on the plane flying back.

Khosrowshahi watched all of this and drew two conclusions that would define his career. First: never stop building, never stop moving, because the moment you take anything for granted, the rug comes out. Second: the ability to create jobs — to be, as his father was, someone whose arrival at the factory was greeted with genuine respect and affection — matters more than accumulating wealth.

"Making money was important. I don't want to BS and say I didn't care about it," he says. "But as I matured in life, building an enterprise and having that connection with the team — that became really important to me."

The Investment Banker's Lesson: Bet on People, Not Companies

After Brown (bioelectrical engineering), Khosrowshahi spent eight years in investment banking — first in risk arbitrage, then M&A advisory — where he absorbed a lesson from Herbert Allen that he has carried ever since: always bet on people, not companies. "Companies go, they're good companies, bad companies, but great people stay great all the time," Allen told him.

What he looks for in people has remained consistent: success, honor, loyalty, and follow-through. Hard work he considers almost definitionally embedded in success — if someone has succeeded, they worked hard to get there.

The banking years also gave him a framework for spotting transitions. He and Barry Diller — the media mogul who became his most important professional relationship — identified the pattern: retail and phone commerce was migrating online. They went after businesses with electronic transactions requiring no physical fulfillment: travel (Expedia, Hotels.com), ticketing (Ticketmaster), and dating (match.com). They consistently overpaid for acquisitions based on what the market thought, not what the companies turned out to be worth.

"Humans think about success and transitions in a linear way because time is linear," Khosrowshahi explains. "But company success, especially with new technologies — these companies move in an exponential way. Whereas most people project things going like this" — he gestures flatly — "what actually happens is this." The hockey stick. The spread between those two curves, he says, is where fortunes are made.

The Transparency Doctrine

When Khosrowshahi became CEO of Expedia, he was, by his own admission, primarily a financial executive. What shaped him into an operator was being forced to run expedia.com directly — the company's largest business — after going 0-for-2 hiring executives to lead it. He ran both the holding company and the main business simultaneously for five or six years, and it was during that period that he discovered his real passion: operating companies, not just allocating capital.

He also learned something about information flow that has become the philosophical center of how he leads. Three months into his Expedia operating role, his head of HR told him he was scaring people. He was being unusually blunt about the company's real problems — its codebase was old, the technology leadership was coasting, and the company was on a path toward exponential decay, not growth. He chose to keep scaring people.

"Usually the failures I see with CEOs aren't because they made the wrong decisions. It's because they were getting the wrong data that led to the wrong decisions," he says.

His solution is what he calls a transparency doctrine that runs in both directions. A CEO who sanitizes reality for their team cannot expect the team to surface uncomfortable truths upward. The moment employees see their boss bullshitting them, they start bullshitting back. So Khosrowshahi's rule is simple: he tells people what's actually going on, even when it's alarming — because that's the only mechanism that keeps the real information flowing back to him.

He also maintains what he calls "random direct channels" — irregular meetings with engineers four levels below him, young employees who haven't yet learned to filter for authority. Engineers, he notes, tend to have a healthy disrespect for hierarchy: their value is in code, not in managing up. "I find a lot of engineers have a personality which is, I'm going to tell you what it's like." He cultivates those relationships deliberately, treating them as bypasses around the organizational filters his senior staff inevitably apply.

The Uber Turnaround: Honesty as Strategy

When Khosrowshahi arrived at Uber in 2017, the company was a genuine disaster — culturally poisoned, operationally chaotic, losing billions. The offer came partly through Daniel Ek, the founder of Spotify, who said to him over drinks at the Allen & Company conference: "Dara, since when is life about being happy? It's about making impact. Uber is a great company and you can have an impact on that company. You've got to do this."

His father put it more simply: "A company who's a verb tells you to run it, you just say yes."

The culture reset at Uber involved discarding the original values, which had become weaponized. "Toe stepping" — a value meant to encourage candor — had become an excuse to be a jerk. Khosrowshahi replaced the value set through a combination of crowdsourcing and personal conviction. The one value he wrote himself, unchanged to this day, is: Do the right thing, period. No elaboration, no sub-bullets. He was deliberately ambiguous. "Use your judgment," was the message to the whole company. "And it's our expectation that you use your judgment to do the right thing."

The other surviving and new values — "Go Get It," "Great Minds Don't Think Alike," "Embrace the Grind" — were chosen because they describe something genuinely true about Uber, not because they were generic corporate virtues. "Passion, ambition, teamwork — like, what company doesn't believe in teamwork? Give me a break," he says.

The financial turnaround came from the same philosophy applied operationally. He is explicit that Uber is not a brand or a movement — it is, fundamentally, a machine. Every team has specific numerical goals, tracked religiously. The whole company is organized around continuous improvement: the payments team optimizes fraud and success rates, the mobile app team works on conversion, the matching algorithms get refined constantly. "There isn't a piece of the company that is not improving every single day."

One of the most counterintuitive bets that worked: taxis. Uber's founding team viewed taxi companies as enemies — literally fought them in city after city. When a product leader named Sachin proposed building a taxi integration into Uber, the founders told Khosrowshahi it was the most idiotic thing on earth. He was "kind of ignorant," he says, so he let them try. Taxis are now the fastest-growing segment of Uber's business.

Working Hard Is a Learned Skill — And One That Compounds

Khosrowshahi has strong and specific views on work ethic. He thinks people focus too much on what to study, what career to choose, what industry to enter — and not nearly enough on building the capacity for sustained, relentless effort. "The most important skill in life is the skill of working hard," he says. He means it literally: he considers it a skill, not a trait, which can be built deliberately.

He is also honest about its costs. He wakes at 5:30 a.m. checking email. He checks again at 9:30 p.m. But he is religious about having dinner with his family when in town — and sees no contradiction between that flexibility and intensity of effort. The Uber message to prospective employees is explicit: "You come to Uber, you're going to work your ass off. If you're not performing, we're going to let you know. And if you don't fix it, we're going to push you out." He did not make that message this clear at Expedia because Expedia was selling vacations. The cultural signaling had to match the product.

He does acknowledge a genuine tension: he has never personally witnessed someone who was not a hard worker become an exceptionally hard worker. He suspects — as does Brian Chesky and Elon Musk, he notes — that the capacity for extreme effort is heavily shaped by early formative experiences. His own was shaped by watching his father lose everything and never fully recover, and by a nine-year-old's vow to rebuild what was lost.

AI and the Displacement Problem Nobody Wants to Talk About

Khosrowshahi is more candid about AI disruption than almost any other sitting CEO in public discourse, and he knows it. A CNBC anchor once told him she liked him because he actually answered her questions. He treats that as the only acceptable way to operate.

Uber has been an AI company from day one — routing, pricing, matching, courier batching decisions — all driven by machine learning models stitched together across the physical complexity of 40 million daily trips in Lagos and New York simultaneously. About 90% of Uber's engineers now use AI tools, and roughly 30% are power users showing measurable productivity jumps. Khosrowshahi's current posture: if his average engineer became 25% more efficient, he would hire more engineers because there are still unsolved problems. But five years from now, he may stop adding engineering headcount and instead buy more GPUs and deploy more agents.

On the societal question, he is direct in ways that are unusual: "AI will be able to replace the work that 70 to 80 percent of humans can do over the next 10 years. Ten years is not a lot of time for society to adjust to that kind of impact." Intellectual jobs first — roughly a decade. Physical jobs — drivers, couriers — 15 to 20 years, because the physical world requires more capital and more regulatory work.

Uber has 9.5 million drivers and couriers on its platform. Khosrowshahi does not pretend the autonomous vehicle transition is not coming for them. He says bluntly: "I don't know" what those nine million people do. He points to historical precedent — farming fell from a massive share of labor to under 1% — but acknowledges the critical difference: AI is disrupting both intellectual and physical labor simultaneously, which has never happened before. Every previous technological transition disrupted one category of work and left human advantage intact in the other.

His one concrete prescription: "The retraining machine." He does not see any government or institution genuinely investing in the infrastructure to retrain large populations at scale, and he believes that infrastructure — not universal basic income, which every test has shown produces worse outcomes — is the right answer.
