People who succeed feel they are creating value. Government-administered income without work does not replicate that feeling.

The personal resonance is not lost on him. His father's devastation after losing his company and his status was not purely financial. It was the loss of meaning — of being someone who mattered to the people around him. "Jobs aren't just about money," Khosrowshahi says quietly, in one of the most honest moments of the conversation.

Career Advice From Someone Who Didn't Plan

Khosrowshahi ended up where he is having planned none of it. His advice to his own four children, and to young people generally, is counterintuitive: stop planning so precisely. "People who have too clear a career plan — they're looking for signals that feed into their career plan. They're not looking around. They're not being curious."

He describes his own trajectory as a sequence of openness to unexpected signals — from banking to Diller to operating Expedia to Uber — none of which was part of a five-year plan. "Before you go out and try to change a world, let the world change you first." The one constant he identifies in every successful person he has ever known: they worked harder than the people around them. Everything else — timing, luck, opportunity — matters, but only if you were open enough to see it and hard-working enough to act on it.

When Bartlett asks what single conversation he would go back and have, Khosrowshahi pauses. He moved from San Francisco back to New York to be near his father at the end of his life, but by then his father was losing his mental faculties. The conversations he wanted — about his father's younger life, his ambitions, his regrets — were no longer possible. He frames his subsequent practice of genuine, honest connection with colleagues, friends, and family as his correction for that loss. "Having those genuine conversations and connections is my way of correcting for the conversation I never had with my dad."

What To Do With This

  • Tell your team the uncomfortable truth before you expect them to tell you theirs. Khosrowshahi's transparency doctrine runs both ways: leaders who sanitize reality for their teams cannot expect honest information to flow back up. If you want real data, model radical candor first.
  • Build deliberate bypass channels to ground-level employees. Schedule irregular, direct meetings with people four or more levels below you — particularly engineers or individual contributors unlikely to manage upward. They will tell you things your senior staff will not.
  • Frame your culture contract honestly before people join. Khosrowshahi is explicit with Uber candidates about the work intensity expected. Mismatches at hire are expensive; honest job previews are cheap. State your actual culture, not an aspirational version of it.
  • When you lose, call it clearly and move on fast. His model from Barry Diller: "They won, we lost, next." Recognize the loss, extract the lesson, stop dwelling. Obsessive post-mortems are as dangerous as ignoring failures entirely.
  • Set goals team by team with measurable specificity, and track them religiously. Uber's improvement machine works because every function — ads, fraud, conversion, courier batching — has its own numerical target. Continuous improvement requires specific accountability, not general exhortations.
  • Deliberately push risk-taking as companies get bigger, not less. Khosrowshahi explicitly pushes his leadership team to become more offensive as Uber grows — because larger companies can absorb more mistakes, not fewer. With $9.8B in free cash flow, some failed bets are survivable. Safe bets that miss the market are not.
  • When hiring someone you've gotten wrong twice, do the job yourself until you understand it. Khosrowshahi's rule: if he had missed a third hire for expedia.com's leadership role, the board should have fired him. So he ran the business himself for five years until he understood it well enough to hire correctly. This is how he learned to operate.
  • Test AI integration in your engineering team now and measure diffs. At Uber, 30% of engineers are already power users of AI coding tools, and they show measurable productivity gains in code output. Start measuring your own team's AI adoption and differential output today — the gap between power users and non-users will compound.
  • With your children and close relationships, have the actual deep conversations — do not defer them. Khosrowshahi's single biggest regret is the conversation with his father he never had because he assumed there would be more time. The regret is not abstract; he works to correct it daily in his relationships with colleagues and friends.
  • When evaluating major career moves, ask whether they create impact, not comfort. Daniel Ek's question — "Since when is life about being happy? It's about making impact" — prompted Khosrowshahi to call the Uber headhunter the next morning after saying no. Audit your current role against that standard.

Lines Worth Keeping

"At my core, I never feel safe. That feeling of having the rug pulled out of you — of building everything — that's a feeling that never leaves you."
— Dara Khosrowshahi
"Usually the failures I see with CEOs aren't because they made the wrong decisions. It's because they were getting the wrong data that led to the wrong decisions."
— Dara Khosrowshahi
"They won, we lost, next."
— Barry Diller, on losing a hostile tender offer for Paramount, as recalled by Khosrowshahi
"Do the right thing, period."
— Dara Khosrowshahi, on the only Uber value he wrote himself, and his deliberate choice not to explain it
"Dara, since when is life about being happy? It's about making impact."
— Daniel Ek, to Khosrowshahi over drinks at Allen & Company, prompting him to call the Uber headhunter the next morning
"A company who's a verb tells you to run it, you just say yes."
— Dara Khosrowshahi's father, on the decision to take the Uber CEO role
"Before you go out and try to change a world, let the world change you first."
— Dara Khosrowshahi
"Having those genuine conversations and connections is my way of correcting for the conversation I never had with my dad."
— Dara Khosrowshahi

item.title: Lex Fridman Podcast - #490 – State of AI in 2026: LLMs, Coding, Scaling Laws, China, Agents, GPUs, AGI
item.description:
The State of AI in 2026: A Practitioner's View

The conventional wisdom about who is "winning" the AI race misses the point almost entirely. Sebastian Raschka, machine learning researcher and author of Build a Large Language Model from Scratch, puts it bluntly: "I don't think there will be any company who is having access to a technology that no other company has access to." Researchers rotate between labs, ideas propagate through papers and hiring, and the architectures are, at their core, remarkably similar. The differentiating factor is not secret knowledge — it is budget, hardware access, and organizational culture. That framing, offered in a wide-ranging conversation on the Lex Fridman Podcast with Raschka and Nathan Lambert, post-training lead at the Allen Institute for AI and author of the definitive book on reinforcement learning from human feedback, sets the tone for one of the most grounded and technically precise roundtables on the current state of large language models.

China's Open-Weight Moment — and What Comes Next

The DeepSeek moment of January 2025 — near-frontier performance at dramatically lower reported cost — did not simply produce one impressive model. It catalyzed an entire movement within China analogous to how ChatGPT sparked a thousand chatbot startups in the US. Lambert observes that DeepSeek is already losing its crown as China's preeminent open-weight model provider: "The likes of Z.AI with their GLM models, MiniMax, Kimi Moonshot — especially in the last few months — has shown more brightly." The leapfrogging is structural, not coincidental. Each Chinese company builds on DeepSeek's architectural innovations (mixture of experts, multi-head latent attention) and the newest model is almost by definition the best model at any given moment.

The strategic logic behind Chinese companies releasing open-weight models is explicitly practical, not idealistic. As Lambert explains, IT companies and US enterprises will not pay for API access to Chinese providers for security reasons — a long-standing pattern in enterprise tech. Open-weight models are a way to achieve distribution and influence without requiring a trust relationship. These companies are, in Lambert's reading, "very realistic about this." The Chinese open-weight models also tend to carry more permissive licenses than their American counterparts: Meta's Llama and Google's Gemma have usage caps and reporting requirements above certain user counts, while many Chinese models are released with essentially no strings attached.

The forecast for 2026 is more open-weight models, more of them from China, with consolidation still a few years out. On the closed-model side, Lambert expects Gemini to continue making inroads against ChatGPT on the strength of Google's vertical integration — owning the hardware, the data centers, and the TPUs means Google does not pay Nvidia's margins, and that structural cost advantage compounds over time. Anthropic, meanwhile, has carved out a durable niche by betting hard on code, and Claude Code's remarkable performance relative to competing interfaces represents a real organizational alignment around a specific use case.

Architecture: How Much Has Actually Changed?

The honest answer is: not that much, and that is a feature. From GPT-2 to DeepSeek V3 to GPT-4o, the core architecture — a decoder-only transformer with an attention mechanism predicting one token at a time — has remained intact. What has changed are the knobs and tweaks: Mixture of Experts (MoE), which packs more knowledge into a model without increasing inference cost by routing each token to only a subset of "expert" feed-forward networks; Multi-Head Latent Attention, DeepSeek's compression of the key-value cache to enable efficient long contexts; Group Query Attention; and Sliding Window Attention. Raschka makes the point with characteristic precision: "You can convert one from one, you can go from one into the other by just adding these changes. It fundamentally is still the same architecture."

The real turbulence is happening at the systems and training level. The shift from FP16 to FP8 to FP4 numerical precision means dramatically more tokens processed per second per GPU, which compounds across the entire training and serving stack. The codebases that train frontier models look nothing like the reference implementations in the Hugging Face Transformers library — they are optimized for massively distributed training across tens of thousands of GPUs, with custom parallelism strategies that have to handle hardware failures gracefully.

Alternatives to the autoregressive transformer do exist and are getting serious investment. Text diffusion models — generating all tokens in a completion in parallel rather than one at a time — promise dramatic speed improvements and are reportedly already being used by coding startups to generate large code diffs nearly instantaneously. The limitation is tool use: any task that requires interrupting the generation process to call an external API (a code interpreter, a search engine, a database) does not map cleanly onto the diffusion paradigm. State space models like Mamba offer linear rather than quadratic scaling of the attention mechanism but have not yet displaced the transformer at the frontier. The status quo holds: for state-of-the-art performance, the autoregressive transformer remains unchallenged.

Scaling Laws: Bullish on All Three Axes

The "scaling laws are dead" discourse is, according to both Raschka and Lambert, a vibes problem rather than an empirical one. The scaling law — a predictable power-law relationship between compute and held-out prediction accuracy — has held across thirteen orders of magnitude of compute. Lambert puts the asymmetry plainly: "It's like that technical term is continuing, and then the question is like what do users get out of it."

There are now effectively three separate scaling axes, each with its own characteristics:

  • Pre-training scaling: larger models trained on more data produce better base models. The low-hanging fruit here is not algorithmic — it is increasingly about data quality, synthetic data pipelines, and careful mixing of sources. Training set sizes are measured in trillions of tokens (smaller open models: 5–10 trillion; Qwen reportedly trained on up to 50 trillion; rumors of closed labs at 100 trillion). Pre-training remains the dominant consumer of compute because you always want the best possible foundation before applying other techniques. The gigawatt-scale compute clusters coming online in 2026 — contracts signed in 2022–2023, two to three year lead times — will test pre-training scaling at previously inaccessible scales.
  • Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) scaling: the innovation that made reasoning models possible. The model generates many candidate answers to a verifiable problem (math, code), the correct answer is used as the reward signal, and gradient updates push the model toward behaviors that produce correct answers. Unlike RLHF, which has no demonstrated scaling law (the seminal paper is literally titled "Scaling Laws for Reward Model Over-Optimization"), RLVR follows a log-linear relationship: ten times the compute yields a linear increase in benchmark performance. The Ai2 team ran an RL phase for an additional three and a half weeks after their November deadline and "the model got notably better." RLVR compute is increasingly approaching pre-training compute in wall-clock time, though with very different hardware requirements — more memory bandwidth, less raw FLOP throughput.
  • Inference-time scaling: letting the model generate more tokens before producing a final answer. The reasoning traces visible in models like o1 and DeepSeek R1 are the output of this process. The "aha moment" in the R1 paper — the model recognizing its own errors mid-reasoning and self-correcting — emerges naturally from RLVR training without being explicitly programmed. These behaviors were latent in the pre-training data (the model has seen countless examples of humans explaining their mathematical reasoning step by step) and RLVR amplifies them because they are useful for getting the right answer.

The practical trade-off between these axes is financial. Pre-training is a fixed cost; once trained, the model's capabilities are permanent. Inference scaling is a per-query cost. If a model will be in production for only six months before the next generation replaces it, it may not be worth spending $50 million on pre-training when inference scaling can close much of the gap on a query-by-query basis. Lambert expects $2,000-per-month subscription tiers to emerge in 2026 — the next step after the $200 tier — precisely because there are users who will pay for genuinely more capable inference.

Post-Training: The Art of Unlocking Capability

Lambert's framing of the training pipeline is clarifying: pre-training is soaking up knowledge; RLVR is unlocking the skills to use that knowledge; RLHF is the finishing touch that makes the model pleasant and consistent to interact with. The three stages operate at very different scales and with very different hardware requirements.

The data quality question in pre-training has become a serious scientific discipline. Researchers at Ai2 use a procedure where small models are trained on different data source mixes (GitHub, Stack Exchange, Reddit, Wikipedia, scientific PDFs) and evaluated against target benchmarks, then basic linear regression identifies the optimal data mix. "If your evaluations change, your data set changes a lot" — which means the whole pipeline has to be re-run every time the capability targets shift, as they do whenever a new modality (like code reasoning) becomes important.

RLHF's role has stabilized as a formatting and stylistic finishing step — useful for ensuring the model's tone, response organization, and personality match what users expect, but with hard limits on how much compute investment pays off. RLVR, by contrast, has no known upper bound on benefit from additional compute, which is why research attention has shifted so dramatically toward it.

The frontier of RLVR research is expanding the set of domains where verifiable rewards exist. Math and code are the canonical examples. Scientific domains with wet labs (biology, chemistry) are being explored by well-funded startups. Process Reward Models — which assign scores to intermediate reasoning steps rather than just final answers — and value functions are areas of active research, though with mixed results so far.

Agents, Tool Use, and the Automation Gap

The gap between what AI agents can do in a controlled demo and what they reliably do in production is the central tension in the near-term forecast. Both Raschka and Lambert describe using AI coding tools (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex) in fundamentally different ways: Claude Code as an agentic collaborator that reasons at the design level and autonomously manages a whole project; Cursor or Codex as a chat interface that helps while keeping the human in the loop on individual code changes.

Lambert points out that Claude Code and the underlying Claude Opus 4.5 model are, in practice, meaningfully different experiences even when you select the same model in both interfaces — Claude Code's environment is specifically optimized for the agentic use case in a way that makes the difference visible. Raschka's observation that he is "not quite there yet where I'm comfortable" with fully agentic systems that touch the whole project captures where most practicing developers are today.

The fundamental bottleneck for broader automation is computer use — the ability of an agent to take arbitrary actions in a general computing environment. Multiple labs demoed this in 2025 (Claude's computer use, OpenAI's CUA) and, as Lambert notes bluntly, "they all suck." The problem is structural as much as technical: models are not interfacing with a MacBook, they are working in sandboxed cloud environments that interact with Google, Slack, Amazon, and other services in ways very different from human usage. The specification problem is also real — it is far harder to specify what you want from a general computer-use agent than to specify what you want from a coding assistant, because the failure modes are more varied and the feedback loop is longer.

Raschka offers a concrete near-term example: LLM-generated figures are currently terrible, even though figure generation is in some ways simpler than code generation. This suggests the frontier of obvious, fixable capability gaps is still wide. Lambert's most bullish near-term prediction is that the developer-as-designer model — where a human manages a dashboard of agents each working on a one-to-two day task — becomes the standard workflow within a few years.

The Human Question: Learning, Work, and What Gets Lost

Both guests are careful to distinguish between AI that removes drudgery and AI that removes the productive struggle through which expertise is built. The survey data they cite is striking: senior developers with ten or more years of experience are more likely than junior developers to ship code where over half is AI-generated. Lambert's interpretation is that expertise makes you a better judge of AI output — you can review and verify it faster, and you trust it more appropriately. But Raschka raises the inverse worry: "How will people learn if you never try to do the thing yourself? Would you actually go through the length of struggling?"

The parallel to education is explicit. Raschka recommends a structured two-pass approach to learning: one pass offline with full focus, followed by a second pass using the LLM to generate exercises, clarify confusion, and extend the ideas. Lambert's approach is the reverse — use the LLM first to lay out the full conceptual landscape of a new field, then go deep. Both agree that the model of "just ask the LLM" without any prior investment in the material produces shallower understanding.

The RLHF-as-smoothing-force problem is one of the more philosophically interesting tensions Lambert raises. The preference tuning pipeline is explicitly designed to aggregate human preferences, which means it compresses toward the center — the model learns to be useful to the median user. This is incompatible with having strong voice, being incisive, or preserving the kind of raw, high-information-density writing that Lambert describes as characteristic of good research communication. "It's going to be hard for a model to be very incisive when there's that filter in it." The same filter that makes models safe and pleasant to interact with makes them, in some fundamental sense, less interesting.

AGI, ASI, and the Definitional Muddle

The AGI discussion lands in a pragmatic place. Lambert's working definition — when AI can replace any remote worker, taking in information and solving digital tasks autonomously — is more useful than the philosophical definitions because it identifies a specific capability gap (continual learning from feedback, the ability to update from mistakes as a human employee would) that current systems lack. His counter-argument to his own framing is that in-context learning, providing models with extensive personal context, may produce the appearance of personalization without requiring weight updates — and that may be sufficient for most use cases.

The economic impact question is separated from the AGI question. As Lambert observes, there has not yet been an obvious GDP-level jump from LLM deployment. The more realistic near-term impact is "making all of human knowledge accessible to the entire world" — an enormous, quiet force that won't show up in quarterly reports but will, over decades, drive the kind of diffuse innovation that produces new companies, new sciences, and new ways of solving problems. Raschka's example: an LLM can provide customized, on-demand answers to "which Disneyland tickets should I buy given my specific travel dates and preferences" in a way that no existing resource — not search, not a travel agent, not a guidebook — can match. Multiplied across billions of such queries, this is genuinely transformative.

On timelines, both guests resist specific predictions while acknowledging that the pace of progress in 2025 was faster than most expected. The reasonable near-term bets are domain-specific AlphaFold moments — AI systems with wet lab access proposing and testing scientific hypotheses — and continued dramatic improvement in software development productivity. The one structural bet Lambert makes for 2026: the AI coding tools that seem like magic today will be obviously limited compared to what ships by year end.

What To Do With This

  • Build a small LLM from scratch before you try to fine-tune or deploy a large one. Raschka's recommendation is specific: start with a GPT-2-scale model (~120M parameters) that fits on one GPU, implement the forward pass, the attention mechanism, and the training loop yourself. This gives you a reference implementation you can actually verify — the code either runs or it doesn't — and lets you develop genuine intuition for what happens when you add MoE, change the attention type, or modify the normalization scheme.
  • Use the Hugging Face model hub config files to reverse-engineer architectures you want to understand. Every open-weight model on Hugging Face has a human-readable config.json that lists the exact architecture choices — number of layers, attention heads, vocabulary size, activation function. Load the config, start from your GPT-2 implementation, add the differences one at a time, then verify your implementation reproduces the same outputs as the reference Transformers library implementation. This is faster than reading the paper and more reliable than reading the code cold.
  • If you are an ML researcher with limited compute, focus on evaluation rather than training. Lambert is explicit about the asymmetry: if you can identify a capability gap that frontier models have — something Claude 4.5 or GPT-5 consistently fails at — and build a rigorous evaluation for it, the frontier labs will pick it up and your name will be in the next model's release blog post. This requires zero training compute and has potentially large career impact. The key is being narrow enough that you can actually establish ground truth.
  • When using AI coding tools, match the tool to your comfort with the agentic level. Raschka uses Codex (VS Code plugin, chat interface, human reviews each change) for most work and reserves Claude Code (full agentic, manages the whole project) for cases where he wants to practice reasoning about design rather than implementation. This is not a hierarchy — it is a spectrum. Start with the more constrained tool and expand autonomy as you build trust in the system's failure modes.
  • Adopt a two-pass learning strategy when using LLMs to study technical material. First pass: read or engage with the primary source without the LLM, taking notes, letting confusion accumulate. Second pass: use the LLM to resolve specific confusions, generate example problems, check your understanding, and extend the ideas. The key is that the LLM functions as an enrichment tool rather than a replacement for the initial engagement, which preserves the productive struggle that builds durable expertise.
  • Sandbox your AI agent interactions until you understand the failure modes. Tool use — giving an LLM access to your file system, your email, your shell — is still early enough that the failure modes are novel and sometimes destructive. Raschka's point about containerization is practical advice: run tool-capable models in isolated environments (Docker, a dedicated VM, a cloud sandbox) before you trust them anywhere near production data or important accounts. The gains from tool use are real; the risks from unsupervised tool use are also real.
  • Invest in domain-specific fine-tuning for your industry if you have proprietary data. Raschka's prediction about pharmaceutical, legal, and financial companies building in-house models on proprietary data is not speculation — it is the obvious next step for any organization whose competitive advantage is its internal knowledge base. The playbook is: hire someone who has worked at a frontier lab, use LoRA (low-rank adaptation) to fine-tune an open-weight base model, and keep the weights on-premises. This is becoming feasible at smaller and smaller scales.
  • Do not conflate RLHF and RLVR when thinking about what post-training can and cannot do. RLHF (reinforcement learning from human feedback) shapes style, tone, and safety behaviors — it saturates relatively quickly and does not follow a clean scaling law. RLVR (reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards) unlocks reasoning capability and follows a log-linear scaling law with no known upper bound. If you are evaluating a model's reasoning improvement, the relevant question is how much RLVR compute was allocated, not how much RLHF was done.
  • Track Chinese open-weight model releases alongside American ones. The practical advice from Lambert: use OpenRouter or a similar multi-provider inference platform to actually run Kimi K2, Qwen 3, and MiniMax models alongside Claude, GPT, and Gemini on your specific tasks. The benchmark gap has closed substantially, and the licensing terms are often more permissive. The models are slower (serving infrastructure is leaner) and the platforms are less polished, but the underlying model quality warrants direct evaluation rather than reliance on benchmark charts.

Lines Worth Keeping

"I don't think there will be any company who is having access to a technology that no other company has access to. The differentiating factor will be budget and hardware constraints. The ideas will not be proprietary."

— Sebastian Raschka
"There's no scaling law for RLHF where if you log-increase the compute you get some performance. In fact, the seminal scaling paper for RLHF is called 'Scaling Laws for Reward Model Over-Optimization.' That is a big line to draw with RLVR and the methods we have now."

— Nathan Lambert
"The aha moment in the R1 paper — the model itself recognized it made a mistake and then said, 'I did something wrong, let me try again.' And I think that's just so cool that this falls out of just giving it the correct answer and having it figure out how to do it."

— Sebastian Raschka
"If you have a lab that is trying to do RLVR in scientific domains and they have hundreds of millions of dollars of funding and wet labs where they're having language models propose hypotheses that are tested in the real world — maybe they're early by six months and they make it, or maybe they're early by eight years. You don't really know."

— Nathan Lambert
"We are not actually realizing that it's a gigantic thing that's hard to measure — making all of human knowledge accessible to the entire world. I feel like I can basically ask an LLM anything and get an answer, and it's doing less and less hallucination. Nobody's really talking about that because they just immediately take it for granted that this is awesome."

— Lex Fridman
"I'm continuously disappointed how bad LLMs are at really getting to the core insights — which is what a great summary does. Even with extensive, elaborate prompts where I'm really trying to dig for the insights, it's still not quite there. Particularly, they're all trained with RLHF, which is designed to average how the model behaves. It's going to be hard for a model to be very incisive when there's that filter in it."

— Nathan Lambert
"If you use an LLM to do all your coding for me, now there's no coding — I'm just managing something that is coding for me. Two years later, if I just do that eight hours a day, do I feel fulfilled still? Am I still proud to build something?"

— Sebastian Raschka
"DeepSeek kicked off a movement within China similar to how ChatGPT kicked off a movement in the US where everything had a chatbot. There's now tons of tech companies in China releasing very strong frontier open-weight models to the point where DeepSeek is kind of losing its crown — and that could look back as a big narrative point where DeepSeek came and then provided this platform for way more Chinese companies."

— Nathan Lambert

item.title: How I AI - How to build your own AI developer tools with Claude Code | CJ Hess (Tenex)
item.description:
The Engineer Who Builds His Own Tools

Most developers using AI coding assistants are consumers. They open a chat window, describe what they want, and accept whatever comes back. CJ Hess, a software engineer at Tenex, has taken a different approach: he treats his AI workflow as a software project in its own right — one worth designing, iterating on, and continuously shipping improvements to. The result is a personalized engineering environment that grows more capable the more he uses it, and a philosophy about how serious developers should think about AI tooling that deserves close attention.

The core of CJ's thesis is simple but counterintuitive in an era dominated by SaaS: when you are capable of building something yourself, and the thing you need is a developer tool, you almost certainly should build it. "I used to be when I ran these big product and engineering orgs, they used to ask me build versus buy," he told host Claire Vo on the How I AI podcast. "And I was like, oh my God, please just buy it. Let's not waste our time. And now I've flipped to, of course, we should build this. Until we hit some constraint, we should build it."

That flip is not ideological. It is economic and practical. The cost of spinning up a working V1 of a custom tool has collapsed to the point where the evaluation overhead of finding, procuring, and configuring a third-party solution now exceeds the cost of building. This is especially true for developer tooling, where the users — engineers — are already capable of forking, extending, and maintaining what they build.

Why Claude Code, and Why It Matters That It Feels Steerable

CJ has been using Claude Code since roughly May of the prior year, well before Anthropic's Opus 3.5 became a watershed moment for many developers. His reason for sticking with it over alternatives like Cursor or OpenAI's Codex is not primarily about raw model capability. He's candid that GPT-4.1 / GPT-5 may edge out Claude on pure intelligence benchmarks. What keeps him in Claude Code is something harder to quantify: intent understanding.

"When I want it to dig deep, it just does it," he explained. "It feels like it picks up on my intuition just in the prompts." This steerability — the sense that the model is attending to what you actually want rather than what you literally said — turns out to matter enormously in an agentic loop where you are not reviewing every line the model writes. A model that consistently undershoots or overshoots your intent creates correction overhead that compounds across a session. A model that reliably interprets intent correctly compounds in the other direction.

The practical consequence is that CJ has built an ecosystem of custom skills and tools around Claude Code that assume a high baseline of intent understanding. Those tools would be much harder to maintain if he had to over-specify every instruction to compensate for a model that needed more hand-holding.

Skills as Living Documentation

The foundational concept in CJ's workflow is the skill — a Markdown file that gives Claude structured instructions for performing a specific task. Skills are not exotic. They are just documentation, written for an AI audience rather than a human one. But the way CJ manages them reveals a mature philosophy about how to work with AI systems over time.

The key insight is that skills are living artifacts. They are not written once and filed away. They evolve in response to failures. When Claude produces a bad output while using a skill — wrong colors, misaligned shapes, violated spacing rules — CJ treats that as a feedback signal and updates the skill. "If I'm working with this and something goes wrong, I would essentially hop into the place where I have these skills and say, here's what happened, give me a suggestion on how to improve this skill so this doesn't happen again."

This loop — use the skill, observe the failure, update the skill — is how he has progressively encoded his own preferences and domain knowledge into Claude's behavior without touching the underlying model. The skill becomes a repository of hard-won heuristics, a form of institutional memory that makes Claude increasingly good at the specific things CJ needs it to do.

He also has a meta-skill: a skill for writing skills. This closes the loop further, letting Claude help maintain the very system through which CJ controls Claude. "There's docs for people and there's docs for agents," he noted, "and those just end up being skills."

The structure he recommends for a well-formed skill includes: a clear purpose and name, a quickstart section, a schema or template defining the operating framework, customization options, and concrete examples of what good output looks like. Pre-flight checks — a list of requirements that must be satisfied before the skill executes — are valuable for workflows like Git operations where doing the wrong thing at the wrong time has real consequences.

Flowy: From ASCII Frustration to a Self-Built Visual Planning Tool

The most concrete expression of CJ's build-your-own philosophy is Flowy, a planning and visualization tool he built for himself using Claude Code — a tool designed to make working with Claude Code better. The meta-circularity is intentional.

The origin story starts with a legitimate pain point. CJ, like many developers, had adopted the practice of iterating on plans in Markdown before writing any code. This works well as a discipline. But Markdown plans have a representation problem: when Claude generates ASCII flowcharts and diagrams to illustrate system behavior, they are technically correct but cognitively expensive to read. "I really hate staring at these ASCII diagrams," he said. Mermaid diagrams were an improvement but still felt like a constraint — you are limited to what Mermaid knows how to render, and extending it requires going outside your own system.

Flowy solves this by giving Claude a JSON-based schema for describing flowcharts and UI mockups, then rendering those JSON files as visual diagrams in a browser-based editor. Claude writes JSON. Flowy renders visuals. The human reads visuals and edits them directly in the interface, which writes back to the JSON file. Claude reads the updated JSON. This creates a shared substrate — code, specifically JSON — through which human and AI can communicate about visual design without either party needing to parse the other's native format.

"It's almost like I want to see it visually and Claude wants to see it as Markdown," CJ observed, "so we can kind of speak in our own way."

The Flowy editor functions as a primitive Figma or Excalidraw — drag boxes around, add notes, change timing parameters — with the key difference that every edit persists to a file that Claude can read natively. Want to change an animation from three seconds to four? Edit the timing node in Flowy, drop a note, point Claude back at the file. The human-to-AI handoff becomes seamless because the artifact they are collaborating on is a file, not a screenshot or a verbal description.

Flowy also handles UI mockups at a level of fidelity between wireframe and comp — enough to communicate layout intent and state variations without requiring design tooling. CJ demonstrated this live: asked Claude to plan a spinning wheel feature for a tips-and-tricks screen, Claude used the Flowy flowchart skill to generate an animation timing sequence diagram and a user flow diagram, then used the Flowy UI mockup skill to generate mockups of the multiple states (idle, spinning, result revealed). The entire visual planning phase took minutes. The subsequent build — kicked off with a prompt as short as "based on the flow charts and the mock ups, build this feature" — produced working code that matched the mocks closely on the first pass.

The Multi-Model Review Workflow

One of the more distinctive elements of CJ's practice is using a second AI model — specifically OpenAI's Codex / GPT-4.1 — to review code that Claude has written. This is not about distrust of Claude. It is about what happens when you assign a model a different role than code generation.

The observation that drives this workflow is that the model generating code and the model reviewing code have different failure modes. Claude, CJ finds, is sometimes too eager — it implements what you asked for, but in doing so it makes local decisions that are inconsistent with the broader architecture, duplicates logic that already exists elsewhere, or reaches for a pattern that works but does not fit the codebase as cleanly as an alternative would. Codex, in his experience, is less impressive at writing code from scratch but performs well as a staff-engineer-style reviewer: it reads the diff with fresh eyes and surfaces architectural issues that the author — human or AI — is too close to see.

His review prompt has four buckets: First, does the code accurately reflect the plan or diagram artifacts? Second, are there any general code smells? Third, if you were to refactor this with a different approach, what would improve the codebase overall? The fourth, implicit bucket is catching the vibe-coding anti-patterns: the proliferation of duplicate utility functions, the useEffect callback that should not be there, the component that grew too large because it was easier to keep adding than to restructure.

"Codex is like a really good curmudgeonly staff engineer that will look at your code and tell you what's wrong with it," he said. The live demonstration bore this out: Codex flagged an angular mismatch between the spinner wheel's rotation logic and the dot positions, meaning the arrow was landing between tips rather than on them — a subtle bug that neither CJ nor Claude had caught and that would have been annoying to debug after the fact.

The broader principle here is model-to-model evaluation as a quality gate. Different models have different priors and different blind spots. Running your output through a second model that has not been immersed in your codebase and your session context adds a form of independence to the review that a human skimming the diff might not provide.

Running Dangerously (With Guardrails)

CJ operates Claude Code in bypassed-permissions mode — what he calls "Kevin mode" — as his default. He has named different permission configurations as shell aliases, so the level of autonomy granted to Claude is a one-word command. This is not recklessness. It reflects a considered position about where the real guardrails should live.

His reasoning: for solo projects or well-defined team projects with proper Git hygiene, the version control system is the safety net. If Claude does something wrong, you revert. The overhead of confirmation prompts on every tool use slows the agentic loop significantly without providing commensurate protection. The times he still does things by hand — like crafting a PR — are handled by skills that run preflight checks before pushing anything upstream.

He also sketched a near-future pattern he finds genuinely interesting: on-demand software. The idea is that rather than building a permanent tool like Flowy, an AI agent could spin up a throwaway visualization app mid-session — "we're not on the same page, I just added an app for you to visualize this, go to this URL and look at it, does this look right, and then we'll just delete that app." The collaborative artifact becomes ephemeral, generated on demand, discarded when it has served its purpose.

The Shifting Economics of Build vs. Buy

Running through the entire conversation is a quiet argument about the economics of software tooling in the AI era. CJ and Claire both articulated versions of the same observation: the cost curve for building custom tools has shifted so dramatically that the build-vs-buy decision now defaults to build for a much wider class of problems than it did even two years ago.

This is not purely about the tools being cheaper to build. It is also about the quality ceiling. When you build your own tool, you control the roadmap. When Flowy needs a new feature, CJ ships it. When the skill needs updating to account for the new feature, he updates it. The tool and its documentation evolve together, in lockstep, guided by the actual use cases CJ encounters in production. No third-party product manager will ever prioritize the exact capability you need at the exact moment you need it. Your own build will, because you are both the user and the developer.

Claire, who spent years in traditional product and engineering leadership, put the historical contrast plainly: in the old workflow, a product manager would spend days creating wireframes and user flows, hand them to an engineer, and then wait for a sprint slot, prioritization, and eventually the code. Now the planning artifacts and the implementation are generated in the same session, by the same agent, guided by the same person who had the original idea. The cognitive overhead has not disappeared — the thinking still has to happen — but the execution latency has collapsed to near zero.

For developers willing to invest in building their own tooling ecosystem, the compounding returns are significant. Each skill you write, each custom tool you build, makes the next session faster and more reliable. The developer who spends time on this infrastructure gradually builds an advantage over the developer who does not — not because they have access to a better model, but because they have built a better harness around the same model.

What To Do With This

  • Name your Claude Code permission aliases clearly. Set up shell aliases for different permission levels (e.g., a "safe" mode and a "full autonomy" mode). Switching between them with a single word removes the friction that causes you to either over-restrict or inconsistently grant permissions mid-session.
  • Treat your skills as living documentation, not static files. Every time a skill produces a bad output, treat it as a feedback event: describe what went wrong and ask Claude to suggest an improvement to the skill. After a few iterations, the skill encodes your preferences in a way that survives across sessions and projects.
  • Build a meta-skill for writing skills. Create a Claude skill whose purpose is to generate and update other skills. Point it at the code or behavior you want Claude to understand, and have it produce a structured skill file with purpose, quickstart, schema, customization, and examples. This removes the blank-page problem for new skill creation.
  • Add pre-flight check sections to skills for irreversible actions. For skills that touch Git, deploy pipelines, or any other action that is hard to undo, add an explicit pre-flight section listing required conditions. This prevents Claude from proceeding when the environment is in an unexpected state.
  • Use a second model as a code reviewer, not a code generator. After completing a feature with Claude, prompt Codex or another model to review the Git diff against four criteria: accuracy to the plan/diagrams, code smells, refactoring opportunities, and architectural consistency. Pass the review findings back to Claude (or let Codex implement them directly) before committing.
  • Build your own visual planning tool or adopt a JSON-based diagram format. The key insight from Flowy is not the specific tool — it is that having a shared file format Claude can read and write, and a human can view and edit visually, eliminates the translation layer between planning and implementation. Even a simple approach (a JSON schema for flowcharts rendered by an open-source library) delivers most of the benefit.
  • Skip plan mode when you have sufficient diagram artifacts. Once you have a flow diagram and UI mockups that accurately represent the feature, "based on the flow charts and mockups, build this feature" is often a sufficient prompt. Plan mode re-generates what the diagrams already encode; skip it and trust the artifacts.
  • Use the "code as spec, then rebuild clean" pattern for exploratory development. If you have vibe-coded your way to a working but messy implementation, prompt Claude: "This is reference code. Write me a plan to re-implement this cleanly, then build it." The working prototype serves as a precise functional specification, and the rebuild eliminates the accumulated debt.
  • Parallelize exploration subagents at the start of a new session. When beginning work on a feature in an unfamiliar or large codebase, kick off three to five Claude subagents simultaneously to explore different parts of the codebase. This front-loads context gathering and dramatically reduces the time Claude spends with incomplete information when generating the plan or implementation.
  • Update skill documentation as part of every feature plan for the tool the skill covers. If you are developing a tool that has associated Claude skills, include "update documentation and related skills" as an explicit step in every development plan. Skills that drift behind the code they describe produce subtle, hard-to-diagnose failures that look like model errors but are actually documentation gaps.

Lines Worth Keeping

"Working with Claude is just such a delight. It just feels so steerable. And I think the one thing it really has is intent understanding. When I want it to dig deep, it just does it."

— CJ Hess
"I used to be when I ran these big product and engineering orgs, they used to ask me build versus buy. And I was like, oh my God, please just buy it. And now I've flipped to, of course, we should build this. Until we hit some constraint, we should build it."

— CJ Hess
"There's docs for people and there's docs for agents and those just end up being skills."

— CJ Hess
"It's almost like I want to see it visually and Claude wants to see it as Markdown, so we can kind of speak in our own way."

— CJ Hess
"Codex is like a really good curmudgeonly staff engineer that will look at your code and tell you what's wrong with it."

— Claire Vo
"Claude is very eager sometimes, and maybe jams things in there without thinking about the bigger picture. Codex, when it reviews, it almost always is like — you've implemented this pattern, but it fits nicely if you just rebuild this system a little bit. And that just keeps your code base away from all the vibe coding sins of having ten format-date functions all over your code."

— CJ Hess
"Now you can have a totally different AI engineering workflow than your colleague sitting next to you. And it's totally fine because it's making you individually a lot more efficient and effective. And you're building them yourselves for pretty cheap."

— Claire Vo
"Code is spec — now that code is so cheap to generate, you can say, generate a bunch of code, this isn't production, I'm fine throwing it away, now go build the clean version of it."

— Claire Vo

item.title: Lenny's Podcast: Product | Career | Growth - “Engineers are becoming sorcerers” | The future of software development with OpenAI’s Sherwin Wu
item.description:
The Sorcerer's Apprentice Has a Fleet of Agents

There is a textbook from 1980 that begins with a metaphor. Structure and Interpretation of Computer Programs — known in MIT circles as the Wizard Book — describes programmers as sorcerers. Programming languages are incantations. The challenge has always been: what spell do you need to cast to make the machine do what you want?

Sherwin Wu, Head of Engineering for OpenAI's API and Developer Platform, read that book in college. He has been thinking about its metaphor ever since. "It literally feels like we're wizards now," he says. "It feels like we're closer to making it feel like this magical experience where we're casting all these spells and having software do all these things for you."

But Wu is careful about which part of the Fantasia film he invokes. He does not picture the grand sorcerer. He pictures Mickey Mouse — the Sorcerer's Apprentice — who finds the master's hat, sets the brooms off on tasks, then goes to sleep. "That's vibe coding at its greatest," Wu says. "And then eventually the old sorcerer comes back and cleans everything up." The point is not that AI coding tools are dangerous. The point is that they are extraordinarily high-leverage, and leverage without judgment is how floods happen.

That tension — between the power of the tools and the discipline required to wield them — runs through everything Wu believes about the current moment in software development. At OpenAI, 95% of engineers use Codex daily. 100% of pull requests are reviewed by Codex. Engineers who lean into the tool open 70% more PRs than those who don't, and the gap is growing. By Wu's estimate, the vast majority of code that ships at OpenAI was authored by AI. And yet his message is not one of triumphalism. It is something more like: pay attention to what you're doing.

Engineers Are Becoming Tech Leads

The job of a software engineer has transformed faster than almost any other profession in recent memory. Wu does not know what job has changed more in the past two years. The role has shifted from writing code to managing agents — reviewing, steering, and providing feedback across 10 to 20 parallel Codex threads at a time.

"Engineers are becoming tech leads," Wu says. "They're basically like managers now. They're managing fleets and fleets of agents."

This is not purely additive change. It requires a different kind of expertise. The skill that matters now is knowing what to delegate and what to check, when to trust the output and when to dig in. Wu draws on another book here — Frederick Brooks's The Mythical Man-Month, written in the 1970s — which predicted that software engineering would eventually resemble a surgery room: one person doing the critical work, supported by everyone else in the room. "I want to empower them, make them feel like they're a surgeon," Wu says of his management philosophy. "Looking around corners and unblocking people, especially from an organizational perspective, is extremely, extremely useful."

His management approach has stayed largely stable through the AI transition: spend more than 50% of your time with your top 10% performers, make sure they are unblocked and heard, then let their output raise everyone around them. In an AI-accelerated environment, this principle becomes even more important. The top performers are the ones who learn the tools fastest, develop best practices, and propagate knowledge across the organization. "Just like as a manager, I'm like, yeah, go explore this," Wu says of the team running a 100% Codex-written codebase. "Whatever best practices come out of this, we have to share with the org."

He also sees a coming change in management span. Just as engineers now manage 20 Codex threads, people managers will manage much larger teams — well beyond the current best practice of 6 to 8 reports — because tools like ChatGPT, connected to Notion, Slack, and GitHub, allow managers to maintain organizational context at a scale previously impossible. "There are some teams where their EM is managing quite a few people and they're doing it pretty adeptly because of some of these tools," Wu says.

The 100% Codex Codebase Experiment

Inside OpenAI, one team is running an experiment with no escape hatch. They maintain a codebase written entirely by Codex. No switching to tab-completion or Cursor when the agent gets stuck. No rolling up your sleeves to fix it manually. If the agent cannot do it, the problem is to figure out why the agent cannot do it.

The learnings are substantial. When Codex fails to do what you want, the root cause is almost always context. "It's either under-specified or there's just not enough information around how to do something available to the agent," Wu says. The solution is not to abandon the agent but to encode more tribal knowledge into the repository itself — through code comments, code structure, markdown files, skills files, anything that gives the model better information to work with.

This reframes the problem entirely. The bottleneck is not the model's capability. The bottleneck is the information environment the model operates in. Engineers who understand this stop asking "why won't the agent do this?" and start asking "what does the agent need to know to do this?"

Wu describes the other psychological dimension of this experiment: the stress of agents not working as expected. "I've been hearing more and more there's this stress people feel when their agents aren't working," he says. Engineers who have always had the escape hatch of just writing the code themselves are now confronting what it means to work exclusively through delegation. It is a different cognitive mode. And it is, Wu believes, where the entire profession is heading.

The Models Will Eat Your Scaffolding for Breakfast

One of Wu's most important observations about the AI industry is also one of its most uncomfortable: listening too closely to customers is not always the right strategy. In a normal industry, this would be heresy. In AI, it is almost a survival principle.

"The field and the models themselves are just changing so, so quickly," Wu says. "They tend to disrupt themselves."

The phrase he keeps returning to comes from Nicolas, founder of FinTool: the models will eat your scaffolding for breakfast. In 2022, when ChatGPT launched, the models were raw. The developer ecosystem responded by building elaborate scaffolding — agent frameworks, vector stores, complex orchestration layers — to compensate for model limitations. Those limitations then disappeared, and much of the scaffolding became unnecessary.

"If you rewind back to 2022, there was all this product scaffolding to basically try and steer the model and build a framework around it to get it to do what you want," Wu says. "As you've seen the field play out, the models have just changed so much and gotten so much better that they ended up literally eating some of the scaffolding."

Vector stores are the clearest example. In 2023, vectorizing an entire corpus and optimizing retrieval felt like the essential pattern for bringing organizational context into models. Today, the better approach is to give the model search tools and let it reason — a much simpler architecture that produces better results. The entire ecosystem that had formed around the old approach became, in Wu's word, a local maximum.

The implication for builders is significant. If you listen only to what customers say they want right now — a better vector store, a more robust agent framework — you will build something excellent for where the models are today. You will not build something excellent for where the models are going. And because the models are moving very quickly, "today" is a dangerous place to optimize for.

"My general advice, and I've been giving this to people for a while and I think it's still true today," Wu says, "is make sure you're building for where the models are going and not where they are today." He has watched startups build products for capabilities the models could do at 80% — products that worked but felt almost-there. Then the models improved, and those products became exceptional. Building ahead of the curve requires accepting some friction and some wait time. Given the pace of model improvement, the wait is often short.

Wu is candid that OpenAI's own API team has made this mistake. "We kind of took some left and right turns when we shouldn't have," he says. "But yeah, the models still end up — models get better, and we're all learning the bitter lesson day in and day out." The reference is to the famous "bitter lesson" from AI research: adding human-designed scaffolding and logic tends to lose in the long run to systems that simply let models scale and learn on their own.

The Second and Third Order Effects Nobody Is Pricing In

The idea of the one-person billion-dollar startup has become a kind of ambient meme in AI circles — a vision of what extreme individual leverage looks like at the limit. Wu finds the concept compelling, but he is more interested in what it implies for the economy around it.

"I think people aren't really pricing in the second or third order effects of this," he says.

If one person can run a billion-dollar company, it also means it is far easier to run companies of any size. Wu's prediction: a massive explosion in startups, particularly vertical B2B SaaS. "To enable a one-person billion-dollar startup, there might be 100 other small startups building bespoke software that works extremely well to support other types of small one-person billion-dollar startups." The one-person newsletter or podcast that might become a large business does not need to solve support infrastructure internally. A small startup will build software specifically for that problem, priced for a small team, written by AI in a fraction of the time it once would have taken.

"There might be like 100 million-dollar startups. There might be tens of thousands of 10-million-dollar startups," Wu says. "And as an individual, it's actually pretty great to have a 10-million-dollar business. That's like enough for yourself for life at that point."

The further-order effects are harder to predict but worth thinking about. Wu sketches a world where VC economics shift: if more value accrues to 10- to 50-million-dollar companies rather than the venture-scale giants that deliver 100x returns, the structure of the funding ecosystem changes with it. The high-agency individual who builds a highly profitable micro-company is well-served by this world. The fund manager seeking billion-dollar outcomes has a harder time.

The deeper point is about distribution. In a world full of startups, each fighting for attention, having an existing audience and platform becomes an extraordinary moat. "Distribution becomes increasingly important because there are so many things trying to get your attention," Wu observes.

The Negative ROI Deployment Problem

Wu has a vantage point that almost no one else does: he talks to essentially every AI company and enterprise deploying AI at scale. His observation about a large fraction of AI deployments is bracing. "I would not be surprised if a lot of AI deployments are actually negative ROI," he says.

The diagnosis is not about model capability. It is about adoption dynamics. "We in Silicon Valley just forget that we live in a bubble," Wu says. "We are so — Twitter is a bubble, X is a bubble, Silicon Valley is a bubble, software engineering is a bubble. Most people in the world are not software engineers, are not very AI-pilled, are not following every single model release."

When Wu talks to employees at companies deploying AI outside of tech, he finds something striking: "It's like the most basic thing that they're trying to do. They have very little understanding of exactly how this technology works. They're asking very simple questions of these things. They're really not pushing it just yet."

The failure mode he sees most often is deployment that is purely top-down. C-suite buys the tools, issues the mandate, adds AI usage to performance reviews. Employees are told to use the tools but have no community of practice around them, no examples, no one to learn from. "They're like, I know I'm supposed to use this, and maybe it's on my performance review too, but I'm not sure what to do," Wu says. "They look around, no one else is doing it, there's no one else to learn from."

His recommendation is specific: find the people who are most genuinely excited about AI — often technical-adjacent roles, not software engineers, since most companies don't have software engineers — and build them into an internal tiger team. Let them explore the full range of capabilities, apply them to specific workflows, and then teach everyone else. "Find the high performers in AI adoption and empower them, let them build hackathons, let them hold seminars, do knowledge sharing, create the seeds of excitement internally."

The absence of this bottoms-up layer is why top-down mandates fail. The last-mile intricacies of any job — support, operations, finance, sales — are too specific and varied to be solved centrally. The people who actually do that work have to discover how AI fits into it themselves, learn from each other, and build institutional knowledge. Without that, the tools sit idle.

The Underrated Opportunity: Business Process Automation

Wu is openly bullish on a category that barely registers in most AI discourse: business process automation. Not the open-ended, creative work of software engineering. Not data science. The repeatable, deterministic, procedure-driven work that makes up the majority of economic activity.

"If you talk to any company that's not a tech company, there's a lot of business processes," Wu says. "Repeatable operations that some manager has iterated on. There's usually a standard operating procedure. You don't want to deviate from it that much."

This is the work that runs utilities, manages support lines, handles compliance, processes claims, and coordinates operations across large organizations. It is work where the goal is not ingenuity but consistency. Wu believes AI is extraordinarily well-suited to it, and that the opportunity is substantially larger than people recognize. "It's bigger than you would think it is based off of how people talk about it or don't talk about it on X."

The undervaluation comes from the same bubble problem: people talking about AI are mostly software engineers and AI researchers, for whom open-ended knowledge work is the norm. Business process work looks boring from that vantage point. It is not boring; it is where enormous amounts of labor hours go.

Where the Models Are Going: Multi-Hour Tasks and Native Audio

Wu is most excited about two directions for model capability over the next 12 to 18 months.

The first is the extension of coherent task duration. Current coding tools are optimized for tasks that take minutes, maybe up to 10 minutes. Models can sometimes be pushed to multi-hour tasks, but that is the exception. Wu believes that within 12 to 18 months, models could handle multi-hour tasks reliably — and eventually six-hour or day-long autonomous work. "The types of products you build around that will look very different," he says. Products today assume human interaction and short loops. Products for agents that work independently for hours require different feedback mechanisms, different trust architectures, different user interfaces.

The second is audio. "We're talking in audio. A lot of the world's business is done via audio. A lot of services and operations are done via talking in audio." Native multimodal speech-to-speech models are improving rapidly, and Wu believes audio is a substantially underrated domain — particularly for enterprise and business settings where voice interaction is the default.

OpenAI's Platform Commitment and What Builders Should Know

Wu is direct about a question every startup building on OpenAI's API worries about: when will OpenAI ship something that kills their business?

"The market is so big and so massive," he says. "I've talked to a lot of startups that have not worked out, startups that are doing really well. Every startup that I've seen that has kind of fizzled out is not because OpenAI or a big lab has come to squash them. It's because they built something and it really didn't resonate with customers. Whereas the ones that take off — even in very competitive spaces like coding — it's because they built something that people really love."

The platform commitment runs deep at OpenAI, in Wu's telling, rooted in the original mission. OpenAI cannot reach all of humanity directly — ChatGPT notwithstanding. The API exists because the only way to extend benefit everywhere is to enable others to build. "We don't think we, as a company, will be able to reach all of humanity," Wu says. "In order for us to fulfill our mission, we need to have some platform-style thing here, where we can empower other people to build."

The API stack today offers three levels of abstraction. At the base is the Responses API — low-level, unopinionated, optimized for long-running agent tasks. Above that is the Agents SDK, which provides scaffolding for building multi-agent systems with guardrails and orchestration. Above that are Agent Kit widgets for UI components and an evals API for quantitative testing of agents and workflows. The full stack lets a developer ship an agent quickly. Going low in the stack lets them build whatever they want.

Wu's closing message is less technical than it is existential. "I entered the workforce in 2014. I felt like there was a period of five to six years where it wasn't very exciting in tech. And then in the last three years it's just been the most insanely exciting, energizing period of my career." He is asking people not to let it pass by. Not to be paralyzed by the noise, and not to mistake the noise for the substance. "You don't need to know all of that to really engage with what's happening right now. Even something as simple as just install Codex or Claude and play around with it." This is the worst the models will ever be. The next two to three years will be some of the most interesting in the history of the industry. Pay attention. Participate. Don't go to sleep like the Sorcerer's Apprentice and let the brooms run wild — but don't sit on the sidelines either.

What To Do With This

  • Build for where models are going, not where they are today. Find capabilities the models can do at 80% right now and build for the full version — your product will become excellent faster than you think as models improve.
  • Stop treating agent failures as model problems; treat them as context problems. When Codex or any coding agent fails to do what you want, the root cause is almost always missing or underspecified information — encode tribal knowledge into CLAUDE.md, skills files, code comments, and structured documentation inside the repository.
  • If you are deploying AI in an organization, build a bottoms-up tiger team first. Find the 2-3 people most excited about AI (often technical-adjacent, not engineers), give them dedicated time to explore and build best practices, and have them run seminars and hackathons — top-down mandates alone reliably fail.
  • As an engineering manager, spend more than 50% of your 1:1 time with your top 10% performers. In an AI-accelerated environment, top performers compound fastest — unblocking them has disproportionate impact on team output and knowledge transfer.
  • Use ChatGPT with organizational data (Notion, Slack, GitHub) to anticipate blockers before they happen. Ask the model: "What are the active blockers on my team? What do you anticipate will block this engineer next month?" — this turns organizational context into proactive management.
  • Have Codex review 100% of PRs before human review. The workflow — AI writes code, AI reviews code, human does a lighter pass — compresses code review from 10-15 minutes to 2-3 minutes and removes the most annoying parts of the process.
  • Look seriously at business process automation, not just knowledge work. Repeatable, deterministic, procedure-driven work is a massive underserved opportunity — model it, document the standard operating procedure, then test AI on the workflow.
  • Start small and specific rather than trying to track every AI development. Install one tool (Codex, Claude) and connect ChatGPT to two or three internal data sources (Notion, GitHub, Slack) — this alone puts you ahead of most people in most organizations.
  • When evaluating a vertical B2B SaaS idea, do not rule it out because it seems too niche. Wu's thesis is that the collapsing cost of software creation will produce tens of thousands of 10-million-dollar businesses — deeply bespoke software for narrow use cases is newly viable at scale.

Lines Worth Keeping

"The models will eat your scaffolding for breakfast."

— Nicolas, founder of FinTool (quoted by Sherwin Wu)

"Make sure you're building for where the models are going and not where they are today. You end up building a better product. You may need to wait a little bit, but the models are getting so much better so quickly, you often don't need to wait that long."

— Sherwin Wu

"There's a quote from Kevin Weil, our VP of Science here. He likes saying: this is the worst the models will ever be."

— Sherwin Wu

"For engineers, I don't know what job has changed more in the past couple of years. We're just like the job of an engineer is so different — in the entire lifespan of an engineer, in the past couple of years, it's now shifted to: I don't write any more code."

— Sherwin Wu

"A lot of the time, when the coding agent is not doing what you want, it's usually a problem with context and just information that you've given it. It's either under-specified or there's just not enough information around how to do something available to the agent."

— Sherwin Wu

"I would not be surprised if a lot of AI deployments are actually negative ROI. Part of this is I think there's also general sentiment from folks around the country — basically outside of tech — that AI is being forced onto them."

— Sherwin Wu

"Every startup that I've seen that has kind of fizzled out is not because OpenAI or a big lab has come to squash them. It's because they built something and it really didn't resonate with the customers."

— Sherwin Wu

"We're all learning the bitter lesson day in and day out."

— Sherwin Wu

item.title: The Twenty Minute VC (20VC): Venture Capital | Startup Funding | The Pitch - 20VC: SaaS is Dead: Why Systems of Record Will Die in an Agentic World | What Revenue Multiple Will Software Companies Trade At? | From 7,000 to 3,000: We Need Less People Than Ever with Sebastian Siemiatkowski
item.description:
The CEO Who Shrank His Company in Half — On Purpose

Sebastian Siemiatkowski didn't ask for a single additional dollar when he told Klarna's board he wanted to launch peer-to-peer payments, trading, international remittances, savings accounts, and a full card product. He presented a budget that was actually shrinking. The company had gone from 7,000 employees to fewer than 3,000 — a 50 percent reduction, achieved mostly through natural attrition — and he planned to do more with less. The board approved everything without a fight. That, Siemiatkowski told Harry Stebbings on 20VC, is what happens when AI becomes your operating leverage.

The founder and CEO of Klarna has spent the better part of two decades building toward a single vision: become the digital financial assistant that wakes you up in the morning, tells you you're overpaying on your mortgage, and renegotiates it for you before you've had your coffee. He articulated that vision in 2015, when Klarna was losing the payments wars to Stripe and Adyen. Today, with AI finally capable of executing on that vision, he's running one of the most aggressive AI-first transformations happening inside a large financial company — and he's doing it without hiding the consequences.

The Headcount Thesis

Most CEOs soften the story. Siemiatkowski doesn't. When Klarna's AI customer service system handled the equivalent of 700 agents' worth of work in 2023, he said so publicly. The media simplified it, critics were angry, and a Bloomberg headline later made it look like he was rolling it back. He wasn't. He was making a more nuanced point: AI will handle the transactional, low-context interactions cheaply, while the premium human offering will shift toward genuine relationship — the kind of personalized, high-touch service that feels like a privilege precisely because it's rare.

To operationalize that shift, Klarna built what Siemiatkowski calls an "Uber model" for customer service — recruiting their most passionate customers, people who actually love the product, and letting them pick up flexible shifts handling support. Net Promoter Scores for those interactions have been exceptional. The gig-economy mechanic isn't a fallback; it's a deliberate upgrade of who's on the other end of the conversation.

Meanwhile, the remaining workforce at Klarna is compensated dramatically better. As the company shrank, per-head compensation grew nearly 50 percent. The deal Siemiatkowski struck with his employees was explicit: this transformation will generate more profit, and you will share in it. That's not just good ethics — it's practical. It creates organizational safety during a period of profound change, which is the prerequisite for the kind of risk-taking that transformation requires.

By 2030, he expects Klarna's headcount to be well below 2,000. The jobs that remain will be relational ones — the people in Portland talking to Nike, in Amsterdam talking to Adyen, in China talking to merchants. The jobs AI is taking are not those jobs. They're the ones that were never great human work to begin with.

The SaaS Funeral

Siemiatkowski is bearish on enterprise software in a way that goes beyond the standard "AI is disrupting SaaS" talking point. His argument has a specific mechanism. Right now, the only cost that's collapsed is the cost of generating software. The next domino is switching costs — and agents will knock it over. Once AI can pull your data out of one vendor's model and migrate it to another with a single command, the moat that made SaaS so defensible dissolves. The companies that built empires on proprietary data models and high switching costs become utilities. And utilities don't trade at 20 to 30 times sales; they trade at one to two.

He doesn't think large enterprises will vibe-code their own systems. But he does think the winner in enterprise software will look less like a collection of integrated point solutions and more like a "company in a box" — a single intelligent system built on open-source components with an AI layer on top. He built a rough prototype on a weekend to test the idea: open-source accounting software, open-source CRM, Claude agent on top, natural language queries to book invoices and check balances. It worked well. The accounting firms that currently handle those tasks for small businesses? Threatened.

For Klarna specifically, the reason to build rather than buy was epistemic. Customer service AI needs context — not documentation, which is always slightly wrong, but actual source code. How does Klarna calculate interest? The truth lives deep in the codebase. Once you realize your AI needs to read your source code to do its job properly, you realize you can't buy this off the shelf. It becomes part of your technology stack, which means it has to be yours.

AI as Compression Technology

The most intellectually distinctive segment of the conversation wasn't about Klarna at all. It was Siemiatkowski's theory of why AI models can compress all of human knowledge into a few hundred gigabytes — and what that implies for the data center boom.

His framing: AI is fundamentally a compression technology. Every large enterprise has the same information duplicated across Slack, Salesforce, Google Docs, emails — thousands of slightly different representations of the same facts. Wikipedia solved this problem with extraordinary discipline, building the world's most successful knowledge graph by making it almost impossible to create a new article (you can only create one if you search for something that doesn't exist). Enterprises have never had that discipline, which is why their data is a mess.

When a model trains on this redundant information, it doesn't store duplicates. If it hears the same fact enough times, it compresses it into a single representation. That's how the entire internet fits on a USB stick. And that's also, Siemiatkowski argues, why enterprise AI will require dramatically less compute over time, not more. As AI helps organizations identify duplication and consolidate to single sources of truth, the need to run redundant inference across redundant data collapses. He's cautious about overstating this — he doesn't want to be the guy who said there would only ever be four computers in the world — but the directional logic is clean.

The counterforce is generative entertainment: the Star Wars movie with your face on Luke's body, rendered on demand. That requires generation, not compression, and generation is compute-hungry. Which force wins — enterprise compression or consumer generation — determines whether the data center boom continues or plateaus. He genuinely doesn't know. Neither does anyone else, but most people aren't framing the question this clearly.

The Race to Be the Financial Assistant

Klarna's long game is straightforward to describe, even if it's hard to execute: take 110 million customers who trust you with their shopping and turn them into full banking relationships. The card is growing at pace, with 2-3 million active cardholders in the US in just a few months.
